topics.title: Basic Object-Oriented Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Object-Oriented Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** is a programming paradigm built around the concept of *objects* — data structures that encapsulate **state** (attributes/fields) and **behaviour** (methods/functions) into a single cohesive unit. Instead of writing a sequence of instructions that operate on separate data, OOP models software as a collection of interacting objects that mirror real-world entities.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PEMRT415)** the foundational vocabulary of OOP consists of the following primitive terms:

| # | Term | One-line Meaning |
|---|------|------------------|
| 1 | **Object** | A runtime instance of a class containing real values. |
| 2 | **Class** | A blueprint / template that defines the structure of objects. |
| 3 | **Encapsulation** | Binding data and the methods that operate on it into one unit, and restricting direct access. |
| 4 | **Abstraction** | Hiding internal implementation and exposing only the essential features. |
| 5 | **Inheritance** | Mechanism by which one class acquires properties and behaviour of another. |
| 6 | **Polymorphism** | Ability of a single interface to represent different underlying forms (many shapes). |
| 7 | **Message Passing** | The way objects communicate — one object invokes another’s method. |
| 8 | **Dynamic Binding** | Linking a procedure call to its actual code at runtime (late binding). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Focus:** The first 8 terms in the table above are the *exact vocabulary* the examiner expects when defining OOP. Writing "OOP is about objects and classes" without naming all eight concepts typically loses **2 of the 3 marks** allotted to a definition question.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you own a **Car Manufacturing Company (say, KTU Motors)**.

* You do not build *one* car at a time describing every nut and bolt from scratch. Instead, you design a **blueprint** (the **Class** — `CarBlueprint`).
* From that single blueprint, you produce **thousands of physical cars** (the **Objects** — `car1`, `car2`, `car3`). Each car has its *own* colour, fuel level, mileage — its **state**.
* Each car *knows how* to `start()`, `accelerate()`, `brake()` — its **behaviour**.
* The dashboard shows you only the **speed** and **fuel** — the internal engine combustion is hidden. That is **Abstraction**.
* The engine bay is sealed; you cannot poke at pistons directly from the driver seat — that is **Encapsulation** (data hiding).
* KTU Motors later designs an **ElectricCar** blueprint that *reuses* the original Car blueprint (wheels, brakes) and only adds a battery system — that is **Inheritance**.
* A single `start()` button works differently for a Petrol car vs. a Diesel car vs. an Electric car — that is **Polymorphism**.

```
[BLUEPRINT: Car Class]
        |
        |----instantiate----> car1 (red, petrol, 0 km)
        |----instantiate----> car2 (blue, diesel, 12 km)
        |----instantiate----> car3 (white, electric, 50 km)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **State vs Behaviour rule of thumb:** If you can describe it with a *noun* (colour, speed, balance) it is **state/attribute**. If you can describe it with a *verb* (start, withdraw, draw) it is **behaviour/method**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

OOP itself has no physical constants, but the discipline quantifies software quality using standard software-engineering metrics that examiners love:

* **Coupling:** degree of interdependence between modules — *Low Coupling* is the design goal (target value: as low as possible, ideally **0**).
* **Cohesion:** degree to which elements of a module belong together — *High Cohesion* is the design goal (target value: as high as possible, ideally **1.0** on a normalized scale).
* **Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe):** $M = E - N + 2P$ where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components of the control-flow graph. Target: $M \le 10$ per method.
* **Lines of Code (LOC):** industry-standard function size target is **$\le 50$ LOC** per method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six (or Eight) Pillars — Operational Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Object
* **What:** A concrete runtime entity with identity, state, and behaviour.
* **Why:** It maps directly to a noun in the problem domain — gives software *real-world feel*.
* **How:** Created in memory when `new ClassName(...)` is executed by the JVM / runtime engine.

#### 2.1.2 Class
* **What:** A user-defined data type (prototype) declared with the keyword `class`.
* **Why:** Provides reusability and a single source of truth for the structure.
* **How:** Defines member variables (state) and member methods (behaviour).

#### 2.1.3 Encapsulation
* **What:** Wrapping data + code into a single unit and protecting it via access modifiers.
* **Why:** Prevents external code from putting an object into an *invalid state* (e.g., negative age).
* **How:** Declare fields as `private`; expose `public` getter/setter methods that contain validation logic.

#### 2.1.4 Abstraction
* **What:** Showing only *essential* features and hiding *background* details.
* **Why:** Reduces complexity for the *user of the class*; allows the *implementer* to change internals without breaking callers.
* **How:** Achieved via `abstract` classes (0–100% abstraction in Java) or `interface` (100% abstraction in Java, before Java 8 default methods).

#### 2.1.5 Inheritance
* **What:** "IS-A" relationship — a subclass reuses and extends the superclass.
* **Why:** Promotes code reuse and establishes a natural taxonomy.
* **How:** Use `extends` (Java/C++) or `:` (Python). Java supports **single inheritance** for classes but **multiple inheritance** via interfaces.

#### 2.1.6 Polymorphism
* **What:** Same interface, different behaviour depending on the underlying type.
* **Why:** Allows generic code to operate uniformly on heterogeneous objects.
* **How:**
  * *Compile-time polymorphism* — **Method overloading** (same name, different parameter list).
  * *Run-time polymorphism* — **Method overriding** (subclass redefines a superclass method; resolved via dynamic dispatch).

#### 2.1.7 Message Passing
* **What:** Objects interact by *sending messages* (calling each other's methods).
* **Why:** Enforces encapsulation — only public methods are reachable from outside.
* **How:** Syntax `receiver.methodName(arguments)`.

#### 2.1.8 Dynamic Binding
* **What:** The actual method to execute is determined *at runtime* based on the object's real type, not the reference type.
* **Why:** Enables the Liskov Substitution Principle and true polymorphism.
* **How:** Achieved in Java through *virtual method dispatch* via the **vtable** (virtual method table) in the JVM.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Trigger Syntax (Java) | Memory Location | Key Benefit | Common Mistake |
|---------|-----------------------|-----------------|-------------|----------------|
| Class | `class Student { }` | Method Area (Metaspace) | Reusable template | Forgetting the **semicolon-free** curly braces. |
| Object | `Student s = new Student();` | Heap memory | Real entity | Declaring only the reference, never calling `new`. |
| Encapsulation | `private` fields, `public` getters/setters | Same as object | Data protection | Returning mutable references (e.g., arrays) directly. |
| Abstraction | `abstract class` / `interface` | Method Area | Hides complexity | Trying to instantiate an abstract class. |
| Inheritance | `class PGStudent extends Student` | Heap (parent + child layout) | Code reuse | Using `private` fields — they are NOT inherited accessibly. |
| Polymorphism (compile) | Same method name, different signature | Stack (resolved at compile) | Readability | Differentiating only by return type → **compile error**. |
| Polymorphism (run) | `@Override` annotation | Stack (resolved at runtime) | Extensibility | Calling a `private` method as overridden — not overridden. |
| Message Passing | `s.display()` | Stack frame | Decoupled communication | Calling a non-`public` method from outside the class. |
| Dynamic Binding | Upcast reference + overridden call | Heap object header (Klass pointer) | Late decisions | Using `final` methods — they are statically bound. |

**Standard Software Metrics (used in OOP quality questions):**

$$
\text{Cohesion (LCOM)} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\sum \text{pairs sharing instance vars}}{\text{Total pairs of methods}}
$$

$$
\text{McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity} \;=\; M \;=\; E - N + 2P
$$

$$
\text{Depth of Inheritance Tree (DIT)} \;=\; \text{maximum path length from root to leaf}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU theory answers, whenever you write a metric formula, **always state the target/ideal value** beside it. Examiners award the second mark only when both the formula and its ideal target are present.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Field | OOP Use-Case | Specific Concept |
|-------|--------------|------------------|
| Embedded Systems (Robotics) | Modelling sensors, actuators as objects | Encapsulation |
| Banking Software | Account, Customer, Transaction classes | Inheritance (SavingsAccount $\rightarrow$ Account) |
| CAD / CAM | Shape hierarchy — Circle, Square $\rightarrow$ Shape | Polymorphism (`draw()` per shape) |
| Web Back-Ends (Spring / Django) | MVC controllers as singletons | Abstraction via interfaces |
| Simulation Software | Vehicle, Pedestrian, TrafficLight objects | Message Passing (event bus) |
| Game Development | Player, Enemy, Item classes | Polymorphism + Inheritance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — From Problem Statement to Class Design

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** *"Design a class to model a Bank Account. A customer should be able to deposit, withdraw and check the balance. The balance must never be allowed to become negative."*

This single paragraph is enough to derive the complete class. Below is the *exhaustive* step-by-step derivation:

#### Step 1 — Identify Nouns (Candidate Classes & Attributes)
From the sentence we extract:
* `Account` → candidate class.
* `balance` → candidate attribute (state).
* `accountNumber`, `customerName` → other obvious attributes.

**Logic:** A noun that has both *data* and *operations* on it is a strong candidate for a class.

#### Step 2 — Identify Verbs (Candidate Methods)
* `deposit` → method.
* `withdraw` → method.
* `checkBalance` → method (often named `getBalance`).

**Logic:** A verb that operates *on* the noun becomes a member function.

#### Step 3 — Identify Constraints (Encapsulation Requirements)
* *"balance must never be negative"* → this is a **business rule** → must be enforced inside the `withdraw` method → therefore `balance` must be **private**, not directly modifiable.

**Logic:** A rule that protects the integrity of state implies encapsulation.

#### Step 4 — Final Class Blueprint

```java
public class BankAccount {
    // --- Attributes (state) — ENCAPSULATED as private ---
    private String accountNumber;
    private String customerName;
    private double balance;

    // --- Constructor ---
    public BankAccount(String accountNumber, String customerName, double openingBalance) {
        this.accountNumber = accountNumber;
        this.customerName = customerName;
        this.balance = openingBalance;
    }

    // --- Behaviour (methods) ---
    public void deposit(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0) {
            this.balance = this.balance + amount;
        }
    }

    public boolean withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > 0 && amount <= this.balance) {
            this.balance = this.balance - amount;
            return true;
        }
        return false;          // rule violated — balance protected
    }

    public double getBalance() {
        return this.balance;
    }
}
```

**Derivation of encapsulation correctness:**
The constraint `balance $\geq 0$` is enforced because the only place `balance` is mutated is inside `deposit()` (adds only positive) and `withdraw()` (subtracts only if sufficient). External code cannot set `balance = -5000` because the field is `private`.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Demonstrating All Four Pillars in One Program

```java
// ---------- Abstraction via interface ----------
interface Shape {
    double area();                 // abstract — no body
    double perimeter();
}

// ---------- Inheritance + Polymorphism ----------
class Circle implements Shape {
    private double radius;         // encapsulated state
    public Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }
    @Override public double area()     { return Math.PI * radius * radius; }
    @Override public double perimeter(){ return 2 * Math.PI * radius; }
}

class Rectangle implements Shape {
    private double length, width;
    public Rectangle(double l, double w) { this.length = l; this.width = w; }
    @Override public double area()     { return length * width; }
    @Override public double perimeter(){ return 2 * (length + width); }
}

// ---------- Driver — Message Passing + Dynamic Binding ----------
public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void printReport(Shape s) {   // upcast to abstraction
        System.out.println("Area = " + s.area() + ", Perimeter = " + s.perimeter());
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s1 = new Circle(5.0);
        Shape s2 = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);
        printReport(s1);   // resolved at runtime → Circle.area()
        printReport(s2);   // resolved at runtime → Rectangle.area()
    }
}
```

#### Line-by-line analytical matrix

| Line | OOP Concept Illustrated | Explanation |
|------|-------------------------|-------------|
| `interface Shape` | **Abstraction** | Only the *what* (area, perimeter) is declared; the *how* is unknown. |
| `class Circle implements Shape` | **Inheritance** (interface implementation) | Circle *is-a* Shape. |
| `private double radius` | **Encapsulation** | Radius cannot be changed to a negative value from outside. |
| `printReport(Shape s)` | **Message Passing + Polymorphism** | The same `s.area()` call sends different messages to different objects. |
| `s.area()` at runtime | **Dynamic Binding** | JVM consults the vtable of the actual object to resolve the method. |

#### Mathematical confirmation of polymorphism correctness

For a Circle with $r = 5$:

$$
\text{Area}_c \;=\; \pi r^{2} \;=\; \pi \times 5^{2} \;=\; 25\pi \;\approx\; 78.5398
$$

For a Rectangle with $l = 4$, $w = 6$:

$$
\text{Area}_r \;=\; l \times w \;=\; 4 \times 6 \;=\; 24
$$

The program prints both without changing the call site `s.area()` — confirming polymorphism.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Overloading vs Overriding

| Parameter | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| When resolved | **Compile time** | **Run time** |
| Where | Same class | Across two classes (inheritance) |
| Signature | Must **differ** | Must be **identical** |
| Return type | May differ (if signatures differ) | Must be **same or covariant** |
| Access modifier | No restriction | Cannot be more restrictive |
| Keyword in Java | None | `@Override` (recommended) |
| Static binding? | Yes (early binding) | No (dynamic binding) |
| Polymorphism type | Compile-time polymorphism | Run-time polymorphism |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU trap question:** *"Is method overloading a form of polymorphism?"* — **YES**, it is **compile-time** (static) polymorphism. Students often wrongly answer "No" because they associate polymorphism only with overriding.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — OOP Concept Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    clsA["Class"] --> objA["Object 1"]
    clsA --> objB["Object 2"]
    clsA --> objC["Object 3"]

    clsA --> enc["Encapsulation"]
    clsA --> abs["Abstraction"]
    clsA --> inh["Inheritance"]
    clsA --> pol["Polymorphism"]

    inh --> ovr["Method Overriding"]
    inh --> ovl["Method Overloading"]
    pol --> dyn["Dynamic Binding"]
    pol --> msg["Message Passing"]

    objA -. uses .-> msg
    objB -. uses .-> msg
    objC -. uses .-> msg

    style clsA fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style objA fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000
    style objB fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000
    style objC fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000
    style enc fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
    style abs fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
    style inh fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
    style pol fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
* One **Class** can instantiate multiple **Objects**.
* The four pillars (Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism) are *implemented inside* the class.
* **Inheritance** feeds **Overriding**, which feeds **Dynamic Binding**.
* **Message Passing** is the communication channel *between* objects.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Class Hierarchy — Demonstrating Inheritance

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Shape {
        <<interface>>
        +area() double
        +perimeter() double
    }
    class Circle {
        -radius double
        +Circle(double r)
        +area() double
        +perimeter() double
    }
    class Rectangle {
        -length double
        -width double
        +Rectangle(double l, double w)
        +area() double
        +perimeter() double
    }
    class Square {
        -side double
        +Square(double s)
        +area() double
        +perimeter() double
    }

    Shape <|.. Circle
    Shape <|.. Rectangle
    Rectangle <|-- Square
```

**Interpretation of UML arrows:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `<\|..` | Realization (class implements interface) |
| `<\|--` | Generalization (inheritance / "is-a") |
| `-` | `private` member |
| `+` | `public` member |
| `<<interface>>` | Abstract type, cannot be instantiated |

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Layout During Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stack["STACK MEMORY"] --> refS["ref s : Shape"]
    refS -. points to .-> heapCircle["HEAP : Circle object radius = 5.0"]
    heapCircle -. klass pointer .-> metaCircle["METHOD AREA : Circle.class vtable area -> Circle.area perimeter -> Circle.perimeter"]

    refR["ref r : Shape"] --> heapRect["HEAP : Rectangle object length = 4.0, width = 6.0"]
    heapRect -. klass pointer .-> metaRect["METHOD AREA : Rectangle.class vtable area -> Rectangle.area"]

    style stack fill:#fef9c3
    style heapCircle fill:#bbf7d0
    style heapRect fill:#bbf7d0
    style metaCircle fill:#fbcfe8
    style metaRect fill:#fbcfe8
```

**Why this matters for Dynamic Binding:** When `s.area()` is called, the JVM follows the reference to the heap, reads the *Klass pointer*, jumps into the Method Area vtable, and selects the correct `area()` method. This is precisely what makes polymorphism work even though the **reference type** (`Shape`) does not contain the method body.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**  
Define Object-Oriented Programming. List any **four** basic concepts of OOP.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm that uses *objects* — data structures consisting of data fields and methods together with their interactions — to design and implement software systems. It models real-world entities as classes and objects that communicate via message passing.

**Four Basic Concepts (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Objects** — runtime instances of classes containing state and behaviour.  
2. **Classes** — blueprints that define the structure (attributes) and behaviour (methods) common to all objects of a type.  
3. **Encapsulation** — binding data and methods that operate on the data into a single unit and restricting external access through access modifiers.  
4. **Polymorphism** — ability of the same interface (method call) to invoke different implementations depending on the actual object type.

*(Valid alternatives for the 4th concept: Inheritance, Abstraction, Message Passing, Dynamic Binding.)*

---

#### **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**  
Differentiate between **Class** and **Object** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Class | Object |
|-------|-------|--------|
| Definition (1 mark) | A class is a *blueprint/template* that defines attributes and methods. | An object is a *runtime instance* of a class occupying real memory. |
| Memory (½ mark) | No memory is allocated when a class is declared. | Memory is allocated in the heap when an object is created using `new`. |
| Declaration (½ mark) | Declared once using `class ClassName { … }`. | Multiple objects can be created from a single class. |
| Example (1 mark) | `class Student { String name; int rollNo; }` | `Student s1 = new Student();`  `Student s2 = new Student();` — `s1` and `s2` are two distinct objects of the same `Student` class. |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question has an **internal choice** between two full questions of 14 marks each (7 + 7).

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**  —  **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **four basic concepts** of Object-Oriented Programming with a real-world analogy for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to define a class `Employee` with private members `empId`, `name`, and `salary`. Provide methods to accept and display employee details, and a method `incrementSalary(double percent)` that increases the salary by the given percentage. Demonstrate encapsulation. **(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a)

**1. Encapsulation (2 marks)**  
*Analogy:* A **capsule** (medicine) wraps several ingredients into one shell. You take the whole capsule without knowing/exposing its inner contents. In OOP, a class binds variables and methods together and hides the internal state from outside.  
*Java Example:* `private double balance;` accessed only via `deposit()` and `withdraw()`.

**2. Abstraction (2 marks)**  
*Analogy:* When you drive a car, you interact only with the steering wheel, pedals and gear — you do not need to know how the internal combustion engine works. OOP abstraction exposes only the *essential* features via `abstract` classes or `interface`s.  
*Java Example:* `interface Shape { double area(); }` — the *what* is exposed, the *how* is hidden.

**3. Inheritance (1.5 marks)**  
*Analogy:* A child inherits physical traits from parents. In OOP, a subclass inherits attributes and methods from its superclass.  
*Java Example:* `class Manager extends Employee { … }` — `Manager` reuses fields of `Employee`.

**4. Polymorphism (1.5 marks)**  
*Analogy:* A single word **"draw"** behaves differently for a Circle (round boundary) vs. a Rectangle (four sides). In OOP, the same method name produces different behaviour based on the object.  
*Java Example:* Method overriding — `Circle.area()` and `Rectangle.area()` override `Shape.area()`.

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b)

```java
class Employee {
    // ---- ENCAPSULATION: fields are private ----
    private int empId;
    private String name;
    private double salary;

    // ---- Accept details ----
    public void setDetails(int id, String n, double s) {
        this.empId = id;
        this.name  = n;
        if (s >= 0)        // validation — encapsulation guarantee
            this.salary = s;
        else
            this.salary = 0;
    }

    // ---- Display details ----
    public void display() {
        System.out.println("ID : " + empId);
        System.out.println("Name : " + name);
        System.out.println("Salary : " + salary);
    }

    // ---- Salary increment ----
    public void incrementSalary(double percent) {
        if (percent > 0) {
            this.salary = this.salary + (this.salary * percent / 100.0);
        }
    }
}

public class EmpDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e = new Employee();
        e.setDetails(101, "Anand", 30000);
        e.display();
        e.incrementSalary(10);     // 10% increment
        System.out.println("After increment:");
        e.display();
    }
}
```

**Validation:** Initial salary = 30000. Increment 10% adds $30000 \times 10 / 100 = 3000$. Final salary = **33000**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**  —  **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare **Procedure-Oriented Programming (POP)** and **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** on at least **six** parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **Message Passing** and **Dynamic Binding** with a suitable Java example showing how they enable run-time polymorphism. **(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(a)

| Parameter | Procedure-Oriented (POP) | Object-Oriented (OOP) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Primary unit | Function / Procedure | Class / Object |
| Approach (1 mark) | Top-down decomposition. | Bottom-up object modelling. |
| Data handling (1 mark) | Functions operate on *global* data; data is exposed. | Data is *encapsulated* within objects. |
| Reusability (1 mark) | Achieved via function libraries. | Achieved via inheritance and composition. |
| Extensibility (1 mark) | Adding features requires modifying many functions. | Adding features usually means *adding* new classes (Open/Closed Principle). |
| Security (1 mark) | Low — data accessible globally. | High — access through public interface only. |
| Example (1 mark) | C, Pascal, Fortran. | Java, C++, Python, C#. |
| Overloading/Polymorphism | Not natively supported. | Natively supported. |

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Q(b)

**Message Passing (3.5 marks):**  
Message passing is the process by which an object invokes (sends a message to) the method of another object. In Java, the syntax `objectReference.methodName(arguments)` represents a message.

**Dynamic Binding (3.5 marks):**  
Dynamic binding (late binding) defers the decision of *which* method implementation to execute until **runtime**. The JVM uses the *actual* object type (stored in the heap) — not the reference type — to resolve the method via the virtual method table (vtable).

```java
class Animal {
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}
class Cat extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void sound() {
        System.out.println("Cat meows");
    }
}
public class PolyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal a;          // reference of superclass type
        a = new Dog();     // message passing via assignment
        a.sound();         // dynamic binding → Dog.sound() runs
        a = new Cat();
        a.sound();         // dynamic binding → Cat.sound() runs
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Dog barks
Cat meows
```

The call site `a.sound()` is identical in both lines, but the JVM chooses different methods based on the heap object — proving dynamic binding.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marking deductions in KTU PEMRT415 scripts (Module 1):**
> 1. **Defining OOP without naming all primitive terms** (object, class, encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, polymorphism, message passing, dynamic binding) — loses up to **2 of 3 marks** in Part-A definitions.
> 2. **Writing `class` members as `public` and claiming "this is encapsulation"** — encapsulation requires **private fields + public controlled accessors**, not merely "data and methods together". Examiners specifically deduct **1–2 marks** here.
> 3. **Confusing *overloading* with *overriding*** — they are *different* mechanisms and produce *different* polymorphism. Writing one where the other is asked costs **full sub-question marks**.
> 4. **Missing a constructor when declaring a class with private fields** — the program then cannot initialise objects properly. Always include a constructor in OOP code answers.
> 5. **Drawing UML but using wrong arrow heads** — Realization should use a **dashed** arrow with hollow triangle `<|..`; Inheritance uses a **solid** arrow with hollow triangle `<|--`. Mixing these is a frequent visual deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the *target/ideal* value** when citing metrics like cohesion, coupling, or cyclomatic complexity — examiners allocate a separate mark for the ideal value.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **OOP = programming using *objects*** that bundle **state (data)** + **behaviour (methods)**.
- [ ] **Class** = blueprint, **Object** = instance. Use `new` to instantiate.
- [ ] **Encapsulation** = `private` data + `public` getters/setters with validation. Hides *implementation*.
- [ ] **Abstraction** = `abstract class` (0–100% abstraction) or `interface` (100% abstraction pre-Java-8). Hides *complexity*.
- [ ] **Inheritance** = `extends` keyword in Java. Establishes **IS-A** relationship. Single inheritance for classes; multiple via interfaces.
- [ ] **Polymorphism** has **two flavours**:
   * *Compile-time* — **Method overloading** (same name, different parameters).
   * *Run-time* — **Method overriding** (subclass redefines parent method, resolved by **Dynamic Binding** via the vtable).
- [ ] **Message Passing** = `obj.method()` — the *only* legal way objects communicate.
- [ ] **Dynamic Binding** = method resolved at runtime using the *actual object type*, not the reference type.
- [ ] **Coupling ↓ (Low)** and **Cohesion ↑ (High)** are the twin goals of good OOP design.
- [ ] **Cyclomatic Complexity** $M = E - N + 2P$; keep $M \le 10$.
- [ ] **Final methods** and **static methods** are **NOT** virtually dispatched — they use **static binding**.
- [ ] **Constructor** is mandatory when class has only `private` fields (to initialise them).
- [ ] In UML: `<|--` = inheritance, `<|..` = interface realization, `-->` = association, `*--` = composition, `o--` = aggregation.
- [ ] Java example to remember forever: `Shape s = new Circle(5); s.area();` — proves all four pillars in **3 lines**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Java - Java Programming and Runtime Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Java - Java Programming and Runtime Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Java (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java** is a **high-level, strongly-typed, object-oriented, platform-independent, general-purpose, concurrent, and class-based programming language** developed by **James Gosling** and his team at **Sun Microsystems** in **1991**, officially released in **1995**. It is currently maintained by **Oracle Corporation**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 syllabus for *PEMRT415 – Object Oriented Programming*, the language Java is introduced as the primary vehicle for teaching the **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** paradigm. The acronym **JAVA** was originally named **"Oak"** (after an oak tree outside Gosling's office) and later renamed **JAVA** (a slang term for coffee).

### 1.2 The Java Runtime Environment (JRE) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> The **Java Runtime Environment (JRE)** is a software layer that provides the **libraries, Java Virtual Machine (JVM), and other components** necessary for a Java program (or any program compiled to Java bytecode) to *run* on a given operating system. It is the **runtime portion** of the Java platform — it contains everything required to *execute* Java applications, but **not** to develop them.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you wrote a beautiful letter (your **Java program**) in a special universal language called **Bytecode**. Now, you want the postman to deliver it anywhere in the world — to a Windows house, a Mac apartment, or a Linux bungalow.

- The **letter (Bytecode)** is universal.
- But the postman (the **OS/CPU**) only understands the local dialect.
- The **JRE** is like a **personal translator** living in every house (every machine). It instantly converts your universal letter into the local dialect so the postman (OS) can deliver the message.

| Real-World Analogy | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The letter you write | Java Source Code (`.java`) |
| Universal translated letter | Bytecode (`.class`) |
| The translator in each country | **JRE** (Java Runtime Environment) |
| The translator's brain/mouth | **JVM** (Java Virtual Machine) |
| The full toolkit (paper, pen, translator) | **JDK** (Java Development Kit) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *JRE = JVM + Libraries*. *JDK = JRE + Development Tools*.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars: JDK, JRE, JVM

| Component | Full Form | Purpose | Who Uses It? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Executes bytecode | Internal engine |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | Provides runtime environment | End users running Java apps |
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit | Compiles + Debug + Run | Developers/Programmers |

- **JDK = JRE + Compiler (javac) + Debugger (jdb) + Tools (javadoc, jar)**
- **JRE = JVM + Core Libraries (rt.jar) + Supporting Files**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU-Standard):** A typical modern **JDK 17 / JDK 21 LTS** installation size ≈ **150–200 MB**; **JRE** alone ≈ **50–80 MB**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Salient Features of Java (The 11 Pillars — KTU High-Yield List)

The KTU 2024 syllabus stresses these **11 fundamental features** of Java. Every examiner expects you to know and explain them.

1. **Simple** — Syntax derived from C/C++ but removes complex features like pointers, operator overloading, and multiple inheritance via classes.
2. **Object-Oriented** — Everything in Java is an object (except primitives). Built on the four OOP pillars: *Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism*.
3. **Platform-Independent** — Compile once (`javac`), run anywhere (`java`). Achieved via bytecode + JVM.
4. **Secured** — No explicit pointers; programs run inside the **sandbox** of the JVM; bytecode verifier checks code before execution.
5. **Robust** — Strong memory management (no manual `malloc`/`free`); automatic **garbage collection**; strong exception handling.
6. **Architecture-Neutral** — Size of primitive types is fixed (e.g., `int` is always **32 bits**, regardless of OS).
7. **Portable** — Bytecode can be transported across any platform with a conforming JRE.
8. **High-Performance** — Uses **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler** to convert hot bytecode paths into native machine code.
9. **Multi-threaded** — Built-in `Thread` class; supports concurrent execution of multiple parts of a program.
10. **Dynamic** — Loads classes on demand at runtime; supports reflection.
11. **Distributed** — Designed for the internet (RMI, EJB, URL handling built-in).

### 2.2 Java Execution Architecture — The 4-Stage Pipeline

When you write and run a Java program, KTU examiners love asking the **"Compile + Execute"** flow.

**Stage 1 — Source Code Creation:** The programmer writes `MyProgram.java`.

**Stage 2 — Compilation:** The Java compiler (`javac`) converts `.java` → `.class` (**bytecode**, not machine code). The bytecode is *not* native to any processor; it is the instruction set of the **JVM**.

**Stage 3 — Class Loading:** At runtime, the **Class Loader Subsystem** of the JVM locates and loads the `.class` files into memory (Bootstrap → Extension → Application Class Loaders).

**Stage 4 — Bytecode Verification & Execution:** The **Bytecode Verifier** checks the code for illegal operations (security). Then the **Execution Engine** (Interpreter + JIT Compiler + Garbage Collector) executes the bytecode. The JIT compiles frequently-executed ("hot") methods into native machine code, caching it for performance.

### 2.3 Components of the JRE (Detailed)

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** | Abstract computing machine; executes bytecode |
| **Core Libraries (`rt.jar` / `java.base` module)** | Pre-compiled classes (`java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`) |
| **Supporting Files** | Property files, config files, fonts, etc. |
| **Class Loader** | Loads, links, and initializes classes at runtime |
| **Bytecode Verifier** | Ensures bytecode adheres to JVM rules (security) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to students:** The "formulas" below are conceptual equivalences that the KTU board examiner expects you to write in answer scripts.

| # | KTU Conceptual Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{JDK} = \text{JRE} + \text{Development Tools}$ | JDK is a superset of JRE |
| 2 | $\text{JRE} = \text{JVM} + \text{Class Libraries}$ | JRE is a superset of JVM |
| 3 | $\therefore \text{JDK} = \text{JVM} + \text{Class Libraries} + \text{Dev Tools}$ | Combined |
| 4 | $\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{javac}} \text{Bytecode} \xrightarrow{\text{java}} \text{Output}$ | Execution pipeline |
| 5 | $\text{Bytecode Size} = f(\text{Source Code})$ | Bytecode ≈ 1× to 2× source size |
| 6 | $\text{Int size in Java} = 32 \text{ bits (always)}$ | Architecture-neutral guarantee |
| 7 | $\text{Java Applets (legacy)} + \text{Security Manager (deprecated)}$ | Old security model |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Java platform powers:
- **Android OS (mobile computing)** — Java is the original language for Android apps (Kotlin now dominant).
- **Enterprise backends** — Banking systems, e-commerce (Spring, Jakarta EE).
- **Big Data** — Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark are JVM-based.
- **Embedded & IoT** — Java ME, Raspberry Pi GPIO libraries.
- **Web servers** — Apache Tomcat, Jetty.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: How a Java Program is Executed

Let us trace a simple `HelloWorld` program from the moment of writing to the moment of seeing "Hello, World!" on the screen.

**Step 1 — Programmer writes source code (file: `HelloWorld.java`):**

```java
public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, World!");
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Compilation by `javac`:**
The JDK's compiler parses the source, performs semantic analysis, and emits bytecode.

```
$ javac HelloWorld.java
```

This produces **`HelloWorld.class`** — a binary file containing **JVM bytecode** (not machine code).

**Step 3 — Class Loading by the JVM's Class Loader Subsystem:**

The Class Loader performs three operations:

(a) **Loading** — Reads `.class` bytes from disk via the *Bootstrap Class Loader → Platform Class Loader → Application Class Loader* chain.

(b) **Linking** — Three sub-steps:
- **Verify:** Bytecode verifier ensures no stack overflow, type safety, no illegal access.
- **Prepare:** Static variables are allocated memory and initialized to default values (`int` → 0, `boolean` → false, references → null).
- **Resolve:** Symbolic references (e.g., `java/lang/System`) are replaced with direct memory addresses.

(c) **Initialization:** Static blocks and static variable assignments execute in textual order.

**Step 4 — Execution by the Execution Engine:**

The Execution Engine contains:

- **Interpreter:** Reads bytecode line-by-line and executes. Slow for hot loops.
- **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** Detects "hot methods" (executed >10,000 times by default). Compiles them to **native machine code** and caches in *Code Cache*. Future calls run at native speed.
- **Garbage Collector (GC):** Automatically reclaims memory from unreachable objects on the **heap**.

**Step 5 — The `main()` method becomes the entry point.** The JVM invokes `HelloWorld.main(String[])` and the program prints output.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Why is Java Platform-Independent?

| Step | Action | Output Format | Platform-Specific? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Write source code | `.java` text file | No |
| 2 | Compile with `javac` | `.class` bytecode | **No** (JVM-agnostic) |
| 3 | Transport `.class` over network | Bytecode | **No** |
| 4 | Execute on Windows JRE | Windows-native calls | Yes (JRE handles it) |
| 5 | Execute on Linux JRE | Linux-native calls | Yes (JRE handles it) |
| 6 | Execute on macOS JRE | macOS-native calls | Yes (JRE handles it) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** The bytecode remains **identical** across all platforms. The **JRE (containing the JVM) is what changes per platform**. Hence, the slogan **"Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)"** holds.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Class Loading Hierarchy

The Java Class Loader follows the **Delegation Model** (parent-first delegation):

$$
\text{Application Class Loader} \rightarrow \text{Platform Class Loader} \rightarrow \text{Bootstrap Class Loader} \rightarrow \text{Native (C/C++) code}
$$

When a class like `String` is requested:

1. Application CL asks Platform CL.
2. Platform CL asks Bootstrap CL.
3. Bootstrap CL finds it in `rt.jar` (core library) and returns it.
4. If not found, the search bubbles back down.

This prevents malicious code from replacing core classes (security).

### 3.4 The Three-Tier Component Model

$$
\boxed{
\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}
}
$$

- If you are an **end-user** installing Java to run a game → install **JRE** only.
- If you are a **developer** writing Java code → install **JDK** (which automatically brings JRE).
- The **JVM** is *not* installed standalone — it always comes bundled inside the JRE.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Java Program Execution Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer writes HelloWorld.java] --> B[Source Code .java]
    B --> C{javac compiler}
    C --> D[Bytecode HelloWorld.class]
    D --> E[Transport over network or filesystem]
    E --> F[JVM Class Loader Subsystem]
    F --> G[Bytecode Verifier Security Check]
    G --> H[Execution Engine]
    H --> I[Interpreter]
    H --> J[JIT Compiler native code]
    H --> K[Garbage Collector]
    I --> L[Final Output Hello World]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 JDK vs JRE vs JVM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JDK[Java Development Kit]
        subgraph JRE[Java Runtime Environment]
            subgraph JVM[Java Virtual Machine]
                CL[Class Loader]
                BV[Bytecode Verifier]
                EE[Execution Engine]
                GC[Garbage Collector]
            end
            LIB[Core Libraries java.lang, java.util, java.io]
        end
        TOOLS[Dev Tools javac, jdb, javadoc, jar]
    end
    JDK --> JRE
    JDK --> TOOLS
    JRE --> JVM
    JRE --> LIB
```

### 4.3 Class Loader Delegation Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Class Loader] -->|Delegates first| B[Platform Class Loader]
    B -->|Delegates first| C[Bootstrap Class Loader]
    C -->|Searches rt.jar core libs| D{Found?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Return Class to App CL]
    D -->|No| B
    B -->|Searches ext libs| F{Found?}
    F -->|Yes| E
    F -->|No| A
    A -->|Searches classpath| G{Found?}
    G -->|Yes| E
    G -->|No| H[ClassNotFoundException]
```

### 4.4 Java Compilation vs C/C++ Compilation (Comparison Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CC[C / C++ Workflow]
        C1[source.c] --> C2[Compile to object.o]
        C2 --> C3[Link with libs]
        C3 --> C4[Native Executable.exe specific to OS]
    end
    subgraph JV[Java Workflow]
        J1[Hello.java] --> J2[Compile to bytecode.class]
        J2 --> J3{On any OS with JRE}
        J3 --> K1[Windows JVM]
        J3 --> K2[Linux JVM]
        J3 --> K3[macOS JVM]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
>
> **Q1. Define the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). Mention any two of its main functions.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is an abstract computing machine, or *runtime engine*, that enables a computer to run Java (or any JVM-compatible) bytecode. It is the component of the JRE that actually executes a compiled Java program.
>
> **Two main functions:**
> 1. **Loads, verifies, and executes** Java bytecode.
> 2. **Provides runtime memory** for the program (heap, stack, method area) and performs **garbage collection** to reclaim unused memory.
> 3. *(Optional 3rd)* Provides a **platform-independent execution environment**, converting bytecode into platform-specific native instructions.

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | JDK | JRE | JVM |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Full Form | Java Development Kit | Java Runtime Environment | Java Virtual Machine |
> | Purpose | To **develop** Java applications | To **run** Java applications | To **execute bytecode** |
> | Contains | JRE + Development tools | JVM + Libraries | Engine + Memory subsystems |
> | Includes `javac`? | **Yes** | No | No |
> | Audience | Developers | End users | Internal to JRE |
>
> **Relation:** $\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the salient features of Java. Mention any **seven** features with a one-line description for each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the architecture of the Java Runtime Environment with a neat block diagram. List its main components.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### (a) Seven Salient Features of Java [7 marks = 7 × 1 mark]

1. **Simple** — Java omits complex C++ features like pointers, operator overloading, and multiple inheritance, making it easier to learn.
2. **Object-Oriented** — Everything in Java is an object (except primitives), supporting encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction.
3. **Platform-Independent** — A Java program compiles into bytecode that runs on any machine with a JRE, achieving **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**.
4. **Secured** — Programs run inside the JVM sandbox, with no explicit pointers and a bytecode verifier that checks code for malicious operations.
5. **Robust** — Strong memory management via automatic **garbage collection**, strict type checking, and comprehensive exception handling.
6. **Multi-threaded** — Java has built-in support for concurrent execution using the `Thread` class and `synchronized` keyword.
7. **Architecture-Neutral** — Primitive data types have a fixed size (e.g., `int` is always 32 bits), so programs behave identically on any processor.

> **Examiner's Note:** Listing **any 7** of the 11 features with a **one-line description** is sufficient for 7 marks. A student who writes 4–5 features only will be capped at 4–5 marks.

#### (b) Architecture of JRE [7 marks]

**Block diagram description (write this in exam):**

```
┌─────────────────────── JDK ──────────────────────────┐
│                                                      │
│   ┌────────────── JRE ───────────────────────┐       │
│   │                                          │       │
│   │  ┌────── JVM ────────────────────┐      │       │
│   │  │ • Class Loader Subsystem      │      │       │
│   │  │ • Bytecode Verifier           │      │       │
│   │  │ • Execution Engine            │      │       │
│   │  │   – Interpreter               │      │       │
│   │  │   – JIT Compiler              │      │       │
│   │  │   – Garbage Collector         │      │       │
│   │  └───────────────────────────────┘      │       │
│   │                                          │       │
│   │  Core Libraries (rt.jar / java.base)     │       │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────┘       │
│                                                      │
│   Development Tools: javac, jdb, javadoc, jar, appletviewer │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Main components of JRE (list for 2 marks):**

1. **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** – The core engine that loads, verifies, and executes bytecode.
2. **Core Class Libraries** – Pre-compiled standard classes (`java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`, `java.sql`).
3. **Class Loader Subsystem** – Loads `.class` files at runtime using the *delegation model*.
4. **Bytecode Verifier** – Validates bytecode for type safety, stack bounds, and access control.
5. **Execution Engine** – Contains the interpreter, JIT compiler, and garbage collector.
6. **Supporting Files** – Properties, configuration, and native libraries (`.dll`/`.so`).

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the process of compiling and executing a Java program. What is bytecode?

**(b) [7 marks]** What is a Class Loader in Java? Explain the *Delegation Model* and the three phases of class loading: *Loading, Linking, and Initialization*.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### (a) Compiling and Executing a Java Program [7 marks]

**Step-by-step explanation (3 marks):**

1. **Source Code Creation:** The programmer creates a file with a `.java` extension, e.g., `HelloWorld.java`.
2. **Compilation:** The JDK's `javac` compiler compiles `HelloWorld.java` into a `.class` file (`HelloWorld.class`) containing **bytecode**.
3. **Class Loading:** At runtime, the JVM's class loader reads the `.class` file into memory.
4. **Bytecode Verification:** The verifier checks the code for safety (no illegal memory access, type mismatches).
5. **Execution:** The Execution Engine interprets the bytecode line by line. For "hot" methods, the JIT compiler translates them into native machine code for performance.
6. **Output:** The `main()` method is invoked, and the program produces output.

**Block diagram (4 marks — write in exam):**

```
   HelloWorld.java   ──javac──▶  HelloWorld.class
   (Source Code)                  (Bytecode)
                                       │
                                       ▼
                              ┌─────────────────┐
                              │  JVM / JRE       │
                              │ ┌──────────────┐ │
                              │ │Class Loader  │ │
                              │ ├──────────────┤ │
                              │ │Bytecode      │ │
                              │ │Verifier      │ │
                              │ ├──────────────┤ │
                              │ │Execution     │ │
                              │ │Engine (JIT)  │ │
                              │ └──────────────┘ │
                              └─────────────────┘
                                       │
                                       ▼
                                OUTPUT on Screen
```

**Bytecode (definition for 1 mark):**
Bytecode is the **intermediate, platform-neutral instruction set** generated by the Java compiler. It is the *machine language of the JVM*, not of any physical processor. The `.class` file contains this bytecode along with metadata (constant pool, method signatures, etc.).

#### (b) Class Loader in Java [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **Class Loader** is a subsystem of the JVM responsible for **loading, linking, and initializing** Java classes dynamically at runtime. It transforms fully-qualified class names (e.g., `java.util.ArrayList`) into in-memory `Class` objects.

**Three Phases of Class Loading (3 marks):**

1. **Loading:** The class loader reads the `.class` file from the file system / JAR / network, and stores the resulting byte data in the **Method Area** of JVM memory.
2. **Linking:** This phase has three sub-steps:
   - **Verify** — Ensures the bytecode is valid and follows JVM specifications.
   - **Prepare** — Allocates memory for static variables and initializes them to defaults.
   - **Resolve** — Replaces symbolic references with direct references.
3. **Initialization:** The JVM executes static variable assignments and static blocks in textual order.

**Delegation Model (3 marks):**
Java follows the **parent-first delegation model**:

- When a class is requested, the **Application Class Loader** first delegates the request to its parent, the **Platform Class Loader**.
- The Platform Class Loader in turn delegates to the **Bootstrap Class Loader** (which loads core classes from `rt.jar` / `java.base` module).
- If the Bootstrap CL finds the class, it returns it. If not, control returns downward.
- This prevents untrusted code from loading its own version of core classes — a **critical security feature** of the JVM sandbox.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse JRE with JVM.** Saying *"JVM runs the program"* without mentioning that JRE = JVM + libraries costs you 1 mark. Always write the relation **JRE = JVM + Libraries**.
> 2. **Do NOT say "Java is interpreted only."** It is **both interpreted AND compiled (JIT)**. The KTU 2019/2023 question paper explicitly tested this — students lost 2 marks for omitting the JIT compiler.
> 3. **Do NOT write "Java has pointers."** Java has *references*, not pointers. Stating the opposite will cost you 1–2 marks in any OOP question.
> 4. **Bytecode ≠ Machine Code.** Bytecode runs on the JVM; machine code runs on the physical CPU. Mixing these up is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **For the 7-mark feature question**, listing fewer than 7 features is the **#1 reason students lose marks**. Aim for 7–8 features with crisp one-line descriptions.
> 6. **Diagrams are mandatory** for 7-mark questions. A neat block diagram (boxes + arrows) is worth **at least 2–3 marks** by itself.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print and Memorize**

- ☑ **Java** = high-level, OOP, platform-independent language by **James Gosling, Sun Microsystems, 1995** (now Oracle).
- ☑ **Original name** = **Oak**; renamed to **Java** (coffee slang).
- ☑ **Three components hierarchy:** $\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$.
- ☑ **JDK** = JRE + `javac` + tools (for developers).
- ☑ **JRE** = JVM + core libraries (for end users).
- ☑ **JVM** = the runtime engine that executes bytecode.
- ☑ **WORA** = *Write Once, Run Anywhere* (Java's signature slogan).
- ☑ **Source code (`.java`)** → `javac` → **Bytecode (`.class`)** → `java` → Output.
- ☑ **Bytecode** = platform-neutral JVM instructions (NOT machine code).
- ☑ **JIT Compiler** = converts hot bytecode to native code at runtime → boosts performance.
- ☑ **Garbage Collector** = automatic memory manager (heap).
- ☑ **Bytecode Verifier** = security check before execution.
- ☑ **Class Loader** uses the **parent-first Delegation Model** with three loaders: Bootstrap → Platform → Application.
- ☑ **Three phases of class loading:** Loading → Linking (Verify, Prepare, Resolve) → Initialization.
- ☑ **11 Features to remember** (memorize all): Simple, OOP, Platform-Independent, Secured, Robust, Architecture-Neutral, Portable, High-Performance, Multi-threaded, Dynamic, Distributed.
- ☑ **No pointers, no multiple inheritance, no operator overloading** — Java removed these for safety and simplicity.
- ☑ **Java has references, not pointers.**
- ☑ **`int` is always 32 bits, `long` is always 64 bits** — architecture neutrality.
- ☑ **Latest LTS versions**: Java 17, Java 21 (as of 2024).
- ☑ **Java Editions**: Java SE (Standard), Java EE / Jakarta EE (Enterprise), Java ME (Micro/Embedded).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development Platforms - Java Virtual Machine (JVM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development Platforms - Java Virtual Machine (JVM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is the cornerstone of the Java platform. It is an **abstract computing machine** — a runtime engine — that enables a computer to execute Java **bytecode**. The JVM is the component responsible for loading, verifying, linking, initializing, and executing Java class files, while providing memory management, thread synchronization, and exception handling services at runtime.

A more rigorous KTU-aligned definition:

> The **JVM** is a **specification** (documented in JLS/JVMS by Oracle) that defines an abstract machine whose instruction set, registers, stack, heap, and method area are realized in software. Concrete implementations (HotSpot, OpenJ9, Eclipse, GraalVM) provide platform-dependent realizations, thereby guaranteeing **platform independence** for compiled Java programs.

The JVM performs three primary roles:

1. **Code Loader** — Loads `.class` files (bytecode) into memory.
2. **Verifier** — Ensures bytecode integrity and security (no illegal memory access).
3. **Executor** — Interprets or compiles bytecode into native machine instructions.

### Bytecode — The "Language" of the JVM

When a `.java` file is compiled by `javac`, the output is a `.class` file containing **bytecode** — a low-level, platform-neutral instruction set. Bytecode is **not** machine code of any physical CPU. It is the JVM's *own* ISA (Instruction Set Architecture).

```java
// Source
public class Hello { public static void main(String[] a) { System.out.println("Hi"); } }
```
```text
// Resulting Bytecode (partial)
0: getstatic  #2   // Field System.out
3: ldc        #3   // String "Hi"
5: invokevirtual #4 // Method println
8: return
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The JVM as a Universal Power Socket Adapter**
>
> Imagine every computer in the world has a different type of electrical socket (Windows, Linux, macOS, Raspberry Pi…). Your Java appliance (the `.class` file with bytecode) has a **universal plug**. The **JVM** is the *adapter* that fits that universal plug into *any* socket. You don't modify the appliance; you simply provide the right adapter for the country you are visiting. This is the essence of **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**.

Another useful analogy:

| Real-World Concept | JVM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A diplomat who speaks many languages | JVM — interprets one universal language (bytecode) into native calls |
| An interpreter in a UN assembly | **Bytecode Interpreter** — translates each instruction on-the-fly |
| A court stenographer recording words | **JIT Compiler** — records and replays faster translations |
| A city's waste-collection service | **Garbage Collector** — frees unused memory automatically |

## 1.3 Key Standards, Specifications, and Metrics

The following constants and acronyms are *standard KTU exam terminology* and should be memorized verbatim:

- **JDK** — Java Development Kit (compiler + JRE + tools). Versions: **JDK 8, 11, 17, 21** (LTS releases).
- **JRE** — Java Runtime Environment (JVM + core libraries). Required only to *run* Java programs.
- **JVM** — Java Virtual Machine (the runtime engine itself).
- **JIT** — Just-In-Time Compiler (performance accelerator inside JVM).
- **GC** — Garbage Collector (automatic memory manager).
- **WORA** — **Write Once, Run Anywhere** — the design goal achieved by bytecode + JVM.
- **JNI** — Java Native Interface (bridge to C/C++ libraries).
- **TIERED Compilation** — Modern JVMs use C1 (client) + C2 (server) compilers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spec vs Implementation Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU)**
> The JVM is **a specification**, not a single piece of software. The same spec is implemented differently by:
> - **HotSpot** (Oracle/OpenJDK, the reference implementation)
> - **OpenJ9** (Eclipse/IBM)
> - **GraalVM** (Oracle's polyglot VM)
> - **Eclipse (Excelsior)** (commercial AOT)
>
> All of these are *valid JVMs* because they conform to the JVMS spec.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & JVM Architecture Master Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Subsystems of the JVM

The JVM is conventionally divided into **three major subsystems**. Each is logically distinct but tightly integrated:

### A. Class Loader Subsystem (CLS)
Responsible for **loading**, **linking**, and **initializing** `.class` files.

**Phase 1 — Loading**
- Reads the `.class` binary stream.
- Creates a binary representation in **Method Area** (Class metadata, Constant Pool, Field/Method data).
- A `Class<?>` object is materialized in the **Heap** as a representation of the class's metadata.

**Phase 2 — Linking**
1. **Verify** — Bytecode verifier checks structural correctness, type safety, stack consistency. *Prevents malformed class files from damaging the JVM.*
2. **Prepare** — Allocates memory for `static` fields and initializes them to **default values** (0, false, null).
3. **Resolve** — Symbolic references (e.g., `java/lang/Object`) are replaced with direct references (memory addresses / CPTE indices).

**Phase 3 — Initialization**
- Executes `static` initializers and static-block assignments in source order.
- Triggered by: `new`, static field access (not compile-time constant), static method call, subclass initialization, reflection entry, or JVM start-up of `Main` class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ClassLoader Hierarchy (Delegation Model — "Parents First")**
> 1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** — Loads core Java (`rt.jar`, `java.lang`, `java.util`) from `<JAVA_HOME>/jre/lib`. Implemented in native C/C++. Has no parent; represented as `null` in Java.
> 2. **Platform (Extension) ClassLoader** — Loads extension classes (`<JAVA_HOME>/lib/ext`, `java.ext.dirs`). Parent: Bootstrap.
> 3. **Application (System) ClassLoader** — Loads classes from the **classpath** (`-cp`, `CLASSPATH`). Parent: Platform.
> 4. **Custom ClassLoaders** — User-defined (e.g., Tomcat's `WebAppClassLoader`). Parent: Application.
>
> **Rule:** When asked to load a class, a loader **delegates to its parent first**. Only if the parent fails (`ClassNotFoundException`) does the child attempt to load. This prevents core classes from being spoofed — a critical **security feature**.

### B. Runtime Data Areas (Memory Architecture)

These are the **memory regions** allocated when the JVM starts:

| Memory Area | Per-Thread? | Contents | Throws |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Method Area** | Shared | Class metadata, Constant Pool, static fields, method bytecode, JIT-compiled native code | `OutOfMemoryError: PermGen` (pre-Java 8) / `Metaspace` |
| **Heap** | Shared | All **objects**, instance variables, arrays | `OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space` |
| **Stack (JVM Stack)** | Per-Thread | Stack frames: local variables, operand stack, return address | `StackOverflowError`, `OutOfMemoryError` |
| **PC Register** | Per-Thread | Address of the current executing JVM instruction | — |
| **Native Method Stack** | Per-Thread | State for C/C++ native methods (JNI) | `StackOverflowError` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack Frame Anatomy** (created per method call)
> - **Local Variable Array** — `this`, method parameters, locals.
> - **Operand Stack** — Workspace for bytecode arithmetic (e.g., `iadd` pops 2 ints, pushes sum).
> - **Frame Data** — Constant-pool reference, exception table, return address.

### C. Execution Engine

The "brain" that actually executes bytecode. Three components:

**1. Interpreter**
- Reads bytecode line-by-line and dispatches to native CPU instructions.
- **Pro:** Fast startup, low memory overhead.
- **Con:** Slow steady-state execution (no optimization).

**2. JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler**
- Detects **hot methods** (called many times — default threshold ≈ **10,000 invocations**).
- Compiles them to native machine code, **in-memory**, for direct CPU execution.
- **C1 (Client) Compiler** — Optimizes for fast startup.
- **C2 (Server) Compiler** — Aggressive optimizations (inlining, loop unrolling, escape analysis).
- **Tiered Compilation** — Combines C1 + C2 for balanced performance.

**3. Garbage Collector (GC)**
- Reclaims memory occupied by **unreachable objects** on the heap.
- Mark unreachable objects via **reachability analysis** starting from **GC Roots** (stack locals, active threads, JNI refs, static fields).
- Common algorithms:
  - **Serial GC** — Single-threaded, stop-the-world.
  - **Parallel GC** (Java 8 default) — Multi-threaded throughput-oriented.
  - **G1 GC** (Java 9+ default) — Region-based, predictable pauses.
  - **ZGC / Shenandoah** — Sub-millisecond pauses (Java 11+).

### D. JNI + Native Method Libraries

The **Java Native Interface** allows the JVM to invoke platform-specific C/C++ libraries (e.g., system calls, hardware drivers). The **Native Method Stack** holds state for these calls.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision cheat sheet** mapped to standard KTU questions.

| # | Concept | Symbol / Definition | Boundary / Limit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | JVM | Abstract stack-based machine | Specification (JVMS Ch. 2–7) | Spec is platform-neutral |
| 2 | Bytecode | 1-byte opcode + operands | 256 opcodes (0x00–0xFF) | Stored in `.class` file |
| 3 | Stack-based ISA | Operand stack, not registers | JVM is **stack-based** | x86 is register-based |
| 4 | JIT Threshold | `CompileThreshold = 10000` | Tunable via `-XX:` | Hotspot detection count |
| 5 | Heap formula (rough) | `MaxHeap ≈ Xms` | `-Xms`, `-Xmx` | Default ratio 1:64 of RAM |
| 6 | Stack size | `-Xss<size>` | Default **512 KB / 1 MB** | Per-thread |
| 7 | Metaspace | Native memory | Replaces PermGen (Java 8+) | Auto-grows by default |
| 8 | GC Root | Set of live references | Static fields, stack, JNI | Unreachable = eligible |
| 9 | WORA | "Write Once, Run Anywhere" | Bytecode is portable | Achievable via JVM |
| 10 | ClassLoader parent | `parent.loadClass(name)` | `null` = Bootstrap | Delegation model |

### Real-World Utility of the JVM

The JVM is one of the most successful abstractions in computing history. Its influence extends far beyond Java:

| Domain | JVM Application |
|---|---|
| **Android (Dalvik/ART)** | Bytecode evolved from JVM; ART executes DEX (similar stack machine) |
| **Big Data** | Apache Spark, Hadoop, Kafka — all run on JVM |
| **Financial Trading** | Low-latency trading systems use G1/ZGC for deterministic pauses |
| **Serverless** | GraalVM Native Image compiles JVM bytecode ahead-of-time (AOT) |
| **Polyglot Programming** | Kotlin, Scala, Groovy, Clojure all compile to JVM bytecode |
| **Cloud Microservices** | Quarkus, Micronaut, Spring Boot leverage JVM optimization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Execution Walk-through & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The JVM Lifecycle — Derivation of the Startup Sequence

The JVM is *constructed* by the operating system the moment you execute `java MyApp`. Each step is mandatory and order-sensitive:

> **Step 1 → JVM instance creation**
> The OS process `java.exe`/`java` is launched. A new JVM instance is allocated.

> **Step 2 → Bootstrap ClassLoader creation**
> The first ClassLoader instantiated is the **Bootstrap** loader (native, C-implemented). It reads `<JAVA_HOME>/lib` and loads `java.lang.Object`, `Class`, `ClassLoader`, and other essentials.

> **Step 3 → Platform + Application ClassLoader creation**
> These Java-implemented loaders are instantiated; their `parent` field is wired.

> **Step 4 → Main class loading (Initialization Phase)**
> The class passed to `java` command (e.g., `MyApp`) is loaded, linked, **initialized**. This triggers the cascade of `<clinit>` method executions in dependency order.

> **Step 5 → `main(String[])` invocation**
> A new **JVM Stack** is created for the main thread. A stack frame for `main` is pushed. Execution begins.

> **Step 6 → Program execution**
> The **Execution Engine** dispatches each bytecode instruction. The interpreter runs cold methods; the **JIT** kicks in for hot methods. The **GC** runs periodically or when allocation pressure exceeds thresholds.

> **Step 7 → Daemon thread shutdown**
> When only **daemon threads** remain, the JVM exits. The OS process terminates.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — `new Object()` Allocation Walkthrough

We trace exactly what happens in the JVM when the following Java statement executes:

```java
Object o = new Object();
```

> **Step 1 — Bytecode emission by `javac`**
>
> The compiler emits bytecode equivalent to:
> ```
> 0: new           #2      // class java/lang/Object
> 3: dup
> 4: invokespecial #3      // Method java/lang/Object."<init>":()V
> 7: astore_1
> 8: return
> ```

> **Step 2 — Class resolution**
>
> Before `new` can execute, the symbolic constant-pool entry `#2 = Class java/lang/Object` must be **resolved** (if not already). Resolution checks if `Object` class is loaded; if not, the ClassLoader subsystem is invoked.

> **Step 3 — `new` opcode execution**
>
> The JVM computes the memory footprint of `Object` (which has zero instance fields ⇒ minimal size, typically **16 bytes** on 64-bit HotSpot with compressed oops).
> Memory is allocated on the **Eden** region of the **Heap** (Young Generation).
> A pointer to this allocated memory is **pushed onto the Operand Stack**.

> **Step 4 — `dup` opcode**
>
> Duplicates the top of the Operand Stack (so we have *two* copies of the reference — one for `<init>` consumption, one for `astore_1`).

> **Step 5 — `invokespecial #3 // <init>`**
>
> A new **stack frame** is created for the instance initializer. The JVM zero-initializes the object (done by `new` itself actually), then executes `<init>` body. Pop the consumed reference off the operand stack. Return address pushed.

> **Step 6 — `astore_1`**
>
> The remaining object reference is stored into **local variable slot 1** (variable `o`).

> **Step 7 — `return`**
>
> The `main` method's stack frame is popped. The main thread continues; the object `o` is **reachable** via local variable `1` of the current frame, so it is a **GC Root**.

### Analytical Matrix — Memory Region Used Per Opcode

| Bytecode | Memory Touched | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `new` | Heap (Eden) | Allocate raw object |
| `getstatic` | Method Area | Resolve & push static field value |
| `ldc` | Constant Pool (Method Area) | Push literal onto operand stack |
| `istore`, `astore` | Stack (local variable array) | Pop top, store in locals |
| `iload`, `aload` | Stack (local variable array) | Push local onto operand stack |
| `iadd` | Operand Stack | Pop 2, push sum |
| `invokevirtual` | Heap (vtable lookup), Stack (new frame) | Polymorphic dispatch |
| `return` | Stack (frame pop) | Return to caller |
| `athrow` | Stack (exception handler table lookup) | Locate & jump to handler |

## 3.3 Heap Generational Model — Mathematical Intuition

Modern JVMs use a **generational hypothesis**: *most objects die young.* The heap is therefore partitioned:

$$\text{Heap} = \text{Young Generation} + \text{Old Generation} + \text{Metaspace (off-heap)}$$

with

$$\text{Young} = \text{Eden} + \text{Survivor}_0 + \text{Survivor}_1$$

> **Object age promotion rule:**
> Each time an object survives a **Minor GC**, its *age counter* increments. After **MaxTenuringThreshold** (default 15), it is promoted to **Old Generation**.

$$\text{Promote to Old} \iff \text{age} \geq \text{MaxTenuringThreshold}$$

### GC Trigger Conditions

1. **Minor GC** — Triggered when **Eden** becomes full.
2. **Major GC (Full GC)** — Triggered when **Old** fills up or `System.gc()` invoked (hint only).
3. **OOME** — Triggered when even after a full GC, allocation still fails.

### Tuning Knobs (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Flag | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `-Xms<size>` | Initial heap size | 1/64 of RAM |
| `-Xmx<size>` | Maximum heap size | 1/4 of RAM |
| `-Xss<size>` | Stack size per thread | 512 KB–1 MB |
| `-XX:NewRatio=n` | Old:Young ratio | 2 (Old = 2× Young) |
| `-XX:MaxTenuringThreshold=n` | Promotion age | 15 |
| `-XX:+UseG1GC` | Select G1 collector | Java 9+ default |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JVM Master Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classCls["Class File .class Bytecode"]
    subgraph clsSys["Class Loader Subsystem"]
        loaderA["Loading"]
        linkerA["Linking Verify Prepare Resolve"]
        initA["Initialization static blocks"]
        bootA["Bootstrap ClassLoader"]
        platA["Platform ClassLoader"]
        appA["Application ClassLoader"]
    end
    subgraph rda["Runtime Data Areas"]
        methA["Method Area Class Metadata Constant Pool Static Fields"]
        heapA["Heap Young Gen Old Gen Objects"]
        stackA["JVM Stack Per Thread Local Vars Operand Stack"]
        pcA["PC Register Per Thread"]
        natA["Native Method Stack"]
    end
    subgraph exEng["Execution Engine"]
        interpA["Interpreter"]
        jitA["JIT Compiler C1 Client C2 Server"]
        gcA["Garbage Collector G1 ZGC Parallel"]
    end
    jniA["JNI Native Method Libraries"]

    classCls --> loaderA
    loaderA --> linkerA
    linkerA --> initA
    bootA --> platA
    platA --> appA
    appA --> loaderA

    loaderA --> methA
    loaderA --> heapA

    initA --> exEng
    exEng --> stackA
    exEng --> pcA
    exEng --> methA
    exEng --> heapA
    interpA -. hot method detected .-> jitA
    jitA -. native code cached .-> methA
    gcA --> heapA

    exEng --> jniA
    jniA --> natA
```

## 4.2 ClassLoader Delegation Hierarchy (Parent-First Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    req["Request loadClass com.example.MyApp"]
    appL["Application ClassLoader"]
    platL["Platform ClassLoader"]
    bootL["Bootstrap ClassLoader rt.jar / java.base"]
    found["Class found? Yes Return"]
    fail["ClassNotFoundException Child tries next"]
    end1["End"]

    req --> appL
    appL -->|"parent.loadClass()"| platL
    platL -->|"parent.loadClass()"| bootL
    bootL --> found
    found --> end1
    bootL -->|"not found"| fail
    fail --> platL
    platL -->|"not found"| appL
    appL -->|"not found"| end1
```

## 4.3 Heap Generational Layout — Region-Based View (G1 GC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph yg["Young Generation"]
        ed["Eden Region new allocations"]
        s0["Survivor 0 age 1 to 5"]
        s1["Survivor 1 age 6 to 15"]
    end
    subgraph og["Old Generation"]
        oh["Old Regions age greater than MaxTenuringThreshold 15"]
    end
    subgraph hum["Humongous Regions"]
        h["Objects greater than 50 percent of region size"]
    end
    meta["Metaspace Off Heap Native Memory Class Metadata"]
    ed -->|"Minor GC survivor age less than threshold"| s0
    s0 -->|"age 15 or threshold"| oh
    ed -->|"large object greater than 50 percent of region"| h
    oh -->|"Major GC Full GC"| meta
```

## 4.4 JIT Tiered Compilation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Bytecode Source"]
    s2["Interpreter Mode Cold Execution"]
    s3["C1 Compiler Simple Optimizations Profiling"]
    s4["C2 Compiler Aggressive Optimizations Inlining Escape Analysis"]
    s5["Native Machine Code Cached in Code Cache"]
    s6["Direct CPU Execution"]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 -->|"invocation count greater than 1500"| s3
    s3 -->|"invocation count greater than 10000"| s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 -->|"deoptimization rare path"| s2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** patterns: Part A carries 3 marks (short answer), Part B carries 14 marks (internal choice between Question A and Question B with sub-parts 7 + 7 marks).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Component | Full Form | Purpose | Includes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Runtime engine executing bytecode | Spec + Implementation |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | To **run** Java programs | JVM + Core Libraries (rt.jar) |
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit | To **develop** + run Java programs | JRE + Compiler (`javac`) + Debugger + Tools |

**Key sentence (mandatory for full marks):** *The JDK contains the JRE, which in turn contains the JVM. The JVM alone is only a specification; a real runtime environment (JRE) bundles a concrete JVM implementation along with the standard class libraries.*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**What is bytecode? Why is it called the key to Java's platform independence?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Bytecode** is the intermediate, platform-neutral instruction set produced by the Java compiler (`javac`) and stored in `.class` files. It consists of **single-byte opcodes** (0–255) followed by zero or more operands, designed to be executed by the JVM rather than any physical CPU.

**Platform independence reasoning (key for marks):**
1. The same `.class` file can be executed on **any OS/hardware** that has a conforming JVM.
2. The JVM acts as an **abstraction layer** translating bytecode into the underlying machine's native instructions.
3. Hence **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** is achieved — code is written and compiled once, but runs anywhere a JVM exists.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Mistake:** Students often say "bytecode is machine code" or "bytecode is platform dependent." Both are **wrong**. Bytecode is platform-*independent* and is *not* machine code. −1 mark.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`**

> **A.** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of the Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** in detail. Discuss the role of each subsystem and memory area.

#### Part (a) — Class Loader Subsystem and Runtime Data Areas (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The JVM architecture consists of **three major subsystems**: the **Class Loader Subsystem**, the **Runtime Data Areas**, and the **Execution Engine**.

**(1) Class Loader Subsystem (3 marks for explanation):**

The Class Loader is responsible for loading `.class` files into memory. It works in **three phases**:

- **Loading:** Reads the `.class` binary file and creates a `Class` object in the heap representing class metadata. The constant pool, field/method data are stored in the Method Area.
- **Linking:**
  - **Verify** — Bytecode verifier ensures type safety and structural correctness (prevents stack overflow / illegal casts).
  - **Prepare** — Memory is allocated for `static` fields and default-initialized (`int → 0`, `Object → null`).
  - **Resolve** — Symbolic references are replaced with direct references from the constant pool.
