KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` > **Differentiate between single-line and multi-line comments in Java with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** | Aspect | Single-Line Comment | Multi-Line Comment | |---|---|---| | **Syntax** | Begins with `//` | Begins with `/*` and ends with `*/` | | **Scope** | Only up to the end of the current line | Spans multiple lines | | **Nesting** | N/A (only one line) | **Not allowed** in Java | | **Termination** | Implicit at line end | Explicit with `*/` | | **Typical Use** | Quick inline notes | Larger block explanations | **Example:** ```java // This is a single-line comment explaining the next line int age = 20; // age of the applicant /* * This is a multi-line comment. * It can describe a method, a class, or a complex algorithm * in several lines of free text. */ ``` --- > **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]` > **Explain the four integer primitive data types in Java. Mention their size and range.** **Model Answer:** Java provides four signed integer primitive types, all based on two's-complement representation. The size and range are as follows: 1. **`byte`** — 8 bits (1 byte); range $-128$ to $127$. 2. **`short`** — 16 bits (2 bytes); range $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$. 3. **`int`** — 32 bits (4 bytes); range $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ (about $-2.147 \times 10^9$ to $2.147 \times 10^9$). 4. **`long`** — 64 bits (8 bytes); range $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ (about $-9.22 \times 10^{18}$ to $9.22 \times 10^{18}$). > [!NOTE] > **Rule of thumb:** If unsure, use `int` (Java's default integer type). Use `long` only when the value may exceed 2 billion. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - University Pattern, Module 1, 14 Marks]** > **Question A:** Discuss the different types of comments supported by Java. Write a Java program demonstrating the use of all three comment types along with all four integer primitive data types. Justify the choice of each integer type used. #### **Model Solution — Question A:** **Part (a) — Discussion of Comment Types [7 Marks]** Java supports three categories of comments, each serving a distinct purpose: 1. **Single-line comment (`//`)** — The compiler ignores everything from `//` to the end of the current line. Used for quick, inline notes. ```java int count = 0; // initialise loop counter ``` 2. **Multi-line comment (`/* ... */`)** — Begins with `/*` and ends with `*/`. Used to explain larger blocks like methods, classes, or algorithms. Java **does not allow nesting** of multi-line comments — the first `*/` closes the comment. ```java /* * This method computes the factorial * of a non-negative integer n. */ ``` 3. **Documentation comment (`/** ... */`)** — A special form of multi-line comment with a **double asterisk**. Contains structured tags (`@param`, `@return`, `@author`, `@version`, `@throws`) that are processed by the `javadoc` tool to auto-generate HTML API documentation. ```java /** * Calculates the sum of two integers. * @param a first addend * @param b second addend * @return the sum a + b * @author KTU Student * @since 2024 */ public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } ``` **Part (b) — Demonstration Program [7 Marks]** ```java /** * File: IntegerTypesDemo.java * Author: KTU Student * @since 2024 * Demonstrates all three comment types and the four integer primitive types. */ public class IntegerTypesDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // ---- Section 1: All four integer primitive types ---- byte smallNum = 100; // 8 bits : -128 to 127 short mediumNum = 30_000; // 16 bits : -32768 to 32767 int standardNum = 2_000_000_000; // 32 bits : ~ -2.1e9 to 2.1e9 long largeNum = 9_000_000_000_000L; // 64 bits : ~ -9.2e18 to 9.2e18 /* * Print each variable along with its type and a short justification. * Using a multi-line block to keep the output readable. */ System.out.println("byte value: " + smallNum + " (sensor reading fits in 0-255)"); System.out.println("short value: " + mediumNum + " (city population in thousands)"); System.out.println("int value: " + standardNum + " (national currency units)"); System.out.println("long value: " + largeNum + " (universe age in milliseconds approx)"); // ---- Section 2: Demonstrate overflow danger ---- int maxInt = 2_147_483_647; // int maximum System.out.println("max int: " + maxInt); // maxInt + 1 would overflow; we use long to demonstrate long safeAdd = (long) maxInt + 1; System.out.println("max int + 1 in long: " + safeAdd); } } ``` **Justification of integer-type choices:** | Variable | Chosen Type | Justification | |---|---|---| | `smallNum` | `byte` | Sensor reading naturally lies in 0–255, so `byte` is the most memory-efficient fit. | | `mediumNum` | `short` | Population in thousands fits comfortably in the 16-bit range, avoiding 32-bit wastage. | | `standardNum` | `int` | A value near 2 billion is the default case — `int` is the natural and default integer type. | | `largeNum` | `long` | The value 9 trillion vastly exceeds `int` range, mandating the 64-bit `long` type and the `L` suffix. | --- > **[Same Paper, Internal Choice]** > **Question B:** Explain integer type promotion in Java expressions with examples. What is the role of the `L` suffix in `long` literals? Why does `byte + byte` return an `int`? Discuss with at least three illustrative code snippets. #### **Model Solution — Question B:** **Part (a) — Integer Type Promotion Rules [7 Marks]** Java applies **binary numeric promotion** before performing arithmetic on operands. The rules (in priority order) are: 1. If either operand is `double`, the other is promoted to `double`; result is `double`. 2. Else if either is `float`, the other is promoted to `float`; result is `float`. 3. Else if either is `long`, the other is promoted to `long`; result is `long`. 4. **Otherwise, both operands are promoted to `int`**; result is `int`. This is why `byte + byte`, `short + short`, and even `char + char` all return `int` — none of them triggers rules 1–3, so rule 4 takes over. **Snippet 1 — `byte + byte`:** ```java byte a = 10, b = 20; int result = a + b; // OK, a+b is int // byte wrong = a + b; // COMPILE ERROR: lossy conversion int -> byte byte correct = (byte)(a + b); // explicit cast required ``` **Snippet 2 — `int * long` promotion:** ```java int count = 5; long factor = 1_000_000_000_000L; long total = count * factor; // count promoted to long; result is long ``` **Snippet 3 — Mixing with no promotion possible (forces int result):** ```java short p = 100, q = 200; int sum = p + q; // p, q promoted to int; sum must be int // short bad = p + q; // COMPILE ERROR ``` **Part (b) — Role of the `L` Suffix and Why `byte+byte` Yields `int` [7 Marks]** **The `L` Suffix:** ```java // Without L: compile error because 9_000_000_000 > int.MAX_VALUE // long big = 9_000_000_000; // ERROR // With L: literal is interpreted as long long big = 9_000_000_000L; // OK ``` The `L` (or lowercase `l`) suffix tells the compiler to treat the numeric literal as a `long` instead of the default `int`. Without it, the compiler first attempts to fit the literal into an `int`, fails, and raises a compile-time error. **Why `byte + byte` is `int`:** The Java Language Specification (JLS §5.6.2) mandates that operands smaller than `int` (i.e., `byte`, `short`, `char`) are first **widened** to `int` before the operator is applied. This widening happens on the JVM stack and is invisible in source. Consequently, the result of any arithmetic on `byte` (or `short`, or `char`) is an `int` and cannot be assigned back to a `byte` without an explicit narrowing cast. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Confusing `l` and `1`:** Always use uppercase `L` in `long` literals. Lowercase `l` is legal but looks like `1` and will be **marked down** in lab and exam evaluations. > 2. **Forgetting the cast:** `byte z = a + b;` will fail to compile. Students frequently lose 2 marks by omitting the explicit `(byte)` cast in the corrected line. > 3. **Mixing octal with decimal:** `int x = 010;` stores **8**, not 10. This is a classic KTU MCQ trap — always check for a leading zero. > 4. **Misnaming `char` as a "character type" only:** `char` is a **16-bit unsigned integer** in numeric contexts. It is part of the integer family for arithmetic. Missing this costs 1–2 marks on type questions. > 5. **Comments inside strings:** `String s = "Hello // world";` — the `//` is **not** a comment because it lies inside a string literal. Remember: comments are recognised by the **lexer before** string parsing contexts are entered. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > **Comments — Quick Recall** > - `//` — single line, no terminator. > - `/* ... */` — multi-line, **cannot be nested**. > - `/** ... */` — Javadoc, processed by `javadoc` tool, supports `@param`, `@return`, `@author`, `@since`, `@throws`. > - Comments are **discarded at compile time** — they never reach the JVM. > - Comments inside string literals are not comments. > **Integer Primitive Types — Quick Recall** > - `byte`: 8 bits, $-128$ to $127$, default `0`. > - `short`: 16 bits, $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$, default `0`. > - `int`: 32 bits, $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$, default `0`, **default integer type**. > - `long`: 64 bits, $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$, default `0L`, **requires `L` suffix on literals**. > - `char`: 16 bits **unsigned**, $0$ to $65{,}535$, default `'\u0000'`; behaves as integer in numeric contexts. > **Range Formula — Must Memorise** > - Signed $n$-bit: range is $-2^{n-1}$ to $2^{n-1} - 1$. > - Unsigned $n$-bit: range is $0$ to $2^{n} - 1$. > **Promotion — Must Memorise** > - Hierarchy: `double` > `float` > `long` > `int`. > - `byte`, `short`, `char` are **always first promoted to `int`** before any arithmetic. > - `byte + byte` returns `int`; reassigning to `byte` requires explicit cast. > **Literal Suffixes — Must Memorise** > - Decimal: no prefix, e.g., `255`. > - Hex: `0xFF`. > - Octal: leading `0`, e.g., `0377`. > - Binary: `0b11111111`. > - `long` literal: append `L` (uppercase preferred). > **Common Compile-Time Errors to Watch** > - `long x = 9999999999;` — missing `L`. > - `byte z = a + b;` — missing cast. > - Underscore at start/end of literal. > - Nested `/* ... /* ... */ ... */` — not allowed. > **KTU 2024 CO Mapping for This Topic** > - **CO1:** Apply object-oriented concepts to develop Java programs. > - **Bloom's Levels Covered:** Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3). > - **Module 1 Weightage in Typical ESE:** ~10–15% of Part A and a frequent sub-part of a 14-mark question in Part B.

topics.title: Floating Point Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Floating Point Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In Java's strongly-typed, object-oriented type system (as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus for PEMRT415), **Floating Point Types** are the primitive data types used to represent real numbers — that is, numbers that contain a *fractional component* (a decimal portion) and can take both positive and negative values, including very large and very small magnitudes.

Java (and the JVM specification) defines exactly two floating-point primitive types:

| Type | Size | Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| Single-precision | **32 bits** | `float` |
| Double-precision | **64 bits** | `double` |

Both types strictly conform to the **IEEE 754 Standard for Binary Floating-Point Arithmetic (IEEE 754-1985, revised in 2008 and 2019)**. This is a KTU syllabus-mandated requirement and is the single most important fact to remember.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE 754 Compliance:** Java is one of the few mainstream languages where `float` and `double` are **guaranteed by the language specification** to be IEEE 754 binary32 and binary64 representations — independent of the underlying hardware platform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Scientific Calculator" Mental Model

Think of a floating-point number as a **digital scientific calculator display** like the one you used in school:

$$\text{Number} = \text{Mantissa} \times \text{Base}^{\text{Exponent}}$$

For example, the number `6500000.0` on a calculator appears as `6.5 × 10^6`.

- The **6.5** is the *mantissa* (the "significant digits").
- The **10** is the *base* (always 2 in binary, conceptually 10 in decimal).
- The **6** is the *exponent* (tells you "how big" the number is).

Floating-point types work exactly this way — they store the *significant digits* and the *scale* (where the decimal point "floats"), rather than storing every digit explicitly. This is why they are called **"floating-point"** types: the decimal point's position is not fixed; it *floats* based on the exponent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine writing a number on a whiteboard. With integer types, you are limited to fixed positions (e.g., a 4-digit odometer showing `0042` or `1234`). With floating-point, you also have a *sliding ruler* (the exponent) that lets you say "this number is `1.234 × 10^k`" — so the same 32 or 64 bits can represent `0.0000001` or `10000000` depending on where you slide the ruler.

## 1.3 The Default Type — A Critical KTU Trap

In Java, a *literal* decimal number such as `3.14` written in source code is **always `double` by default** — NOT `float`. To make it a `float`, you must append the suffix `f` or `F`.

```java
double d = 3.14;   // OK — 3.14 is a double literal
float  f = 3.14;   // COMPILE ERROR — possible lossy conversion
float  g = 3.14f;  // OK — explicitly a float literal
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting the `f` suffix is the #1 reason for "type mismatch" compile errors in OOP lab exams. KTU examiners frequently test this!

## 1.4 Why Two Types? — Precision vs Memory Trade-off

`float` uses **4 bytes (32 bits)** → less memory → less precision (about **7 decimal digits**).
`double` uses **8 bytes (64 bits)** → more memory → more precision (about **15-17 decimal digits**).

The KTU-recommended best practice is: **prefer `double` over `float` unless memory is strictly constrained** (e.g., GPU shaders, embedded systems, large arrays in numerical computing).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEEE 754 Internal Bit Layout

Both `float` and `double` follow the same structural template: **Sign + Exponent + Mantissa (Significand)**.

### 2.1.1 `float` (IEEE 754 binary32) — 32 bits total

$$\text{Total Bits} = 1\;(\text{Sign}) + 8\;(\text{Exponent}) + 23\;(\text{Mantissa})$$

### 2.1.2 `double` (IEEE 754 binary64) — 64 bits total

$$\text{Total Bits} = 1\;(\text{Sign}) + 11\;(\text{Exponent}) + 52\;(\text{Mantissa})$$

### 2.1.3 How a Value Is Represented

For a *normalized* positive number, the value is computed as:

$$(-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - \text{bias})}$$

Where:
- $S$ = sign bit (0 for positive, 1 for negative)
- $M$ = mantissa bits (the fractional part after the implied leading 1)
- $E$ = exponent bits (interpreted as unsigned)
- **Bias** = $2^{k-1} - 1$, where $k$ = number of exponent bits

So:
- For `float`: **Bias = $2^7 - 1$ = 127**
- For `double`: **Bias = $2^{10} - 1$ = 1023**

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Implied Leading 1" Trick:** A normalized binary floating-point number always has the form `1.xxxxxx`. The leading `1.` is NOT stored — it is *implicit*. This gives you **one extra bit of precision for free**, which is why a 23-bit mantissa yields 24 bits of effective precision.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | `float` (binary32) | `double` (binary64) |
|---|---|---|
| Total storage | **32 bits (4 bytes)** | **64 bits (8 bytes)** |
| Sign bits | 1 | 1 |
| Exponent bits | 8 | 11 |
| Mantissa bits | 23 (24 effective) | 52 (53 effective) |
| Bias | **127** | **1023** |
| Min positive normal | $\approx 1.4 \times 10^{-45}$ | $\approx 4.9 \times 10^{-324}$ |
| Max finite value | $\approx 3.4028235 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx 1.7976931348623157 \times 10^{308}$ |
| Decimal precision | ~7 digits | ~15–17 digits |
| Default literal? | No | **Yes (default)** |
| Wrapper class | `java.lang.Float` | `java.lang.Double` |
| Min exponent | $-126$ | $-1022$ |
| Max exponent | $+127$ | $+1023$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Exponent Patterns (Reserved):**
> - All-zero exponent + zero mantissa → **Zero** (with sign)
> - All-one exponent + zero mantissa → **Infinity** ($\pm\infty$)
> - All-one exponent + non-zero mantissa → **NaN** (Not a Number)
> - All-zero exponent + non-zero mantissa → **Denormalized** number (gradual underflow)

## 2.3 Range Derivation (For `float`)

**Minimum positive normal value:**
$$1.0 \times 2^{(1 - 127)} = 2^{-126} \approx 1.175 \times 10^{-38}$$

Wait — the table shows $1.4 \times 10^{-45}$. The correction: the *smallest normal* is $2^{-126}$, but the *smallest denormalized* is $2^{-149} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-45}$. Both are KTU-relevant.

**Maximum finite value:**
$$\left(2 - 2^{-23}\right) \times 2^{127} \approx 3.4028235 \times 10^{38}$$

## 2.4 Wrapper Classes & Autoboxing

Java provides object-oriented wrappers that "wrap" the primitive floating-point types:

- `float` → **`java.lang.Float`**
- `double` → **`java.lang.Double`**

These wrappers are needed when an `Object` reference is required (collections, generics, etc.). **Autoboxing** (primitive → wrapper) and **unboxing** (wrapper → primitive) happen automatically since Java 5.

```java
Double dObj = 3.14;       // autoboxing: double → Double
double d = dObj;          // unboxing:  Double → double
```

## 2.5 Special Floating-Point Values

Every `float` and `double` in Java supports the following constants (accessible via the wrapper classes):

| Constant | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY` | $+\infty$ |
| `Double.NEGATIVE_INFINITY` | $-\infty$ |
| `Double.NaN` | Not a Number |
| `Double.MAX_VALUE` | Largest finite value |
| `Double.MIN_VALUE` | Smallest positive normal value ($\approx 2^{-1022}$) |
| `Float.NaN`, etc. | Same set for `float` |

These are produced by:
- Division by zero: `1.0 / 0.0` → `Infinity` (does NOT throw an exception in floating-point!)
- `0.0 / 0.0` → `NaN`
- Overflow of arithmetic operations

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

Floating-point types are the backbone of:
- **Scientific simulation** (CFD, FEA in mechanical/aerospace engineering)
- **Signal processing** (DSP, audio/video codecs)
- **Machine learning** (weights, gradients in neural networks — typically `float` on GPU)
- **Computer graphics** (3D coordinates, transformations, ray tracing)
- **Financial rough estimates** (NEVER for exact currency — use `BigDecimal`)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: Decoding a `float` Bit Pattern

**Problem (KTU-style):** Given the 32-bit IEEE 754 binary32 representation of a `float`:
$$\text{bits} = \texttt{0 10000001 10100000000000000000000}$$
Decoded as: sign=`0`, exponent=`10000001`, mantissa=`10100000000000000000000`. Find the decimal value.

### Step 1 — Parse the sign bit $S$

$$S = 0 \implies \text{Number is positive}$$

### Step 2 — Convert the exponent bits from binary to decimal

$$E_{\text{raw}} = (10000001)_2 = 1 \times 2^7 + 0 \times 2^6 + \cdots + 0 \times 2^1 + 1 \times 2^0$$

Breaking it down:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{raw}} &= 1 \cdot 128 + 0 \cdot 64 + 0 \cdot 32 + 0 \cdot 16 \\
&\quad + 0 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 \\
&= 128 + 1 = 129
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the bias

For `float`, **bias = 127**.

$$E_{\text{actual}} = E_{\text{raw}} - \text{bias} = 129 - 127 = 2$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the mantissa

The mantissa bits are `10100000000000000000000`. The **implied leading 1** is prepended:

$$M_{\text{binary}} = 1.10100000000000000000000_2$$

To convert to decimal, expand each fractional bit by its place value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{decimal}} &= 1 \cdot 2^0 + 1 \cdot 2^{-1} + 0 \cdot 2^{-2} + 1 \cdot 2^{-3} \\
&\quad + 0 \cdot 2^{-4} + \cdots + 0 \cdot 2^{-23} \\
&= 1 + 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 \\
&= 1.625
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the IEEE 754 formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Value} &= (-1)^S \times M_{\text{decimal}} \times 2^{E_{\text{actual}}} \\
&= (-1)^0 \times 1.625 \times 2^2 \\
&= 1 \times 1.625 \times 4 \\
&= 6.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\text{Decoded value} = 6.5}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** `6.5` in binary is `110.1`, which in normalized form is `1.101 × 2^2`. Exponent bits for $E=2$ require raw $= 2 + 127 = 129 = 10000001_2$ ✓. This confirms the decoding is correct.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Encoding `−9.625` as a `float`

**Problem:** Encode $-9.625$ in IEEE 754 binary32 form.

### Step 1 — Sign bit

The number is negative, so $S = 1$.

### Step 2 — Convert to binary

Integer part `9` and fractional part `0.625`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
9_{10} &= 1001_2 \\
0.625_{10} &= 0.5 + 0.125 = 2^{-1} + 2^{-3} = 0.101_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Combined: $9.625_{10} = 1001.101_2$.

### Step 3 — Normalize

Shift the binary point so that exactly one digit remains to its left:

$$1001.101_2 = 1.001101_2 \times 2^3$$

So $E_{\text{actual}} = 3$.

### Step 4 — Compute exponent bits

$$E_{\text{raw}} = E_{\text{actual}} + \text{bias} = 3 + 127 = 130 = 10000010_2$$

### Step 5 — Extract the mantissa

The bits after the binary point in `1.001101` are `001101`. Pad with zeros to 23 bits:

$$M = 00110100000000000000000$$

### Step 6 — Assemble the 32-bit pattern

Concatenate in order: `Sign | Exponent | Mantissa`

$$\text{bits} = \texttt{1 10000010 00110100000000000000000}$$

### Final Encoded Result

$$\boxed{\text{float bits} = \texttt{1\ 10000010\ 00110100000000000000000}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: `float` vs `double`

| Property | `float` | `double` | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory footprint | 4 bytes | 8 bytes | `double` costs 2× memory |
| Decimal digits of precision | ~7 | ~15–17 | `double` is far more accurate |
| Range upper bound | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $\approx 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | `double` covers vastly more |
| Execution speed on modern CPUs | Often slower (promoted internally) | Native width | `double` is usually faster |
| Default literal type | No (requires `f`) | Yes | `double` is the natural choice |
| Wrapper class | `Float` | `Double` | Equivalent |
| Suitable for | Graphics, large arrays, embedded | Scientific computing, general math | Use `double` unless constrained |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Hardware Insight:** On most 64-bit CPUs (including the x86-64 and ARM64 chips in KTU lab computers), `double` operations are as fast or faster than `float` because the CPU's native floating-point registers are 64 bits wide. The old advantage of `float` (cache-fitting more values) has largely disappeared unless you are doing GPU programming.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Detecting NaN

NaN has a unique property: it is **not equal to itself**. This is the canonical way to test for it.

```java
double x = 0.0 / 0.0;
if (x != x) {
    System.out.println("x is NaN");
}
// Preferred way (uses the wrapper class):
if (Double.isNaN(x)) {
    System.out.println("x is NaN");
}
```

Logical reasoning:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x = \text{NaN} &\implies x \neq x = \text{true} \\
y = \text{any finite number} &\implies y = y = \text{true} \;\Rightarrow\; y \neq y = \text{false}
\end{aligned}
$$

The IEEE 754 standard mandates this reflexivity-breaking behavior so that NaN propagates through computations without silently masquerading as a valid value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 754 Bit Layout Diagram (`float`)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IEEE 754 binary32 float Layout] --> B[Sign bit S - 1 bit]
    A --> C[Exponent E - 8 bits]
    A --> D[Mantissa M - 23 bits]
    B --> B1["bit 31: 0 = positive, 1 = negative"]
    C --> C1["bits 30 to 23: unsigned exponent"]
    C --> C2["Bias = 127"]
    C --> C3["Range of raw E: 0 to 255"]
    D --> D1["bits 22 to 0: fractional part"]
    D --> D2["Implied leading 1: not stored"]
    D --> D3["Effective precision: 24 bits"]
```

## 4.2 IEEE 754 Bit Layout Diagram (`double`)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IEEE 754 binary64 double Layout] --> B[Sign bit S - 1 bit]
    A --> C[Exponent E - 11 bits]
    A --> D[Mantissa M - 52 bits]
    B --> B1["bit 63: 0 = positive, 1 = negative"]
    C --> C1["bits 62 to 52: unsigned exponent"]
    C --> C2["Bias = 1023"]
    C --> C3["Range of raw E: 0 to 2047"]
    D --> D1["bits 51 to 0: fractional part"]
    D --> D2["Implied leading 1: not stored"]
    D --> D3["Effective precision: 53 bits"]
```

## 4.3 Exponent Bit Pattern Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Exponent bits received] --> Q1{Are all exponent bits 0?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Mantissa is 0?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Are all exponent bits 1?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Zero[Value is positive or negative ZERO]
    Q2 -- No --> Denorm[Value is DENORMALIZED - gradual underflow]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Mantissa is 0?}
    Q3 -- No --> NaN[Value is NaN - Not a Number]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Inf[Value is positive or negative INFINITY]
    Q4 -- No --> NaN
    Start --> Normal[Otherwise: Value is NORMALIZED number]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Floating-Point Type System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JAVA[JAVA PRIMITIVE FLOATING POINT TYPES]
        F[float: 32 bit binary32]
        D[double: 64 bit binary64]
    end

    subgraph WRAPPERS[OBJECT WRAPPERS java lang]
        WF[Float]
        WD[Double]
    end

    subgraph CONST[STANDARD CONSTANTS]
        INF[POSITIVE INFINITY]
        NINF[NEGATIVE INFINITY]
        NAN[NaN]
        MAXD[MAX VALUE]
        MIND[MIN VALUE]
    end

    subgraph OPS[SUPPORTED OPERATIONS]
        ARITH[Arithmetic plus minus times divide]
        REL[Relational less greater equal]
        MATH[Math library sqrt sin log etc]
    end

    F --> WF
    D --> WD
    WF --> CONST
    WD --> CONST
    F --> OPS
    D --> OPS

    USER[Java Source Code] -->|3.14 literal| D
    USER -->|3.14f literal| F
    USER -->|autoboxing| WD
    WD -->|unboxing| D
```

## 4.5 Encoding-Decoding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Decimal Real Number] --> B[Step 1: Convert to Binary]
    B --> C[Step 2: Normalize to 1 dot M form]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Raw Exponent E plus bias]
    D --> E[Step 4: Pack Sign Exponent Mantissa]
    E --> F[32 bit or 64 bit IEEE 754 Representation]

    F --> G[Step 1: Unpack Sign Exponent Mantissa]
    G --> H[Step 2: Subtract bias from Exponent]
    H --> I[Step 3: Reassemble 1 dot M times 2 power E]
    I --> J[Decimal Real Number reconstructed]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and explain any three characteristics of floating-point types in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

Floating-point types in Java are primitive data types used to represent real numbers containing a fractional component. The two floating-point types are `float` (32 bits) and `double` (64 bits). The three key characteristics are:

1. **IEEE 754 Compliance:** Both `float` and `double` strictly follow the IEEE 754 standard for binary floating-point arithmetic, ensuring platform-independent representation. `float` uses the binary32 format (1 sign + 8 exponent + 23 mantissa bits) and `double` uses binary64 (1 sign + 11 exponent + 52 mantissa bits).

2. **Signed Magnitude Representation:** They store a separate sign bit (0 for positive, 1 for negative) combined with a biased exponent and an implicit-leading-1 mantissa. This allows representation of negative values directly without using two's complement.

3. **Special Values:** In addition to ordinary numbers, both types support special reserved bit patterns representing `+Infinity`, `-Infinity`, `NaN` (Not-a-Number), and signed zero. Division by zero (e.g., `1.0 / 0.0`) produces `Infinity` instead of throwing an exception, which is unlike integer arithmetic.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the difference between `float` and `double` in Java? Why is `double` the default choice?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `float` | `double` |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | 32 bits (4 bytes) | 64 bits (8 bytes) |
| Precision | ~7 decimal digits | ~15–17 decimal digits |
| Range | up to $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | up to $\pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ |
| Default literal | No (requires `f` suffix) | **Yes** |

`double` is the default choice because: (i) it offers roughly double the precision and an enormously larger range, (ii) on modern 64-bit CPUs, `double` operations execute as fast as `float` because the hardware's native floating-point registers are 64 bits wide, and (iii) using `double` avoids accidental precision loss when an expression mixes `int` and floating-point values.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the internal IEEE 754 representation of `float` and `double` with neat bit-layout diagrams. Mention the role of the bias and the implied leading 1. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Encode the decimal value $-12.375$ in IEEE 754 single-precision (`float`) format, showing all steps clearly. Decode the resulting bit pattern back to decimal to verify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The IEEE 754 standard represents floating-point numbers using three fields: a sign bit $S$, a biased exponent $E$, and a fractional mantissa $M$. For a *normalized* number, the value is:

$$(-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - \text{bias})}$$

**Bit layout for `float` (binary32, 32 bits):**

| Field | Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
|---|---|---|---|
| Width | 1 bit | 8 bits | 23 bits |
| Position | bit 31 | bits 30–23 | bits 22–0 |
| Bias | — | 127 | — |

**Bit layout for `double` (binary64, 64 bits):**

| Field | Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
|---|---|---|---|
| Width | 1 bit | 11 bits | 52 bits |
| Position | bit 63 | bits 62–52 | bits 51–0 |
| Bias | — | 1023 | — |

**Role of the bias:** The bias allows the exponent to represent both positive and negative powers of two without using a separate sign bit. The stored exponent is shifted by `+bias`, so an actual exponent of 0 is stored as `bias`, $-1$ as `bias - 1`, and so on.

**Role of the implied leading 1:** For any normalized binary number, the leading digit is always `1` (because we adjust the exponent until this is true). Since this `1.` is guaranteed to be present, IEEE 754 does not store it, freeing one extra bit of precision. Thus a `float` with 23 stored mantissa bits effectively has 24 bits of precision.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Convert $-12.375$ to binary.**

The magnitude is $12.375$. Split into integer and fractional parts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
12_{10} &= 1100_2 \\
0.375_{10} &= 0.25 + 0.125 = 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} = 0.011_2
\end{aligned}
$$

So $12.375_{10} = 1100.011_2$.

**Step 2 — Normalize.**

Shift the binary point 3 places to the left:

$$1100.011_2 = 1.100011_2 \times 2^3$$

Therefore $E_{\text{actual}} = 3$.

**Step 3 — Sign bit.**

The number is negative, so $S = 1$.

**Step 4 — Biased exponent bits.**

$$E_{\text{raw}} = E_{\text{actual}} + \text{bias} = 3 + 127 = 130 = 10000010_2$$

**Step 5 — Mantissa bits.**

The fractional part after the leading `1.` is `100011`. Pad with zeros to 23 bits:

$$M = 10001100000000000000000$$

**Step 6 — Concatenate.**

$$\boxed{\texttt{1\ 10000010\ 10001100000000000000000}}$$

**Verification — decode the bit pattern:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{negative} \\
E_{\text{raw}} &= 10000010_2 = 130 \\
E_{\text{actual}} &= 130 - 127 = 3 \\
M_{\text{binary}} &= 1.100011_2 \\
M_{\text{decimal}} &= 1 + 0.5 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0.125 + 0.0625 = 1.6875 \\
\text{Value} &= -1.6875 \times 2^3 = -1.6875 \times 8 = -13.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — there is a discrepancy. Let us recheck.

$12.375 = 1100.011_2$. Splitting this: integer part `1100` is $8 + 4 = 12$ ✓; fractional part `0.011` is $0.25 + 0.125 = 0.375$ ✓. The mantissa after the leading `1` is the bits after the leading `1.`, namely `100011`, which is $0.5 + 0.0625 = 0.5625$. Then $1 + 0.5625 = 1.5625$? Let us recount.

Normalize $1100.011$: shift 3 places to get $1.100011$. The bits after `1.` are `100011` (six bits). As decimal:

$$0.100011_2 = 2^{-1} + 2^{-5} + 2^{-6} = 0.5 + 0.03125 + 0.015625 = 0.546875$$

So $M_{\text{decimal}} = 1.546875$, and $1.546875 \times 8 = 12.375$ ✓.

Correcting the decoding step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{decimal}} &= 1 + 0.5 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0.03125 + 0.015625 = 1.546875 \\
\text{Value} &= -1.546875 \times 2^3 = -12.375 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verified:** $\boxed{-12.375}$

---

### Question B — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]**
**(a)** What are the special values `NaN` and `Infinity` in floating-point arithmetic? Show with Java code examples how they are produced and how they propagate through expressions. Discuss the property that `NaN != NaN`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that reads two `double` values from the user, computes and displays their sum, difference, product, quotient, and the result of dividing the first by zero. Explain the output, paying special attention to the `Infinity` and `NaN` cases. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Special Values Defined by IEEE 754:**

1. **Positive Infinity (`+Infinity`):** Represents a value larger than any finite number. Produced when a positive number is divided by zero (`5.0 / 0.0`) or when an arithmetic operation overflows.

2. **Negative Infinity (`-Infinity`):** Mirror of the above, produced by e.g. `-5.0 / 0.0`.

3. **NaN (Not-a-Number):** Represents an undefined or unrepresentable result, such as `0.0 / 0.0` or the square root of a negative number.

```java
public class SpecialValuesDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double posInf = 1.0 / 0.0;
        double negInf = -1.0 / 0.0;
        double nanVal = 0.0 / 0.0;

        System.out.println("+Infinity : " + posInf);   // Infinity
        System.out.println("-Infinity : " + negInf);   // -Infinity
        System.out.println("NaN       : " + nanVal);   // NaN

        // NaN is NOT equal to itself
        System.out.println("NaN == NaN ? " + (nanVal == nanVal)); // false

        // Correct way to test NaN
        System.out.println("isNaN(NaN)? " + Double.isNaN(nanVal)); // true

        // Propagation: any operation with NaN yields NaN
        System.out.println("NaN + 5 = " + (nanVal + 5));           // NaN
        System.out.println("Infinity - Infinity = " + (posInf - posInf)); // NaN
        System.out.println("Infinity * 0 = " + (posInf * 0));       // NaN
    }
}
```

**Propagation rules (IEEE 754):**
- Any arithmetic operation involving `NaN` produces `NaN`.
- `Infinity - Infinity`, `Infinity * 0`, `0/0` all yield `NaN`.
- `Infinity + Infinity = Infinity`; `Infinity * Infinity = Infinity`.
- `x / 0.0` (where `x` is a positive double) yields `+Infinity`, NOT a runtime exception.

**The `NaN != NaN` Property:**

The IEEE 754 standard defines that all comparisons with `NaN` (except `!=`) return `false`. Therefore:

$$
\text{NaN} = \text{NaN} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{false}, \quad
\text{NaN} \neq \text{NaN} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{true}
$$

This seems contradictory, but it is the deliberate design that allows NaN to "poison" expressions without silently being absorbed. The recommended way to test for NaN is therefore the static method `Double.isNaN(x)` rather than `x == Double.NaN`.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class DoubleArithmetic {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter first number : ");
        double a = sc.nextDouble();
        System.out.print("Enter second number: ");
        double b = sc.nextDouble();

        System.out.println("Sum         : " + (a + b));
        System.out.println("Difference  : " + (a - b));
        System.out.println("Product     : " + (a * b));
        System.out.println("Quotient    : " + (a / b));
        System.out.println("a / 0.0     : " + (a / 0.0));

        if (Double.isInfinite(a / 0.0)) {
            System.out.println("Division by zero produced Infinity, not an error.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Sample Run (input `a = 7.5`, `b = 2.5`):**

```
Enter first number : 7.5
Enter second number: 2.5
Sum         : 10.0
Difference  : 5.0
Product     : 18.75
Quotient    : 3.0
a / 0.0     : Infinity
Division by zero produced Infinity, not an error.
```

**Explanation of Output:**

- The sum, difference, product, and quotient use ordinary IEEE 754 double-precision arithmetic and produce exact results in this case.
- The expression `a / 0.0` produces `+Infinity` because IEEE 754 specifies that any non-zero floating-point value divided by `0.0` (NOT by integer `0`) yields a signed infinity. No `ArithmeticException` is thrown.
- If `a = 0.0`, then `0.0 / 0.0` would yield `NaN` instead of `Infinity`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using `int` literals in the division:** Writing `a / 0` (with integer `0`) throws `ArithmeticException`. The question asks for `a / 0.0` — always use the floating-point zero literal.
> 2. **Forgetting to import `java.util.Scanner`** — the program will not compile.
> 3. **Comparing NaN with `==`:** NaN never equals itself; use `Double.isNaN(x)`.
> 4. **Confusing the default literal type:** writing `float x = 3.14;` without the `f` suffix causes a compile error.
> 5. **Incorrect bias subtraction:** the most common arithmetic error in encoding/decoding problems. Always subtract the bias (127 for float, 1023 for double) from the *raw* exponent.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Floating-point types in Java: **`float` (32 bits)** and **`double` (64 bits)**.
- Both strictly conform to **IEEE 754** — `float` is binary32, `double` is binary64.
- Default decimal literal in Java is **`double`**, NOT `float`. Use the **`f` or `F` suffix** to make a `float` literal.
- Internal layout: **Sign (1 bit) + Exponent (8 or 11 bits) + Mantissa (23 or 52 bits)**.
- **Bias** = $2^{k-1} - 1$: **127 for `float`**, **1023 for `double`**.
- **Implied leading 1** in mantissa gives 1 extra bit of effective precision (24 and 53 bits respectively).
- Value formula for normalized numbers: $(-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - \text{bias})}$.
- Special bit patterns: all-zero exponent + zero mantissa = **±0**; all-one exponent + zero mantissa = **±Infinity**; all-one exponent + non-zero mantissa = **NaN**.
- **`NaN != NaN`** — always use `Double.isNaN(x)` / `Float.isNaN(x)` to test.
- Floating-point division by zero (e.g., `1.0 / 0.0`) yields **`Infinity`**, not an exception.
- Wrapper classes: **`java.lang.Float`** and **`java.lang.Double`** (autoboxing since Java 5).
- Precision: `float` ≈ **7 decimal digits**, `double` ≈ **15–17 decimal digits**.
- **Best practice:** prefer `double` over `float` unless memory/bandwidth is strictly limited.
- **Never** use floating-point for exact monetary calculations — use `java.math.BigDecimal` instead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
In Object Oriented Programming, a **Character** is a fundamental primitive data type used to store a single alphanumeric symbol, punctuation mark, or control code. In **C++**, the `char` data type occupies **1 byte (8 bits)** of memory and represents characters in the **ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange)** encoding scheme. In **Java**, the `char` data type occupies **2 bytes (16 bits)** and supports **Unicode (UTF-16)** encoding, enabling representation of international characters.

A character literal is always enclosed within **single quotes** (e.g., `'A'`, `'7'`, `'@'`), distinguishing it from a string literal which uses double quotes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In C++, characters are internally stored as their corresponding integer ASCII codes. For example, the character `'A'` is stored as the integer **65**, and `'a'` is stored as **97**. This dual nature (character/integer) is the foundation of character arithmetic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of characters as **labeled mailboxes** in a post office. Every mailbox (memory location) has:
- A **name** (the character we see, like `'A'`)
- A **numeric address** (the underlying ASCII code, like `65`)

When you look at the mailbox, you see a letter, but the post office tracks it by its number. Similarly, your computer displays `'A'` on screen but stores `65` in memory. This dual identity is what makes characters special — they can be treated as symbols (for display) or as numbers (for arithmetic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - `'A'` is a **character literal** (size 1 byte, value 65)
> - `"A"` is a **string literal** (size depends on language, contains a null terminator in C++)
> - `65` is an **integer literal** (size typically 4 bytes)

### 1.3 Standard Character Encoding Metrics
| Encoding Scheme | Size | Range | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ASCII** | 7 bits | 0 – 127 | C++ `char` (subset) |
| **Extended ASCII** | 8 bits | 0 – 255 | C++ `char` |
| **UTF-16 Unicode** | 16 bits | 0 – 65,535 | Java `char` |
| **UTF-8** | 8–32 bits (variable) | Full Unicode | Modern applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ASCII Standard Range is 0 to 127**, covering English alphabets (A–Z, a–z), digits (0–9), punctuation marks, and 33 control characters (non-printable codes like null, bell, carriage return).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Character Representation

- **Step 1 — Memory Allocation:** When a `char` variable is declared, the compiler reserves **1 byte (8 bits)** of memory. In Java, **2 bytes (16 bits)** are reserved.
- **Step 2 — Encoding Lookup:** The character literal (e.g., `'Z'`) is converted to its corresponding numeric code by referencing the encoding table (ASCII for C++, Unicode for Java).
- **Step 3 — Binary Storage:** The numeric code is stored in binary. For `'Z'` (ASCII 90), the binary representation is `01011010`.
- **Step 4 — Display Decoding:** When output via `cout` or `System.out`, the binary value is mapped back to the visual character for display.

### 2.2 Character Categories (ASCII Classification)

- **Uppercase Alphabets (A–Z):** ASCII range **65 to 90**
- **Lowercase Alphabets (a–z):** ASCII range **97 to 122**
- **Digits (0–9):** ASCII range **48 to 57**
- **Space Character:** ASCII **32**
- **Null Character (`\0`):** ASCII **0** (used as string terminator in C++)
- **Newline (`\n`):** ASCII **10**
- **Horizontal Tab (`\t`):** ASCII **9**

### 2.3 The Magic Number — Case Difference

A critical relationship that appears in **every KTU exam**:

$$\text{Difference} = \text{ASCII}('a') - \text{ASCII}('A') = 97 - 65 = 32$$

This constant value of **32** is the key to all case conversion algorithms.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Operation | Formula / Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Uppercase to Lowercase | `ch + 32` | `'A' + 32 = 'a'` (65 + 32 = 97) |
| Lowercase to Uppercase | `ch - 32` | `'z' - 32 = 'Z'` (122 − 32 = 90) |
| Character to ASCII | Implicit cast to `int` | `(int)'B' = 66` |
| ASCII to Character | Implicit cast to `char` | `(char)67 = 'C'` |
| Digit Char to Int | `ch - '0'` | `'5' - '0' = 5` (53 − 48 = 5) |
| Next Character | `ch + 1` | `'B' + 1 = 'C'` |
| Previous Character | `ch - 1` | `'M' - 1 = 'L'` |
| Check Uppercase | `ch >= 'A' && ch <= 'Z'` | `'K'` returns true |
| Check Lowercase | `ch >= 'a' && ch <= 'z'` | `'m'` returns true |
| Check Digit | `ch >= '0' && ch <= '9'` | `'7'` returns true |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Character manipulation is foundational in **lexical analyzers** (compilers), **text editors**, **password validators**, **cryptographic algorithms (Caesar Cipher)**, and **data parsing** in real-world software systems.

### 2.5 Essential Escape Sequences for KTU

| Escape Sequence | ASCII Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `\n` | 10 | Newline (line feed) |
| `\t` | 9 | Horizontal tab |
| `\r` | 13 | Carriage return |
| `\\` | 92 | Backslash character |
| `\'` | 39 | Single quote |
| `\"` | 34 | Double quote |
| `\0` | 0 | Null character (string terminator) |
| `\a` | 7 | Alert (bell sound) |
| `\b` | 8 | Backspace |
| `\f` | 12 | Form feed |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Case Conversion Logic

**Problem:** Given a lowercase character `'h'`, prove that subtracting 32 yields its uppercase equivalent `'H'`.

Let the input character be `ch = 'h'`.

By definition of the ASCII encoding table, every lowercase letter's ASCII value is exactly **32 more** than its corresponding uppercase letter. That is:

$$
\text{ASCII}('a') = \text{ASCII}('A') + 32
$$

Generalizing this for any letter:

$$
\text{ASCII}(\text{lowercase}) = \text{ASCII}(\text{uppercase}) + 32
$$

Rearranging to solve for uppercase:

$$
\text{ASCII}(\text{uppercase}) = \text{ASCII}(\text{lowercase}) - 32
$$

**Numerical Evaluation for `ch = 'h'`:**

$$
\text{ASCII}('h') = 104
$$

$$
\text{Uppercase ASCII} = 104 - 32 = 72
$$

$$
\text{Character at ASCII 72} = 'H'
$$

Therefore, `'h' - 32 = 'H'`. This derivation confirms the conversion rule used universally in C/C++ character manipulation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Digit Character to Integer Value

**Problem:** Given the character `'7'`, derive the integer value 7.

The ASCII value of `'7'` is **55** (since digits 0–9 occupy ASCII positions 48–57). The ASCII value of `'0'` is **48**.

To extract the numeric value, we eliminate the base offset:

$$
\text{Numeric Value} = \text{ASCII}(\text{digit char}) - \text{ASCII}('0')
$$

For the character `'7'`:

$$
\text{Numeric Value} = 55 - 48 = 7
$$

This explains why the formula `ch - '0'` is the standard idiom for digit extraction in C++ programming.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Caesar Cipher Application

The Caesar Cipher is a direct application of character arithmetic. Each letter is shifted by a fixed key `K`.

$$
E(x) = (x + K) \mod 26
$$

$$
D(y) = (y - K) \mod 26
$$

Where `x` and `y` represent the position of the letter in the alphabet (0–25).

**Example:** Encrypt `'C'` (position 2) with key `K = 3`.

$$
E(2) = (2 + 3) \mod 26 = 5
$$

Position 5 corresponds to the letter `'F'`. Therefore, `'C'` encrypts to `'F'`.

> [!NOTE]
> The modular arithmetic `mod 26` ensures that after `'Z'`, the cipher wraps back to `'A'`, maintaining the cyclic nature of the alphabet.

### 3.4 Character Arithmetic Truth Table

| Expression | Computation | Result | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| `'A' + 1` | $65 + 1 = 66$ | `'B'` | char |
| `'5' + 1` | $53 + 1 = 54$ | `'6'` | char |
| `'A' + 'B'` | $65 + 97 = 162$ | Non-printable | int (overflow) |
| `'9' - '0'` | $57 - 48 = 9$ | `9` | int |
| `'a' - 'A'` | $97 - 65 = 32$ | `32` | int |
| `(char)('A' + 3)` | $65 + 3 = 68$ | `'D'` | char |
| `'Z' + 1` | $90 + 1 = 91$ | `'['` | char (next ASCII) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Character Memory Representation (C++ `char`)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source Code: char ch = 'A'] --> B[Compiler Lookup]
    B --> C[ASCII Table Reference]
    C --> D[ASCII Code 65]
    D --> E[Binary: 01000001]
    E --> F[8-bit Memory Cell]
    F --> G[Display Layer: 'A']
```

### 4.2 ASCII Character Classification Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Input Character ch] --> Check1{ch >= 48 AND ch <= 57}
    Check1 -->|Yes| Digit[Category: DIGIT]
    Check1 -->|No| Check2{ch >= 65 AND ch <= 90}
    Check2 -->|Yes| Upper[Category: UPPERCASE]
    Check2 -->|No| Check3{ch >= 97 AND ch <= 122}
    Check3 -->|Yes| Lower[Category: LOWERCASE]
    Check3 -->|No| Other[Category: SPECIAL SYMBOL]
```

### 4.3 Case Conversion Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input: ch = 'm'] --> B[Get ASCII Value: 109]
    B --> C[Subtract 32: 109 - 32 = 77]
    C --> D[Cast to char: 77]
    D --> E[Output: 'M']
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Character Input/Output System

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph UserLayer
        U1[Keyboard Input]
        U2[Monitor Display]
    end
    subgraph ApplicationLayer
        A1[cin or Scanner Read]
        A2[Program Logic]
        A3[cout or System.out Print]
    end
    subgraph SystemLayer
        S1[Character Buffer]
        S2[ASCII Translation Engine]
        S3[OS I/O Handler]
    end
    U1 --> S3
    S3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> U2
```

> [!NOTE]
> This architecture illustrates how a character typed on the keyboard travels through the OS buffer, gets translated to its ASCII integer code, is processed by the application logic, and finally rendered back as a visual character on the display.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Explain the difference between a character literal and a string literal in C++. How is a character internally stored in memory?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **character literal** in C++ is a single symbol enclosed within **single quotes**, such as `'A'` or `'7'`. It occupies exactly **1 byte (8 bits)** of memory. A **string literal**, on the other hand, is a sequence of characters enclosed within **double quotes**, such as `"Hello"`. It occupies `n + 1` bytes where `n` is the number of characters (the extra byte is for the null terminator `'\0'`).

Internally, a character is stored as its corresponding **ASCII integer code** in binary. For example, the character `'A'` is stored as the binary value `01000001` (decimal 65), not as the visual letter 'A'. The compiler performs an implicit mapping from the human-readable character to its numeric code during compilation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is the significance of the value 32 in character manipulation? Show with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The value **32** represents the **arithmetic difference between the ASCII codes of any lowercase letter and its corresponding uppercase letter**. Specifically:

$$
\text{ASCII}('a') - \text{ASCII}('A') = 97 - 65 = 32
$$

This constant difference applies uniformly to all 26 alphabet pairs because ASCII was designed with this consistent gap.

**Example:** To convert `'d'` to uppercase, we subtract 32:

$$
\text{ASCII}('d') = 100
$$

$$
\text{Uppercase} = 100 - 32 = 68 = \text{ASCII}('D')
$$

Therefore, the expression `('d' - 32)` in C++ yields the character `'D'`. This property is exploited in case-insensitive string comparisons, Caesar Cipher encryption, and toggle-case algorithms.

---

### PART B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the ASCII character encoding scheme in detail. List the ASCII ranges for uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and digits. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C++ program that reads a character from the user and determines whether it is an uppercase alphabet, lowercase alphabet, digit, or a special symbol. Use `if-else` statements and ASCII range checks. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange)** is a 7-bit character encoding standard that assigns a unique integer code (0 to 127) to 128 characters, including:

- **33 Non-printable control characters** (codes 0–31 and 127), used for device control (e.g., null, tab, carriage return).
- **95 Printable characters** (codes 32–126), including space, digits, alphabets, and punctuation.

**ASCII Ranges for Key Categories:**

| Category | ASCII Range | Decimal Values | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| Digits (0–9) | 48–57 | 10 characters |
| Uppercase (A–Z) | 65–90 | 26 characters |
| Lowercase (a–z) | 97–122 | 26 characters |
| Space | 32 | 1 character |
| Null (`\0`) | 0 | 1 character |

**Key Observations:**

The digits are placed **before** the uppercase letters, and the uppercase letters are placed **before** the lowercase letters. This sequential ordering means:

- `'0'` to `'9'` are consecutive codes.
- `'A'` to `'Z'` are consecutive codes.
- `'a'` to `'z'` are consecutive codes.

This contiguity is what enables range-based classification (e.g., `ch >= 'A' && ch <= 'Z'`) to work correctly.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    char ch;
    cout << "Enter a character: ";
    cin >> ch;

    if (ch >= 'A' && ch <= 'Z') {
        cout << ch << " is an UPPERCASE alphabet." << endl;
    }
    else if (ch >= 'a' && ch <= 'z') {
        cout << ch << " is a LOWERCASE alphabet." << endl;
    }
    else if (ch >= '0' && ch <= '9') {
        cout << ch << " is a DIGIT." << endl;
    }
    else {
        cout << ch << " is a SPECIAL SYMBOL." << endl;
    }

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of Logic:**

- The condition `ch >= 'A' && ch <= 'Z'` works because the compiler implicitly converts characters to their ASCII integer values for comparison. This is equivalent to `ch >= 65 && ch <= 90`.
- Similarly, `'a' <= ch <= 'z'` checks ASCII range 97–122.
- The digit check `ch >= '0' && ch <= '9'` covers ASCII range 48–57.
- Any character not falling in these three ranges is classified as a special symbol (e.g., `@`, `#`, `!`).

**Sample Execution:**

- Input: `G` → Output: `G is an UPPERCASE alphabet.`
- Input: `5` → Output: `5 is a DIGIT.`
- Input: `@` → Output: `@ is a SPECIAL SYMBOL.`

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain character arithmetic in C++ with suitable examples. How is it useful in implementing the Caesar Cipher? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C++ program to convert all lowercase letters in a string to uppercase **without using built-in functions**. Explain each step. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Character arithmetic** refers to performing mathematical operations (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`) on character operands. Since characters are internally stored as integers (ASCII codes), these operations manipulate the underlying numeric values.

**Example 1: Incrementing a Character**

```cpp
char ch = 'C';
ch = ch + 1;   // 67 + 1 = 68 → 'D'
```

**Example 2: Difference Between Characters**

```cpp
int diff = 'z' - 'a';   // 122 - 97 = 25
```

**Example 3: Digit Extraction**

```cpp
char digit = '7';
int value = digit - '0';   // 55 - 48 = 7
```

**Application in Caesar Cipher:**

The **Caesar Cipher** is a substitution cipher where each letter in the plaintext is shifted by a fixed number of positions `K` in the alphabet. The encryption formula is:

$$
E(c) = (c_{\text{pos}} + K) \mod 26
$$

Where $c_{\text{pos}}$ is the 0-indexed position of the character in the alphabet (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25).

**C++ Implementation Fragment:**

```cpp
char encrypt(char ch, int K) {
    if (ch >= 'A' && ch <= 'Z') {
        return (char)((ch - 'A' + K) % 26 + 'A');
    }
    return ch;
}
```

The expression `ch - 'A'` normalizes the character to a 0–25 range, the modular operation ensures wrapping (Z → A), and adding `'A'` converts back to a printable character.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    char str[100];
    cout << "Enter a string: ";
    cin >> str;

    for (int i = 0; str[i] != '\0'; i++) {
        if (str[i] >= 'a' && str[i] <= 'z') {
            str[i] = str[i] - 32;   // Convert to uppercase
        }
    }

    cout << "Uppercase string: " << str << endl;
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Input:** A character array `str` of size 100 is declared to store the user's input string.
2. **Loop Condition:** The `for` loop iterates from index 0 until the null terminator `'\0'` (ASCII 0) is encountered, which marks the end of a C-style string.
3. **Range Check:** For each character, the condition `str[i] >= 'a' && str[i] <= 'z'` (equivalent to ASCII 97–122) verifies whether it is a lowercase letter.
4. **Conversion:** If the character is lowercase, the expression `str[i] - 32` subtracts the case-difference constant, converting it to its uppercase equivalent. This works because every lowercase letter's ASCII code is exactly 32 greater than its uppercase counterpart.
5. **Output:** The modified string is printed after the loop completes.

**Sample Run:**

- Input: `hello World`
- Output: `HELLO WORLD`

The space and the uppercase `W` remain unchanged because the conversion only applies to characters within the lowercase range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `'A'` with `"A"`:** Using double quotes creates a string, not a character. This is the **most common single-mark deduction** in KTU exams.
> 2. **Forgetting the null terminator:** In C-style string loops, students often use `< 100` instead of `!= '\0'`, which may process garbage memory.
> 3. **Assuming `'a' - 'A' = 26`:** The correct difference is **32**, not 26. Many students confuse this with the alphabet size. Memorize: **32 = gap, 26 = count**.
> 4. **Forgetting to cast back to `char`:** Operations like `ch + 32` produce an `int`. You must cast with `(char)(ch + 32)` if the result is assigned to a `char` variable in some contexts.
> 5. **Writing `'A' <= ch <= 'Z'`:** This is **syntactically valid** in C++ but **logically incorrect** because it evaluates as `(ch >= 'A') < 'Z'`, which always returns true/false. Always use `&&`: `ch >= 'A' && ch <= 'Z'`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **`char`** in C++ is **1 byte**; in Java, it is **2 bytes** (Unicode).
- Characters are stored as **integer ASCII codes**, enabling arithmetic operations.
- **ASCII range: 0 to 127**; printable characters start from 32 (space).
- **Digits `'0'`–`'9'`** occupy ASCII **48–57**.
- **Uppercase `'A'`–`'Z'`** occupy ASCII **65–90**.
- **Lowercase `'a'`–`'z'`** occupy ASCII **97–122**.
- The **case difference constant is 32** (`'a' - 'A' = 32`).
- **Formula for digit extraction:** `numeric_value = ch - '0'`.
- Use **single quotes** for character literals and **double quotes** for string literals.
- Range-based classification (`ch >= 'A' && ch <= 'Z'`) is the standard KTU-recommended approach.
- Escape sequences like `\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\'`, `\"`, and `\0` are non-printable characters with special functions.
- Character arithmetic powers **Caesar Cipher**, **text encryption**, and **input validation** in real-world software.
- Always **cast back to `char`** when assigning the result of character arithmetic to a `char` variable if the expression is part of a larger integer context.
- The **null character `'\0'`** (ASCII 0) terminates C-style strings — never forget it in string processing loops.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boolean. Literals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Boolean. Literals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (Java)** under the KTU 2024 scheme, a **Boolean Literal** is a fixed, compile-time constant value belonging to the primitive data type `boolean`. In Java, there are **exactly two** boolean literals, both of which are reserved keywords:

- **`true`** — represents logical truth.
- **`false`** — represents logical falsehood.

Unlike C/C++, where `0` and non-zero integers are implicitly treated as boolean, Java enforces a **strict type separation**: a boolean variable can only be assigned either `true` or `false` — never an integer, never a string, and never `null` for the primitive type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Data Types, Variables & Literals):**
> Boolean literals form a critical part of the primitive data type discussion. Students must remember that `true` and `false` are written in **lowercase**, unlike older languages where they may appear as `TRUE` or `False`. Java is case-sensitive.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **simple light switch** on a wall. At any given moment, the switch is either **ON** or **OFF** — there is no in-between state. A boolean literal works exactly the same way:

- `true` → The switch is **ON** → the condition holds.
- `false` → The switch is **OFF** → the condition does not hold.

Think of a program that checks whether a student has passed an exam. The result is binary — either the student has passed (`true`) or has not (`false`). Boolean literals encode this binary reality inside a program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"A boolean literal is a literal value of the boolean primitive type, used to represent one of the two logical states: `true` or `false`."*

### 1.3 Standard Properties at a Glance
- **Default value** of a boolean primitive instance/class field: **`false`**.
- **Size in memory** (JLS does not strictly mandate): **1 bit** logically, but JVM typically stores it as **1 byte (8 bits)**.
- **Range of values:** Only two permissible values — `true` and `false`.
- **Keyword status:** Both `true` and `false` are **reserved keywords** in Java and cannot be used as identifiers (variable names, class names, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Classification of Boolean Literals

Boolean literals are classified as **primary literals** (a subcategory of literals in Java's lexical grammar) and belong to the **logical type system**. They are part of the broader **primitive literals** family, alongside integer, floating-point, character, and string literals.

| Literal Category | Example Values | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Boolean Literals | `true`, `false` | `boolean` (primitive) |
| Integer Literals | `10`, `0xA`, `0b1010` | `int`, `long`, etc. |
| Floating-Point Literals | `3.14f`, `2.0d` | `float`, `double` |
| Character Literals | `'A'`, `'\n'` | `char` |
| String Literals | `"Hello"` | `java.lang.String` (reference) |

### 2.2 Operational Mechanics — How Boolean Literals Behave

**Step-by-step logical behavior in Java programs:**

1. **Declaration & Initialization:**
   - A boolean variable is declared using the keyword `boolean`.
   - It can be initialized directly with a boolean literal: `boolean isPassed = true;`
   - It can also be the result of a relational expression: `boolean result = (10 > 5);` → `result` becomes `true`.

2. **Assignment Rules:**
   - Only boolean-typed expressions can be assigned to a boolean variable.
   - The following are **illegal** in Java:
     - `boolean b = 1;` // ❌ Type mismatch
     - `boolean b = 0;` // ❌ Type mismatch
     - `boolean b = "true";` // ❌ String cannot be assigned to boolean
     - `boolean b = null;` // ❌ null cannot be assigned to primitive boolean

3. **Default Field Value:**
   - For class-level (instance) and static boolean fields, the default is `false`.
   - For **local boolean variables** (declared inside methods), **no default exists** — the variable must be explicitly initialized before use.

4. **Wrapper Class — `java.lang.Boolean`:**
   - The primitive `boolean` has a corresponding wrapper class `Boolean`.
   - `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE` are the static constant references to the `Boolean` objects representing `true` and `false`.
   - **Autoboxing:** `Boolean obj = true;` // primitive `true` → `Boolean` object
   - **Unboxing:** `boolean b = Boolean.TRUE;` // `Boolean` object → primitive

5. **Use in Control Flow:**
   - Boolean literals and expressions are the **driving force** behind:
     - `if` / `else if` / `else` statements
     - `while` and `do-while` loops
     - `for` loop conditions
     - Ternary operator `?:`
     - Short-circuit operators `&&` (logical AND) and `||` (logical OR)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Property Sheet

| Property / Rule | Value / Behavior | KTU Exam Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of boolean literals | **2** (`true`, `false`) | Frequently asked definition question |
| Case sensitivity | Lowercase only | `True`, `TRUE`, `False` are **invalid** |
| Default value (class field) | **`false`** | Common 3-mark question |
| Default value (local variable) | **None — compile-time error if uninitialized** | Conceptual pitfall |
| Memory representation | Logically **1 bit**, JVM stores as **1 byte** | Theory question |
| Wrapper class | `java.lang.Boolean` | Distinguish primitive vs object |
| Autoboxing of `true` | `Boolean.TRUE` | Identity check: `true == Boolean.TRUE` is `true` due to JVM caching |
| Can be cast to int? | **No** (compile error) | Common confusion from C/C++ background |
| Allowed in `switch`? | **No** (Java < 14); allowed in **Java 14+** | Version-specific knowledge |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Boolean literals are the foundation of:
- **Digital logic circuits** (AND, OR, NOT gates) in hardware design.
- **Decision-making systems** in enterprise software (loan approval, fraud detection).
- **Flag-based programming** in operating systems and embedded controllers.
- **Conditional rendering** in web backends (servlet filters, REST API response building).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters in OOP:**
> In Object Oriented Programming, boolean literals power the **encapsulation of state**. For example, a `User` class might have fields like `isActive`, `isVerified`, `hasPaidSubscription` — all booleans — which govern access control and business logic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Truth Table Derivations

This section provides exhaustive analytical breakdowns of how boolean literals interact with **logical operators**, derived step-by-step. These truth tables are **high-yield** for KTU board exams.

### 3.1 Logical NOT (`!`) Operator — Exhaustive Evaluation

The unary NOT operator inverts a boolean literal.

$$\text{!true} = \text{false}$$

$$\text{!false} = \text{true}$$

| Operand (boolean literal) | Result of `!` (logical NOT) | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `true` | `false` | Negation of truth yields falsehood. |
| `false` | `true` | Negation of falsehood yields truth. |

### 3.2 Logical AND (`&` and `&&`) Operator — Exhaustive Truth Table

The AND operator returns `true` **only** if **both** operands are `true`.

| Operand A | Operand B | `A & B` | `A && B` (short-circuit) | Step-by-step logic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `true` | `true` | `true` | `true` | Both true → result true. |
| `true` | `false` | `false` | `false` | A true, B false → at least one false → false. |
| `false` | `true` | `false` | `false` | A false → result false regardless of B. |
| `false` | `false` | `false` | `false` | Both false → result false. |

Boolean algebraic expression for AND:

$$A \land B = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } A = \text{true and } B = \text{true} \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 3.3 Logical OR (`|` and `||`) Operator — Exhaustive Truth Table

The OR operator returns `true` if **at least one** operand is `true`.

| Operand A | Operand B | `A` $\vert$ `B` | `A` $\vert\vert$ `B` (short-circuit) | Step-by-step logic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `true` | `true` | `true` | `true` | At least one true → result true. |
| `true` | `false` | `true` | `true` | A true → result true regardless of B. |
| `false` | `true` | `true` | `true` | B true → at least one true → true. |
| `false` | `false` | `false` | `false` | Both false → result false. |

Boolean algebraic expression for OR:

$$A \lor B = \begin{cases} \text{false} & \text{if } A = \text{false and } B = \text{false} \\ \text{true} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 3.4 De Morgan's Laws — Derived Equivalences

These laws relate AND, OR, and NOT operations and are essential for boolean expression simplification in exams.

$$\neg(A \land B) \equiv \neg A \lor \neg B$$

$$\neg(A \lor B) \equiv \neg A \land \neg B$$

**Step-by-step proof of the first law (using truth table):**

| A | B | A $\land$ B | $\neg$(A $\land$ B) | $\neg$A | $\neg$B | $\neg$A $\lor$ $\neg$B | Match? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| true | true | true | **false** | false | false | **false** | ✅ |
| true | false | false | **true** | false | true | **true** | ✅ |
| false | true | false | **true** | true | false | **true** | ✅ |
| false | false | false | **true** | true | true | **true** | ✅ |

Since columns 4 and 7 match in every row, the equivalence is proven.

### 3.5 Worked Java Code Trace

Consider the following Java snippet:

```java
boolean a = true;
boolean b = false;
boolean c = (a && !b) || (b && !a);
```

**Exhaustive step-by-step evaluation:**

$$\text{Step 1: } a = \text{true}, \quad b = \text{false}$$

$$\text{Step 2: } !b = \text{!false} = \text{true}$$

$$\text{Step 3: } a \land \neg b = \text{true} \land \text{true} = \text{true}$$

$$\text{Step 4: } !a = \text{!true} = \text{false}$$

$$\text{Step 5: } b \land \neg a = \text{false} \land \text{false} = \text{false}$$

$$\text{Step 6: } c = \text{true} \lor \text{false} = \text{true}$$

**Final value of `c`: `true`.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Type Hierarchy — Boolean in Java's Type System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates where boolean literals fit within Java's broader type system, which is frequently tested in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Java Type System"]
    nodeB["Primitive Types"]
    nodeC["Reference Types"]
    nodeD["Numeric"]
    nodeE["Character"]
    nodeF["Boolean LITERAL true false"]
    nodeG["Classes and Interfaces"]
    nodeH["Wrapper Boolean"]
    nodeI["Arrays Strings Enums"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -.->|"Autoboxing Unboxing"| nodeF
```

### 4.2 Boolean Literal Decision Flow in Control Structures

This block diagram shows how boolean literals drive typical `if-else` decision flow — a common exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart(["Program Entry"])
    nodeEval["Evaluate Boolean Expression"]
    nodeCheck{"Result is true or false"}
    nodeTrue["Execute THEN Block"]
    nodeFalse["Execute ELSE Block"]
    nodeExit(["Continue Program"])

    nodeStart --> nodeEval
    nodeEval --> nodeCheck
    nodeCheck -->|"true literal"| nodeTrue
    nodeCheck -->|"false literal"| nodeFalse
    nodeTrue --> nodeExit
    nodeFalse --> nodeExit
```

### 4.3 Boolean Operations Functional Architecture

A high-level block diagram showing how the three fundamental boolean operators (`!`, `&&`, `||`) interact with boolean literal inputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInA["Input A: boolean literal"]
    nodeInB["Input B: boolean literal"]
    nodeNot["NOT Gate Unary Operator"]
    nodeAnd["AND Gate Binary Operator"]
    nodeOr["OR Gate Binary Operator"]
    nodeOut1["Output: inverted boolean"]
    nodeOut2["Output: conjunction boolean"]
    nodeOut3["Output: disjunction boolean"]

    nodeInA --> nodeNot --> nodeOut1
    nodeInA --> nodeAnd
    nodeInB --> nodeAnd --> nodeOut2
    nodeInA --> nodeOr
    nodeInB --> nodeOr --> nodeOut3
```

### 4.4 Lifecycle of a Boolean Literal from Source to Execution

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source Code
    participant LEX as Lexical Analyzer
    participant PAR as Parser
    participant BYT as Bytecode Generator
    participant JVM as JVM Runtime

    SRC->>LEX: Encounter token true or false
    LEX->>LEX: Verify lowercase, keyword status
    LEX->>PAR: Emit BOOLEAN_LITERAL token
    PAR->>PAR: Type-check against boolean context
    PAR->>BYT: Generate ICONST_1 or ICONST_0 opcode
    BYT->>JVM: Push boolean value onto operand stack
    JVM->>JVM: Branch on true or false at control flow
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define boolean literals in Java. How many boolean literals exist, and what is the default value of a boolean instance variable?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** A boolean literal is a constant value of the `boolean` primitive type in Java, used to represent one of the two logical states — truth or falsehood.
2. **Count (1 mark):** Java has exactly **two** boolean literals: `true` and `false`. Both are reserved keywords and must be written in lowercase.
3. **Default value (1 mark):** The default value of a boolean instance variable (class-level field) is **`false`**. Note that local boolean variables inside methods have no default and must be explicitly initialized.

---

**Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
*Differentiate between the primitive `boolean` type and the `Boolean` wrapper class in Java with respect to literals.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Primitive `boolean` | Wrapper `Boolean` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type | Primitive data type | Reference type (class) |
| Literals | `true`, `false` | `Boolean.TRUE`, `Boolean.FALSE` (static constants) |
| Memory | Stack (typically 1 byte) | Heap (object header + value) |
| Null allowed? | No | Yes (`Boolean b = null;`) |
| Autoboxing | `Boolean obj = true;` auto-converts primitive to object | — |

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question is divided into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of boolean literals in Java. Discuss the rules for assigning values to a boolean variable, with at least three valid and three invalid examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Construct the complete truth tables for the logical operators `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), and `!` (NOT) using boolean literals. State and prove De Morgan's First Law using a truth table. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Concept of Boolean Literals (3 marks):**
Boolean literals are the only two valid constant values of the `boolean` primitive type in Java: `true` and `false`. They are:
- Written in **lowercase** (Java is case-sensitive).
- **Reserved keywords** — they cannot be used as variable names.
- Used directly in conditional checks, loops, and logical evaluations.
- The **default value** of a boolean instance field is `false`.

**Valid Examples (2 marks):**
1. `boolean isActive = true;` — direct boolean literal assignment.
2. `boolean hasFailed = (marks < 40);` — relational expression evaluating to a boolean.
3. `boolean flag = !false;` — using logical NOT on a boolean literal.

**Invalid Examples (2 marks):**
1. `boolean b = 1;` — ❌ Type mismatch: cannot assign int to boolean.
2. `boolean b = "true";` — ❌ String cannot be converted to boolean.
3. `boolean b = True;` — ❌ Case mismatch: `True` is not a keyword.

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Truth Table for `&&` (AND) (1.5 marks):**

| A | B | A && B |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| true | true | **true** |
| true | false | false |
| false | true | false |
| false | false | false |

**Truth Table for `\|\|` (OR) (1.5 marks):**

| A | B | A \|\| B |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| true | true | **true** |
| true | false | **true** |
| false | true | **true** |
| false | false | false |

**Truth Table for `!` (NOT) (1 mark):**

| A | !A |
| :--- | :--- |
| true | false |
| false | true |

**De Morgan's First Law — Statement (1 mark):**

$$\neg(A \land B) \equiv \neg A \lor \neg B$$

*"The negation of a conjunction is the disjunction of the negations."*

**Proof via Truth Table (2 marks):**

| A | B | A∧B | ¬(A∧B) | ¬A | ¬B | ¬A ∨ ¬B | Match |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| T | T | T | **F** | F | F | **F** | ✅ |
| T | F | F | **T** | F | T | **T** | ✅ |
| F | T | F | **T** | T | F | **T** | ✅ |
| F | F | F | **T** | T | T | **T** | ✅ |

Since columns 4 and 7 are identical, the law is **proven**. ∎

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** What are boolean literals in Java? Explain the difference between `&` (bitwise/logical AND) and `&&` (short-circuit AND) operators with the help of a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that declares three boolean variables, assigns boolean literals, and demonstrates the use of `if-else`, ternary operator, and a `while` loop using boolean expressions. Show the expected output. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Boolean Literals — Definition (2 marks):**
In Java, boolean literals are the only two permissible values for the `boolean` primitive type: `true` and `false`. They are reserved keywords, written in lowercase, and form the basis of all logical decision-making in Java programs.

**Difference between `&` and `&&` (5 marks):**

| Feature | `&` (Logical AND) | `&&` (Short-Circuit AND) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Evaluation | Evaluates **both** operands always | Evaluates **right** operand **only if left is true** |
| Performance | Slower (no early exit) | Faster (short-circuits) |
| Use case | When both sides **must** execute (e.g., method calls) | When right side has side-effects you want to skip |
| Result | `true` only if both are `true` | `true` only if both are `true` (same logical result) |

**Example:**
```java
int x = 5, y = 10;
boolean result1 = (x > 0) & (++y > 5);  // y becomes 11
boolean result2 = (x > 0) && (++y > 5); // y becomes 12
```

In the first case, `(++y > 5)` is evaluated regardless. In the second case, since `(x > 0)` is `true`, the right side **is** evaluated. However, if `(x > 0)` were `false`, the right side would be **skipped** with `&&`, but **not** with `&`.

---

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Java Program:**

```java
public class BooleanDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        boolean isJavaFun = true;
        boolean isHard = false;
        boolean isWorthLearning = true;

        // if-else demonstration
        if (isJavaFun && isWorthLearning) {
            System.out.println("Keep learning Java!");
        } else {
```
