```mermaid
    Check3 -- No --> Next[Next statement in body]
    Next --> Update
    Cond -- false --> ExitLoop
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Iteration Use Cases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Domain]
        A1[Array Traversal]
        A2[Menu Repetition]
        A3[Search Algorithms]
        A4[Numerical Methods]
    end
    subgraph LoopCore[Iteration Engine]
        B1[Counter-Controlled for]
        B2[Condition-Controlled while]
        B3[Post-test do while]
    end
    subgraph ControlLayer[Jump Statements]
        C1[break - early exit]
        C2[continue - skip]
        C3[return - method exit]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Engineering Application]
        D1[Embedded Polling]
        D2[Signal Processing]
        D3[State Machines]
        D4[Matrix Computation]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    B1 --> D4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between `while` and `do…while` loops in C++. Give one example of a situation where a `do…while` is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis of Difference | `while` Loop | `do…while` Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of loop** | Pre-test (entry-controlled) | Post-test (exit-controlled) |
| **Condition check** | Condition is tested *before* body execution | Condition is tested *after* body execution |
| **Minimum executions** | Zero (body may not execute even once) | One (body always executes at least once) |
| **Syntax terminator** | No semicolon after `while(condition)` | **Semicolon mandatory** after `while(condition);` |

**Preferred use case of `do…while`:** *Menu-driven programs* in which the menu must be displayed at least once before asking the user whether to continue.

```cpp
do {
    cout << "1. Add  2. Subtract  3. Exit\n";
    cout << "Enter choice: ";
    cin >> choice;
} while (choice != 3);
```

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is the role of the `break` and `continue` statements inside a loop? Explain with a short C++ snippet.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`break`** — Terminates the **innermost** enclosing loop (or `switch`) and transfers control to the statement immediately following the terminated block.
- **`continue`** — Skips the **remaining statements** in the current iteration and proceeds with the **next iteration** of the loop.

```cpp
for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
    if (i == 5) break;      // exits the loop when i is 5
    if (i % 2 == 0) continue; // skips even numbers
    cout << i << " ";       // prints 1 3
}
```

**Output:** `1 3`
After printing `1` and `3`, when `i` reaches `4`, `continue` skips the print, and at `i = 5` the `break` exits the loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the syntax and execution flow of the `for` loop in C++ with a neat flowchart. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a C++ program to read `N` integers into an array and print the **sum, average, maximum, and minimum** using a `for` loop. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) `for` loop syntax and flow (7 marks):**

**Syntax:**
```cpp
for (initialization; condition; update) {
    // body of loop
}
```

**Execution Flow:**
1. The **initialization** expression executes **exactly once** at loop entry.
2. The **condition** is evaluated.
3. If **true**, the **body** executes; if **false**, control exits the loop.
4. After body execution, the **update** expression executes.
5. Control returns to step 2; the cycle repeats until the condition becomes false.

**Generalized equation (iteration count):**

$$
N_{\text{iter}} =
\begin{cases}
\left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{limit} - \text{start}}{\text{step}} \right\rfloor + 1, & \text{step} > 0,\ \text{start} \le \text{limit} \\[2mm]
\infty, & \text{otherwise (logical error)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Flowchart:**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialization: i = start]
    B --> C{i less than equal to limit?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute body]
    D --> E[Update: i = i + step]
    E --> C
    C -- No --> F([End of loop])
```

**Note on C++ flexibility:** Any of the three expressions in the `for` header may be omitted (but semicolons must remain). For example, `for(;;)` creates an infinite loop; `for(int i=0;;i++)` is a loop with no terminating condition.

---

**(b) C++ Program — Statistical analysis using `for` loop (7 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int N;
    cout << "Enter N: ";
    cin >> N;
    
    int arr[100], sum = 0, maxVal, minVal;
    
    cout << "Enter " << N << " integers:\n";
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        cin >> arr[i];
        sum += arr[i];
        if (i == 0) {
            maxVal = minVal = arr[i];
        } else {
            if (arr[i] > maxVal) maxVal = arr[i];
            if (arr[i] < minVal) minVal = arr[i];
        }
    }
    
    float avg = (float)sum / N;
    
    cout << "Sum     = " << sum     << endl;
    cout << "Average = " << avg     << endl;
    cout << "Maximum = " << maxVal  << endl;
    cout << "Minimum = " << minVal  << endl;
    
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter N: 5
Enter 5 integers: 10 25 5 40 15
Sum     = 95
Average = 19
Maximum = 40
Minimum = 5
```

**Explanation of key steps:**
- The `for (int i = 0; i < N; i++)` iterates `N` times.
- `sum` accumulates the running total using `sum += arr[i]`.
- `maxVal` and `minVal` are initialized at `i == 0` and updated via conditional checks.
- The average uses **explicit cast** `(float)sum` to avoid integer division truncation — a KTU-favoured mark-earning detail.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the working of `while` and `do…while` loops in C++. Compare them with a tabular form. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Predict the output of the following C++ program. Show the step-by-step trace. *(7 marks)*

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int main() {
    int i = 1, j = 1;
    while (i <= 3) {
        j = 1;
        while (j <= i) {
            cout << "*";
            j++;
        }
        cout << endl;
        i++;
    }
    return 0;
}
```

#### Model Solution

**(a) `while` and `do…while` — Comparison (7 marks):**

**`while` loop:**
```cpp
while (condition) {
    // body
    // update
}
```
- Pre-test loop: condition checked first.
- If initially false, body never executes.
- Use when: the number of repetitions is **not known** in advance; depends on a runtime condition.

**`do…while` loop:**
```cpp
do {
    // body
    // update
} while (condition);   // semicolon mandatory
```
- Post-test loop: body executes first, then condition is checked.
- Body always executes at least once.
- Use when: the body must execute at least once (e.g., menu display, input prompt with validation).

**Tabular Comparison:**

| Feature | `while` | `do…while` |
|---|---|---|
| Loop type | Entry-controlled (pre-test) | Exit-controlled (post-test) |
| Minimum body executions | 0 | 1 |
| Semicolon after header | Not required | **Required** |
| Risk if condition is initially false | Body skipped | Body still runs once |
| Typical use | Polling, search | Menu, input validation |

---

**(b) Output Prediction with Step-by-Step Trace (7 marks):**

| Outer `i` | Inner `j` loop range | Stars printed | Newline after | Resulting line |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | j = 1 to 1 | 1 star | Yes | `*` |
| 2 | j = 1 to 2 | 2 stars | Yes | `**` |
| 3 | j = 1 to 3 | 3 stars | Yes | `***` |

**Final Output:**
```
*
**
***
```

**Explanation:**
- For `i = 1`: inner loop runs once → prints `*` → newline.
- For `i = 2`: inner loop runs twice → prints `**` → newline.
- For `i = 3`: inner loop runs thrice → prints `***` → newline.
- When `i` becomes `4`, the condition `i <= 3` fails; loop terminates.

This program is the classic **right-angled triangle star pattern** — a frequent KTU question under iteration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the semicolon** after `while (condition);` in `do…while` (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing `break` with `continue`** in nested loops — `break` only exits the innermost loop.
> 3. **Off-by-one errors** in `for` loop boundaries (`i <= N` vs `i < N`).
> 4. **Integer division** when computing average — must use explicit cast to `float` or `double`.
> 5. **Modifying the loop variable** inside a `for` body unnecessarily (legal but bad practice; can cause infinite loops).
> 6. **Not initializing variables** before use in `while` loops (e.g., forgetting `i = 0` outside the loop).
> 7. **Misinterpreting `i++` vs `++i`** in trace questions — they produce different evaluation orders in expressions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Three iteration statements:** `while` (pre-test, 0+ executions), `do…while` (post-test, 1+ executions, semicolon mandatory), `for` (pre-test, counter-driven).
- **Enhanced `for` (Java only):** for-each traversal of arrays/collections; cannot modify structure.
- **Iteration count formula** for `for (i = a; i < b; i += s)`:
  $$N = \left\lfloor \frac{b - a}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$
- **`break`:** Exits the **innermost** enclosing loop or `switch`; transfers control to the next statement after the loop.
- **`continue`:** Skips the rest of the current iteration; in `for`, the update expression still runs.
- **`return`:** Exits the current method; in `void` methods → `return;`; in value methods → `return expr;`.
- **`goto`:** Available in C++ (discouraged); reserved but unusable in Java.
- **Labeled `break`/`continue`:** Java feature to break/continue an **outer** loop directly.
- **Infinite loop idioms:** `while(true) { }`, `do { } while(true);`, `for(;;) { }`.
- **Common patterns:** Counter-controlled (`for`), sentinel-controlled (`while`), menu-driven (`do…while`), collection traversal (enhanced `for`).
- **Engineered applications:** Polling in embedded systems, finite-state machines, signal sample iteration, iterative numerical solvers (Newton-Raphson, Gauss-Seidel), matrix traversal in image processing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Oriented Programming in Java - Class Fundamentals
Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Declaring Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object Oriented Programming (OOP) under the Java/C++ paradigm, **Declaring Objects** is the two-stage process by which a programmer brings a class's blueprint to life in memory. Formally, it comprises:

1. **Declaration of a Reference Variable** — A typed identifier is introduced on the stack frame of the current execution context. This variable is capable of holding the memory address (reference) of an object instance, not the object itself.
2. **Object Instantiation (Construction)** — Memory of the required size is dynamically allocated on the **Heap** (or managed runtime area), the object is initialized, and the reference variable is bound to that memory address.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT415 Module 2):**
> Declaring Objects is the foundational gateway concept that bridges *Class Definition* (compile-time) with *Object Usage* (run-time). Every subsequent OOP feature — inheritance, polymorphism, exception handling — operates on live object instances created through this very mechanism.

The generalized syntax is:
```
ClassName referenceVariable;        // Step 1: Reference declaration
referenceVariable = new ClassName(); // Step 2: Object instantiation
```

These two steps are conventionally combined into a single line:
```
ClassName referenceVariable = new ClassName();
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **Class** is an **architectural blueprint** of a house. The blueprint itself is *not* a house — you cannot live in it.

- **Declaring a reference variable** is like writing "Plot No. 42" in your property register. The plot number is real (it occupies a slot in the register — your stack), but **no house is built yet**.
- **Instantiating an object** with the `new` keyword is like a construction company actually *building* the house on Plot 42 using the blueprint, and then writing the postal address (memory address) of the finished house into your register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Truths of Object Declaration:**
> 1. The **class** is the *type* (blueprint).
> 2. The **reference variable** is the *handle* (property register entry — stored on the stack).
> 3. The **object** is the *actual entity* built in memory (stored on the heap).

## 1.3 Physical & Runtime Metrics

The following standard metrics govern object declaration:

- **Default value of an uninitialized reference variable** = `null` (Java) / undefined behaviour if dereferenced (C++).
- **Size of a reference variable** is platform-dependent but typically **4 bytes** on 32-bit JVMs and **8 bytes** on 64-bit JVMs (compressed oops may reduce this to 4 bytes).
- **Object header overhead** in Java: typically **12–16 bytes** (mark word + class pointer) plus instance data, aligned to **8-byte boundaries**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Process: Granular Breakdown

### Stage 1 — Declaration of Reference Variable
- Compiler allocates space on the **call stack** of the current method.
- The variable is statically typed to `ClassName`.
- Its initial content is `null` (Java) or indeterminate (C++).
- No class constructor is invoked at this stage.
- The reference is **not yet usable** for method calls (would throw `NullPointerException` in Java).

### Stage 2 — Object Instantiation via `new`
The `new` operator triggers the following JVM/compiler-internal sequence:

1. **Class Loading Check** — ClassLoader verifies `ClassName` is loaded; if not, loads and links it.
2. **Memory Allocation** — A contiguous block of memory sufficient for the object's instance variables and header is allocated on the **heap** (Eden space in Java's Young Generation).
3. **Default Initialization** — All instance fields are set to JVM defaults:
   - Numeric types → `0`
   - `boolean` → `false`
   - Object references → `null`
4. **Constructor Invocation** — The matched constructor executes, possibly running explicit field initializers and a constructor body.
5. **Reference Binding** — The memory address of the newly created object is returned and assigned to the reference variable on the stack.

## 2.2 Why the Two-Stage Design Matters

- **Decoupling of type and identity** allows a single reference variable to be reassigned to *different* objects of the *same* type during its lifetime.
- **Polymorphism prerequisite** — Superclass references can hold subclass objects (upcasting), which is the foundation of dynamic method dispatch.
- **Memory efficiency** — References can be passed around (8 bytes) instead of copying entire objects (potentially kilobytes).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Concept | Java Syntax | C++ Syntax | Memory Location of Variable | Memory Location of Object |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reference declaration | `Box b;` | `Box* b;` (pointer) | **Stack** | *(none yet)* |
| Object creation | `b = new Box();` | `Box b;` *(value)* or `b = new Box();` *(heap)* | Stack | **Heap** (via `new`) |
| Default reference | `null` | Undefined (raw pointer) | — | — |
| Combined statement | `Box b = new Box();` | `Box* b = new Box();` | Stack | Heap |
| Reassignment | `b = new Box();` | `b = new Box();` | Stack (same slot) | Heap (new address) |
| Garbage collection | Automatic (JVM) | Manual (`delete b;`) | — | Deallocated memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU 2024:**
> In **Java**, local variables of object type are *always* references (pointers under the hood, but Java disallows pointer arithmetic). In **C++**, declaring `Box b;` creates the object on the *stack* directly, while `Box* b = new Box;` places it on the *heap*. The KTU PEMRT415 syllabus primarily examines **Java semantics**, but C++ contrast is a frequent 14-mark question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems**: Stack-allocated objects in C++ avoid heap fragmentation and deterministic cleanup — vital in real-time automotive/aerospace firmware.
- **Enterprise Java Servers**: Pooling pre-declared references and re-binding them to new objects via factory patterns reduces GC pressure.
- **Game Engines**: Object pools declared in advance prevent frame-rate hitches caused by mid-render `new` calls.
- **Microservices**: Stateless service objects are declared once at startup and reused across thousands of requests — a direct application of the "declare once, instantiate efficiently" principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Trace Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Memory Trace Walkthrough — The Canonical Example

Consider the following Java code segment (a classic KTU 14-mark setup):

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;

    Student(int r, String n) {
        rollNo = r;
        name   = n;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println(rollNo + " " + name);
    }
}

public class TestStudent {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1;                 // Stage 1
        s1 = new Student(101, "Anu"); // Stage 2

        Student s2 = new Student(102, "Bala");
        s1 = s2;                    // Reassignment
        s1.display();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Memory Trace

**Line:** `Student s1;`
- Stack frame of `main` pushes a 4-byte slot named `s1`.
- Value: `null` (default for object reference).
- Heap: untouched.

**Line:** `s1 = new Student(101, "Anu");`
- JVM allocates ~24 bytes on the heap (12-byte header + 4-byte `rollNo` + 8-byte `name` reference + alignment).
- Fields are zero-initialized: `rollNo=0`, `name=null`.
- Constructor runs: `rollNo=101`, `name` points to a `String` object `"Anu"` in the String Pool.
- Reference address (e.g., `0x7A3F`) is written into stack slot `s1`.

**Line:** `Student s2 = new Student(102, "Bala");`
- New object `0x7B12` is created on the heap.
- Stack slot `s2 = 0x7B12`.

**Line:** `s1 = s2;`
- The value of `s2` (`0x7B12`) is copied into `s1`.
- The original object at `0x7A3F` (Anu) loses its only reference → becomes **eligible for Garbage Collection**.

**Line:** `s1.display();`
- Dereferences `s1` → reaches object at `0x7B12` → prints `102 Bala`.

### Mathematical Abstraction of Reference Copying

Let the heap address of an object be $a$ and the stack slot be $v$. The declaration and instantiation follow the assignment:

$$
v \leftarrow \text{NEW}(\text{ClassName}) \quad \text{where } \text{NEW}(\cdot) \text{ returns a unique heap address } a
$$

Upon reassignment `s1 = s2`:

$$
v_{s1} \leftarrow v_{s2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{s1} = a_{s2}
$$

The reference count $R(o)$ of the abandoned object $o$ becomes:

$$
R(o_{\text{Anu}}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{GC-Eligible}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Scenarios & Outcomes

| Code Action | Heap State (Objects) | Stack State (Refs) | Pitfall / Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Student s;` | No object | `s = null` | Calling `s.display()` → `NullPointerException` |
| `s = new Student();` | 1 object at `0xA1` | `s = 0xA1` | Valid; constructor ran |
| `Student s2 = s;` | 1 object at `0xA1` (ref count = 2) | `s = 0xA1`, `s2 = 0xA1` | Both refer to **same** object — mutating via `s2` reflects in `s` |
| `s = new Student();` | 2 objects: `0xA1`, `0xB2` | `s = 0xB2`, `s2 = 0xA1` | Original `0xA1` still alive via `s2` |
| `s = s2; s2 = null;` | 1 object at `0xA1` | `s = 0xA1`, `s2 = null` | Object preserved via `s` |
| `s = null; s2 = null;` | 1 object at `0xA1` (orphan) | Both null | Object becomes **GC-eligible** |

## 3.3 Default Field Values Upon Object Instantiation

When the `new` operator allocates memory, the JVM initializes all instance fields to documented defaults before any constructor body executes:

$$
\text{default}(f) = \begin{cases} 0, & f \text{ is } \texttt{byte, short, int, long} \\ 0.0, & f \text{ is } \texttt{float, double} \\ \texttt{'\textbackslash u0000'}, & f \text{ is } \texttt{char} \\ \texttt{false}, & f \text{ is } \texttt{boolean} \\ \texttt{null}, & f \text{ is any reference type} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This default initialization is performed by the JVM at the **beginning** of every constructor — even before the first line of the constructor body executes. It is the reason why instance variables in Java never have indeterminate values, unlike C++ primitive members.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Reference Arithmetic

Suppose a 64-bit JVM is used. Each reference occupies $r = 8$ bytes. The object header occupies $h = 12$ bytes (mark word 8 + class pointer 4 with compressed oops disabled). An object has two instance fields: one `int` (4 bytes) and one reference (8 bytes). Total heap footprint with 8-byte alignment:

$$
\text{Size} = h + 4 + 8 = 12 + 12 = 24 \text{ bytes}
$$

Since 24 is already a multiple of 8, no padding is added. If, however, a `boolean` (1 byte) were added:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{raw}} = 12 + 4 + 8 + 1 = 25 \text{ bytes}
$$

Rounded up to the next 8-byte boundary:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{aligned}} = \lceil 25 / 8 \rceil \times 8 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

This **7-byte padding** is a frequent KTU exam question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Two-Stage Object Declaration Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: ClassName refVar = new ClassName] --> B{Compiler Parses Statement}
    B --> C[Stage 1: Declare Reference Variable]
    C --> C1[Allocate 4-8 bytes on Stack]
    C --> C2[Initialize refVar to null]
    C1 --> D[Stage 2: new Operator Triggered]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[ClassLoader Verifies ClassName]
    E --> F[Allocate Memory on Heap Eden]
    F --> G[Default Initialize Instance Fields]
    G --> H[Invoke Matched Constructor]
    H --> I[Constructor Body Executes Explicit Initializations]
    I --> J[Return Heap Address to refVar]
    J --> K[refVar Now Points to Live Object]
    K --> L[Object Ready for Method Invocation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Memory Layout (Stack vs Heap)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STK["STACK FRAME of main"]
        s1["s1 : 0x7B12"]
        s2["s2 : 0x7B12"]
    end
    subgraph HEP["HEAP MEMORY"]
        o1["Object 0x7A3F ANU (GC-Eligible)"]
        o2["Object 0x7B12 BALA rollNo=102 name=Bala"]
    end
    s1 --> o2
    s2 --> o2
    o1 -. "no incoming references" .-> GC["Garbage Collector"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPILE["COMPILE TIME"]
        C1[Class Declaration: type ClassName]
        C2[Reference Variable: refVar : ClassName]
    end
    subgraph RUNTIME["RUNTIME"]
        R1[new ClassName Activated]
        R2[Heap Allocation Module]
        R3[Constructor Dispatcher]
        R4[Field Initializer]
    end
    subgraph MEM["MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"]
        M1[Stack Frame Allocator]
        M2[Heap Manager Eden Space]
        M3[Garbage Collector Mark Sweep Compact]
    end
    C1 --> R1
    C2 --> M1
    R1 --> R2 --> M2
    R2 --> R4
    R2 --> R3
    M1 --> R3
    M2 --> M3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Reassignment & GC Eligibility Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Object A at addr 0xA1 created, s1 points to A] --> P2[Object B at addr 0xB2 created, s2 points to B]
    P2 --> P3[Statement s1 = s2 executes]
    P3 --> P4[s1 now holds address 0xB2]
    P3 --> P5[Object A loses its only reference]
    P5 --> P6{Reference Count of A equals zero?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Object A becomes GC Eligible]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Object A remains alive]
    P7 --> P9[GC Sweep frees memory of A]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between a class and an object in Java. How are objects declared and instantiated?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **class** is a blueprint or template that defines the structure (attributes) and behaviour (methods) of entities. It exists only at compile-time as a `*.class` file. An **object** is a runtime instance of a class occupying real memory on the heap.

Objects are declared in two stages:
1. **Reference declaration**: `ClassName ref;` — allocates a reference slot on the stack, initialized to `null`.
2. **Instantiation**: `ref = new ClassName(args);` — allocates heap memory, initializes fields, invokes constructor, and binds the address to `ref`.

Both can be combined: `ClassName ref = new ClassName(args);` **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*What is the default value of a reference variable in Java? What happens if you invoke a method using an uninitialized reference?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The default value of any object reference variable in Java is **`null`**, assigned by the JVM during stack allocation. If a method is invoked using such a reference (e.g., `null.display()`), the JVM throws a **`java.lang.NullPointerException`** at runtime, terminating the current thread abnormally. Java does not allow pointer arithmetic, so the reference cannot be incremented or dereferenced arbitrarily like in C++. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

(a) *Explain with a neat diagram the two-step process of declaring and instantiating objects in Java. Illustrate the difference between stack and heap memory during this process.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *Given the following Java code, draw the memory diagram showing stack and heap contents after each line of execution. Identify the object(s) eligible for garbage collection at the end.* **(7 marks)**

```java
class Box {
    int length, width;
    Box(int l, int w) { length = l; width = w; }
    int volume() { return length * width; }
}

public class TestBox {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box(2, 3);
        Box b2 = new Box(4, 5);
        b1 = b2;
        b2 = null;
        Box b3 = b1;
        b1 = new Box(6, 7);
    }
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-step process (7 marks):**
The two-step process comprises:
1. **Reference Variable Declaration** — A typed identifier is created on the call stack. Compiler reserves 4–8 bytes depending on JVM architecture. Initial value is `null`. No constructor is called.
2. **Object Instantiation via `new`** — JVM performs: (i) class loading check, (ii) heap memory allocation, (iii) default field initialization, (iv) constructor invocation, (v) returns heap address which is bound to the reference variable.

**Memory diagram:**

```
STACK (main)            HEAP
+-----------------+     +--------------------+
| b1 : 0x100      | --> | 0x100: l=2, w=3   |
| b2 : 0x200      | --> | 0x200: l=4, w=5   |
+-----------------+     +--------------------+
```

**(b) Memory trace (7 marks):**

| After Line | b1 | b2 | b3 | Heap Objects Alive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Box b1 = new Box(2,3);` | `0x100` | — | — | `0x100 {2,3}` |
| `Box b2 = new Box(4,5);` | `0x100` | `0x200` | — | `0x100`, `0x200` |
| `b1 = b2;` | `0x200` | `0x200` | — | `0x100` (orphan), `0x200` |
| `b2 = null;` | `0x200` | `null` | — | `0x100` (orphan), `0x200` |
| `Box b3 = b1;` | `0x200` | `null` | `0x200` | `0x100` (orphan), `0x200` |
| `b1 = new Box(6,7);` | `0x300` | `null` | `0x200` | `0x200`, `0x300`; `0x100` GC-eligible |

**Object eligible for GC**: The object originally at `0x100` (l=2, w=3). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

(a) *Compare object declaration in Java and C++. Highlight the role of the `new` keyword and the placement of objects in memory in both languages.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *Write a Java program to declare a class `Car` with fields `model` (String) and `price` (double). Instantiate three Car objects, store their references in an array, and demonstrate that reassigning one array element affects the original reference variable. Show output.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Java vs C++ object declaration (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Java | C++ |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration | `Car c;` — only reference created | `Car c;` — full object on stack |
| Instantiation | `c = new Car();` mandatory for objects | `Car c;` auto on stack; `new` for heap |
| Memory of var | Stack (reference) | Stack (object) or Stack (pointer if `new`) |
| Memory of object | Always heap | Stack or heap (programmer choice) |
| Deallocation | Automatic GC | Manual `delete` (heap objects) |
| Default ref | `null` | Undefined (dangling pointer risk) |
| Pointer arithmetic | Forbidden | Allowed |

**Role of `new`**: In both languages, `new` allocates heap memory, invokes the constructor, and returns the address. In C++, omission of `new` creates a stack object with automatic destruction at scope exit. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Java program (7 marks):**

```java
class Car {
    String model;
    double price;

    Car(String m, double p) {
        model = m; price = p;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println(model + " : Rs." + price);
    }
}

public class TestCar {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car c1 = new Car("Honda", 750000);
        Car c2 = new Car("Toyota", 950000);
        Car c3 = new Car("Tesla", 5500000);

        Car[] arr = {c1, c2, c3};

        System.out.println("Before reassignment:");
        c1.display();

        arr[0] = new Car("BMW", 6500000);

        System.out.println("After reassignment arr[0]:");
        c1.display();         // Still points to Honda
        arr[0].display();     // Now points to BMW
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Before reassignment:
Honda : Rs.750000.0
After reassignment arr[0]:
Honda : Rs.750000.0
BMW : Rs.6500000.0
```

**Explanation**: `arr[0] = new Car("BMW", 6500000);` creates a **new** object and stores its address in `arr[0]`. The original reference `c1` still holds the address of the "Honda" object, proving that **references are passed by value** and array element reassignment does not mutate the original reference variable. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing reference with object** — Writing "b1 is an object" instead of "b1 is a *reference* to an object" costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting default initialization** — Many students skip the zero-initialization step of the `new` process. Examiners allocate **1 dedicated mark** for this.
> 3. **Drawing memory diagrams incorrectly** — Stack and heap must be shown as **separate regions** with arrows from stack to heap, not as a single flat memory. Getting this wrong costs up to 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Missing GC eligibility reasoning** — When asked "which object is GC-eligible?", simply naming the object is insufficient (1 mark). You must show the **reference count reaching zero**.
> 5. **Java vs C++ confusion** — Writing `delete obj;` in Java code or forgetting `delete` in C++ heap code is a recurring error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **class** is a *type*; an **object** is a *runtime instance*. Never use the terms interchangeably in exam answers.
- Object declaration is a **two-stage process**: (1) reference declaration on stack, (2) heap allocation + construction via `new`.
- The `new` operator performs: **class-load check → heap allocation → default field initialization → constructor invocation → address return**.
- Default value of any uninitialized reference is **`null`** in Java. Dereferencing null throws `NullPointerException`.
- Multiple reference variables can point to the **same** object — mutating through one is visible through all (aliasing).
- An object becomes **GC-eligible** when its reference count drops to zero (no live references reach it).
- **Stack** stores references and primitive local variables; **Heap** stores objects. Stack is LIFO and method-scoped; heap is shared and GC-managed.
- In **Java**, object type variables are *always* references. In **C++**, `Box b;` creates a stack object, while `Box* b = new Box;` creates a heap object via pointer.
- Object header overhead is typically **12 bytes** in 64-bit JVMs; align to **8-byte boundary** when computing total object size.
- For 14-mark memory-trace questions, always produce a **table of stack/heap states after each line** and a final **GC-eligibility verdict**.
- Remember: Java has **no pointer arithmetic** — a reference can only be reassigned, never incremented.

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Method (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *method* in Java is a named, reusable block of statements enclosed within a class that performs a specific, well-defined task. It is the equivalent of a *function* or *procedure* in traditional procedural languages, but is fundamentally tied to a class, embodying the object-oriented principle of **encapsulation of behavior**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education), a method is described as a **member of a class** that exposes the **behavioral contract** of an object. Every executable Java program must contain at least one method — the `main` method — which serves as the JVM's entry point.

A method declaration binds together **four essential components**:
1. **Access Modifier** — visibility scope (`public`, `private`, `protected`, default).
2. **Return Type** — the data type of the value the method hands back.
3. **Method Name** — a valid Java identifier used to invoke the method.
4. **Parameter List** — the typed inputs the method accepts (may be empty).

The full prototype is commonly referred to as the **method signature** (name + parameter list only — return type is *not* part of the signature).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Think of a method as a **recipe card pinned inside your kitchen**.

- The **recipe name** ("MakeDosa") is like the **method name**.
- The **ingredients list** ("2 cups flour, 1 cup water") is the **parameter list** — you must supply values (arguments) of matching type and order.
- The **cooking instructions** inside the card are the **method body** — the actual statements that execute.
- The **dish that comes out of the pan** is the **return value**.
- Some recipes produce a dish (return a value), while others just heat the kitchen (perform an action and return nothing — `void`).
- The kitchen itself is the **class/object**, and the recipe is locked inside it — you cannot use the recipe outside the kitchen. This mirrors how methods belong to a class.

When you want the dish, you "call" the recipe by name and pass in the ingredients. The chef (JVM) follows the steps and hands you the result.

---

## 1.3 Why Methods Exist — The Three Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Engineering Justifications for Methods (KTU Board Favourite):**
> 1. **Modularity** — Large problems are decomposed into small, named sub-problems.
> 2. **Reusability** — A method written once can be invoked thousands of times (DRY — *Don't Repeat Yourself*).
> 3. **Abstraction** — The caller does not need to know *how* the method works, only *what* it accepts and returns (black-box thinking).

---

## 1.4 Method vs. Function — The KTU Nuance

| Aspect | Method | Standalone Function (C/Pascal) |
|---|---|---|
| Belongs to | A class | No enclosing scope |
| Implicit `this` | Yes (for instance methods) | No |
| Can be overridden | Yes (`@Override`) | No |
| Polymorphic | Yes (via inheritance) | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In Java, **there are no free-floating functions**. Every executable code block *must* live inside a class as a method. Students from a C background often write "functions" outside classes — this is a **compilation error** in Java.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Method — Component-by-Component Breakdown

A Java method declaration is parsed left-to-right by the compiler in this strict order:

```
[access modifier] [static] [final] [abstract] [return_type] method_name([parameter_list]) [throws_clause] { method_body }
```

> Only the **return type, method name, and parameter list** are mandatory. Everything else is optional.

### 2.1.1 Access Modifiers — Visibility Rules

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (diff. pkg) | Other Packages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| *default* (no keyword) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (via inheritance) | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

### 2.1.2 Non-Access Modifiers

| Keyword | Meaning | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `static` | Belongs to the class, not instances. Called via `ClassName.method()`. | Asked frequently in 3-mark questions. |
| `final` | Cannot be overridden by subclasses. | Tied to polymorphism module. |
| `abstract` | Has no body; must be overridden. | Tied to abstraction module. |
| `synchronized` | Thread-safe (concurrency control). | Rare in Module 2; revisit in advanced modules. |

### 2.1.3 Return Type Semantics

- The return type can be **any primitive** (`int`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`, …), **any reference type** (`String`, arrays, objects), or **`void`**.
- A method declared `void` **must not** return a value via `return value;` — only `return;` (optional) is allowed to exit early.
- A non-void method **must** have a `return` statement that returns a value compatible with the declared type. **Failing to do so is a compile-time error.**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table — Memorise Before ESE**

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Method Declaration | `<modifiers> RT name(params) { body }` | `public int sum(int a, int b)` |
| Method Signature | `name + parameter list` (no return type) | `sum(int, int)` |
| Invocation | `objectRef.methodName(args);` | `obj.sum(5, 7);` |
| `void` return | No value returned | `public void greet() { ... } |
| Pass-by-Value | Primitives → copy of value; Objects → copy of reference | Always — Java has NO pass-by-reference |
| `return` statement | Terminates method and optionally sends back a value | `return result;` |
| Method Overloading | Same name, different parameter list (compile-time polymorphism) | `add(int,int)` & `add(double,double)` |
| `main` method | `public static void main(String[] args)` | Entry point for JVM |
| `this` keyword | Refers to current object (instance methods only) | `this.name = name;` |
| Recursion | Method calls itself with a base case | `fact(n) = n * fact(n-1)` |

---

## 2.3 The Call Stack — What Happens When You Invoke a Method

When a method is invoked, the JVM performs the following sequence:

1. A new **stack frame** (also called *activation record*) is pushed onto the **call stack**.
2. The frame contains space for **local variables**, **parameters**, and the **return address**.
3. Control transfers to the first statement of the called method.
4. When the method finishes (via `return` or end-of-body), the frame is **popped**, and control returns to the caller at the saved address.
5. Any value passed by `return` becomes available to the caller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Understanding the call stack is critical for debugging `StackOverflowError` (infinite recursion), analysing recursive algorithms (factorial, Fibonacci, tree traversals), and writing thread-safe code.

---

## 2.4 Parameter Passing — Java is Strictly *Pass-by-Value*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Java is **never** pass-by-reference. There is a long-standing student misconception here.

- For **primitives**: a *copy* of the value is passed. Modifying the parameter inside the method does **not** affect the caller's variable.
- For **object references**: a *copy of the reference* (the memory address) is passed. The callee can mutate the object's *state* (fields), but **cannot** make the caller's variable point to a different object.

Mathematical notation of the value-copy operation:

$$
\text{value}_{\text{caller}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{copy}}\; \text{value}_{\text{callee}}
$$

For object references:

$$
\text{ref}_{\text{caller}} = \text{ref}_{\text{callee}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{both point to same heap object}
$$

$$
\text{But: } \text{ref}_{\text{callee}} = \text{newObject} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{caller's reference unchanged}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Method Execution Flow — Exhaustive Trace

Consider the following canonical KTU-style example:

```java
public class Calculator {
    public static int square(int n) {
        return n * n;
    }

    public static int sumOfSquares(int a, int b) {
        return square(a) + square(b);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int result = sumOfSquares(3, 4);
        System.out.println(result);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 — JVM invokes `main`.**
A stack frame for `main` is pushed. Local variable `args` is created (referencing the command-line argument array).

**Step 2 — `main` evaluates `sumOfSquares(3, 4)`.**
A new stack frame for `sumOfSquares` is pushed on top. Parameters `a = 3` and `b = 4` are bound.

**Step 3 — `sumOfSquares` evaluates `square(a)` → `square(3)`.**
A new stack frame for `square` is pushed. Parameter `n = 3`.

**Step 4 — `square` computes `n * n`.**

$$
n \times n = 3 \times 3 = 9
$$

**Step 5 — `square` executes `return 9;`.**
The frame for `square` is popped. The value `9` is handed back to `sumOfSquares` and substituted into the expression.

**Step 6 — `sumOfSquares` now evaluates `square(b)` → `square(4)`.**
Another frame is pushed; `n = 4`; computes $4 \times 4 = 16$; returns $16$; frame popped.

**Step 7 — Final addition inside `sumOfSquares`:**

$$
\text{return value} = \text{square}(3) + \text{square}(4) = 9 + 16 = 25
$$

The frame for `sumOfSquares` is popped. The value $25$ is bound to `result` in `main`.

**Step 8 — `println` prints `25`.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — What the Compiler Checks

| Validation Rule | Compile-Time? | Runtime? | Consequence if Violated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Return type matches `return` expression | ✅ | — | Compile error: *incompatible types* |
| Method name is a valid identifier | ✅ | — | Compile error: *identifier expected* |
| Argument types match parameter types | ✅ | — | Compile error, *unless* an overload exists |
| Every code path in non-void method returns | ✅ | — | Compile error: *missing return statement* |
| `void` method returns a value | ✅ | — | Compile error: *unexpected return value* |
| Recursion has a base case | — | ✅ | `StackOverflowError` |
| Integer division by zero | ✅ (literal) | ✅ (variable) | ArithmeticException at runtime |

---

## 3.3 Formal Definition of the Method Signature (KTU Board Precise)

$$
\text{Signature}(m) \;=\; \langle \text{name}(m),\; \langle T_1, T_2, \dots, T_k \rangle \rangle
$$

where $T_i$ is the declared type of the $i$-th parameter and $k \geq 0$.

$$
\text{Return type} \;\not\in\; \text{Signature}(m)
$$

**Implication for Overloading:**
Two methods $m_1$ and $m_2$ may coexist in the same class if and only if their signatures differ. The compiler resolves the call using **static binding** (decided at compile time) based on the static types of the arguments.

$$
\text{Resolve}(\text{call}) = \arg\max_{m \in \text{overloads}} \; \text{MatchScore}(\text{argTypes},\; \text{paramTypes of } m)
$$

---

## 3.4 Recursion — A Method Calling Itself

A recursive method $f$ is defined as a function from a problem of size $n$ to a smaller sub-problem:

$$
f(n) = \begin{cases}
\text{base value}, & \text{if } n = 0 \quad (\text{base case}) \\
g(n, f(n-1)), & \text{if } n > 0 \quad (\text{recursive case})
\end{cases}
$$

**Example — Factorial:**

$$
\text{fact}(n) = \begin{cases}
1, & n = 0 \\
n \times \text{fact}(n-1), & n \geq 1
\end{cases}
$$

```java
public static int fact(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 1;        // base case
    return n * fact(n - 1);       // recursive case
}
```

Trace for `fact(4)`:

$$
\text{fact}(4) = 4 \times \text{fact}(3) = 4 \times 3 \times \text{fact}(2) = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times \text{fact}(1)
$$

$$
= 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 \times \text{fact}(0) = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 \times 1 = 24
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Method Call Stack — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[JVM invokes main] --> B[Push frame: main]
    B --> C[main calls sumOfSquares]
    C --> D[Push frame: sumOfSquares]
    D --> E[sumOfSquares calls square]
    E --> F[Push frame: square]
    F --> G[square returns 9]
    G --> H[Pop frame: square]
    H --> I[sumOfSquares calls square again]
    I --> J[Push frame: square]
    J --> K[square returns 16]
    K --> L[Pop frame: square]
    L --> M[sumOfSquares returns 25]
    M --> N[Pop frame: sumOfSquares]
    N --> O[main prints 25]
    O --> P[Pop frame: main]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each "Push frame" corresponds to a stack-frame allocation on the JVM call stack. The deeper the recursion / nesting, the taller the stack. If the stack exceeds the JVM's `-Xss` limit, a `StackOverflowError` is thrown.

---

## 4.2 Anatomy Block Diagram of a Method Declaration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Meth["Method Declaration Anatomy"]
        direction LR
        A1[Access Modifier public protected private default] --> A2[Non-Access Modifier static final abstract]
        A2 --> A3[Return Type int double void String ...]
        A3 --> A4[Method Name valid identifier]
        A4 --> A5[Parameter List type1 name1 type2 name2]
        A5 --> A6[Throws Clause optional]
        A6 --> A7[Method Body statements inside curly braces]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Parameter Passing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is being passed?} -->|Primitive int double char ...| P1[Copy of value]
    Q1 -->|Object reference| P2[Copy of reference address]
    P1 --> R1[Callee modifies copy]
    R1 --> R2[Caller variable unaffected]
    P2 --> S1[Both refer to same object on heap]
    S1 --> S2[Callee can mutate fields]
    S1 --> S3[Callee cannot reassign caller reference]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Method Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DC[Declaration Phase compile time] --> VS[Visibility Scope resolved]
    VS --> ST[Static or Instance binding determined]
    ST --> SG[Signature registered in Class Constant Pool]
    SG --> READY[Method Ready for Invocation]

    INV[Invocation Phase runtime] --> ARGS[Arguments evaluated]
    ARGS --> SF[Stack Frame pushed]
    SF --> EXEC[Method body executes]
    EXEC --> RET{Encountered return?}
    RET -->|Yes| UNWIND[Unwind stack frame]
    RET -->|No| EXEC
    UNWIND --> DONE[Control returns to caller]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a method in Java. List any four components of a method declaration.**

**Model Answer:**
A *method* in Java is a named block of executable statements defined inside a class that performs a specific task and may optionally return a value.

The four essential components of a method declaration are:

1. **Access Modifier** — controls visibility (`public`, `private`, `protected`, default).
2. **Return Type** — the data type of the value returned (or `void`).
3. **Method Name** — the identifier used to invoke the method.
4. **Parameter List** — comma-separated typed inputs enclosed in parentheses (may be empty).

```java
public int add(int a, int b) {   // 1 2 3 4
    return a + b;
}
```

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the difference between *pass-by-value* and *pass-by-reference*. Which one does Java use?**

**Model Answer:**

| Mechanism | Primitives | Objects | Caller Variable Affected? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pass-by-Value | Copy of value is passed | Copy of reference address is passed | No |
| Pass-by-Reference | Direct link to caller's variable | Direct link to caller's reference | Yes |

**Java uses strictly *Pass-by-Value*.** For primitives, a copy of the actual value is made. For objects, a copy of the *reference* (memory address) is made — meaning the callee can mutate the object's internal state but **cannot** change which object the caller's variable points to.

```java
void modify(int x) { x = 99; }       // caller's int unchanged
void mutate(StringBuilder sb) { sb.append("X"); } // caller's object content changed
```

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **anatomy of a Java method declaration**. Describe each component with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that contains a **method `power(int base, int exp)`** to compute $base^{exp}$ using **recursion**, and a `main` method to test it for `power(2, 10)`. Show the **step-by-step trace** of the call stack. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

```java
access_modifier  static  return_type  method_name(parameter_list)  throws_clause
{
    // method body
}
```

| Component | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Access Modifier | Visibility control | `public`, `private` |
| `static` | Class-level, no `this` | `static int count` |
| Return Type | Type of value returned | `int`, `double`, `void` |
| Method Name | Identifier for invocation | `calculateArea` |
| Parameter List | Typed input values | `(int radius, double pi)` |
| `throws` Clause | Checked exceptions declared | `throws IOException` |
| Method Body | Executable statements | `{ return r*r*pi; }` |

**Complete Example:**

```java
public static double calculateArea(double radius) throws IllegalArgumentException {
    if (radius < 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Negative radius");
    return Math.PI * radius * radius;
}
```

#### Model Solution (b)

```java
public class PowerDemo {
    public static long power(int base, int exp) {
        if (exp == 0) return 1;                 // base case
        return base * power(base, exp - 1);     // recursive case
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        long result = power(2, 10);
        System.out.println("2^10 = " + result);
    }
}
```

**Call-Stack Trace for `power(2, 10)`:**

| Call # | Invocation | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | `power(2, 10)` → `2 * power(2,9)` | waits |
| 9 | `power(2, 9)` → `2 * power(2,8)` | waits |
| 8 | `power(2, 8)` → `2 * power(2,7)` | waits |
| … | … | … |
| 1 | `power(2, 1)` → `2 * power(2,0)` | waits |
| 0 | `power(2, 0)` | `1` |

Unwinding:

$$
2^1 = 2 \times 1 = 2,\;\; 2^2 = 2 \times 2 = 4,\;\; 2^3 = 8,\;\; \dots,\;\; 2^{10} = 1024
$$

**Output:** `2^10 = 1024`

---

### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **parameter passing in Java** with suitable examples. Why is Java said to use *pass-by-value* even for objects? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program containing a method `swap(int a, int b)` that attempts to swap two integers. Explain with output **why the caller's variables are not swapped**, and modify the program to swap two objects of a `Wrapper`-like class successfully. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

Java's parameter passing is governed by a single rule: **a copy of the value held in the caller's variable is passed to the callee.**

- **Primitives:** The bit-pattern of the value is copied. The callee's parameter is a wholly independent variable.

```java
static void increment(int x) { x++; }

int a = 5;
increment(a);
System.out.println(a);   // Output: 5  (unchanged)
```

- **Object References:** The *reference value* (i.e., the memory address) is copied. Both the caller's variable and the callee's parameter point to the **same object** on the heap. The callee can mutate the object's fields, but assigning a new object to the parameter has no effect on the caller.

```java
static void rename(Dog d) { d.setName("Bruno"); }   // caller's dog IS renamed
static void reassign(Dog d) { d = new Dog("Max"); } // caller's dog NOT changed
```

**Why "pass-by-value" for objects?** Because the value being copied **is the reference**, not the object itself. The reference is passed by value. Java never gives the callee direct access to the caller's stack variable.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Attempt — fails for primitives:**

```java
static void swap(int a, int b) {
    int t = a; a = b; b = t;
    System.out.println("Inside swap: a=" + a + ", b=" + b);
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x = 10, y = 20;
    swap(x, y);
    System.out.println("After swap: x=" + x + ", y=" + y);
}
```

**Output:**

```
Inside swap: a=20, b=10
After swap: x=10, y=20
```

**Explanation:** The values of `x` and `y` are *copied* into the parameters `a` and `b`. Swapping `a` and `b` only swaps the local copies — the originals `x` and `y` are untouched.

**Successful swap using a wrapper (simulating pass-by-reference via object mutation):**

```java
class IntBox {
    int value;
    IntBox(int v) { value = v; }
}

public class SwapDemo {
    static void swap(IntBox p, IntBox q) {
        int t = p.value;
        p.value = q.value;
        q.value = t;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        IntBox a = new IntBox(10);
        IntBox b = new IntBox(20);
        swap(a, b);
        System.out.println("a=" + a.value + ", b=" + b.value);   // a=20, b=10
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
a=20, b=10
```

**Why this works:** We did not reassign the references themselves; we only mutated the *fields* (`value`) of the objects they point to. Since both `p` and `a` reference the same object, the change is visible to the caller.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Writing `void` methods that `return value;`** — instant zero for that sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting the base case in recursion** — code compiles, but `StackOverflowError` at runtime → full marks lost.
> 3. **Claiming Java is pass-by-reference for objects** — KTU examiners *deduct 2 marks* explicitly for this misconception.
> 4. **Missing method signature** when asked — *signature = name + parameter list only*. Including return type is a common error.
> 5. **Not showing the call-stack trace** in recursion questions — examiners expect at least 3–4 levels of unwinding.
> 6. **Confusing method overloading with overriding** — overloading is *same class, different signature*; overriding is *parent-child, same signature* (covered in Module 3).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 A **method** is a named block of code inside a class that performs a task and may return a value.
- 🔹 **Anatomy:** `[modifier] [static] returnType name(params) [throws] { body }`.
- 🔹 **Method signature** = name + parameter list (return type is **excluded**).
- 🔹 `void` methods return nothing; non-`void` methods **must** `return` a compatible value on every code path.
- 🔹 Java is **strictly pass-by-value** — primitives copy the value, objects copy the reference.
- 🔹 Calling a method pushes a **stack frame**; returning pops it.
- 🔹 **Recursion** = method calling itself, with a mandatory **base case** to terminate.
- 🔹 **Overloading** (same name, different params) is resolved at **compile time** (static binding).
- 🔹 The `main` method signature is **fixed**: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- 🔹 The `this` keyword refers to the **current object** and is valid only in **instance methods**.
- 🔹 No method exists outside a class in Java — there are **no free-floating functions**.
- 🔹 Know the **four access modifiers** table cold — it is the single most-asked 3-mark question in Module 2.
- 🔹 Be ready to **trace a recursive call** end-to-end (push frames, base case hit, unwind with values).
- 🔹 Memorise the canonical example: `factorial`, `power`, `fibonacci`, `sumOfDigits`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **DEFINITION — Constructor (KTU 2024 OOP Module 2)**
> A **constructor** in Java (and in OOP) is a special member method of a class that is automatically invoked the moment an object (instance) of that class is created using the `new` keyword. Its primary responsibility is to **initialize the newly allocated object** to a valid, well-defined state by assigning initial values to its instance variables (data members).

### Formal Syntactic Rules of a Java Constructor
A method qualifies as a constructor in Java **if and only if** it satisfies these three structural conditions:

1. **Exact-Name Rule:** The constructor name must be **identical** to the class name (case-sensitive).
2. **No-Return-Type Rule:** The constructor declaration must **not** specify any return type — not even `void`.
3. **Automatic-Invocation Rule:** It is invoked implicitly by the JVM at the time of object creation via the `new` operator (or via reflection).

Mathematically, we can express the invocation contract as:

$$\text{Object Creation} \implies \text{Memory Allocation (Heap)} \longrightarrow \text{Default Initialization} \longrightarrow \text{Constructor Invocation} \longrightarrow \text{User Initialization}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint's Birth Certificate"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — Filling an Admission Form**
> Imagine every student joining a college as an **"object"** of the class `Student`. The college *cannot* let a student exist in their database without knowing his/her **Name, Roll Number, Branch, and Semester**. A **constructor** is precisely the *admission form* that the system forces the new student to fill out the instant their record is created (`new Student(...)`). The form ensures the object is **born into a valid state** — never left half-empty or with garbage data.

| Real-World Entity | OOP Counterpart |
|---|---|
| A government-issued birth certificate | The **Default Constructor** (gives baseline identity) |
| A detailed college admission form with name, branch, etc. | A **Parameterized Constructor** |
| Photocopying an existing student's record to admit a sibling | A **Copy Constructor** |
| Multiple forms depending on the applicant's category | **Constructor Overloading** |

---

## 1.3 Why Constructors are Essential in OOP

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Justification:** Without constructors, a freshly created object would have its instance variables set to **default values only** (e.g., `0`, `null`, `false`). This is dangerous because business logic often assumes an object is *fully formed* the moment it is referenced. A constructor enforces the **Invariant Establishment Principle** — a class invariant is guaranteed to hold immediately after construction.

The two foundational guarantees provided by a constructor are:

- **Validity Guarantee:** The object enters a state consistent with the class invariants.
- **Encapsulation Guarantee:** The internal data is set up safely *before* any external method is allowed to interact with the object.

---

## 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Syllabus

In the **PEMRT415 – Object Oriented Programming** course under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, Module 2 covers the foundational pillars of class design in Java. Constructors form the **bridge between Class Declaration and Object Lifecycle**, sitting conceptually between:
- *Data members & methods* (Module 2 sub-topic: Class fundamentals)
- *Inheritance & Polymorphism* (Module 3 onwards)

**Expected Course Outcome (CO) mapping:** This topic directly contributes to **CO2 — Apply object-oriented principles to design and implement robust Java classes**, aligned with Bloom's Level 3 (*Apply*) and Level 4 (*Analyze*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Constructors in Java

Java supports **four major categories** of constructors, each with a distinct purpose. The classification tree is:

$$
\text{Constructors}
\begin{cases}
\text{I. Default (No-arg) Constructor} \\
\text{II. Parameterized Constructor} \\
\text{III. Copy Constructor} \quad (\text{User-defined in Java}) \\
\text{IV. Private / Static / Chained Constructors} \quad (\text{Design-pattern level})
\end{cases}
$$

---

### 2.1.1 Default Constructor (No-Argument Constructor)

> [!NOTE]
> A **default constructor** is a constructor that takes **zero parameters**. It is the most basic constructor and is responsible for assigning default or initial values to the instance variables.

**Two sub-cases exist:**

| Sub-Case | Trigger Condition | What the Compiler Inserts |
|---|---|---|
| **Implicit Default Constructor** | Programmer defines **no** constructor at all | Compiler auto-generates a `public ClassName() { }` |
| **Explicit Default Constructor** | Programmer defines `ClassName() { }` manually | User-written, but still parameterless |

**Critical Rule:** The moment you write **any** parameterized constructor, Java **stops providing** the implicit default. You must then write it manually if needed.

```java
class Box {
    int length, width;
    
    // Explicit default constructor
    Box() {
        length = 10;
        width  = 10;
    }
}

Box b = new Box();  // length=10, width=10
```

---

### 2.1.2 Parameterized Constructor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **parameterized constructor** accepts one or more arguments, allowing the caller to pass initial values at the time of object creation. This is the **most widely used** constructor type in real-world Java applications.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    
    // Parameterized constructor
    Student(String n, int r) {
        name   = n;
        rollNo = r;
    }
}

Student s1 = new Student("Arjun", 45);  // Direct initialization at birth
```

**The "Why":** Parameterized constructors **eliminate the anti-pattern of "create-then-set"**, where an object exists in a partially valid state between `new` and the first setter call.

---

### 2.1.3 Copy Constructor

> [!NOTE]
> A **copy constructor** is a constructor that takes an object of the **same class** as its parameter and creates a new object as a **field-by-field copy** of the passed object.

```java
class Point {
    int x, y;
    
    Point(int x, int y) {        // Parameterized
        this.x = x; 
        this.y = y;
    }
    
    Point(Point p) {              // Copy constructor
        this.x = p.x;
        this.y = p.y;
    }
}

Point p1 = new Point(5, 10);
Point p2 = new Point(p1);        // p2 is a fresh, independent copy
```

**Engineering Note:** Unlike C++, Java does **not** have a built-in copy constructor. Programmers must write it manually. This is essential for:
- Cloning objects before passing them into unsafe methods.
- Defensive copying in immutable class design (e.g., `String`, `Integer`).

---

### 2.1.4 Constructor Chaining using `this()`

> [!TIP]
> **Constructor chaining** is the technique of calling one constructor from another constructor within the same class using the `this(...)` keyword.

**The Two Hard Rules of `this()` chaining:**
1. The call `this(...)` **must be the first statement** inside the constructor body.
2. The chain must **eventually terminate** at a base constructor (or `super()`).

```java
class Account {
    String holder;
    double balance;
    String type;
    
    Account() { 
        this("Unknown", 0.0, "Savings");           // Chain → 4-arg
    }
    Account(String h) { 
        this(h, 0.0, "Savings");                  // Chain → 4-arg
    }
    Account(String h, double b, String t) {       // Terminal constructor
        holder  = h; 
        balance = b; 
        type    = t;
    }
}
```

---

## 2.2 Constructor vs. Method — The Definitive Comparison

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Focus Area:** This comparison is a **favourite 3-mark short-answer question**. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Feature | Constructor | Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Initializes a newly created object | Represents behaviour / action of an object |
| **Name** | Must match the class name exactly | Any valid identifier (except reserved words) |
| **Return Type** | **None** (not even `void`) | Must specify a return type (`void`, `int`, etc.) |
| **Invocation** | Implicit, via `new` keyword | Explicit, via `object.methodName()` |
| **Inheritance** | **NOT inherited** by subclasses | Inherited normally |
| **Overriding** | **Cannot be overridden** | Can be overridden (polymorphism) |
| **Number per Class** | Multiple (via overloading) | Multiple |
| **`final` / `static` / `abstract` modifiers** | **Not allowed** | Allowed |
| **Memory Impact** | Allocates + initializes the object | Operates on an existing object |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Rule Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 rules — they are the most-tested points in KTU University Exams for this topic.**

| # | Rule / Formula | Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Constructor name ≡ Class name | Definition-level question |
| **R2** | Constructors have **no return type** | Common error: writing `void` |
| **R3** | Implicit `super()` call inserted by compiler if no `this()` exists | Required to trace execution order |
| **R4** | `this()` must be the **first statement** | Frequently asked as true/false |
| **R5** | Constructor overloading → **compile-time polymorphism** | Directly maps to Module 3 (P1 of polymorphism) |
| **R6** | If any constructor is defined, **default constructor is NOT auto-generated** | Very common 3-mark question |
| **R7** | Constructors **cannot be `abstract`, `static`, `final`, or `synchronized`** | Direct question |
| **R8** | Constructors **cannot be inherited** but **can be overloaded** | Conceptual |
| **R9** | A constructor can call **only one** `this()` or `super()` | Pitfall: trying both |
| **R10** | Instance initializer blocks execute **before** the constructor body | Tie-breaker question |
| **R11** | Static initializer blocks execute **once**, at class loading | One-shot question |
| **R12** | `private` constructor ⇒ Class **cannot be subclassed** (utility class pattern) | Design pattern question |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Constructors

Constructors are not academic curiosities — they are **the backbone of robust enterprise software**:

- **Database Connection Objects:** `Connection conn = new DbConnection(url, user, pass);` — The connection is **valid and authenticated** at the moment of creation; no half-open state.
- **Dependency Injection (Spring Framework):** The IoC container uses **reflection to invoke constructors** with required dependencies.
- **Immutable Class Design (e.g., `String`, `BigDecimal`):** All fields set via `final` constructor parameters; no setters exist.
- **Factory Design Pattern:** Factory methods internally call constructors to produce objects in a controlled manner.
- **JUnit Test Fixtures:** `@BeforeEach` methods act as parameterized constructors for test objects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Trace & Code Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> The following section walks through the **complete object-creation pipeline** and traces the **exact order of execution** — a question pattern that appears almost every KTU exam cycle as a 7-mark analytical problem.

---

## 3.1 The Four-Phase Object Creation Pipeline

Whenever the JVM encounters `new MyClass(args)`, it executes a precise **four-phase pipeline**:

$$
\boxed{\text{Phase 1: Class Loading} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Phase 2: Memory Allocation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Phase 3: Default Zeroing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Phase 4: Constructor Execution}}
$$

### Phase 1 — Class Loading
The **ClassLoader Subsystem** locates the `MyClass.class` bytecode file, reads it into the **Method Area** of the JVM memory, and prepares it for instantiation. *At this moment, `static` variables are initialized to their default values.*

### Phase 2 — Memory Allocation
A contiguous block of memory in the **Heap** is allocated to hold the new object. The size is determined by the sum of all instance variables:

$$
\text{Object Size} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(\text{instanceVar}_i) + \text{Object Header Overhead (typically 12–16 bytes)}
$$

### Phase 3 — Default Zeroing
All instance variables are assigned JVM default values **before** the constructor runs:

| Data Type | Default Value |
|---|---|
| `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long` | `0` or `0L` |
| `float`, `double` | `0.0f` or `0.0d` |
| `char` | `'\u0000'` |
| `boolean` | `false` |
| Any reference type | `null` |

### Phase 4 — Constructor Execution
The explicit constructor body runs, **overwriting** the default values with programmer-defined initial values.

---

## 3.2 Execution Order Trace — Worked Analytical Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the SINGLE most-asked KTU question pattern on constructors.** Predicting the output of a code snippet with mixed static blocks, instance blocks, and constructors.

### Reference Program

```java
class Trace {
    static int s = 10;
    int       x = 20;
    
    // Static initializer block
    static {
        System.out.println("STATIC BLOCK  → s = " + (s = 100));
    }
    
    // Instance initializer block
    {
        System.out.println("INSTANCE BLOCK → x = " + (x = 200));
    }
    
    // Default constructor
    Trace() {
        System.out.println("DEFAULT C'TOR → x = " + (x = 300));
    }
    
    // Parameterized constructor
    Trace(int v) {
        System.out.println("PARAM C'TOR   → x = " + (x = v));
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("--- main() entered ---");
        Trace a = new Trace();
        Trace b = new Trace(999);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Dry-Run Execution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{STEP 1: } &\texttt{main()} \text{ is invoked. JVM loads class \texttt{Trace}.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Static variable } s = 10 \text{ is set, then overwritten to } 100 \text{ in static block.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Output: "STATIC BLOCK → s = 100"} \\[4pt]

\textbf{STEP 2: } &\texttt{new Trace()} \text{ executes.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Heap memory allocated.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Instance variable } x = 20 \text{ is set as default-declared initialization.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Instance initializer block runs: } x = 200. \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Output: "INSTANCE BLOCK → x = 200"} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Default constructor body runs: } x = 300. \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Output: "DEFAULT C'TOR → x = 300"} \\[4pt]

\textbf{STEP 3: } &\texttt{new Trace(999)} \text{ executes.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Fresh heap allocation, new \texttt{x = 20} default.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Instance block runs: } x = 200. \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Output: "INSTANCE BLOCK → x = 200"} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Parameterized constructor body runs: } x = 999. \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Output: "PARAM C'TOR → x = 999"}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Predicted Output

```
STATIC BLOCK  → s = 100
--- main() entered ---
INSTANCE BLOCK → x = 200
DEFAULT C'TOR → x = 300
INSTANCE BLOCK → x = 200
PARAM C'TOR   → x = 999
```

### The Master Rule for Such Problems

> [!TIP]
> **Order of execution per object creation:**
> 1. `static` blocks → **once per class load** (in textual order)
> 2. Instance variable `= value` initializers → **per object**
> 3. Instance initializer blocks `{ ... }` → **per object** (in textual order)
> 4. Constructor body → **per object**

---

## 3.3 Constructor Overloading Resolution — Compile-Time Binding

When multiple constructors exist, the compiler selects the right one based on the **argument list signature**. This is an example of **static (compile-time) polymorphism**.

### Resolution Algorithm

$$
\text{Resolve } \texttt{new MyClass}(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Match constructor with parameter types } (T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n) \text{ where } a_i \xrightarrow{\text{assignable}} T_i
$$

**Match priorities (in order):**
1. Exact type match
2. Widening primitive conversion (`int → long → float → double`)
3. Widening reference conversion (subclass → superclass)
4. Autoboxing / unboxing (rarely preferred over widening)

If **no match or ambiguity** is found → **Compile-Time Error: ambiguous constructor call**.

### Worked Example

```java
class Calc {
    Calc(int    a)       { System.out.println("int");    }
    Calc(long   a)       { System.out.println("long");   }
    Calc(double a)       { System.out.println("double"); }
    Calc(String a)       { System.out.println("String"); }
}
```

| Call | Resolved Constructor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `new Calc(5)`        | `Calc(int)`        | Exact match (preferred over widening) |
| `new Calc(5L)`       | `Calc(long)`       | Exact match |
| `new Calc(5.0)`      | `Calc(double)`     | Exact match |
| `new Calc("hello")`  | `Calc(String)`     | Exact match |
| `new Calc('A')`      | `Calc(int)`        | `char` widens to `int` |

---

## 3.4 Constructor Chaining Trace — Multi-Level `this()` Resolution

```java
class Chain {
    Chain()             { this(10);      System.out.println("A"); }
    Chain(int x)        { this(x, 20.5); System.out.println("B"); }
    Chain(int x, double y)              { System.out.println("C"); }
    
    public static void main(String[] a) { new Chain(); }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{new Chain()} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{calls } \texttt{Chain(int)} \quad \text{(via this(10))} \\
&\;\;\longrightarrow\; \text{calls } \texttt{Chain(int, double)} \quad \text{(via this(10, 20.5))} \\
&\;\;\;\;\longrightarrow\; \text{prints "C" (terminal)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{returns} \\
&\;\;\longrightarrow\; \text{prints "B"} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{returns} \\
&\longrightarrow\; \text{prints "A"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:**
```
C
B
A
```

### The Inversion Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The chain executes *outwards-in*, but returns *inwards-out*.** Think of it like nested Russian dolls — the innermost constructor finishes first, then the middle, then the outermost. The "first statement" rule of `this()` enforces this stack-like discipline.

---

## 3.5 Memory Diagram — Constructor Invocation in Heap

For the statement `Student s1 = new Student("Arjun", 45);`:

| Memory Region | What is Stored |
|---|---|
| **Stack** (Stack Frame of `main`) | Local reference variable `s1` holding the **heap address** (e.g., `0x7A3F`) |
| **Heap** | The actual `Student` object with `name = "Arjun"`, `rollNo = 45`, and an **object header** (class pointer, hash code, GC metadata) |
| **Method Area** | The `Student.class` blueprint: method definitions, static fields, constructor bytecode |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** If asked to draw the memory diagram, always include all three regions. Many students forget the **Method Area**, losing 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructor Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Constructors in Java]
    nodeB[Default Constructor]
    nodeC[Parameterized Constructor]
    nodeD[Copy Constructor]
    nodeE[Chained Constructor]
    nodeF[Private Constructor]
    
    nodeB1[Implicit - Compiler Generated]
    nodeB2[Explicit - User Defined]
    
    nodeC1[Single Parameter]
    nodeC2[Multi Parameter]
    nodeC3[Constructor Overloading]
    
    nodeD1[Shallow Copy]
    nodeD2[Deep Copy - Manual]
    
    nodeE1[Within Same Class - this]
    nodeE2[To Superclass - super]
    
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    
    nodeB --> nodeB1
    nodeB --> nodeB2
    
    nodeC --> nodeC1
    nodeC --> nodeC2
    nodeC --> nodeC3
    
    nodeD --> nodeD1
    nodeD --> nodeD2
    
    nodeE --> nodeE1
    nodeE --> nodeE2
    
    style nodeA fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#8c564b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 Object Creation Pipeline (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    start([Program Counter hits new keyword]) --> load1[Step 1: ClassLoader loads .class file]
    load1 --> mem[Step 2: Heap memory allocated for new object]
    mem --> zero[Step 3: All instance vars set to JVM defaults]
    zero --> static[Step 4: Static initializers run ONCE per class]
    static --> inst[Step 5: Instance variable initializers run]
    inst --> iblock[Step 6: Instance initializer blocks run]
    iblock --> chain{this or super call present?}
    chain -- Yes --> resolve[Resolve to target constructor]
    chain -- No --> exec[Execute current constructor body]
    resolve --> exec
    exec --> return([Object reference returned to caller])
    
    style start fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style load1 fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style mem fill:#45b7d1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style zero fill:#f9ca24,stroke:#000
    style static fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style inst fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style iblock fill:#fd79a8,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style exec fill:#00b894,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style return fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Constructor Chaining Call Stack Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    mainCall[main: new Chain]
    c1[Chain - no args]
    c2[Chain - int x]
    c3[Chain - int x, double y]
    
    mainCall -- this 10 --> c1
    c1 -- this x, 20.5 --> c2
    c2 -- terminal --> c3
    
    c3 -- print C returns --> c2
    c2 -- print B returns --> c1
    c1 -- print A returns --> mainCall
    
    style mainCall fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style c1 fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style c2 fill:#fd79a8,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style c3 fill:#00b894,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.4 Lifecycle of an Object — From `new` to Garbage Collection

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Source Code: new MyClass]
```
