```mermaid
    s2[Class Loaded by JVM]
    s3[Memory Allocated in Heap]
    s4[Default Values Assigned]
    s5[Constructor Invoked]
    s6[Object in Use - Strong References]
    s7[References Removed]
    s8[Object Eligible for GC]
    s9[Finalizer may run]
    s10[Memory Reclaimed]
    
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7
    s7 --> s8
    s8 --> s9
    s9 --> s10
    
    style s1 fill:#ff7675,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style s5 fill:#00b894,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style s10 fill:#636e72,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a constructor and a method in Java. Can a constructor be declared `final` or `static`? Justify."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **constructor** is a special member used to initialize a newly created object, whereas a **method** defines the behaviour or actions of an object.

| Aspect | Constructor | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Name | Same as class name | Any valid identifier |
| Return type | None (not even `void`) | Mandatory |
| Invocation | Implicit, via `new` | Explicit, via object reference |
| Inheritance | Not inherited | Inherited |

A constructor **cannot be declared `final`** because `final` prevents overriding, and constructors are not overridden. It also **cannot be `static`** because static members belong to the class, not to an instance, while constructors are bound to object creation. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**"What happens if a programmer defines only a parameterized constructor in a class and tries to instantiate the class with `new ClassName()` (no arguments)? Explain with a suitable example."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

If a class defines **only** a parameterized constructor and the programmer writes `new ClassName()` (no arguments), the program will throw a **compile-time error**: *"no suitable constructor found for ClassName(no arguments)"*.

**Reason:** The moment a programmer defines any constructor manually, the Java compiler **stops providing the implicit default (no-arg) constructor**. Hence, no-arg invocation becomes invalid.

**Example:**
```java
class Demo {
    Demo(int x) { }       // Only parameterized
}
class Test {
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        Demo d = new Demo();    // COMPILE-TIME ERROR
    }
}
```
**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **Module Internal Choice** in Part B. Below are TWO alternative question choices, each carrying 14 marks split into 7+7 sub-parts.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU Model Paper — 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **constructor overloading** in Java with a suitable example. State any **three rules** that govern constructor overloading. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **copy constructor**? Write a Java program to demonstrate a copy constructor for a class `Rectangle` having data members `length` and `width`. Explain the difference between **shallow copy** and **deep copy** with reference to your program. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer

#### (a) Constructor Overloading (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Constructor overloading is the technique of defining **multiple constructors within the same class**, each having a **different parameter list** (different number, type, or order of parameters). The compiler resolves the correct constructor at **compile time** based on the arguments passed — this is an example of **static (compile-time) polymorphism**.

**Example Program (3 marks):**
```java
class Time {
    int hours, minutes;
    
    Time() {                                  // (1) Default
        hours = 0; minutes = 0;
    }
    Time(int h) {                             // (2) Single param
        hours = h; minutes = 0;
    }
    Time(int h, int m) {                      // (3) Two params
        hours = h; minutes = m;
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.println(hours + ":" + minutes);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        Time t1 = new Time();
        Time t2 = new Time(5);
        Time t3 = new Time(5, 30);
        t1.display();   // 0:0
        t2.display();   // 5:0
        t3.display();   // 5:30
    }
}
```

**Three Rules of Constructor Overloading (2 marks):**
1. Overloaded constructors must differ in **parameter list** — name alone being the same is not enough.
2. The compiler selects the constructor based on **compile-time argument matching**, not runtime type.
3. Constructor overloading can be combined with `this()` chaining to reduce code duplication.

#### (b) Copy Constructor — Rectangle (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** A copy constructor is a constructor that accepts an object of the **same class** as a parameter and creates a new object as a copy of the given object. Java does **not** provide a built-in copy constructor (unlike C++).

**Java Program (3 marks):**
```java
class Rectangle {
    int length, width;
    
    Rectangle(int l, int w) {                 // Parameterized
        length = l; width = w;
    }
    Rectangle(Rectangle r) {                  // Copy constructor
        length = r.length;
        width  = r.width;
    }
    int area() { return length * width; }
}
class TestRect {
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle(10, 5);
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(r1);     // Copy
        System.out.println("R1 area: " + r1.area());
        System.out.println("R2 area: " + r2.area());
    }
}
```

**Shallow vs Deep Copy (2 marks):**
- **Shallow Copy** copies primitive values directly. For **reference fields**, it copies only the memory address, so both objects **share** the same referenced object.
- **Deep Copy** creates a **new instance** of every referenced object too, making the copy fully independent. In the above program, since `length` and `width` are primitives, the copy is effectively deep for these fields.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **order of execution** of static blocks, instance initializer blocks, and constructors in Java. Illustrate with a self-contained program and its expected output. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **constructor chaining**? Explain with a Java program how the `this()` keyword is used to chain constructors. State **two rules** that must be followed while using `this()`. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer

#### (a) Order of Execution (7 Marks)

**Theoretical Order (3 marks):**

For every class, the **first** thing that runs (only once) when the class is loaded is the **static initializer blocks** in textual order. Then, **for every object creation**, the order is:
1. Instance variable initializers (`int x = 20;`)
2. Instance initializer blocks (`{ ... }`)
3. Constructor body

**Program (3 marks):**
```java
class Demo {
    static { System.out.println("S1"); }
    static { System.out.println("S2"); }
    int x = 10;
    { System.out.println("I1 - x=" + x); }
    Demo() { 
        x = 50; 
        System.out.println("C - x=" + x); 
    }
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        System.out.println("Main Start");
        new Demo();
        new Demo();
        System.out.println("Main End");
    }
}
```

**Output (1 mark):**
```
S1
S2
Main Start
I1 - x=10
C - x=50
I1 - x=10
C - x=50
Main End
```

#### (b) Constructor Chaining with `this()` (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Constructor chaining is the process of **calling one constructor from another constructor** within the same class. The `this()` keyword (with appropriate parameters) is used to invoke another constructor of the same class.

**Program (3 marks):**
```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    int id;
    double salary;
    
    Employee() { 
        this("Unknown", 0, 0.0);          // (1) Chain to 3-arg
    }
    Employee(String n, int i) { 
        this(n, i, 25000.0);              // (2) Chain to 3-arg
    }
    Employee(String n, int i, double s) { // (3) Terminal
        name = n; id = i; salary = s;
    }
    void show() { 
        System.out.println(id + " " + name + " " + salary); 
    }
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        new Employee().show();
        new Employee("Anu", 7).show();
        new Employee("Ravi", 3, 80000).show();
    }
}
```

**Two Rules (2 marks):**
1. The statement `this(...)` **must be the first line** inside the constructor body. Otherwise, a compile-time error occurs.
2. Java prevents **cyclic chaining** — constructor A calling constructor B which calls A again is detected and rejected at compile time. The chain must terminate at a constructor that does **not** use `this()`.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Points**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that compiler stops providing default constructor** when you write any constructor → students call `new ClassName()` and the program "fails" — losing 2 marks on code output.
> 2. **Placing `this(...)` as the second statement** — compile error. Many students write it as the last line. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. **Writing `void` in front of a constructor** — turns it into a regular method, not a constructor. Result: no error at compile, but the object is never initialized. Marks lost: 2.
> 4. **Confusing "constructor returns an object" with "constructor has a return type"** — the JVM returns the object reference; the constructor body itself declares **no** return type. Marks lost: 1.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the Method Area** in memory diagrams — partial diagrams lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Order-of-execution errors in trace questions** — students often print static blocks per object. Static blocks run **once per class load**, not per object. Marks lost: 2–3.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section twice on the morning of the exam.**

- A **constructor** is a special method that initializes a new object. **It has the same name as the class and no return type.**
- Constructors are invoked **automatically** at object creation via `new`.
- There are **four types**: default (no-arg), parameterized, copy, and chained (via `this()`).
- **Constructor overloading** = multiple constructors with different parameter lists = **compile-time polymorphism**.
- If you write **any** constructor, the **implicit default constructor is removed** — write your own no-arg if needed.
- Constructors **cannot** be `static`, `final`, `abstract`, or `synchronized`.
- Constructors **cannot be inherited** or **overridden** — only **overloaded**.
- The call `this(args)` **must be the first statement** of the constructor body.
- The call `super(args)` (covered in Module 3) also must be the first statement — and `this()` and `super()` **cannot coexist** in the same constructor.
- **Order of execution** per object creation: `static` (once per class) → instance variable initializers → instance initializer blocks → constructor body.
- **Instance initializer blocks** are often used to share code between multiple constructors in the same class.
- A **private constructor** prevents both instantiation and subclassing — used in **utility classes** (e.g., `java.lang.Math`).
- A **copy constructor** must be written manually in Java; C++ provides it implicitly.
- The **four phases of object creation** are: Class Loading → Heap Allocation → Default Zeroing → Constructor Execution.
- **Memory regions involved**: Stack (reference variable), Heap (object), Method Area (class blueprint).
- **Constructor vs Method**: constructors initialize, methods operate; constructors have no return type, methods must have one.
- The keyword **`new`** performs four jobs: loads class, allocates memory, zeroes fields, invokes constructor.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — CONSTRUCTORS**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 NEP Scheme | PEMRT415 — Object Oriented Programming*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** this Keyword

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In Java, the **`this` keyword** is a **reference variable** in Java that refers to the **current object** — i.e., the instance of the class on which a method or constructor is currently being invoked. It is an **implicit final reference** automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and passed as a hidden argument to every instance method and constructor of a class.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-Grade):**
> *"`this` is a keyword in Java that acts as a reference to the current object whose method or constructor is being called. It is used to eliminate confusion between instance variables and parameters having the same name."*

> [!NOTE]
> - `this` is a **keyword** (not an identifier), so it cannot be used as a variable name.
> - `this` is **final** by design — you cannot reassign `this` to point to another object.
> - `this` is **non-static** — it cannot be used inside a `static` method, `static` block, or `static` variable initializer.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a classroom full of students. The teacher asks, *"Who has submitted the assignment?"* and wants to know whether **you specifically** have done it. In English, you would point to yourself and say, **"I have submitted it"** or **"Me, myself"**.

In Java, when an object receives a message (method call), it often needs to refer to **itself** — its own instance variables, its own methods, or pass itself to some other method. The keyword **`this`** is Java's way of saying *"ME"* — *"the object on which this very code is currently executing."*

### 1.3 Real-World Engineering Analogy

Consider an **IoT sensor node** that records its own temperature:

- The sensor has fields like `deviceId`, `temperature`, `location`.
- When it logs data, it must distinguish **its own** temperature from any parameter passed in.
- The keyword `this.deviceId` clearly identifies **the current sensor's** ID, disambiguating from a local variable also called `deviceId`.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** `this` = *"the object that called this method, viewed from inside the method."*

---

### 1.4 Where `this` Exists in the JVM

| Location | Valid? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Instance Method | ✅ Yes | Object is available |
| Constructor | ✅ Yes | Object is being built |
| Static Method | ❌ No | No current object exists |
| Static Block | ❌ No | No current object exists |
| Local Variable Initializer | ⚠️ Allowed only if instance context exists | JVM rule |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Core Uses of `this` Keyword (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests **all six uses**. Mastery of these is mandatory for the 14-mark module questions.

#### Use 1: `this` to refer to **current class instance variables**
- Disambiguates between **instance variables** and **local variables / parameters** when they share the same name.
- This is the **most frequently tested** scenario in KTU exams.

#### Use 2: `this` to invoke **current class method**
- Implicitly done by the JVM, but can be written explicitly for clarity.
- Example: `this.display();` is equivalent to `display();`

#### Use 3: `this()` to invoke **current class constructor**
- Used for **constructor chaining**.
- Must be the **first statement** in a constructor.
- Helps in implementing **constructor reuse** and reducing code duplication.

#### Use 4: `this` as a **method argument (passing the current object)**
- Used when one object must be passed to another method or object so that the receiver can interact with the original object.

#### Use 5: `this` as a **constructor argument**
- Pass the current object to another constructor of a different class.

#### Use 6: `this` as a **return value from a method**
- Enables **method chaining** (fluent interface pattern). Returns the current instance so multiple method calls can be chained: `obj.setA(1).setB(2).setC(3);`

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA BOX — Memorize the Table Below**

| # | Usage Pattern | Syntax | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refer to instance variable | `this.varName = varName;` | Resolve name conflict | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Invoke current method | `this.methodName();` | Explicit clarity | ⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Invoke current constructor | `this(args);` or `this();` | Constructor chaining | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Pass as method argument | `method(this);` | Allow external access | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Pass as constructor argument | `new ClassName(this);` | Object hand-off | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Return current instance | `return this;` | Method chaining | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Rules (Exam-Critical)

> [!WARNING]
> These rules are **favourite KTU question patterns** — examiners often frame 3-mark Part A questions around them.

| Rule | Statement | Consequence if Violated |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | `this` must be the **first statement** inside a constructor when calling `this()`. | Compile-time error |
| **R2** | `this()` and `super()` **cannot be used together** in the same constructor. | Compile-time error |
| **R3** | `this` **cannot** be used in a `static` context. | Compile-time error: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context"* |
| **R4** | `this` is implicitly `final` — it cannot be reassigned (`this = obj2;` is illegal). | Compile-time error |
| **R5** | Recursive constructor invocation via `this()` leads to **infinite recursion** and stack overflow. | `StackOverflowError` at runtime |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of `this` |
|---|---|
| **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX)** | Event handlers refer to the current component using `this` |
| **Builder Pattern (e.g., Lombok, StringBuilder)** | Fluent APIs rely on `return this;` for chaining |
| **Data Structures (Linked List)** | Nodes reference themselves via `this` while inserting/deleting |
| **IoT / Embedded Java** | Sensor class methods return `this` to chain configuration calls |
| **JDBC / Spring Framework** | Beans often use `this` for dependency hand-off in constructor injection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every Java code block below is **exhaustively annotated line-by-line**. KTU examiners award marks for **commented code**, so adopt this discipline in your answer sheets.

---

### 3.1 Use Case 1: Resolving Name Conflict (Most Frequent KTU Pattern)

```java
// File: Student.java
class Student {
    // ---- Instance Variables (fields belong to every object) ----
    String name;
    int rollNo;

    // ---- Parameterized Constructor ----
    Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        // Here, 'name' on RHS is the LOCAL parameter (just arrived).
        // 'this.name' refers to the INSTANCE variable (the object's own field).
        this.name = name;     // LHS = object's field,  RHS = incoming parameter
        this.rollNo = rollNo; // disambiguation happens via 'this'
    }

    // ---- Instance Method ----
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + this.name + ", Roll No: " + this.rollNo);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student("Arjun", 45);
        Student s2 = new Student("Meera", 12);
        s1.display();
        s2.display();
    }
}
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**

| Line | Explanation |
|---|---|
| `String name;` | Declares an **instance variable** belonging to each `Student` object. |
| `Student(String name, int rollNo)` | Parameters `name` and `rollNo` are **local** to this constructor. |
| `this.name = name;` | `this.name` → the object's field. `name` → the parameter. Without `this`, both refer to the parameter (shadowing bug). |
| `s1.display();` | JVM implicitly passes `s1` as `this`. Inside `display()`, `this` IS `s1`. |

**Output:**
```
Name: Arjun, Roll No: 45
Name: Meera, Roll No: 12
```

---

### 3.2 Use Case 2: Constructor Chaining Using `this()`

```java
class Box {
    double width, height, depth;

    // Constructor 1: Cube (all sides equal)
    Box(double side) {
        this(side, side, side); // delegates to Constructor 3 — MUST be first line
        System.out.println("Cube constructor invoked.");
    }

    // Constructor 2: Default box
    Box() {
        this(1, 1, 1); // delegates to Constructor 3
        System.out.println("Default constructor invoked.");
    }

    // Constructor 3: Master / Canonical constructor
    Box(double w, double h, double d) {
        this.width  = w;
        this.height = h;
        this.depth  = d;
        System.out.println("Master constructor invoked.");
    }

    double volume() { return width * height * depth; }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("--- Creating Cube ---");
        Box cube = new Box(5.0);
        System.out.println("Volume = " + cube.volume());

        System.out.println("\n--- Creating Default Box ---");
        Box def  = new Box();
        System.out.println("Volume = " + def.volume());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
--- Creating Cube ---
Master constructor invoked.
Cube constructor invoked.
Volume = 125.0

--- Creating Default Box ---
Master constructor invoked.
Default constructor invoked.
Volume = 1.0
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

1. `new Box(5.0)` calls **Constructor 1**.
2. `this(5.0, 5.0, 5.0)` immediately transfers control to **Constructor 3**.
3. Constructor 3 finishes all initializations (prints "Master constructor invoked.").
4. Control returns to Constructor 1, which prints "Cube constructor invoked.".

> [!TIP]
> **Design Principle:** Identify one constructor as the *canonical* (master) constructor. All other constructors should delegate to it using `this()`. This avoids code duplication — a hallmark of clean OOP design.

---

### 3.3 Use Case 3: `this` as Method Argument (Object Hand-off)

```java
class Helper {
    void process(Student s) {        // Receives a Student object
        System.out.println("Helper received: " + s.name);
    }
}

class Student {
    String name;

    Student(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    void register() {
        Helper h = new Helper();
        h.process(this);   // 'this' = the current Student object, passed to Helper
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student("Kavya");
        s.register();
    }
}
```

**Trace:**
- `s.register()` → inside `register()`, `this` points to object `s`.
- `h.process(this)` → passes the SAME object `s` into `Helper.process()`.
- `Helper.process()` prints the name of `s` through the parameter reference.

---

### 3.4 Use Case 4: Returning `this` for Method Chaining

```java
class Config {
    String mode;
    int    timeout;

    Config setMode(String m)    { this.mode = m;     return this; }
    Config setTimeout(int t)    { this.timeout = t;  return this; }

    void show() { System.out.println("Mode=" + mode + ", Timeout=" + timeout); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Config c = new Config();
        c.setMode("FAST").setTimeout(30).show();
        //      ↑ returns this → .setTimeout() is called on same object
        //                                  ↑ returns this → .show() is called
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Mode=FAST, Timeout=30
```

This is the foundation of the **Builder / Fluent Interface** design pattern widely used in Java libraries (e.g., `StringBuilder.append().append()`).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which `this` Pattern

| Exam Scenario in Question | Correct `this` Use | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter and field share the same name | `this.field = param;` | Disambiguation |
| Avoiding code duplication across constructors | `this(args);` | Constructor chaining |
| Pass current object to another class's method | `obj.method(this);` | Object hand-off |
| Enable chaining like `a.b().c().d()` | `return this;` | Fluent API |
| Reuse same object's method explicitly | `this.method();` | Clarity (optional) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Model — `this` as a Hidden Argument

The following diagram illustrates how the JVM passes the current object reference as a hidden `this` argument to every instance method.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Stack Frame\n(main)"] -->|"s1.display()"| B["Stack Frame\n(display)\nthis → 0xABC"]
    B --> C["Heap Memory\nObject s1 @ 0xABC\nname='Arjun'\nrollNo=45"]
    A2["Stack Frame\n(main)"] -->|"s2.display()"| B2["Stack Frame\n(display)\nthis → 0xDEF"]
    B2 --> C2["Heap Memory\nObject s2 @ 0xDEF\nname='Meera'\nrollNo=12"]

    classDef stack fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef heap fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    class A,A2,B,B2 stack
    class C,C2 heap
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When `s1.display()` is called, the JVM pushes a new stack frame for `display()` containing a hidden `this` parameter pointing to `s1`'s heap address (`0xABC`). When `s2.display()` is invoked, a *new* stack frame is created with `this` pointing to `s2`'s heap address (`0xDEF`). This is why `this` is **dynamic** — its value changes with each call.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of `this()` Constructor Chaining

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["new Box(5.0)"]) --> C1["Constructor 1\nBox(double side)"]
    C1 -->|"this(side,side,side)\nFIRST STATEMENT"| C3["Constructor 3\nBox(double w, double h, double d)\n(Master Constructor)"]
    C3 --> Assign["Assign width, height, depth"]
    Assign --> ReturnC3["Return control to Constructor 1"]
    ReturnC3 --> RestC1["Execute remaining lines of Constructor 1"]
    RestC1 --> Done(["Object fully constructed"])

    classDef callBox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef masterBox fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    class Start,Done callBox
    class C1,C3,Assign,ReturnC3,RestC1 masterBox
```

---

### 4.3 Architectural Flow — Method Chaining via `return this;`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as main()
    participant Obj  as Config object
    participant SM   as setMode()
    participant ST   as setTimeout()
    participant SH   as show()

    User->>Obj: new Config()
    User->>SM: setMode("FAST")
    SM->>Obj: this.mode = "FAST"
    SM-->>User: return this (Config object)
    User->>ST: setTimeout(30)
    ST->>Obj: this.timeout = 30
    ST-->>User: return this (Config object)
    User->>SH: show()
    SH-->>User: prints Mode=FAST, Timeout=30
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Insight:** Notice how each setter method returns the **same object reference** via `this`. The `main()` thread receives the reference back each time, allowing the next method in the chain to be called on the *same* object.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 3 marks each (short answer / definition)
> - **Part B:** 14 marks each (with internal choice, sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks)

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the `this` keyword in Java? Why is it called an implicit reference?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `this` keyword in Java is a **reference variable** that refers to the **current object** — the instance of the class on which a method or constructor is currently being invoked. It is called an *implicit reference* because the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) automatically creates and passes it as a hidden argument to every non-static method and constructor — programmers do not declare it explicitly. Inside a method, `this` allows unambiguous access to the current object's instance variables, especially when local parameters shadow field names. *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Can `this` keyword be used inside a `static` method in Java? Justify your answer with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**No**, the `this` keyword **cannot be used inside a static method or static block** in Java. This is because static members belong to the **class itself**, not to any specific object. Since `this` refers to the *current object* and no current object exists in a static context, the compiler raises an error: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context"*.

```java
class Demo {
    int x = 10;
    static void show() {
        System.out.println(this.x); // COMPILE-TIME ERROR
    }
}
```

The correct approach is to create an object and use it: `Demo d = new Demo(); System.out.println(d.x);` *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B (Module Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 2024]`**

#### **Option A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the different uses of the `this` keyword in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **constructor chaining** using the `this()` call. Explain how control transfers between constructors with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks:**

Java provides **six major uses** of the `this` keyword:

**1. Referring to current class instance variables:**
```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    Employee(String name) {
        this.name = name; // 'this.name' = field, 'name' = parameter
    }
}
```
Here `this.name` differentiates the instance variable from the constructor parameter, both named `name`. *(1 mark)*

**2. Invoking current class method:**
```java
void show() {
    this.display(); // explicit, but 'display();' is equivalent
}
```
Used for clarity, though Java implicitly invokes methods on `this`. *(1 mark)*

**3. Invoking current class constructor (constructor chaining):**
```java
class Car {
    Car()              { this("Default"); System.out.println("No-arg"); }
    Car(String model)  { System.out.println("Model: " + model); }
}
```
When `new Car()` is called, the no-arg constructor delegates to the parameterized one via `this("Default")`. The call **must be the first statement** in the constructor. *(2 marks)*

**4. Passing `this` as a method argument:**
```java
void register() {
    Helper.process(this); // passes current object to Helper
}
```
Allows another object to interact with the current one. *(1 mark)*

**5. Passing `this` as a constructor argument:**
```java
new LinkedList(this); // current object passed to another class's constructor
```
Used in object composition and dependency injection. *(1 mark)*

**6. Returning `this` from a method (method chaining):**
```java
class Builder {
    Builder setName(String n) { /*...*/ return this; }
}
// Enables: new Builder().setName("A").setName("B");
```
Powers the fluent interface / builder pattern. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Java Program Demonstrating Constructor Chaining:**
```java
class Rectangle {
    double length, breadth;

    // (i) Master / canonical constructor
    Rectangle(double l, double b) {
        System.out.println("Master constructor (l,b) called");
        this.length  = l;
        this.breadth = b;
    }

    // (ii) Square constructor — delegates to master
    Rectangle(double side) {
        this(side, side); // MUST be first statement
        System.out.println("Square constructor called");
    }

    // (iii) Default constructor — delegates to master
    Rectangle() {
        this(1, 1); // MUST be first statement
        System.out.println("Default constructor called");
    }

    double area() { return length * breadth; }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("--- new Rectangle() ---");
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();
        System.out.println("Area = " + r1.area());

        System.out.println("\n--- new Rectangle(4) ---");
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(4);
        System.out.println("Area = " + r2.area());

        System.out.println("\n--- new Rectangle(3,5) ---");
        Rectangle r3 = new Rectangle(3, 5);
        System.out.println("Area = " + r3.area());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
--- new Rectangle() ---
Master constructor (l,b) called
Default constructor called
Area = 1.0

--- new Rectangle(4) ---
Master constructor (l,b) called
Square constructor called
Area = 16.0

--- new Rectangle(3,5) ---
Master constructor (l,b) called
Area = 15.0
```

**Control Flow Explanation (with diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["new Rectangle()"] -->|"this(1,1) first statement"| B["Master Constructor\nRectangle(double l, double b)"]
    B --> C["Assign length=1, breadth=1"]
    C --> D["Print Master line"]
    D --> E["Return to Default Constructor"]
    E --> F["Print Default line"]
    F --> G["Object ready"]
```

**Key Rules Demonstrated:** *(2 marks for explanation)*
- `this()` **must be the first statement** inside a constructor.
- Constructors execute in a **chain**, with the master (canonical) constructor always executing first.
- Code duplication is eliminated — initialization logic lives in only one place (the master).

---

#### **Option B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice:**

**(a)** What is the significance of the `this` keyword? Write a Java program to show how `this` can be used to **resolve ambiguity** between instance variables and parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **`return this;`** enables **method chaining**. Write a Java program demonstrating a chained configuration builder using `this`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks:**

The `this` keyword is significant because it provides a **self-reference** for the current object. Its most important role is **resolving naming conflicts** between instance variables (fields) and local parameters that share the same name — a situation called **variable shadowing**.

**Java Program:**
```java
class Account {
    private String holder;
    private double balance;

    Account(String holder, double balance) {
        this.holder  = holder;   // LHS: field, RHS: parameter
        this.balance = balance;
    }

    void printDetails() {
        System.out.println("Holder: " + this.holder + ", Balance: " + this.balance);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account a1 = new Account("Ravi", 5000.0);
        Account a2 = new Account("Priya", 12000.0);
        a1.printDetails();
        a2.printDetails();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Holder: Ravi, Balance: 5000.0
Holder: Priya, Balance: 12000.0
```

**Explanation:**
- Without `this`, the statement `holder = holder;` would simply assign the parameter to itself — the field would remain `null` (its default value).
- `this.holder` explicitly targets the **current object's field**, while bare `holder` refers to the **local parameter**.
- This pattern is essential in **encapsulated classes** with `private` fields and public constructors. *(7 marks — 3 for theory + 4 for program & trace)*

---

#### **Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Concept:** When a method's return type is the **class type itself** and it returns `this`, the method returns a reference to the **current object**. The caller can then immediately invoke another method on the returned reference, creating a **chain** of calls on the **same object**.

**Java Program — Chained HTTP Request Builder:**
```java
class RequestBuilder {
    String method  = "GET";
    String url     = "";
    int    timeout = 30;

    RequestBuilder setMethod(String m)   { this.method  = m;  return this; }
    RequestBuilder setURL(String u)     { this.url     = u;  return this; }
    RequestBuilder setTimeout(int t)    { this.timeout = t;  return this; }

    void execute() {
        System.out.println("[" + method + "] " + url + " (timeout=" + timeout + "s)");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        RequestBuilder req = new RequestBuilder();
        req.setMethod("POST")
           .setURL("https://api.ktu.edu/submit")
           .setTimeout(60)
           .execute();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
[POST] https://api.ktu.edu/submit (timeout=60s)
```

**Trace (left-to-right chaining):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as main()
    participant R as RequestBuilder object
    M->>R: new RequestBuilder()
    M->>R: setMethod("POST")
    R-->>M: return this
    M->>R: setURL("https://...")
    R-->>M: return this
    M->>R: setTimeout(60)
    R-->>M: return this
    M->>R: execute()
    R-->>M: prints request details
```

**Why it works:** Every setter mutates the *same* object (`this`) and returns the same reference. The chain `req.setMethod(...).setURL(...).setTimeout(...).execute()` therefore operates on a **single instance** — exactly what fluent APIs need. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on `this` Keyword Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "first statement" rule** for `this()` in constructors — *−2 marks instantly.*
> 2. **Using `this` inside `static` methods** without realizing the compile-time error — examiners deduct marks for missing the static-vs-instance distinction.
> 3. **Not commenting the code** — KTU valuation key explicitly awards 1–2 marks for *clear comments* on lines like `this.name = name;`.
> 4. **Confusing `this` with `super`** — `this` refers to the *current* class object; `super` refers to the *parent* class. Mixing them up costs the full question.
> 5. **Writing `return this;` but forgetting to change the return type** from `void` to the class name — compile error, full marks lost.
> 6. **In constructor chaining, recursive `this()` calls** — students often write `A(){ this(); }` causing `StackOverflowError`; examiners expect you to *warn* about this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read 5 Minutes Before Exam**

- ☐ `this` is a **reference variable** pointing to the **current object**.
- ☐ `this` is **implicit, final, and non-static** — created by JVM, cannot be reassigned, unavailable in static context.
- ☐ **Six uses:** (1) field disambiguation, (2) method invocation, (3) constructor chaining via `this()`, (4) method argument, (5) constructor argument, (6) return value for chaining.
- ☐ `this()` **must be the first statement** in any constructor that uses it.
- ☐ `this()` and `super()` **cannot coexist** in the same constructor.
- ☐ **Recursive `this()`** → infinite loop → `StackOverflowError`.
- ☐ **Method chaining** (`return this;`) requires return type = class name.
- ☐ Without `this`, parameters shadow instance variables → silent bug → field stays default.
- ☐ `this` is passed as a **hidden argument** to every instance method by the JVM.
- ☐ Practical patterns: **Builder / Fluent API**, **Constructor Chaining**, **Event Handlers (GUI)**, **Linked List Node operations**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Method Overloading** (also termed *Compile-time Polymorphism* or *Static Binding Polymorphism*) is a fundamental feature of Object-Oriented Programming wherein a class declares **two or more methods** that share the **same name** but differ in their **method signature** — specifically, the number of parameters, the data types of the parameters, or the order in which parameters of different data types appear.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT415 – OOP, Module 2)**, method overloading is classified under the umbrella of **Polymorphism** and is implemented strictly at **compile time**. The Java compiler resolves the binding between the call site and the correct method definition during the translation phase, which is why it is also known as **Early Binding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT415 – Module 2):** Method overloading must be studied in conjunction with *type promotion*, *automatic type conversion in arguments*, *ambiguity errors*, and *overloading with respect to access modifiers and return types*. The KTU board frequently tests these peripheral concepts.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are at a coffee vending machine. The machine has a single labelled button — **"Brew"** — but the actual drink produced depends on the parameters you supply *before pressing the button*:

- Brew(**1** sugar cube, **small** cup) → produces a small, lightly sweetened espresso
- Brew(**3** sugar cubes, **large** cup) → produces a large, sweet cappuccino
- Brew(**0** sugar, **medium** cup, **with** milk) → produces a medium black coffee

The **verb** ("Brew") remains identical, yet the **result varies based on the signature** of the input. Method overloading applies the same principle in Java: a single method name dispatches to different implementations based on the *shape* of the argument list.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single name, multiple behaviours — resolved at compile time.** That is the essence of method overloading in one line.

## 1.3 Why Method Overloading Matters

| Engineering Reason | Practical Benefit |
|---|---|
| Improves **code readability** | Programmers remember one name, not many |
| Reduces **naming clutter** | Eliminates need for `addInt`, `addDouble`, `addFloat` |
| Enables **intuitive APIs** | `System.out.println()` prints 10+ types via overloading |
| Supports **polymorphic dispatch** at compile time | Foundation for OOP design principles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Emphasis:** The 2024 Scheme stresses that overloading is resolved by the **compiler**, not the **JVM at runtime**. Confusing this with *method overriding* (which is runtime polymorphism) results in direct loss of marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Method Overloading

A method signature in Java comprises the **method name** and the **ordered list of parameter types** (the *formal parameter list*). Overloading is permitted when **at least one** of the following differentiating criteria is satisfied:

1. **Variation in the number of parameters** (`arity` differs)
2. **Variation in the data types of parameters** (`type signature` differs)
3. **Variation in the order of parameter types** (only when parameter types themselves differ)

> [!NOTE]
> **Return type alone is NOT a valid differentiator.** Two methods with identical names and parameter lists but different return types will cause a **compile-time error**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Rule / Property | Valid? | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Different number of parameters | ✅ Yes | `add(int, int)` vs `add(int, int, int)` |
| Different parameter types | ✅ Yes | `add(int, int)` vs `add(double, double)` |
| Different parameter order | ✅ Yes | `set(int, String)` vs `set(String, int)` |
| Different return type only | ❌ No | Compiler error — ambiguous call site |
| Different access modifier only | ❌ No | Access specifier is not part of signature |
| Different exception list only | ❌ No | Exceptions are not part of signature |
| `static` vs non-static | ❌ No | Same signature → duplicate method error |
| Varargs (`...`) considered carefully | ⚠️ Conditional | Lower priority than exact match |

## 2.3 Type Promotion Hierarchy (The Most-Tested Concept)

When an overloaded method is invoked, the compiler first attempts an **exact match**. If none exists, Java applies **automatic type promotion** following a strict promotion ladder:

$$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$

$$\text{char} \rightarrow \text{int}$$

A special promotion chain exists for character and numeric types when no exact match is found. This is one of the most heavily tested topics in the KTU ESE for Module 2.

## 2.4 The Method Resolution Algorithm (KTU Board Favourite)

The compiler follows a deterministic 3-step algorithm to resolve an overloaded call:

$$\text{Step 1: Exact Match (no conversion)} \rightarrow \text{if found, use it}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Type Promotion (widening conversion)} \rightarrow \text{if unique match, use it}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Ambiguity Check} \rightarrow \text{if two or more equally valid matches, compile-time error}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** A `null` literal cannot disambiguate between two overloaded methods that both accept reference types in the same promotion path — this often appears as a tricky conceptual question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Method overloading is the backbone of many production-grade Java APIs:

- **`PrintStream.println()`** — overloaded for `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `String`, `Object`, `boolean`
- **`String.valueOf()`** — overloaded for every primitive type
- **`Arrays.sort()`** — overloaded for `int[]`, `long[]`, `double[]`, `Object[]`, `Comparator<T>[]`
- **`Math.max()` / `Math.min()`** — overloaded for `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`

These libraries are engineered to provide a **uniform interface** across diverse data types, demonstrating the design philosophy that overloading is not merely syntactic sugar but a **legitimate architectural tool**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line** resolution of method overloading scenarios frequently tested in KTU university examinations. Every step of the resolution is explicitly justified.

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Case — Type Promotion Ladder

**Problem Statement:** Given the overloaded method definitions and a calling expression, derive which method is invoked.

**Code Under Analysis:**

```java
class Demo {
    void show(int a)      { System.out.println("int"); }
    void show(double a)   { System.out.println("double"); }
    void show(long a)     { System.out.println("long"); }
}
```

**Calling Expression:**

```java
new Demo().show(5);   // Step 1: literal 5 is of type int
```

### Step-by-Step Compiler Resolution

We now walk through the binding derivation rigorously. Let the argument's static type be denoted $T_a$ and the formal parameter type of candidate method $i$ be $T_{p_i}$. The compiler searches candidates in the order defined below.

**Step 1 — Exact Match Identification**

The literal `5` in Java has a static type $T_a = \text{int}$. We test the first candidate.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_a &= \text{int} \\
T_{p_1} &= \text{int} \\
T_a \equiv T_{p_1} &\Rightarrow \text{Exact match found.}
\end{aligned}$$

The compiler binds the call to `show(int)` and terminates the search.

### Analytical Matrix for the Same Class with a `byte` Argument

**Calling Expression:** `new Demo().show((byte)5);` — Here, $T_a = \text{byte}$.

| Resolution Stage | Candidate Tested | Promotion Path | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stage 1: Exact | `show(int)` | `byte` $\nrightarrow$ `int` (no implicit conversion) | ❌ Skip |
| Stage 2: Widening (integer) | `show(long)` | `byte` $\rightarrow$ `short` $\rightarrow$ `int` $\rightarrow$ `long` | ✅ **Match** |
| Stage 3: Widening (floating) | `show(double)` | `byte` $\rightarrow$ ... $\rightarrow$ `double` | ✅ Also valid |
| **Final Outcome** | **`show(long)`** | **Shorter promotion chain wins** | **Bound to `show(long)`** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When multiple widening paths exist, the compiler selects the **most specific match** — i.e., the path with the **shortest promotion chain**. If two paths have equal length, an **ambiguity error** is raised.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Case — Ambiguity Derivation

**Code Under Analysis:**

```java
class Demo {
    void compute(long a, int b)   { System.out.println("long-int"); }
    void compute(int a, long b)   { System.out.println("int-long"); }
}
```

**Calling Expression:** `new Demo().compute(10, 20);` — Both arguments are `int` literals.

**Resolution Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{For } compute(long, int): & \quad T_a = \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \quad (\text{1 promotion step}) \\
\text{For } compute(int, long): & \quad T_b = \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \quad (\text{1 promotion step}) \\
\text{Total promotions each} &= 1 \quad \text{(equal)} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Ambiguity} &\Rightarrow \text{Compile-time error}
\end{aligned}$$

The compiler raises: **reference to compute is ambiguous**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked:** When asked to *resolve* an ambiguous overload, the only valid answer is that the code **fails to compile**. Do not suggest type casting the arguments unless the question explicitly asks for a fix.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Case — Varargs Priority

**Code Under Analysis:**

```java
class Demo {
    void test(int a)         { System.out.println("int-arg"); }
    void test(int a, int... b) { System.out.println("varargs"); }
}
```

**Calling Expression:** `new Demo().test(5);` and `new Demo().test(5, 10);`

**Resolution Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Call 1: } test(5) &\Rightarrow T_a = \text{int}, \text{ arity} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{Exact match with } test(int) \\
\text{Call 2: } test(5, 10) &\Rightarrow \text{arity} = 2, \text{ no exact match} \Rightarrow \text{binds to } test(int, int...)
\end{aligned}$$

**Compiler Priority for Matching:** exact match $>$ widening match $>$ autoboxing match $>$ varargs match.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume varargs is the highest priority. In reality, **varargs is the lowest** — it is chosen only when no fixed-arity method matches.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Promotion Chain Reference (Tabular Form)

| Source Type | First Widening | Second Widening | Third Widening | Fourth Widening | Fifth Widening |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | `short` | `int` | `long` | `float` | `double` |
| `short` | `int` | `long` | `float` | `double` | — |
| `char` | `int` | `long` | `float` | `double` | — |
| `int` | `long` | `float` | `double` | — | — |
| `long` | `float` | `double` | — | — | — |
| `float` | `double` | — | — | — | — |
| `boolean` | (none — no promotion) | — | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> `boolean` is **never** promoted to any other type. Passing a `boolean` to a method expecting `int` is a compile-time error.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compiler Resolution Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the decision tree the Java compiler traverses when binding an overloaded method call. This corresponds directly to the algorithm discussed in Section 2.4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Method Invocation Detected] --> B{Exact Match Found?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Bind to Exact Match - TERMINATE]
    B -- No --> D{Type Promotion Match?}
    D -- Yes and Unique --> E[Bind to Widened Match - TERMINATE]
    D -- No --> F{Autoboxing / Unboxing Match?}
    F -- Yes and Unique --> G[Bind via Autoboxing - TERMINATE]
    F -- No --> H{Varargs Match?}
    H -- Yes and Unique --> I[Bind via Varargs - TERMINATE]
    H -- No --> J[AMBIGUITY ERROR - COMPILE-TIME FAILURE]
    D -- Multiple Equal --> J
    F -- Multiple Equal --> J
    H -- Multiple Equal --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each diamond node represents a decision point. The compiler proceeds top-to-bottom, and at each stage, if **exactly one** candidate survives, binding occurs. If **zero or more-than-one** candidates survive at any stage, an error is raised.

## 4.2 Type Promotion Ladder (Block Architecture)

The following block diagram represents the promotion hierarchy as a functional pipeline. Each block maps an input type to its next promoted form.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[byte] --> P2[short]
    P2 --> P3[int]
    P3 --> P4[long]
    P4 --> P5[float]
    P5 --> P6[double]
    P7[char] --> P3
    P8[boolean] -.X.-> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** The dashed line from `boolean` to `int` represents an **illegal transition** — it is marked with an `X` to emphasize that `boolean` participates in **no promotion chain**. This visual cue is critical for KTU multiple-choice questions.

## 4.3 Overloading Decision Matrix (Method-Resolution Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CompileTimeBinding[Compile-Time Binding Engine]
        Q1[Step 1: Match by Number of Parameters]
        Q2[Step 2: Match by Parameter Types]
        Q3[Step 3: Match by Parameter Order]
        Q4[Step 4: Apply Type Promotion]
        Q5[Step 5: Apply Autoboxing]
        Q6[Step 6: Apply Varargs]
    end
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> R{Bound Successfully?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Generate Bytecode INVOKEVIRTUAL]
    R -- No --> T[Error: Reference is Ambiguous]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The `INVOKEVIRTUAL` bytecode instruction (or its overload-resolving counterpart) is emitted at compile time — the JVM does **not** re-resolve the binding at runtime. This is the defining difference between overloading (static) and overriding (dynamic).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define method overloading. List the criteria used to distinguish overloaded methods in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Method overloading is a compile-time polymorphism mechanism in Java that allows a class to define multiple methods sharing the same name but differing in their **method signatures**. The criteria for distinguishing overloaded methods are:

1. **Number of parameters** must differ between the methods.
2. **Data types of parameters** must differ.
3. **Order of parameters** must differ (only when the types themselves differ).

> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1M) + Three criteria (1M + 1M)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is automatic type promotion in the context of method overloading? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Automatic type promotion is the process by which the Java compiler widens an argument's data type when no exact-match overloaded method is found. The promotion follows a fixed hierarchy:

$$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$

**Example:** If a class defines `show(int)` and `show(double)`, and is called with a `byte` argument, the compiler promotes `byte` $\rightarrow$ `int` and binds the call to `show(int)`.

> **Mark Allocation:** Definition (1M) + Promotion chain (1M) + Example (1M)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Method Overloading Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** state that return type differentiates overloaded methods — this is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in KTU answer sheets.
> - **Do NOT** confuse method overloading with method overriding; the binding timing (compile-time vs. runtime) must be stated explicitly.
> - **Do NOT** claim that access modifiers or `throws` clauses can differentiate overloaded methods — these are not part of the method signature.
> - **Always** mention the **type promotion chain** when an explanation involves a non-exact argument match.
> - When an **ambiguity** is present, the only correct conclusion is a **compile-time error** — do not suggest a workaround unless the question asks for one.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of method overloading in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules that must be followed while overloading methods. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** What is type promotion in method overloading? Write a Java program to demonstrate automatic type promotion when overloaded methods are invoked with arguments of different data types. Explain the output produced. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 Marks):** Method overloading is a feature of Java that allows a class to declare multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** (varying number, type, or order of parameters). It is a form of **compile-time polymorphism** because the compiler decides which method to invoke at compile time based on the argument signature.

**Rules for Method Overloading (3 Marks):**

1. Overloaded methods **must** differ in the parameter list (number, type, or order).
2. The **return type** alone cannot differentiate overloaded methods.
3. **Access modifiers**, **exception lists**, and `static`/`non-static` nature are **not** part of the method signature and therefore cannot be used to distinguish overloads.
4. **Varargs** can be used, but they have the **lowest priority** during resolution.

**Example Code (2 Marks):**

```java
class Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    double add(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }
    int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}
class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator c = new Calculator();
        System.out.println(c.add(2, 3));        // Calls add(int, int) -> 5
        System.out.println(c.add(2.5, 3.5));    // Calls add(double, double) -> 6.0
        System.out.println(c.add(1, 2, 3));     // Calls add(int, int, int) -> 6
    }
}
```

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Concept of Type Promotion (2 Marks):** When an overloaded method is called and no method has an exact parameter type match for the argument, the Java compiler automatically **promotes** (widens) the argument to a larger type following a defined hierarchy. The promotion chain is:

$$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$

Additionally, `char` is promoted to `int` as the first step.

**Demonstration Code (3 Marks):**

```java
class TypePromotion {
    void display(int a) {
        System.out.println("int method: " + a);
    }
    void display(double a) {
        System.out.println("double method: " + a);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        TypePromotion obj = new TypePromotion();
        obj.display(100);          // int literal -> exact match
        obj.display(100L);         // long literal -> exact match NOT available, promotes to float? No, exact match is needed
        obj.display('A');          // char -> promotes to int
        obj.display(10.5f);        // float -> promotes to double
    }
}
```

**Output Explanation (2 Marks):**

| Call | Argument Type | Match Path | Method Invoked |
|---|---|---|---|
| `obj.display(100)` | `int` | Exact match | `display(int)` |
| `obj.display(100L)` | `long` | Promotes `long` $\rightarrow$ `float` (no `display(float)`) $\rightarrow$ `double` | `display(double)` |
| `obj.display('A')` | `char` | Promotes `char` $\rightarrow$ `int` | `display(int)` |
| `obj.display(10.5f)` | `float` | Promotes `float` $\rightarrow$ `double` | `display(double)` |

> **Mark Allocation Justification:** Concept (2M) + Code (3M) + Output table with explanation (2M)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding in Java. Provide a code snippet that illustrates both concepts within a single program. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the concept of ambiguity in method overloading with a suitable example. Under what circumstances does the Java compiler raise an ambiguity error? How can it be resolved? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Differentiation Table (4 Marks):**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| Polymorphism type | Compile-time (static) | Runtime (dynamic) |
| Location | Same class | Two classes (inheritance) |
| Signature | Must differ | Must be identical |
| Return type | Can differ | Must be same (or covariant) |
| Binding performed by | Compiler | JVM |
| Access modifier | Can differ | Cannot be more restrictive |
| `static` methods | Can be overloaded | Cannot be overridden |
| `final` methods | Can be overloaded | Cannot be overridden |

**Code Snippet (3 Marks):**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {                              // (1) Base method
        System.out.println("Drawing Shape");
    }
    void draw(int sides) {                     // (2) Overloaded in SAME class
        System.out.println("Drawing Shape with " + sides + " sides");
    }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {                              // (3) Overriding in SUBCLASS
        System.out.println("Drawing Circle");
    }
}
class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s = new Shape();
        s.draw();           // Calls Shape.draw()         [overload #1]
        s.draw(5);          // Calls Shape.draw(int)      [overload #2]
        Shape c = new Circle();
        c.draw();           // Calls Circle.draw()        [overriding demo]
    }
}
```

> **Mark Allocation Justification:** Table (4M) + Code (2M) + Explanation embedded (1M)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) explanation

In the code above, `draw()` and `draw(int)` are **overloaded** within the `Shape` class (same name, different parameter list, resolved at compile time). The `draw()` method is **overridden** in the `Circle` class with the same signature, and the JVM dispatches the call dynamically based on the actual object type (`new Circle()`) at runtime. This single program demonstrates both forms of polymorphism.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Concept of Ambiguity (2 Marks):** Ambiguity in method overloading occurs when the Java compiler cannot determine a **unique** overloaded method to bind to a method invocation. This happens when two or more methods are equally valid candidates under the type promotion rules.

**Causes of Ambiguity (2 Marks):**

1. **Multiple widening paths of equal length** — e.g., a call where two parameters each have a single-step promotion to two different methods.
2. **No exact match and multiple varargs methods** available.
3. **`null` passed to two methods** that accept different reference types at the same level.

**Example (2 Marks):**

```java
class Demo {
    void process(long a, int b)  { System.out.println("long-int"); }
    void process(int a, long b)  { System.out.println("int-long"); }
}
class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Demo d = new Demo();
        d.process(10, 20);       // COMPILE-TIME ERROR: reference to process is ambiguous
    }
}
```

**Resolution (1 Mark):** Ambiguity can be resolved by:

- Performing an **explicit type cast** at the call site, e.g., `d.process(10L, 20)` forces binding to `process(long, int)`.
- **Renaming** one of the methods to avoid the conflict.
- **Reordering the parameters** in the calling expression to match a unique signature.

> **Mark Allocation Justification:** Concept (2M) + Causes (2M) + Example (2M) + Resolution (1M)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Method Overloading (PEMRT415 – Module 2)**

- ✅ Method overloading = **same method name, different parameter list**, resolved at **compile time**.
- ✅ Differentiators: **number**, **type**, or **order** of parameters.
- ✅ **Return type alone is NOT** a valid differentiator — this is a classic KTU trap.
- ✅ Access modifiers, `static`/`final`, and `throws` clauses are **not part** of the method signature.
- ✅ Type promotion chain: `byte` $\rightarrow$ `short` $\rightarrow$ `int` $\rightarrow$ `long` $\rightarrow$ `float` $\rightarrow$ `double`; `char` $\rightarrow$ `int`.
- ✅ `boolean` participates in **no promotion**.
- ✅ Compiler resolution priority: **exact match $>$ widening $>$ autoboxing/unboxing $>$ varargs**.
- ✅ Ambiguity = compile-time error when **two or more** candidates are equally valid.
- ✅ Overloading is **static polymorphism**; overriding is **dynamic polymorphism** — never confuse the two.
- ✅ Real-world examples: `println()`, `valueOf()`, `Math.max()`, `Arrays.sort()`.
- ✅ Varargs has the **lowest** resolution priority — chosen only when no fixed-arity method matches.
- ✅ KTU board tests *type promotion*, *ambiguity*, and the *priority order* most frequently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Objects as Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using Objects as Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Object Oriented Programming, **methods (member functions) can accept objects as parameters**, just as they accept primitive data type parameters. When an object is passed to a method, the **reference (memory address) of the object** is passed to the formal parameter of the receiving method. This enables methods to access, modify, and operate on the actual data (instance variables) of the calling object's instance, as well as establish inter-object communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:**
> In **Java**, object parameters are passed using **Pass-by-Value of the Reference** (often called "Call-by-Reference semantics from the caller's viewpoint"). The reference variable is copied, but both the original and the copy refer to the **same object** residing in the **heap memory**.

### 1.2 General Syntax Declaration

The formal declaration of a method that accepts an object as a parameter follows the syntax:

$$\text{access\_modifier} \;\; \text{return\_type} \;\; \text{methodName}(\text{ClassName} \;\; \text{parameterName})$$

For example, a method `enlargeShape` that receives a `Shape` object:

$$\text{public} \;\; \text{void} \;\; \text{enlargeShape}(\text{Shape} \;\; s)$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Key Card" Intuition

Imagine you are staying in a hotel room (the **object**) and the front desk gives you a key card (the **reference variable**).

| Step | Real-World Action | Java Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | You check into Room 305 | An object is created in the heap |
| 2 | You hand the key card to your friend | The reference is passed as parameter |
| 3 | Your friend enters Room 305 and moves the bed | The method modifies object state |
| 4 | When you return, the bed is still moved | Original object reflects the change |
| 5 | Your friend photocopies the key and uses it on a different room | Reassigning the parameter does **not** affect the caller |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this analogy powerful for exams?**
> Examiners love questions that test whether students understand that **mutating the object is visible to the caller**, but **reassigning the parameter reference is not**.

### 1.4 Three Pillars of Object Parameter Passing

1. **Object as Argument:** A reference to an existing object is passed during the method call.
2. **Object State Modification:** The method can read or write the instance variables of the passed object.
3. **Object as Return Value:** Methods can also `return` an object, allowing complex object manipulations and chaining.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

When a method is invoked with an object parameter, the following sequence of events takes place in the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**:

- **Step 1 — Object Creation:** The caller instantiates an object using `new` keyword, which allocates memory in the **heap area** of the JVM.
- **Step 2 — Reference Storage:** The address of the heap allocation is stored in a **reference variable** in the caller's stack frame.
- **Step 3 — Method Invocation:** The caller passes the reference variable to the method's formal parameter.
- **Step 4 — Reference Copy:** The JVM **copies the reference value** (not the object) into a new local variable inside the method's stack frame.
- **Step 5 — Same Object Access:** Both the caller and the method now hold references that point to the **same object in the heap**.
- **Step 6 — Modification Window:** Any modification to the object's mutable state (instance variables) is reflected in the original object.
- **Step 7 — Reassignment Isolation:** If the method parameter is reassigned (`param = new Object()`), the caller's reference remains unchanged.
- **Step 8 — Method Return:** The method completes and the stack frame is destroyed, but the object persists in the heap (subject to Garbage Collection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two Arrows, One Target" Principle:**
> The reference is the *arrow*, and the object is the *target*. When we pass an object, we copy the arrow. Both arrows now point to the same target. Modifying the target affects everyone, but shooting a new arrow does not change the old arrow's flight path.

### 2.2 The Core Rules Table (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Action on Parameter | Effect on Caller's Object | Effect on Caller's Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Modify instance variable (e.g., `obj.width = 10`) | **YES** — Visible after return | No effect |
| 2 | Call a mutator method on the object (e.g., `obj.setX(5)`) | **YES** — Visible after return | No effect |
| 3 | Reassign parameter to a new object (`obj = new ClassName()`) | **NO** — Caller's object untouched | **YES** — New object pointed by local copy only |
| 4 | Assign `null` to parameter (`obj = null`) | **NO** — Caller's object untouched | Caller's reference remains valid |
| 5 | Return object reference from method | Enables caller to access the new object | Caller can store the return value |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Notation Used | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Volume of Box object | $V = w \times h \times d$ | $V$ = volume, $w,h,d$ = dimensions | Used in CAD, packaging, logistics software |
| Reference Equivalence | $r_1 \equiv r_2 \Leftrightarrow r_1 == r_2$ in memory | $r_1, r_2$ = references | Validates object identity in collections |
| Pass-by-Value of Reference | $f(o) \rightarrow o_{caller} = o_{callee}$ initially, but $\Delta(o_{state})$ persists | $f$ = method, $o$ = object | Distributed systems, RPC, REST API design |
| Method Overloading with Objects | $m(C_1) \neq m(C_2) \Rightarrow$ unique signatures | $m$ = method, $C$ = class | Used in compiler design and API libraries |
| Returned Object Reference | $o_{new} = f(o_{old})$ | Allows object transformation | Builder pattern, Factory pattern, fluent APIs |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ability to pass objects as parameters is the cornerstone of modern software engineering:

- **GUI Programming:** Event handlers receive `Event` objects containing click coordinates, key codes, and source references.
- **Database Connectivity (JDBC):** `PreparedStatement.setObject(int, Object)` methods pass Java objects directly to SQL queries.
- **Game Development:** Physics engines pass `Collider` objects to collision-detection routines.
- **Distributed Computing:** Serialized Java objects are passed across network sockets between JVMs.
- **Design Patterns:** Strategy, Observer, Command, and Decorator patterns all depend fundamentally on object parameter passing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Primitive Passing:**
> For primitives (`int`, `double`, `char`), Java passes a **copy of the value**, so modifications inside the method do NOT affect the caller. For objects, Java passes a **copy of the reference**, so modifications to the object's state DO affect the caller.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Tracing a Program with Object Parameter

Consider the following Java program that demonstrates object parameter passing using a `Rectangle` class:

```java
class Rectangle {
    double length;
    double breadth;

    Rectangle(double l, double b) {
        length = l;
        breadth = b;
    }

    double area() {
        return length * breadth;
    }
}

class Demo {
    static void modifyRectangle(Rectangle r) {
        r.length = r.length + 10;
        r.breadth = r.breadth + 5;
    }

    static Rectangle createSquare(double side) {
        Rectangle sq = new Rectangle(side, side);
        return sq;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle rect = new Rectangle(20, 10);
        System.out.println("Before: " + rect.area());
        modifyRectangle(rect);
        System.out.println("After:  " + rect.area());

        Rectangle square = createSquare(7);
        System.out.println("Square area: " + square.area());
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Step 1 — Class Loading and Main Method Invocation**

The JVM loads `Demo.class` and `Rectangle.class` into the method area. The `main` method is pushed onto the **call stack**.

**Step 2 — Object Construction in Heap**

The statement `Rectangle rect = new Rectangle(20, 10);` executes. Memory allocation occurs in the **heap**:

$$\text{Heap Address (e.g., 0x1000)} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \text{length} = 20.0, \;\; \text{breadth} = 10.0$$

The reference `rect` (stored in main's stack frame) holds the value `0x1000`.

**Step 3 — Initial Area Calculation**

$$\text{Area} = \text{length} \times \text{breadth} = 20.0 \times 10.0 = 200.0$$

Output: `Before: 200.0`

**Step 4 — Method Invocation `modifyRectangle(rect)`**

A new stack frame is pushed for `modifyRectangle`. The formal parameter `r` receives a **copy of the reference** `0x1000`.

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{callee} &= 0x1000 \\
r_{caller} &= 0x1000 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Both `r` and `rect` point to the **same Rectangle object** at heap address `0x1000`.

**Step 5 — State Modification Inside the Method**

The line `r.length = r.length + 10;` executes:

$$\text{length}_{new} = 20.0 + 10.0 = 30.0$$

Since `r` and `rect` point to the same object, the change is immediately visible to the caller through `rect`. The line `r.breadth = r.breadth + 5;` executes:

$$\text{breadth}_{new} = 10.0 + 5.0 = 15.0$$

**Step 6 — Method Return and Stack Frame Pop**

The `modifyRectangle` method returns. Its stack frame is destroyed. The local variable `r` ceases to exist. However, the object at `0x1000` still exists in the heap because `rect` (in main) still references it.

**Step 7 — Recalculation of Area After Return**

$$\text{Area}_{new} = 30.0 \times 15.0 = 450.0$$

Output: `After:  450.0`

**Step 8 — Calling Method That Returns an Object**

The line `Rectangle square = createSquare(7);` is executed. A new Rectangle object is created inside `createSquare` with `length = 7, breadth = 7`. The reference to this new object is returned to main and stored in `square`.

**Step 9 — Final Area Calculation**

$$\text{Area}_{square} = 7.0 \times 7.0 = 49.0$$

Output: `Square area: 49.0`

### 3.3 Analytical Memory State Matrix

| Program Line Executed | `rect` Reference | `r` Reference | `square` Reference | Heap Object State (rect) | Heap Object State (square) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Rectangle rect = new Rectangle(20,10)` | `0x1000` | — | — | length=20, breadth=10 | — |
| `modifyRectangle(rect)` (entry) | `0x1000` | `0x1000` | — | length=20, breadth=10 | — |
| `r.length = r.length + 10` | `0x1000` | `0x1000` | — | length=30, breadth=10 | — |
| `r.breadth = r.breadth + 5` | `0x1000` | `0x1000` | — | length=30, breadth=15 | — |
| Method returns | `0x1000` | (destroyed) | — | length=30, breadth=15 | — |
| `createSquare(7)` (entry) | `0x1000` | — | — | length=30, breadth=15 | (creating) |
| Object returned | `0x1000` | — | `0x2000` | length=30, breadth=15 | length=7, breadth=7 |

### 3.4 Counter-Example: Why Reassignment Does NOT Affect Caller

To solidify understanding, examine this critical case:

```java
static void tryReassign(Rectangle r) {
    r = new Rectangle(100, 100);   // New object created
    r.length = 999;                 // Modifies new object only
}
```

After `tryReassign(rect)` is called, the caller's `rect` **still points to the original object** with `length=30, breadth=15`. The local parameter `r` was reassigned, but that change is **isolated** to the method's stack frame and is lost upon return.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule for KTU Exams:**
> "**Object state changes are persistent across method calls, but reference reassignments are not.**" Memorize this line — it is worth full marks on any parameter-passing question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Architecture Flow — Object Passing

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the JVM memory model when an object is passed as a parameter, showing the relationship between the call stack and the heap:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Method Call Stack] --> A1[Reference Variable: rect]
    A1 -->|"holds address 0x1000"| H1[Heap Memory: Object 1]
    H1 --> H1a[length = 20]
    H1 --> H1b[breadth = 10]

    B[Method Call: modifyRectangle] --> B1[Local Parameter: r]
    B1 -->|"copy of address 0x1000"| H1

    C[Method Modifies State] --> C1["r.length = 30"]
    C --> C2["r.breadth = 15"]
    C1 --> H1
    C2 --> H1

    D[Method Returns and Stack Frame Destroyed] --> D1[Local r ceases to exist]
    D1 --> H1

    E[Main Continues Execution] --> A1
    A1 --> H1

    F[Second Method: createSquare] --> F1[New Object Created: 0x2000]
    F1 --> H2[Heap Memory: Object 2]
    H2 --> H2a[length = 7]
    H2 --> H2b[breadth = 7]
    F1 --> G[Return Reference to Main]
    G --> A2[Reference Variable: square]
    A2 --> H2

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#5f1e3a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#3a5f1e,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#5f3a1e,color:#ffffff
    style H1 fill:#8B0000,color:#ffffff
    style H2 fill:#006400,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#DAA520,color:#000000
    style C2 fill:#DAA520,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Reassignment vs. State Modification — Decision Flow

This decision flowchart helps students determine whether a change inside a method will be visible to the caller:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Action Performed on Object Parameter] --> Q1{What is being modified?}

    Q1 -->|Instance Variable| M1[Example: obj.length = 50]
    M1 --> R1[Visible to Caller YES]
    R1 --> R1A[Because both references point to same heap object]

    Q1 -->|Reference Itself| M2[Example: obj = new MyClass]
    M2 --> R2[Visible to Caller NO]
    R2 --> R2A[Local copy reassigned only]

    Q1 -->|Reference set to null| M3[Example: obj = null]
    M3 --> R3[Visible to Caller NO]
    R3 --> R3A[Local copy nulled only]

    Q1 -->|Mutator Method Called| M4[Example: obj.setValue 100]
    M4 --> R4[Visible to Caller YES]
    R4 --> R4A[Method internally modifies state]

    style Start fill:#2F4F4F,color:#ffffff
```
