> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** An **Inner Class** (also called a **Nested Class**) in Java is a class that is declared as a member of another class or within a block of code. It logically groups helper classes that are only used in one place, increasing encapsulation and producing more readable and maintainable code.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **Russian Matryoshka Doll** (the wooden nesting dolls). Just as a smaller doll fits *inside* a larger doll and is logically connected to it (you would not find the inner doll on its own), an **Inner Class** lives entirely *inside* an Outer Class. It belongs to the Outer Class and can freely access all private members of the Outer Class — but its existence is bound to the Outer's lifetime. If the Outer Class is the "body," the Inner Class is a "specialized organ" that handles one specific internal task.

**Types of Inner Classes (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
- **Member Inner Class** – Defined at member level of a class (non-static).
- **Static Nested Class** – A static class defined at member level.
- **Local Inner Class** – Defined inside a method or block.
- **Anonymous Inner Class** – A nameless class declared and instantiated in a single expression.

---

### 1.2 Inheritance & Super Class

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** **Inheritance** is an OOP mechanism by which one class (called the **Sub Class** / **Child Class** / **Derived Class**) acquires the properties (fields) and behaviors (methods) of another class (called the **Super Class** / **Parent Class** / **Base Class**). It is implemented in Java using the `extends` keyword and embodies the **"IS-A"** relationship.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of inheritance like a **family tree**. A child inherits physical traits and surname (properties) from parents, but can also develop their own unique skills (overriding). In Java, the `Super Class` is the **parent**, and the class that extends it is the **child**. Just as a child cannot exist before the parent is born, in Java the parent constructor is called *before* the child constructor during object creation.

**The `super` keyword in Java (KTU High-Priority Topic):**
1. `super.variable` – Accesses parent class fields.
2. `super.method()` – Calls parent class methods.
3. `super(args)` – Invokes parent class constructors (must be the first statement in a constructor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Super Class:** In Java, if a class does not explicitly `extend` another class, it implicitly extends `java.lang.Object` — the **root of the class hierarchy**. Every class in Java is a descendant of `Object`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Four Inner Class Types

#### A. Member Inner Class (Non-Static Nested Class)
- Declared at the **member level** of a class (not inside a method).
- **Cannot** have `static` declarations (except `static final` constants).
- Requires an **instance of the outer class** to be instantiated: `OuterObject.new Inner()`.
- Has implicit reference to the enclosing outer class instance.

**Why it exists:** When a helper class is tightly coupled to the outer class and needs access to its instance variables.

#### B. Static Nested Class
- Declared as a `static` member of the outer class.
- Behaves like a **top-level class** but is namespaced inside the outer class for packaging clarity.
- **Does NOT** have access to non-static members of the outer class.
- Instantiated directly: `new Outer.Inner()` — no outer instance needed.

#### C. Local Inner Class
- Declared **inside a method, constructor, or initializer block**.
- Scope is limited to the block in which it is defined.
- Can access effectively final local variables of the enclosing block.

#### D. Anonymous Inner Class
- A **declaration and instantiation in a single expression** — no class name.
- Used to override methods of a class or implement an interface **on the fly**.
- Common in event handling (e.g., GUI button click listeners).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compilation Rule:** The Java compiler generates separate `.class` files for inner classes using naming convention: `OuterClass$InnerClass.class` (e.g., `Outer$Inner.class`).

---

### 2.2 Inheritance Hierarchy & Super Keyword Mechanics

**Single Inheritance in Java:**
A class can extend **only one** parent class (Java does not support multiple class inheritance — achieved via interfaces instead).

**Constructor Chaining Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
When a subclass object is created, the chain of constructor calls flows from the **top-most super class down to the current class**. This chain is established by `super()` calls — explicitly or implicitly.

**Method Overriding Conditions (for `super.method()` to be meaningful):**
- Same method signature (name + parameter list).
- **IS-A** relationship (inheritance must exist).
- Return type must be same or a covariant subtype.
- Access modifier cannot be more restrictive.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Rule / Syntax** | **Access Level** |
|---|---|---|
| Member Inner Class | `class Outer { class Inner { } }` | Accesses all outer members (incl. private) |
| Static Nested Class | `static class Inner { }` | Only static outer members |
| Local Inner Class | Inside method/block | Effectively final locals + outer members |
| Anonymous Inner Class | `new Parent() { ... }` | Single-use override |
| `super.field` | Parent class field access | Resolves to nearest super class member |
| `super.method()` | Parent class method call | Bypasses overridden method |
| `super(args)` | Parent constructor call | Must be first line in constructor |
| Default `super()` | Implicit no-arg call | Inserted by compiler if no super call |
| Object Class | `java.lang.Object` | Root of all Java classes |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Inner Classes** are heavily used in **Android Development** (event listeners), **Swing GUI Programming** (adapter classes), and **Data Structures** (Iterator implementations inside collection classes).
- **Inheritance** models real-world taxonomies (e.g., `Vehicle → Car → ElectricCar`) and is fundamental to **polymorphism** in framework design (Spring, Hibernate, JavaFX).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: How a Member Inner Class is Compiled & Executed

**Step 1 — Source Code Declaration**

```java
class Outer {
    private int data = 10;
    class Inner {
        void display() {
            System.out.println("Data = " + data);
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Compilation by `javac`**
The Java compiler produces **two** `.class` files:
- `Outer.class`
- `Outer$Inner.class`

**Step 3 — Object Creation Rule (CRITICAL — KTU Exam Favorite)**

```java
// WRONG (compile-time error):
Outer.Inner obj = new Outer.Inner();

// CORRECT:
Outer outerObj = new Outer();
Outer.Inner obj = outerObj.new Inner();
```

**Why?** A Member Inner Class is logically tied to an instance of the Outer Class. The compiler enforces creation through an existing outer object so that the inner class can implicitly hold a reference (`Outer$Inner.this`) to it.

**Step 4 — Accessing Outer Members**
Inside the Inner class, the syntax `OuterClassName.this` refers to the enclosing outer instance. For example, `Outer.this.data` resolves to the specific outer's private field.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Constructor Chaining with `super()`

**Program Demonstrating Super Class Constructor Flow:**

```java
class A {   // Super Class
    A() { System.out.println("A constructor"); }
}
class B extends A {   // Sub Class of A
    B() {
        super();   // Calls A() — explicit but not mandatory
        System.out.println("B constructor");
    }
}
class C extends B {   // Sub Class of B
    C() {
        System.out.println("C constructor");
    }
}
```

**Execution when `new C()` is invoked:**

**Step 1:** `new C()` is called → JVM invokes `C()`.

**Step 2:** The first line of `C()` is missing an explicit `super()`, so the compiler **inserts an implicit `super()`** (which calls `B()`).

**Step 3:** The first line of `B()` is `super()`, which calls `A()`.

**Step 4:** `A()` executes — prints **"A constructor"**.

**Step 5:** Control returns to `B()` — prints **"B constructor"**.

**Step 6:** Control returns to `C()` — prints **"C constructor"**.

**Output Order:**
$$
\text{A constructor} \rightarrow \text{B constructor} \rightarrow \text{C constructor}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Rule:** If a constructor in a subclass calls `this(...)` (overloaded constructor of same class), then `super()` **cannot** also be called in the same constructor — because both must be the first statement, and only one can occupy that position.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Inner Class

| **Scenario** | **Recommended Inner Class** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Helper tightly bound to outer instance state | Member Inner Class | Needs direct access to non-static outer fields |
| Helper logically belongs but is independent | Static Nested Class | No need for outer instance — saves memory |
| One-time override of a method during method call | Anonymous Inner Class | Concise, single-expression implementation |
| Implementation needed only within a method | Local Inner Class | Scope-limited, improves readability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inner Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Outer Class] --> B[Member Inner Class]
    A --> C[Static Nested Class]
    A --> D[Local Inner Class]
    A --> E[Anonymous Inner Class]
    B --> B1[Non-static, tied to outer instance]
    C --> C1[Static, independent of outer instance]
    D --> D1[Defined inside method or block]
    E --> E1[Declared and instantiated in one line]
    B1 --> B2[Access: All outer members incl. private]
    C1 --> C2[Access: Only static outer members]
    D1 --> D3[Access: Effectively final local variables]
    E1 --> E3[Common use: Event listeners, callbacks]
```

### 4.2 Inheritance & Super Class Constructor Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    O[Object Class] --> A[Class A: Super Class]
    A --> B[Class B: extends A]
    B --> C[Class C: extends B]
    O -->|Implicit root| A
    A -->|super call| A1[A constructor executes]
    B -->|super call| B1[B constructor executes]
    C -->|implicit super call| C1[C constructor executes]
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: `super` Keyword Resolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    SP[super Keyword] --> S1[super.field]
    SP --> S2[super.method]
    SP --> S3[super args]
    S1 --> S1A[Resolves to parent class variable]
    S2 --> S2A[Invokes parent class method bypassing override]
    S3 --> S3A[Calls parent constructor must be first line]
    S1A --> R1[Used when child hides parent field]
    S2A --> R2[Used in overridden method to extend behavior]
    S3A --> R3[Used to invoke specific parent constructor]
```

### 4.4 Anonymous Inner Class Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST[Start: Interface or Class Reference] --> DC[Declare new with Parent type]
    DC --> OVR[Override methods inside curly braces]
    OVR --> INS[Instantiation occurs immediately]
    INS --> USE[Use object as parent type]
    USE --> END[End: No reusable class name generated]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define an **inner class** in Java. List any **two types** of inner classes.

**Model Answer:**
An **inner class** in Java is a class declared as a member of another class (the outer class). It logically groups helper classes that are used only by the outer class, thereby increasing encapsulation. Java supports four types: **(i) Member Inner Class**, **(ii) Static Nested Class**, **(iii) Local Inner Class**, and **(iv) Anonymous Inner Class**. *(Any two with one-line explanation — 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the role of the **`super`** keyword in Java? Mention its uses.

**Model Answer:**
The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable used to refer to the **immediate parent class object**. Its three uses are:
1. `super.variable` – accesses parent class fields hidden by child class fields.
2. `super.method()` – invokes the parent class version of an overridden method.
3. `super(args)` – calls a parent class constructor (must be the first statement in the subclass constructor). *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of inner classes in Java** with suitable code examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating **inheritance** where a subclass `Dog` inherits from a super class `Animal`. Show the use of the `super` keyword to call the parent class constructor and an overridden method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Member Inner Class:**
```java
class CPU {
    int cores = 8;
    class Processor {
        void show() {
            System.out.println("Cores: " + cores);
        }
    }
}
// Usage: CPU c = new CPU(); CPU.Processor p = c.new Processor();
```

**Static Nested Class:**
```java
class CPU {
    static class Cache {
        void info() { System.out.println("Cache 16MB"); }
    }
}
// Usage: CPU.Cache c = new CPU.Cache();
```

**Local Inner Class:**
```java
class Demo {
    void display() {
        class Local { void msg() { System.out.println("Hello"); } }
        Local l = new Local(); l.msg();
    }
}
```

**Anonymous Inner Class:**
```java
interface Greet { void say(); }
class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        Greet g = new Greet() {
            public void say() { System.out.println("Hi"); }
        };
        g.say();
    }
}
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

```java
class Animal {
    String name;
    Animal(String name) {           // Super class constructor
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Animal constructor called for: " + name);
    }
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    String breed;
    Dog(String name, String breed) {
        super(name);                // Calls parent constructor
        this.breed = breed;
    }
    @Override
    void sound() {
        super.sound();              // Calls parent method
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Breed: " + breed);
    }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog("Bruno", "Labrador");
        d.sound();
        d.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Animal constructor called for: Bruno
Animal makes a sound
Dog barks
Name: Bruno, Breed: Labrador
```

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss **constructor chaining** in Java inheritance with a neat diagram. Explain what happens if the super class has a parameterized constructor and the subclass does not explicitly call `super(args)`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Anonymous Inner Class** with a real-time example (e.g., button click event handling). Write the program and explain each line. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Constructor Chaining** is the mechanism by which constructors are invoked in a hierarchical order from the **top-most super class down to the current subclass**, ensuring proper initialization of inherited members. The `super()` call establishes this chain.

```java
class GrandParent {
    GrandParent() { System.out.println("GrandParent"); }
}
class Parent extends GrandParent {
    Parent() { System.out.println("Parent"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    Child() { System.out.println("Child"); }
}
// new Child() → GrandParent → Parent → Child
```

**If Super class has a parameterized constructor only** (e.g., `Animal(String name)`) and the subclass does not have a no-arg constructor, then the compiler **inserts an implicit `super()` call**, which **fails to compile** because no matching no-arg constructor exists in the parent. The fix: explicitly write `super(args)` in the subclass constructor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake #1 (3 marks lost in ESE):** Many students assume that Java always provides a default no-arg constructor in the parent class. **This is FALSE** the moment the parent defines *any* constructor explicitly. Students forget to call `super(args)` and the code fails to compile.

#### Model Solution for (b):

An **Anonymous Inner Class** is a nameless class declared and instantiated in a single statement. It is widely used in GUI event handling:

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AnonDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JButton btn = new JButton("Click Me");
        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {  // Anonymous class
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Button clicked!");
            }
        });
    }
}
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**
- `new ActionListener() { ... }` — Creates an unnamed class implementing the `ActionListener` interface.
- Inside braces, the `actionPerformed` method is overridden.
- This object is passed directly to `addActionListener()` — no separate class file generated for it.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (High-Frequency Deductions):**
> 1. **Instantiating Member Inner Class without outer object** — Compile-time error; students lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that `super()` must be the FIRST line** in a constructor — Compilation error.
> 3. **Confusing `this()` and `super()`** — `this()` calls same-class constructor; `super()` calls parent. Both cannot be in the same constructor.
> 4. **Anonymous Inner Class assigned multiple times** — Single-use only; cannot be reused across statements.
> 5. **Assuming Object class methods are overridden automatically** — `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()` must be explicitly overridden.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inner Class** is a class declared inside another class; gives better encapsulation and access to outer class private members.
- **Four types:** Member Inner, Static Nested, Local Inner, Anonymous Inner.
- **Member Inner Class** requires outer instance for creation: `outerObj.new Inner()`.
- **Static Nested Class** does NOT need outer instance: `new Outer.Inner()`.
- **Anonymous Inner Class** is a one-time, nameless class — common in event listeners.
- **Inheritance** uses `extends` keyword; Java supports only **single inheritance** for classes.
- **`super` keyword** has three uses: field, method, constructor access — `super()` must be the first line.
- **Constructor chaining** flows from top-most super class to current subclass.
- If parent has only parameterized constructor, subclass **must** explicitly call `super(args)`.
- **Default super class** in Java is `java.lang.Object`.
- **Method overriding** + `super.method()` allows extending (not replacing) parent behavior.
- **Compilation output:** Inner classes generate separate `.class` files named `Outer$Inner.class`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sub Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Sub Class — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub Class (Synonyms: Derived Class, Child Class, Extended Class)**
> A **subclass** is a class that inherits attributes (fields) and behaviors (methods) from another class, which is termed the **superclass** (parent/base class). In the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, a subclass uses the `extends` keyword (Java) or `:` / `public class Derived : public Base` (C++) to establish an **"is-a"** generalization-specialization relationship with its superclass.

$$
\text{Subclass} \xrightarrow{\text{extends (inheritance)}} \text{Superclass}
$$

The subclass *specializes* the superclass by:
1. **Inheriting** all non-private members of the superclass.
2. **Adding** new fields and methods unique to itself.
3. **Overriding** existing methods to provide specialized behavior (Runtime Polymorphism).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

Imagine a **Vehicle** (superclass). Every vehicle has wheels, an engine, and a horn. Now consider a **Car** (subclass) — it *is a* vehicle, but it adds 4 doors, AC, and a gear system. A **Bike** is also a vehicle, but it has 2 wheels instead of 4.

You do not want to re-describe "wheels" and "engine" inside every Car or Bike definition. Instead, you describe them **once** in Vehicle, and let Car and Bike **inherit** them. That is exactly what a subclass does in Java.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:**
> - **Superclass** = General blueprint (e.g., *Employee*)
> - **Subclass** = Specific category (e.g., *Manager*, *Engineer*, *Intern*)
> - All Managers are Employees → `class Manager extends Employee`

## 1.3 Hierarchical Context in Module 2

Subclassing is the **operational engine** behind three of the four pillars of OOP studied in this module:

| Pillar | Role of Subclass |
|---|---|
| **Inheritance** | Subclass *receives* superclass structure. |
| **Polymorphism** | Subclass *overrides* superclass methods. |
| **Abstraction** | Subclass *implements* abstract methods of an abstract superclass. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A subclass **cannot** inherit `private` members directly. They are accessible only through public/protected getters and setters. This is a frequent **2-mark question** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Subclass

A subclass declaration in Java follows a strict syntactic contract:

```java
[access_modifier] class SubClassName extends SuperClassName {
    // Inherited members (accessible as if declared here)
    // New fields
    // New methods
    // Overridden methods
}
```

### 2.1.1 What is Inherited? (Bullet Logic)

- ✅ **Public members** of the superclass → fully accessible.
- ✅ **Protected members** of the superclass → accessible (within same package or via inheritance).
- ✅ **Package-private (default) members** → accessible only if subclass is in the **same package**.
- ✅ **Static members** → inherited but not overridden (they are hidden, not polymorphic).
- ❌ **Private members** → NOT directly accessible (memory is allocated, but access is blocked).
- ❌ **Constructors** → NOT inherited. Subclass *must* define its own, but can chain via `super(...)`.

## 2.2 The `super` Keyword — KTU High-Yield Concept

The `super` keyword is a reference variable that points to the **immediate parent class object**. It has **three primary uses**:

| Use | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Call superclass constructor | `super(args)` | Must be the **first statement** in subclass constructor. |
| Access superclass field | `super.fieldName` | Resolves name-shadowing between subclass and superclass. |
| Call superclass method | `super.methodName()` | Invoke the overridden parent version from inside the override. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If a subclass constructor does **not** explicitly call `super(...)`, the compiler **automatically inserts** a no-argument `super()` call. If the parent class lacks a no-arg constructor, this is a **compilation error**.

## 2.3 Method Overriding — The Heart of Subclass Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method Overriding** = Redefining an inherited method in the subclass to provide a specialized implementation. Resolved at **runtime** via Dynamic Method Dispatch (upcasting + virtual dispatch).

### 2.3.1 Rules of Valid Overriding (KTU Board Favorite)

| Rule | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Same method signature | Name + parameter list must be **identical**. |
| Same or covariant return type | Return type can be a **subtype** of the original (Java 5+). |
| Access level cannot be more restrictive | e.g., `protected` → `private` is **illegal**. |
| Cannot override `final` methods | `final` blocks overriding at compile time. |
| Cannot override `static` methods | They are *hidden*, not overridden. |
| Instance methods only | Static/class methods follow different rules. |
| `throws` clause restriction | Overriding method cannot throw **broader** checked exceptions. |

## 2.4 Types of Inheritance Involving Subclasses

$$
\text{Types} = \begin{cases} \text{Single Inheritance} \\ \text{Multilevel Inheritance} \\ \text{Hierarchical Inheritance} \\ \text{Multiple Inheritance (via interfaces only in Java)} \\ \text{Hybrid Inheritance (via interfaces)} \end{cases}
$$

### 2.4.1 KTU Formula / Rule Sheet

| Concept | Rule | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Subclass declaration | `class Sub extends Super` | 2-mark direct |
| Constructor chaining | First line must be `super(...)` or `this(...)` | 3-mark |
| Overriding signature | `name + parameter types` must match exactly | 2-mark |
| Access modifier rule | Cannot *reduce* visibility in subclass | 2-mark |
| `final` class | Cannot be subclassed (e.g., `String`, `Math`) | 2-mark |
| Abstract superclass | Subclass *must* override all abstract methods or declare itself abstract | 3-mark |
| Diamond problem | Resolved in Java by disallowing multiple class inheritance | 3-mark |
| Object class | **Every class in Java implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`** | 2-mark |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Subclassing powers **virtually every production framework**:
- **Java Collections:** `ArrayList` extends `AbstractList` extends `AbstractCollection`.
- **JavaFX/Swing:** UI components form deep subclass hierarchies (e.g., `JButton` → `AbstractButton` → `JComponent` → `Container` → `Component` → `Object`).
- **JDBC:** Driver implementations extend abstract `Driver` class.
- **Design Patterns:** Template Method, Strategy, Factory all rely on subclass polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Constructor Chaining in Multilevel Inheritance

We will trace the **execution order of constructors** when a subclass object is instantiated. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation question**.

### 3.1.1 Source Code

```java
class A {
    A() {
        System.out.println("A's constructor");
    }
}

class B extends A {
    B() {
        System.out.println("B's constructor");
    }
}

class C extends B {
    C() {
        System.out.println("C's constructor");
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        C obj = new C();
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1:** `main()` invokes `new C()`. The JVM allocates memory for object `obj` of class `C`.

**Step 2:** The compiler inserts `super()` as the **first statement** of `C()`'s constructor (since none is written).

**Step 3:** Class `B`'s constructor `B()` is invoked. The compiler again inserts `super()` at its top.

**Step 4:** Class `A`'s constructor `A()` is invoked. `A` extends `Object` implicitly, but `Object()` is automatically handled by the JVM.

**Step 5:** Execution now unwinds back through the chain, executing the *bodies* of each constructor in **top-down (parent → child)** order.

$$
\text{Output} = \begin{aligned} &\text{A's constructor} \\ &\text{B's constructor} \\ &\text{C's constructor} \end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Generalization

For an inheritance chain of depth $n$:

$$
\text{Execution Order} = \left\{ \text{Super}_1.\text{ctor}, \ \text{Super}_2.\text{ctor}, \ \ldots, \ \text{Super}_{n-1}.\text{ctor}, \ \text{Sub}_n.\text{ctor} \right\}
$$

Where $\text{Super}_1$ is the **root-most ancestor** (typically `Object`) and $\text{Sub}_n$ is the **leaf subclass** being instantiated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Constructors execute **from root to leaf**. Methods, however, dispatch **from leaf to root** (overriding priority).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Access Modifiers Across Packages

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (Subclass) | Same Package (Non-Subclass) | Different Package (Subclass) | Different Package (Non-Subclass) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `default` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Type:** "Construct a class hierarchy showing access to private, default, protected, and public members and explain with a program."

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Method Overriding with Dynamic Dispatch

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle");
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a square");
    }
}

public class PolymorphismDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape ref;          // Superclass reference variable
        ref = new Circle(); // Upcasting
        ref.draw();         // Output: Drawing a circle

        ref = new Square(); // Upcasting again
        ref.draw();         // Output: Drawing a square
    }
}
```

### 3.3.1 Execution Trace

**Step 1:** `Shape ref` declares a reference of type `Shape` (compile-time type).

**Step 2:** `ref = new Circle()` performs **upcasting** — implicit, always safe since `Circle IS-A Shape`.

**Step 3:** `ref.draw()` is invoked. Compiler checks: *Does `Shape` have a `draw()` method?* → Yes.

**Step 4:** At **runtime**, JVM checks: *What is the actual object type pointed to by `ref`?* → `Circle`. The Circle's overridden `draw()` is dispatched.

**Step 5:** This **dynamic method dispatch** is the mechanism enabling runtime polymorphism.

$$
\text{Compile-time check} : \text{Superclass defines method?} \rightarrow \text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Compile OK}
$$

$$
\text{Runtime dispatch} : \text{Actual object type} \rightarrow \text{Call overridden version}
$$

## 3.4 The `final` Keyword Matrix (Subclass Interaction)

| Context | Effect on Subclass | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| `final class A` | A **cannot** be extended (subclassing disallowed) | 2-mark |
| `final void method()` | Cannot be overridden in subclass | 3-mark |
| `final variable` | Becomes a constant; cannot be reassigned | 1-mark |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse `final` with `finally` and `finalize`. These are **three entirely different constructs** in Java. The KTU board frequently asks: *"Differentiate between final, finally, and finalize"* (2 marks).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Object Class java.lang.Object"] --> A1["Superclass Shape"]
    A1 --> A2["Subclass Circle"]
    A1 --> A3["Subclass Square"]
    A1 --> A4["Subclass Triangle"]
    A2 --> A5["Subclass ColoredCircle"]
```

## 4.2 Subclass Object Construction Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["main calls new SubClass"] --> stepB["JVM allocates memory for SubClass + all superclasses"]
    stepB --> stepC["Implicit super call inserted at top of SubClass constructor"]
    stepC --> stepD["Control transfers to SuperClass constructor"]
    stepD --> stepE["SuperClass body executes top to bottom"]
    stepE --> stepF["Control returns to SubClass constructor"]
    stepF --> stepG["SubClass body executes top to bottom"]
    stepG --> stepH["Object reference returned to main"]
```

## 4.3 Method Dispatch Flow (Runtime Polymorphism)

```mermaid
graph TD
    m1["Compile Time: Check Superclass has method?"] --> m2{"Method found?"}
    m2 -->|"No"| m3["Compile Error"]
    m2 -->|"Yes"| m4["Runtime: Identify actual object type"]
    m4 --> m5{"Object is Subclass with override?"}
    m5 -->|"Yes"| m6["Execute Subclass overridden method"]
    m5 -->|"No"| m7["Execute Superclass method"]
```

## 4.4 Access Modifier Visibility Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    B0["Class A with member x"] --> B1["private x"]
    B0 --> B2["default x"]
    B0 --> B3["protected x"]
    B0 --> B4["public x"]
    B1 --> R1["Only same class"]
    B2 --> R2["Same package"]
    B3 --> R3["Same package OR subclass anywhere"]
    B4 --> R4["Everywhere"]
```

## 4.5 Multilevel Inheritance Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["Employee Superclass"] --> L2["Manager Subclass"]
    L2 --> L3["SeniorManager Subclass"]
    L3 --> L4["Director Subclass"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping for 4.5:**
> - `Employee` → base attributes: `name`, `empId`, `salary`
> - `Manager` → adds: `teamSize`, `department`
> - `SeniorManager` → adds: `budget`, `strategicGoals`
> - `Director` → adds: `boardReporting`, `profitLossAuthority`

This mirrors how **enterprise HR systems** model organizational hierarchies using OOP subclassing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define a subclass. How is it declared in Java?**

**Model Answer:**

A **subclass** (also called a *derived class* or *child class*) is a class that inherits properties and behaviors from another class, known as the **superclass**. It enables code reuse and establishes an "is-a" relationship.

In Java, a subclass is declared using the `extends` keyword:

```java
class SuperClass {
    // parent members
}

class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    // inherited members + new members
}
```

The subclass automatically receives all accessible members of the superclass and may add new fields, methods, or override existing ones.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is method overriding? State any two rules of overriding.**

**Model Answer:**

**Method overriding** is the mechanism by which a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass. It is essential for achieving **runtime polymorphism**.

**Two Rules:**
1. The method in the subclass must have the **same name and parameter list** as in the superclass.
2. The **access modifier** in the overriding method cannot be more restrictive than the one in the superclass (e.g., overriding a `public` method with a `private` method is illegal).

---

## Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of inheritance in Java with suitable example. Discuss how a subclass can access members of its superclass using the `super` keyword. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **multilevel inheritance** with constructor chaining. Show the output and explain the order of execution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Inheritance is an OOP mechanism where one class (**subclass**) acquires the fields and methods of another class (**superclass**). It promotes **code reuse** and models the "is-a" relationship.

```java
class Animal {
    String name;
    void eat() {
        System.out.println(name + " is eating.");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    void bark() {
        System.out.println(name + " is barking.");
    }
}

public class InheritanceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.name = "Bruno";
        d.eat();   // inherited method
        d.bark();  // subclass-specific method
    }
}
```

**The `super` keyword** has three uses:

1. **`super.fieldName`** → Access hidden superclass fields.

```java
class Animal {
    String name = "Generic Animal";
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Dog";
    void printNames() {
        System.out.println(super.name); // Generic Animal
        System.out.println(name);       // Dog
    }
}
```

2. **`super.methodName()`** → Invoke overridden superclass method.

3. **`super(arguments)`** → Call superclass constructor (must be first line).

#### Model Solution for (b):

```java
class GrandParent {
    GrandParent() {
        System.out.println("GrandParent constructor");
    }
}

class Parent extends GrandParent {
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent constructor");
    }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}

public class MultiLevelDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
GrandParent constructor
Parent constructor
Child constructor
```

**Explanation:**
When `new Child()` is executed, the JVM chains constructor calls from the **root-most ancestor to the leaf subclass**. The compiler implicitly inserts `super()` as the first statement of every constructor. Therefore, control flows: `GrandParent → Parent → Child` for execution, but the constructor *bodies* execute in this same **top-down** order.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q: (a)** Discuss the different types of inheritance supported in Java with diagrams. Why does Java not support multiple inheritance using classes? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **dynamic method dispatch** with a Java program demonstrating runtime polymorphism using a superclass reference variable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Java supports the following types of inheritance:

| Type | Description | Java Support |
|---|---|---|
| **Single** | One subclass extends one superclass | ✅ Yes |
| **Multilevel** | Chain: A → B → C | ✅ Yes |
| **Hierarchical** | Multiple subclasses from one superclass | ✅ Yes |
| **Multiple** | One subclass extends multiple superclasses | ❌ No (only via interfaces) |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more types | ❌ No (via interfaces only) |

**Diagram for Hierarchical Inheritance:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1["Vehicle"] --> N2["Car"]
    N1 --> N3["Bike"]
    N1 --> N4["Truck"]
```

**Why Java disallows multiple class inheritance:**

If a class extends two classes that both define the same method, the compiler cannot determine which version to inherit. This is called the **Diamond Problem**:

```mermaid
graph TD
    D1["Class A method show"] --> D2["Class B"]
    D1 --> D3["Class C"]
    D2 --> D4["Class D ambiguous"]
    D3 --> D4
```

To avoid this ambiguity, Java restricts multiple inheritance to **interfaces**, which only declare method signatures (no state, no method bodies in pre-Java 8 interfaces).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Dynamic Method Dispatch** is the mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at **runtime** rather than compile time, based on the actual object type rather than the reference type.

```java
class Payment {
    void process() {
        System.out.println("Processing generic payment");
    }
}

class CreditCardPayment extends Payment {
    @Override
    void process() {
        System.out.println("Processing credit card payment");
    }
}

class UPIPayment extends Payment {
    @Override
    void process() {
        System.out.println("Processing UPI payment");
    }
}

public class DispatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Payment ref;  // superclass reference

        ref = new CreditCardPayment();
        ref.process();  // CreditCardPayment version runs

        ref = new UPIPayment();
        ref.process();  // UPIPayment version runs
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Processing credit card payment
Processing UPI payment
```

**Explanation:** The reference `ref` is of type `Payment` (compile-time), but at runtime, the JVM dispatches the call to the **actual object's** overridden version. This enables a single interface to control diverse behaviors — a cornerstone of extensible system design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `super()` insertion rule:** If the superclass has only a parameterized constructor, the subclass *must* explicitly call `super(args)`. Omitting this is a compile-time error. Students lose **2–3 marks** here frequently.
>
> 2. **Confusing overriding with overloading:** Overriding = same signature in subclass. Overloading = same name, different parameters within the same class. Mixing these in a definition question costs full marks.
>
> 3. **Assuming `private` members are inherited:** They are *not directly accessible*. Memory is allocated, but visibility is blocked. Always mention this distinction.
>
> 4. **Writing `static` methods as overridden:** Static methods are *hidden*, not overridden. They follow compile-time binding. KTU explicitly tests this with `Base.display()` vs `Derived.display()` questions.
>
> 5. **Diamond problem answer:** Always include a diagram. Examiners award bonus clarity marks for visual representations.
>
> 6. **Missing `@Override` annotation:** While not mandatory in Java, KTU evaluators *expect* it as a documentation best practice in your code listings.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Subclass** = class declared with `extends` keyword; inherits from exactly **one** superclass (Java).
- 🎯 **`Object`** class is the implicit superclass of *every* Java class.
- 🎯 **Constructors** are **not inherited**, but are chained via `super()`.
- 🎯 **`super()` must be the first statement** of a subclass constructor.
- 🎯 **`private` members** are allocated but not directly accessible in the subclass.
- 🎯 **Method overriding** requires: same signature, same/covariant return type, no reduction in access level.
- 🎯 **`final` classes** cannot be subclassed (e.g., `String`).
- 🎯 **`final` methods** cannot be overridden.
- 🎯 **Static methods** are hidden, not overridden.
- 🎯 **Dynamic method dispatch** = runtime resolution of overridden calls via actual object type.
- 🎯 **Java disallows multiple class inheritance** due to the diamond problem; uses interfaces instead.
- 🎯 **Abstract superclasses** require subclasses to implement all abstract methods or declare themselves abstract.
- 🎯 **Multilevel inheritance** chains constructor execution from root to leaf.
- 🎯 **Polymorphism** is only possible through inheritance + method overriding + superclass reference upcasting.
- 🎯 **Access hierarchy**: `private < default < protected < public`.
- 🎯 **`protected`** = accessible in same package + subclasses in any package.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Keyword super
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Keyword super

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: The Keyword `super`

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, the keyword `super` is a **reference variable** that is used to refer to the **immediate parent (superclass) object** of the current class instance. It is an implicit reference automatically provided by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) to every subclass object, in direct parallel with the `this` reference (which points to the current object).

The `super` keyword serves three principal syntactic functions within a Java program:

1. **Accessing Parent Class Instance Variables** — When a subclass declares a data member with the same name as one in its superclass, `super` is used to disambiguate and access the hidden parent variable.
2. **Invoking Parent Class Methods** — When a subclass overrides an inherited method, `super` is used to call the overridden version present in the superclass (supporting *dynamic method dispatch* resolution).
3. **Invoking Parent Class Constructors** — `super()` is used to explicitly chain a call to a constructor of the parent class, ensuring proper initialization of the inherited state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The `super` keyword is a direct application of the Object-Oriented Programming pillar of **Inheritance**. It provides the *backward linkage* that enables **method overriding**, **constructor chaining**, and **variable hiding resolution** in a class hierarchy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for Students

Imagine you are a child living in a house built by your parents. You inherit the family house. Now, consider three situations:

- **The Name Collision (Variable Hiding):** Your father named the dining table "The Oak Table." When you, the son, later buy your own dining table and call it "The Oak Table" as well, there is a clash. To refer to *your father's* specific table, you must say, "I want to point to **super**'s Oak Table." In Java, `super.diningTable` refers to the parent's variable, not yours.

- **The Overridden Recipe (Method Overriding):** Your mother had an original recipe for biryani. You learnt it from her, but later modified it to make a Hyderabadi version (the *overridden* method). However, when your mother visits and wants to taste the *original* family recipe, you can explicitly invoke the parent's version using `super.makeBiryani()`.

- **The Foundation Laying (Constructor Chaining):** Before you can build your floor of the house, your father had to lay the foundation. In Java, before the subclass constructor body executes, the parent's constructor must run first to initialize the inherited members. `super(...)` is the mechanism that calls the parent's constructor.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Java Facts for `super`:**
> - The default root of all Java classes is **`java.lang.Object`**.
> - Every class in Java implicitly extends `Object` (unless it explicitly extends another class).
> - The `super` reference is **final-like in nature**: you cannot reassign it, e.g., `super = new Parent();` produces a compile-time error.
> - `super` is **not static**; it is bound at runtime to the actual parent object of the current instance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Categories of `super`

### Category A: `super` to Access Parent Class Instance Variables

When a subclass declares a variable with the **same identifier** as one in its superclass, the subclass variable **hides** the inherited variable. This is known as **variable shadowing** (or *data hiding*). To access the parent's variable, the syntax is:

```
super.variableName;
```

**Operational Logic:**
- The compiler generates a hidden field reference in the subclass object layout for every inherited field.
- When a name collision occurs, the local (subclass) scope wins by default.
- The `super` prefix forces the compiler to resolve the name in the **enclosing parent class's scope** instead of the local one.

### Category B: `super` to Invoke Parent Class Methods

When a subclass provides a method with the exact same signature as a method in the superclass, the parent's method is said to be **overridden**. By default, calls inside the subclass resolve to the overriding version. To invoke the parent's overridden method:

```
super.methodName(argumentList);
```

**Operational Logic — Why this matters:**
- This is the foundation for **runtime polymorphism** and **extending (not replacing)** inherited behavior.
- A common design pattern is for the subclass's overriding method to first call `super.method()` and then add subclass-specific logic (the *Template Method* pattern).

### Category C: `super()` to Invoke Parent Class Constructors

A subclass constructor must chain to a parent class constructor using the call:

```
super(argumentList);   // or super(); for no-arg
```

**Operational Rules:**
- `super(...)` must be the **first executable statement** inside a constructor body. It cannot appear anywhere else in the constructor.
- If the programmer does not write `super(...)` explicitly, the compiler **automatically inserts** a call to the **no-argument constructor** of the parent class.
- If the parent class has **no default (no-arg) constructor** and the programmer fails to call a parameterized `super(...)`, a **compile-time error** is thrown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructor Chaining Order:** When a subclass object is instantiated, the chain of constructors is invoked **from the topmost (Object) down to the actual subclass**. Execution, however, proceeds **top-down in the call, but the constructor bodies execute from parent to child** (i.e., Object → Parent → Child).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax | Where Used | Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access parent variable | `super.varName` | Inside non-static methods/constructors of subclass | Only when variable is hidden by subclass |
| Invoke parent method | `super.method(args)` | Inside non-static methods of subclass | Only when method is overridden |
| Invoke parent constructor | `super(args)` or `super()` | As the first line of a subclass constructor | Mandatory if parent has no default constructor |
| `super` is implicit? | Automatic for `super()` (no-arg) | When programmer omits explicit call | JVM inserts `super();` if no other `this()`/`super()` is present |
| `super` vs `this` | `super` → parent ref; `this` → current ref | Both only in non-static contexts | Cannot be reassigned; both are final references |
| Root class | `java.lang.Object` | Implicit parent of all Java classes | `super` ultimately chains to `Object()` |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Framework Design:** Frameworks like Spring and JavaFX rely heavily on `super` to allow user-defined subclasses to invoke framework-defined lifecycle methods (`init()`, `start()`, `dispose()`).
- **GUI Programming:** Java Swing/AWT classes use the Template Method pattern, where the JRE calls `super.paintComponent(g)` first to render the base, and then the subclass adds custom drawing.
- **Enterprise APIs:** The Java Servlet API requires `super.init(config);` to be invoked in overridden servlet `init()` methods to preserve framework bookkeeping.
- **Plugin Architectures:** Plugin systems use `super` to ensure base initialization happens before plugin-specific logic, preventing inconsistent system states.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown

## 3.1 Exhaustive Class Hierarchy Walkthrough

Let us analyze the following Java code construct step-by-step to demonstrate how `super` operates in each of its three roles.

**Reference Code Block:**

```java
class Animal {
    String name = "Generic Animal";
    
    Animal() {
        System.out.println("Animal constructor called");
    }
    
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Dog";
    
    Dog() {
        super();          // Calls Animal()
        System.out.println("Dog constructor called");
    }
    
    void sound() {
        super.sound();    // Calls Animal's sound()
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
    
    void displayNames() {
        System.out.println("Child name  : " + name);
        System.out.println("Parent name : " + super.name);
    }
}

public class TestSuper {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.sound();
        d.displayNames();
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Analytical Trace — Constructor Chain Execution

**Step 1: Program Entry**
The JVM invokes `main(String[] args)` and locates the `TestSuper` class file. The main method is loaded into the **stack frame**.

**Step 2: Object Instantiation**
The statement `Dog d = new Dog();` is executed.

**Step 3: Dog() Constructor Entry**
Control enters the `Dog()` constructor. The first executable statement is `super();`.

**Step 4: Implicit super() Resolved**
The `super();` call resolves to `Animal()` (the no-argument constructor of the immediate parent). Control transfers up the class hierarchy.

**Step 5: Animal() Execution**
Inside `Animal()`, the statement `System.out.println("Animal constructor called");` is executed. Output so far: **"Animal constructor called"**.

**Step 6: Return to Dog()**
Control returns to the line immediately following `super();` in `Dog()`. The next statement executes: `System.out.println("Dog constructor called");`. Output so far: **"Dog constructor called"**.

**Step 7: Dog() Returns**
The `Dog` object `d` is now fully initialized and assigned to the reference variable `d`.

**Step 8: Method Call d.sound()**
The `sound()` method is dispatched **dynamically** based on the actual object type (`Dog`).

**Step 9: Dog's sound() Execution Begins**
Inside `Dog.sound()`, the first line is `super.sound();`. This explicitly resolves to `Animal.sound()` (not recursive `Dog.sound()`).

**Step 10: Animal.sound() Execution**
`System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");` is printed. Output: **"Animal makes a sound"**.

**Step 11: Return to Dog.sound()**
The next line prints `System.out.println("Dog barks");`. Output: **"Dog barks"**.

**Step 12: Method Call d.displayNames()**
Control enters `Dog.displayNames()`.

**Step 13: Variable Resolution**
The expression `name` resolves to the **local (subclass) field** → `"Dog"`. The expression `super.name` resolves to the **parent's field** → `"Generic Animal"`. Output:
- **"Child name  : Dog"**
- **"Parent name : Generic Animal"**

## 3.3 Mathematical-Style Logical Notation of Resolution

Let us formalize the variable and method resolution rules using symbolic notation.

Let $C$ be the current class, $P$ be the immediate parent of $C$, and $x$ be an identifier (variable or method). The resolution function $\text{Resolve}(C, x)$ is defined as:

$$
\text{Resolve}(C, x) = \begin{cases}
x \in \text{Members}(C) & \rightarrow \text{Bind to } C \text{ (local scope)} \\
x \notin \text{Members}(C) \land x \in \text{Members}(P) & \rightarrow \text{Bind to } P \text{ (inherited)} \\
\text{otherwise} & \rightarrow \text{Compile-time error}
\end{cases}
$$

When the prefix `super.` is used, the resolution function is forced to begin its search at $P$:

$$
\text{Resolve}_{\text{super}}(C, x) = \text{Resolve}(P, x)
$$

For **dynamic method dispatch** (run-time polymorphism), let $o$ be the actual object instance at runtime. The effective method invoked is:

$$
\text{Invoke}(o.\text{methodName}) = \text{method defined in } \text{ActualClass}(o)
$$

When `super.methodName()` is explicitly written, polymorphism is **suspended** for that specific call, and the parent's static binding is used:

$$
\text{Invoke}_{\text{super}}(\text{methodName}) = \text{method defined in } P \text{ (statically bound)}
$$

## 3.4 Constructor Chaining Depth Analysis

For a hierarchy of depth $n$ — say $C_0 \rightarrow C_1 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow C_n$, where $C_0 = \text{Object}$ — instantiating $C_n$ produces a call sequence:

$$
\text{Object}() \rightarrow C_1() \rightarrow C_2() \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow C_n()
$$

The execution of constructor bodies, however, occurs in **top-down order** (the parent's body completes before the child's body begins). This guarantees that no subclass constructor accesses an uninitialized inherited field.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block illustrates how `super` traverses the inheritance chain and how the three usages map to their respective targets in the parent class.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Object Class] --> A2[Animal Class]
    A2 --> A3[Dog Class]

    A2 -- "name = 'Generic Animal'" --> V1[Parent Variable]
    A2 -- "sound()" --> M1[Parent Method]
    A2 -- "Animal()" --> C1[Parent Constructor]

    A3 -- "name = 'Dog'" --> V2[Child Variable]
    A3 -- "sound() override" --> M2[Child Method]
    A3 -- "Dog()" --> C2[Child Constructor]

    S1[super.name] -- "resolves to" --> V1
    S2[super.sound] -- "resolves to" --> M1
    S3[super] -- "resolves to" --> C1

    V2 -. "hides" .- V1
    M2 -. "overrides" .- M1
    C2 -- "first statement: super()" --> C1
```

## 4.2 Execution Flow of Constructor Chaining

This diagram depicts the **call sequence** (left-to-right arrows) versus the **body execution order** (top-to-bottom in the right column) when `new Dog()` is invoked.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as TestSuper.main
    participant Dog as Dog()
    participant Animal as Animal()
    participant Object as Object()

    Main->>Dog: new Dog()
    activate Dog
    Dog->>Animal: super()
    activate Animal
    Animal->>Object: super() [implicit]
    activate Object
    Object-->>Animal: Object body executed
    deactivate Object
    Animal-->>Dog: Animal body executed
    deactivate Animal
    Dog-->>Main: Dog body executed
    deactivate Dog
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout of a Subclass Object (Conceptual)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the in-memory object layout of a `Dog` instance, showing how the inherited `Animal` fields coexist with the child class's own fields.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Dog_Object [Dog Object on Heap]
        direction TB
        F1["Inherited Field: name = 'Generic Animal' (from Animal)"]
        F2["Child Field: name = 'Dog' (declared in Dog)"]
        F3["Inherited Method Pointer: Animal.sound()"]
        F4["Overridden Method Pointer: Dog.sound()"]
        F5["Implicit super reference: super -> parent object view"]
    end

    F5 -. "super.name points to" .-> F1
    F5 -. "super.sound() invokes" .-> F3
    F2 -- "name (without prefix) resolves to" --> F2
    F4 -- "sound() (without prefix) resolves to" --> F4
```

## 4.4 Variable Resolution Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Identifier accessed: x] --> Q1{Is x declared in current class?}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> R1[Bind to current class member]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{Is prefix super used?}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> R2[Search begins in immediate parent]
    Q2 -- "No" --> R3[Search proceeds up inheritance chain]
    R2 --> R4{Found in parent?}
    R3 --> R4
    R4 -- "Yes" --> R5[Bind to that ancestor's member]
    R4 -- "No" --> E1[Compile-time error: cannot find symbol]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What is the purpose of the `super` keyword in Java? List any two of its uses.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `super` keyword in Java is an implicit reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent (superclass) object** of the current instance. It is used to access members of the superclass that have been hidden or overridden by the subclass.

**Two primary uses (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Accessing hidden parent variables:** When the subclass declares a data member with the same name as that in the superclass, `super.variableName` is used to refer to the parent's variable.
2. **Invoking parent constructors:** `super(arguments)` is used as the first statement in a subclass constructor to invoke a specific constructor of the parent class, ensuring the inherited portion of the object is properly initialized.

*(A third valid use — invoking overridden parent methods via `super.methodName()` — may also be credited for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Why must `super()` be the first statement in a constructor? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The call `super()` must be the first statement in a constructor because the Java Language Specification mandates that the inherited portion of an object must be **fully initialized before the subclass-specific initialization logic runs**. This guarantees that no subclass code can access an uninitialized inherited field, preserving object consistency.

**If `super()` is omitted (1 mark):**
The Java compiler **automatically inserts** a no-argument call to the parent's default constructor. However, if the parent class has **no default (no-arg) constructor**, omitting the explicit `super(arguments)` call results in a **compile-time error**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) follows a strict internal choice: students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (both 14 marks). Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the three uses of the `super` keyword in Java with suitable code examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** What happens during constructor chaining when a subclass object is created? Illustrate with a multi-level inheritance example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The `super` keyword in Java has three primary uses:

**Use 1: Accessing parent class instance variables (≈ 2.5 marks)**

When the subclass hides a parent variable:

```java
class Vehicle {
    int speed = 60;
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int speed = 120;
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Car speed: " + speed);
        System.out.println("Vehicle speed: " + super.speed);
    }
}
```

Here, `super.speed` resolves to the `Vehicle` class field (`60`), while `speed` alone resolves to the `Car` field (`120`).

**Use 2: Invoking overridden parent class methods (≈ 2.5 marks)**

```java
class Parent {
    void show() { System.out.println("Parent show"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    void show() {
        super.show();
        System.out.println("Child show");
    }
}
```

When `new Child().show()` is called, the output is:
```
Parent show
Child show
```

**Use 3: Invoking parent class constructors (≈ 2 marks)**

```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    Employee(String n) { name = n; }
}
class Manager extends Employee {
    double bonus;
    Manager(String n, double b) {
        super(n);          // calls Employee(String)
        bonus = b;
    }
}
```

Without `super(n)`, a compile-time error would occur because `Employee` has no default constructor.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Constructor Chaining Explanation (≈ 3.5 marks):**

Constructor chaining is the process by which a call to a subclass constructor automatically triggers a chain of constructor calls up the inheritance hierarchy, ensuring every ancestor's initialization logic runs before the subclass body executes.

**Multi-level Example (≈ 3.5 marks):**

```java
class Grandparent {
    Grandparent() { System.out.println("Grandparent"); }
}
class Parent2 extends Grandparent {
    Parent2() { System.out.println("Parent"); }
}
class Child2 extends Parent2 {
    Child2() { System.out.println("Child"); }
}
// Execution: new Child2();
// Output:
// Grandparent
// Parent
// Child
```

The call sequence is `Child2() → Parent2() → Grandparent() → Object()`, but the bodies execute top-down. This is automatic when no explicit `super()` is written — the compiler inserts `super()` at the top of each constructor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords in Java. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Write a Java program demonstrating method overriding and use `super` to call the parent's overridden method. Explain the output. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Aspect | `this` | `super` |
|---|---|---|
| Refers to | Current class object | Immediate parent class object |
| Variable access | `this.var` accesses current class variable | `super.var` accesses parent class variable |
| Method call | `this.method()` calls current class method (rarely used) | `super.method()` calls overridden parent method |
| Constructor call | `this(args)` calls another constructor of the same class | `super(args)` calls a parent class constructor |
| Implicit default | Compiler inserts `this` reference automatically | Compiler inserts `super()` (no-arg) if not written |
| Position of constructor call | First statement in constructor | First statement in constructor |
| Static context | Cannot be used in static context | Cannot be used in static context |
| Final reference? | Yes, `this` is final (cannot be reassigned) | Yes, `super` is final (cannot be reassigned) |
| Inheritance relation | No relation to inheritance | Direct relation to inheritance hierarchy |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Program (≈ 4 marks):**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() {
        super.draw();
        System.out.println("Drawing a circle");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s = new Circle();
        s.draw();
    }
}
```

**Output and Explanation (≈ 3 marks):**

```
Drawing a generic shape
Drawing a circle
```

**Explanation:**
- `s.draw()` invokes `Circle.draw()` due to **dynamic method dispatch** (the actual object type is `Circle`).
- The first line `super.draw();` inside `Circle.draw()` explicitly calls the **parent's overridden version** `Shape.draw()`, which prints `"Drawing a generic shape"`.
- Control then returns to `Circle.draw()` and prints `"Drawing a circle"`.
- This pattern is widely used in GUI frameworks and is the foundation of the *Template Method* design pattern.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in `super` questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting that `super()` must be the first line.** Writing any statement before `super()` in a constructor causes a compile-time error. Students often write field assignments before `super(...)` and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing `super` with `this` in variable resolution.** When both `super.x` and `this.x` exist, students write `super.x` thinking it points to the current class — it does not.
> 3. **Believing `super` is optional when the parent has a default constructor.** The compiler inserts it *automatically*, but students sometimes claim it is "not needed" and lose the nuance mark.
> 4. **Static-context misuse.** Writing `super` inside a `static` method or static initializer is a compile-time error. Many students incorrectly assume `super` is universally accessible.
> 5. **Forgetting the rule for `super` in inner classes.** Non-static inner classes can use `super` to refer to the enclosing outer class only via the syntax `OuterClassName.super` — many students miss this nuance in advanced questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- `super` is a **reference variable**, not a keyword that creates a new object.
- It refers to the **immediate parent class** of the current class.
- It has **three uses**: parent variable access, parent method invocation, parent constructor chaining.
- `super()` (or `super(args)`) **must be the first statement** in a subclass constructor.
- If not written explicitly, the compiler **automatically inserts** `super()` (no-arg) into every constructor.
- If the parent has **no default constructor**, an explicit `super(args)` is **mandatory**, or a compile-time error occurs.
- Constructor chaining executes **top-down** (parent to child) for body execution, but the **call propagates upward**.
- `super` **cannot be used in static contexts** (static methods, static blocks, or static variable initializers).
- Variable hiding and method overriding are the two situations that necessitate `super` for disambiguation.
- The use of `super.method()` is the foundation of the **Template Method design pattern** widely used in Java frameworks.
- Every class ultimately inherits from `java.lang.Object`, so `super` chains terminate at `Object()`.
- `super` is **final in nature** — you cannot reassign it (e.g., `super = new Parent();` is illegal).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: protected Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** protected Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)** — specifically C++ and Java — a **protected member** is a data member or member function declared using the `protected` access specifier. The `protected` access level forms the intermediate tier of the access control hierarchy and is fundamentally an **inheritance-aware access modifier**.

The exact behavior differs between C++ and Java, and this distinction is a **high-yield KTU topic**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (C++ Context):**
> A `protected` member is accessible:
> 1. **Within its own class** (just like `private` and `public`).
> 2. **Within any derived (child) class** — through the derived class's own members or objects.
> 3. **NOT accessible** by objects of the derived class *outside* the class hierarchy (i.e., not accessible to the outside world, unlike `public`).
> 4. **NOT accessible** by other unrelated classes (i.e., not accessible like `private`).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Java Context Note:**
> In Java, `protected` means package-private **plus** access from subclasses in *any* package (via inheritance only — not via object reference, except within the same package). This subtle difference is a frequently tested KTU question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **family-owned ancestral property**:

| Member Type | Analogy | Who Can Access |
|-------------|---------|----------------|
| `private` | A personal diary locked in your room | **Only YOU** (the class itself) |
| `protected` | A family heirloom kept in the ancestral home | **You + Your direct children/grandchildren** (the class and its descendants) |
| `public` | A public park in the neighborhood | **Everyone** (the whole world) |

> [!TIP]
> Think of `protected` as a **"family secret"**: A child can read the parent's protected notes while studying at home (inside a derived class function), but the child cannot show those notes to a stranger on the street (an outside object).

### 1.3 Access Control Matrix at a Glance

The following table summarizes accessibility across the three C++ access specifiers (and Java's `default`):

> **Accessibility Scope of `protected` Members (C++ Standard)**

| Access Specifier | Same Class | Derived Class | Outside (Non-derived) Class |
|:----------------:|:----------:|:-------------:|:---------------------------:|
| `private` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `protected` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `public` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

Explicit **boundary markers** (must be memorized):
- **Lower bound:** `protected ⊇ private` (everything private can do, protected can do too)
- **Upper bound:** `protected ⊆ public` (everything protected can do, public can do too)
- **Inheritance permeability:** `protected` is the only specifier *specifically designed* to grant access to derived classes while still hiding the member from the outside world.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Rules of `protected` — The "Why" and the "How"

Let us break down the *operational logic* of `protected` into discrete rules that govern its behavior in real programs:

1. **Declaration Rule (How to declare):**
   A member is declared `protected` by prefixing its declaration with the `protected:` keyword inside a class body. Once declared, all members following it (until the next access specifier) inherit the `protected` visibility.

2. **Same-Class Access Rule (Why it works):**
   Within the class where the member is declared, `protected` behaves exactly like `private` and `public` — full unrestricted access. The compiler treats all three specifiers identically *inside* the declaring class.

3. **Derived-Class Access Rule (The core "Why"):**
   This is the defining property. A derived class can directly access a `protected` base-class member **through its own member functions** (or via `this` pointer). This allows the derived class to **reuse and extend** the base class's internals without exposing them to the world.

4. **Outside-World Restriction Rule (Why it is still safe):**
   An object of a derived class, *when used from outside the class hierarchy* (e.g., in `main()` or a non-friend, non-derived class), **CANNOT** access the protected members it inherited. The protection is enforced on the *static type* of the reference/pointer.

5. **Friend Function/Class Interaction:**
   A `friend` function or class of the base class can access its `protected` members (as it can access `private` members). However, a `friend` of the *derived* class cannot access the *base's* `protected` members — friendship is **non-inheritable and non-transitive**.

6. **C++ vs Java — The Subtle "How":**
   - **C++:** `protected` = accessible in derived class *and* through derived class objects *within* derived class member functions.
   - **Java:** `protected` = package-level access (default) **+** subclass access. Subclasses in different packages can access it only via inheritance, not via object reference.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **KTU High-Yield Reference Table — `protected` Members**

| Concept | C++ Rule | Java Rule |
|:--------|:---------|:----------|
| **Keyword** | `protected:` | `protected` |
| **Same class access** | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed |
| **Derived class access (own functions)** | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed |
| **Object-level access outside hierarchy** | ❌ Denied | ❌ Denied (different package) |
| **Package-level (default) access** | ❌ Not applicable | ✅ Implicitly included |
| **Cross-package subclass via reference** | ❌ Not applicable | ✅ Only if reference type is the subclass itself |
| **Inheritance from `protected` base** | `protected` → `protected` in derived (public inheritance) | All inherited members retain `protected` |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where is `protected` Used in Practice?

`protected` is the **workhorse specifier for OOP framework design**. Real-world engineering applications include:

- **GUI Frameworks (e.g., Qt, MFC):** Base widget classes expose internal state as `protected` so derived widgets (custom buttons, sliders) can manipulate them, but end-user code cannot break invariants.
- **Game Engines (e.g., Unreal Engine):** `AActor` and `UObject` base classes declare physics, rendering hooks, and lifecycle methods as `protected`, allowing game-specific subclasses to override behavior securely.
- **Standard Template Library Internals:** Many STL implementations use `protected` for allocator hooks and iterator base logic.
- **Plugin Architectures:** Plugin base classes often declare extension points as `protected`, ensuring only authorized plugin developers can extend behavior, not arbitrary client code.
- **Embedded Systems & Firmware:** Device driver base classes (in HAL — Hardware Abstraction Layers) use `protected` to expose register-mapped variables to vendor-specific derived drivers while keeping them hidden from application code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Overuse of `protected` breaks encapsulation almost as badly as `public`. KTU examiners often pose design questions asking **why** a member should be `protected` rather than `private` or `public` — the answer almost always involves a **legitimate derived-class need**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic `protected` Access in C++ (Derivation of Accessibility)

We will construct a small program that demonstrates the **three** access behaviors of a `protected` member step-by-step.

**Step 1: Declare the base class with a `protected` data member.**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Base {
private:
    int privateVar = 1;       // Accessible only inside Base

protected:
    int protectedVar = 2;     // Accessible in Base and Derived

public:
    int publicVar = 3;        // Accessible everywhere

    Base() {
        // Step 2: Same-class access — allowed for ALL specifiers
        cout << "Inside Base(): "
             << privateVar << " "
             << protectedVar << " "
             << publicVar << endl;
    }
};
```

**Step 2 (Analytical Transition):** At this point, inside `Base()`, we accessed `privateVar`, `protectedVar`, and `publicVar` without error. The compiler makes no distinction between specifiers within the declaring class. The output line prints:
$$
\text{Output} = \texttt{"Inside Base(): 1 2 3"}
$$

**Step 3: Declare a derived class that attempts to access the `protected` member.**

```cpp
class Derived : public Base {
public:
    void accessTest() {
        // cout << privateVar;    // ❌ ERROR — private not inherited accessibly
        cout << protectedVar;      // ✅ ALLOWED — protected is accessible in derived
        cout << publicVar;         // ✅ ALLOWED — public is always accessible
    }
};
```

**Step 4 (Analytical Transition):** The line `cout << protectedVar;` compiles successfully. The compiler resolves `protectedVar` by walking up the inheritance chain from `Derived` → `Base`, finds the `protected:` access label, and **grants access because the calling context (a member function of a derived class) is a trusted context**.

**Step 5: Demonstrate that outside-world access is denied.**

```cpp
int main() {
    Derived d;
    // cout << d.protectedVar;   // ❌ ERROR — cannot access protected member
    cout << d.publicVar;          // ✅ ALLOWED
    d.accessTest();               // ✅ ALLOWED — calls derived's own function
    return 0;
}
```

**Step 6 (Analytical Transition):** The statement `d.protectedVar` fails to compile with the error:

> *"`protectedVar` is protected within this context"*

This proves the **Outside-World Restriction Rule**: although `d` *is* a `Derived` object, the call site is `main()`, which is **outside the class hierarchy**. The access check is based on the *call site*, not the *object type*.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Java's Subtler `protected` Rule

In Java, `protected` is a hybrid of C++ `protected` and the *package-private* (default) specifier.

**Step 1: Define a base class in package `pkgA`.**

```java
package pkgA;
public class Base {
    protected int value = 42;
}
```

**Step 2: Define a subclass in a *different* package `pkgB`.**

```java
package pkgB;
import pkgA.Base;

public class Sub extends Base {
    void test() {
        System.out.println(value);      // ✅ ALLOWED — via inheritance
    }
}
```

**Step 3 (Analytical Transition):** The access in `Sub.test()` is allowed because we are inside a derived class's own method. The compiler checks the *enclosing class* (`Sub extends Base`) and grants access.

**Step 4: Attempt cross-package object access.**

```java
package pkgB;
import pkgA.Base;

public class Stranger {
    void test() {
        Base b = new Base();
        // System.out.println(b.value);  // ❌ ERROR — different package, not a subclass
    }
}
```

**Step 5 (Analytical Transition):** The line `b.value` fails because `Stranger` is not in `pkgA` and does not extend `Base`. This is the **core KTU distinction** between C++ and Java `protected`.

**Step 6: The subtle case — subclass in different package accessing via reference.**

```java
package pkgB;
import pkgA.Base;

public class Sub extends Base {
    void trickyTest() {
        Sub s = new Sub();
        System.out.println(s.value);     // ✅ ALLOWED — reference type is Sub
```
