```java
        Base b = new Sub();
        // System.out.println(b.value);  // ❌ ERROR — reference type is Base, but we are in a different package
    }
}
```

**Step 7 (Analytical Transition):** The Java compiler enforces that cross-package `protected` access is permitted **only** when the reference variable's type is the same class or a subclass of the *enclosing* class. This is one of the most common **trick questions** in KTU Java examinations.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Should I use `protected`?"

> **The KTU Design Decision Flowchart (in tabular form)**

| Situation | Recommended Specifier | Reason |
|:----------|:---------------------|:-------|
| Member is an internal helper, no derived class needs it | `private` | Maximum encapsulation |
| Member is part of the class's public contract (interface) | `public` | External clients must access it |
| Member represents internal state that **derived classes must read/write** to fulfill polymorphism | `protected` | Balances reuse and encapsulation |
| Member is a base-class implementation detail exposed only to direct framework subclasses | `protected` | Standard pattern in frameworks |
| All data members of a class (no inheritance intended) | `private` | Default best practice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (used in KTU 2-mark questions):**
> Use `protected` **only** when there is a clear, documented requirement that a future derived class will need direct access. Otherwise, prefer `private` and provide `public`/`protected` getter/setter methods.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Access Specifier Visibility Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **visibility scope** of each access specifier across the class hierarchy. This is the kind of diagram KTU examiners use in module question papers to test conceptual clarity.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef baseClass fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef derivedClass fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outsideClass fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A[Base Class: Base]:::baseClass
    A --> A1[private: data]:::baseClass
    A --> A2[protected: data]:::baseClass
    A --> A3[public: data]:::baseClass

    B[Derived Class: Derived]:::derivedClass
    A -->|public inheritance| B
    B --> B1[Inherits private: NO]:::derivedClass
    B --> B2[Inherits protected: YES]:::derivedClass
    B --> B3[Inherits public: YES]:::derivedClass

    C[Outside World: main and unrelated classes]:::outsideClass
    C --> C1[Can access public: YES]:::outsideClass
    C --> C2[Can access protected: NO]:::outsideClass
    C --> C3[Can access private: NO]:::outsideClass

    D{Q1: Is caller inside the Base class?}:::decisionNode
    E{Q2: Is caller inside a Derived class?}:::decisionNode
    F{Q3: Is caller outside hierarchy?}:::decisionNode

    A1 --> D
    A2 --> E
    A3 --> F
```

### 4.2 C++ vs Java `protected` Behavior Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef cppNode fill:#00599C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef javaNode fill:#E76F00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef resultNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    Start([Attempt to access protected member]):::resultNode
    Start --> Lang{Which language?}

    Lang -->|C++| C1[Check: Is caller in same class OR derived class?]:::cppNode
    C1 -->|Yes| CAllow[Access GRANTED]:::resultNode
    C1 -->|No| CDeny[Access DENIED]:::resultNode

    Lang -->|Java| J1[Check: Is caller in same package OR a subclass?]:::javaNode
    J1 -->|Yes| JCheck{Is it via object reference AND cross-package?}
    JCheck -->|Yes and ref is base type| JDeny[Access DENIED]:::resultNode
    JCheck -->|Otherwise| JAllow[Access GRANTED]:::resultNode
    J1 -->|No| JDeny2[Access DENIED]:::resultNode
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inheritance with `protected`

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef layer1 fill:#4B0082,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef layer2 fill:#9370DB,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef layer3 fill:#BA55D3,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    L1[BaseClass: Account]:::layer1
    L1 --> L1A[protected: balance]
    L1 --> L1B[protected: accountID]
    L1 --> L1C[public: deposit and withdraw]

    L2[DerivedClass: SavingsAccount]:::layer2
    L1 -->|public inheritance| L2
    L2 --> L2A[Uses balance directly to compute interest]
    L2 --> L2B[Calls deposit from BaseClass]

    L3[DerivedClass: CurrentAccount]:::layer2
    L1 -->|public inheritance| L3
    L3 --> L3A[Uses balance to enforce overdraft limit]
    L3 --> L3B[Overrides withdraw behavior]

    N1[Note: balance is hidden from main but visible to SavingsAccount and CurrentAccount]:::note
    L2 --- N1
    L3 --- N1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrow from `BaseClass` to `SavingsAccount` represents *public inheritance*. The `protected` member `balance` flows downward into the derived classes (denoted by the dashed-style encapsulation), but stops at the boundary of the outside world (not shown). This is the *encapsulation-via-inheritance* pattern.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Explain the `protected` access specifier in C++. How is it different from `private` and `public`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `protected` access specifier in C++ makes a class member accessible in two contexts:
1. **Within the class** where it is declared (same as `private` and `public`).
2. **Within any derived (child) class**, through the derived class's own member functions.

It differs from `private` because `private` members are **not** accessible in derived classes. It differs from `public` because `public` members are also accessible from **outside** the class hierarchy (e.g., from `main()` or unrelated classes), whereas `protected` members are **not**.

The key use-case is enabling derived classes to reuse base-class internals while still hiding them from external code. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for difference from private, 1 for difference from public.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S3 CSE]**
**In Java, what is the meaning of `protected`? Is it the same as C++'s `protected`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In Java, `protected` is a hybrid access level combining **package-private** (default) access and **subclass access**:
- Members declared `protected` are accessible to **all classes in the same package** (equivalent to default access).
- They are **also** accessible to **subclasses in any other package**, but only via inheritance (i.e., when the access happens inside a subclass's own methods, and the reference type is the subclass or its descendant).

It is **not** the same as C++'s `protected`. In C++, `protected` does not grant package-level access (Java has no packages in the C++ sense), and C++ `protected` does not restrict subclass access to a specific reference type. **(3 marks — 1 for Java definition, 1 for subclass rule, 1 for C++ difference.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Full Question]**

**(a)** Explain the three access specifiers in C++ with a suitable program demonstrating how a `protected` member is accessed inside a derived class but not from `main()`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss why a designer would choose `protected` over `private` in an inheritance hierarchy. Illustrate with a real-world example of a banking application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Explanation with Program (7 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Animal {
private:
    int age = 5;             // (1 mark: declaration of private)

protected:
    string species = "Dog";  // (1 mark: declaration of protected)

public:
    void describe() {        // (1 mark: public method)
        cout << "I am a " << species
             << " of age " << age << endl;
    }
};

class Dog : public Animal {
public:
    void accessProtected() {
        // (1 mark: showing protected access inside derived class)
        cout << "Dog class sees species = " << species << endl;
        // cout << age;   // (1 mark: showing private is NOT accessible)
    }
};

int main() {
    Dog d;
    d.describe();                  // ✅ Public — allowed (0.5 marks)
    d.accessProtected();           // ✅ Public method call — allowed (0.5 marks)
    // cout << d.species;          // ❌ Protected — DENIED
    // cout << d.age;              // ❌ Private — DENIED
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
$$
\texttt{Dog class sees species = Dog}
$$
$$
\texttt{I am a Dog of age 5}
$$

**Key Points to Mention (1 mark):**
- `protected` members are accessible in derived classes' own functions.
- `protected` members are NOT accessible through objects of derived classes from outside the hierarchy.
- The error message at compile time: *"`species` is protected within this context"*.

**(b) Why `protected` Over `private`? (7 marks):**

`protected` is preferred over `private` when **future derived classes are anticipated to require direct access** to internal state for legitimate extension or specialization, without exposing that state to the outside world.

**Banking Example:**

```cpp
class Account {                // (1 mark: base class)
protected:
    double balance;            // (2 marks: protected for derived class reuse)

public:
    Account(double b) : balance(b) {}
    virtual void display() = 0;
};

class SavingsAccount : public Account {   // (1 mark: derived class)
public:
    SavingsAccount(double b) : Account(b) {}

    void calculateInterest() {            // (2 marks: derived class directly uses balance)
        double interest = balance * 0.04;
        cout << "Interest: " << interest << endl;
    }

    void display() override {
        cout << "Savings Balance: " << balance << endl;
    }
};
```

**Discussion Points (1 mark):**
- If `balance` were `private`, `SavingsAccount::calculateInterest()` would need a public/protected getter, adding boilerplate.
- If `balance` were `public`, any external code could corrupt it (e.g., `account.balance = -1000000`).
- `protected` provides the **Goldilocks zone**: secure from outside misuse, yet accessible to legitimate derived-class extensions.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is the significance of the `protected` access specifier in inheritance? Explain with a C++ program showing multilevel inheritance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the `protected` access specifier in C++ and Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Multilevel Inheritance Example (7 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Grandparent {            // (1 mark)
protected:
    int gpData = 100;          // (1 mark: protected member)
};

class Parent : public Grandparent {   // (1 mark: intermediate class)
protected:
    int pData = 200;                  // (0.5 marks)
};

class Child : public Parent {         // (1 mark: derived from Parent)
public:
    void show() {
        cout << "Grandparent data: " << gpData << endl;   // (1 mark)
        cout << "Parent data: " << pData << endl;          // (1 mark)
    }
};

int main() {
    Child c;
    c.show();
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
$$
\texttt{Grandparent data: 100}
$$
$$
\texttt{Parent data: 200}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** `protected` data flows through **multilevel inheritance** — `Child` can access `gpData` (from `Grandparent`) and `pData` (from `Parent`) without needing intermediate getter functions. This shows the **transitive nature** of `protected` access across multiple inheritance levels.

---

**(b) C++ vs Java Comparison (7 marks):**

> **Comparison Table (3 marks for table, 4 marks for examples and explanation)**

| Feature | C++ | Java |
|:--------|:----|:-----|
| Package concept | None | First-class |
| `protected` in same class | ✅ | ✅ |
| `protected` in same package | ❌ (no packages) | ✅ (default-style) |
| `protected` in subclass, same package | ✅ | ✅ |
| `protected` in subclass, different package | ✅ | ✅ (only via inheritance) |
| Object-level access outside | ❌ | ❌ (different package) |

**C++ Example (2 marks):**
```cpp
class A { protected: int x = 5; };
class B : public A {
    void f() { cout << x; }   // ✅ Allowed
};
int main() {
    B b;
    // cout << b.x;            // ❌ Denied
}
```

**Java Example (2 marks):**
```java
package pkgA;
public class A { protected int x = 5; }

package pkgB;
public class B extends A {
    void f() { System.out.println(x); }   // ✅ Allowed
}
```

**Key Difference (0 marks — covered in table):** Java adds package-level access, while C++ restricts `protected` strictly to the class hierarchy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing C++ and Java `protected`:** Many students write "Java `protected` is the same as C++ `protected`" — this costs **2 full marks** in a Part B question.
> 2. **Thinking derived-class *objects* can access protected members outside the class:** `d.protectedVar` from `main()` is **always** denied in C++. Students often write that it is "allowed because `d` is a derived object" — wrong, lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the same-package rule in Java:** Not mentioning that Java's `protected` includes default (package) access loses **1–2 marks** in 7-mark questions.
> 4. **Using `protected` for everything:** Examiners penalize over-use. Always justify **why** `protected` is preferred over `private` with a concrete design reason.
> 5. **Inheritance type confusion:** Under `private` inheritance, `protected` base members become `private` in the derived class — this is rarely asked but is a **bonus 2-mark differentiator** if mentioned correctly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — `protected` Members**

- ✅ `protected` is the **middle-tier** access specifier — between `private` (most restrictive) and `public` (least restrictive).
- ✅ In C++, `protected` members are accessible in the **declaring class** and in **all derived classes** (own member functions only).
- ✅ `protected` members are **NOT accessible** through object references from outside the class hierarchy.
- ✅ `protected` enables **inheritance-aware encapsulation** — derived classes can reuse internals without exposing them publicly.
- ✅ In Java, `protected` = **package-private + subclass** access (in any package, via inheritance only).
- ✅ The **object reference type matters in Java** cross-package access: only references of the *enclosing subclass type* (or its descendants) can access a `protected` member.
- ✅ `protected` is **transitive in inheritance**: a grandchild can access grandparent's `protected` members.
- ✅ **Friend functions/classes** of the base can access `protected`; friends of the derived **cannot** access base's `protected`.
- ✅ Use `protected` only when there is a **clear, documented need** for derived-class access. Default to `private`.
- ✅ **Frameworks and libraries** (Qt, Unreal, STL, Java Swing) use `protected` extensively to expose extension points to subclass authors.
- ✅ **Common KTU question pattern:** "Justify why this member is `protected` and not `private` or `public`." Always answer with a **derived-class need** + **outside-world restriction**.
- ✅ **Java vs C++ trap:** Never equate the two `protected` specifiers — Java includes package semantics that C++ does not have.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calling Order of Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calling Order of Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Object-Oriented Programming, the **Calling Order of Constructors** refers to the deterministic, compiler-enforced sequence in which constructors of base classes, member objects, and the derived class itself are invoked during the instantiation of an object. The C++ standard (ISO/IEC 14882) and the Java Language Specification (JLS) guarantee that this order is **well-defined, predictable, and reproducible** across compliant compilers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):**
> *"The order in which constructors are invoked during object creation is: (1) virtual base classes (in order of derivation), (2) non-virtual base classes (in order of declaration), (3) member objects (in order of declaration in the class), and (4) the body of the derived class's own constructor."*

| Term | Meaning in Constructor Order Context |
|---|---|
| **Base Class Constructor** | Constructor of the parent/superclass |
| **Member Object Constructor** | Constructor of an object that is a data member of another class |
| **Derived Class Constructor** | Constructor of the class being instantiated |
| **Destructor** | Cleanup function called in **exactly reverse order** of constructor calls |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Birth Certificate" Metaphor

Imagine you are building a **multi-storey house** 🏠 on a plot of land:

1. **First**, the **foundation** must be laid (Base Class) — you cannot have a house without a strong base.
2. **Then**, the **plumbing and electrical fittings** (Member Objects) are installed into the foundation, in the order they were designed into the blueprint.
3. **Finally**, the **walls, roof, and finishing** (Derived Class Body) are completed on top.

> [!NOTE]
> When the house is demolished (object goes out of scope → destructor is called), the process happens in **strict reverse order**: roof first, then fittings, then the foundation. This is the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** property of destructors.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"Constructors build from the **ground up**; Destructors tear down from the **roof down**."*

## 1.3 Why is This Order Important? (KTU 2024 Relevance)

- **Inheritance Safety:** The derived class constructor body can safely assume that base class sub-objects are *fully initialized*.
- **Composition Safety:** Member functions of a derived class can call inherited methods without null-pointer or uninitialized-state errors.
- **Resource Management:** Predictable destruction prevents **memory leaks** and **dangling pointer** bugs — a common KTU viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Governing Rules of Constructor Calling Order

The order is governed by **five deterministic rules** that every KTU student must memorize:

### Rule 1: Local Objects (Within a Function)
- **Construction Order:** In the **order of their definition** in the function body (top-to-bottom).
- **Destruction Order:** In the **exact reverse** of construction (LIFO).

### Rule 2: Global / Static Objects
- **Construction Order:** Before `main()` begins; in the order they appear across **translation units** (implementation-defined for cross-file order, but deterministic within a single file).
- **Destruction Order:** After `main()` exits; in **reverse** of construction.

### Rule 3: Member Objects (Composition / "Has-A" Relationship)
- **Construction Order:** In the **order of declaration** in the class definition (NOT the order in the initializer list of the constructor).
- **Destruction Order:** In the **reverse** of declaration order.

### Rule 4: Single Inheritance ("Is-A" Relationship)
- **Base class constructor is invoked FIRST**, then the derived class body executes.
- If the base class is itself derived, the chain continues upward recursively (the most-rooted ancestor is constructed first).

### Rule 5: Multiple Inheritance
- **Non-virtual base classes** are constructed in the **order they are declared** in the derived class's inheritance list.
- **Virtual base classes** are constructed **BEFORE any non-virtual base**, in the order they appear in a depth-first, left-to-right traversal of the inheritance DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Scenario | Construction Order | Destruction Order | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Local variables in a function | Top-to-bottom in code | Reverse (LIFO) | Forgetting stack unwinding |
| Global/Static objects | Before `main()` | After `main()`, reverse | Order across files is unspecified |
| Member objects (composition) | Order of **declaration** in class | Reverse of declaration | Initializer-list order is **irrelevant** |
| Single inheritance | Base → Derived | Derived → Base | Calling derived ctor before base |
| Multiple inheritance (non-virtual) | Order in **derived class declaration** | Reverse of construction | Assuming header order in code matters |
| Multiple inheritance (with virtual base) | Virtual base → All non-virtual bases → Derived | Reverse | The "Dreaded Diamond" problem |
| Virtual base class | Once, by the **most-derived** class | Last, by the most-derived class | Each derived class trying to call it |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In multiple inheritance, the order in the **initializer list of the derived constructor** does **NOT** control the order. The order of declaration in the class header does. Many students lose marks on this.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Constructor Order |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | Deterministic hardware initialization order (sensors before controllers) |
| **Game Development (Unreal Engine)** | `UObject` derived classes require parent constructors to complete before component attachment |
| **Database Systems** | Connection pool objects must be initialized before query-handler objects |
| **GUI Frameworks (Qt, JavaFX)** | Window components must be constructed before event handlers can attach to them |
| **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** | Predictable initialization order is critical for boot-time determinism |

## 2.4 Constructor Chaining in Java vs C++

| Feature | C++ | Java |
|---|---|---|
| Base ctor invocation syntax | `Derived(int x): Base(x), member(x) { ... }` | `super(x);` must be **first statement** |
| Member ctor invocation | In **declaration** order, not initializer-list order | In declared order (also enforced) |
| Multiple inheritance | Allowed → complex order | **Not allowed** (interfaces only) |
| Default ctor auto-call | Yes, if base has default | Yes, super() auto-inserted if omitted |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Trace Analysis & Constructive Derivations

Since this topic is inherently **analytical and trace-based**, the derivations below take the form of **complete execution traces** of multi-class programs — the exact problem type KTU examiners set in the 14-mark questions.

## 3.1 Trace 1: Single Inheritance with Member Object

Consider the following C++ program. We will trace the **exact line-by-line execution sequence**.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Engine {
public:
    Engine()  { cout << "Engine constructed\n"; }
    ~Engine() { cout << "Engine destroyed\n"; }
};

class Vehicle {
    Engine e;          // Member object
public:
    Vehicle() { cout << "Vehicle constructed\n"; }
    ~Vehicle() { cout << "Vehicle destroyed\n"; }
};

class Car : public Vehicle {
public:
    Car()  { cout << "Car constructed\n"; }
    ~Car() { cout << "Car destroyed\n"; }
};

int main() {
    Car c;
    return 0;
}
```

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1:** Program execution reaches `main()`. The statement `Car c;` is encountered.

**Step 2:** Compiler sees `Car` is derived from `Vehicle`. Before constructing `Car`, it **must** construct the `Vehicle` sub-object.

**Step 3:** Compiler sees `Vehicle` contains a member `Engine e`. Before executing the `Vehicle` constructor body, it **must** construct the `Engine` member object (Rule 3: member objects constructed before enclosing class body).

**Step 4:** `Engine` default constructor is called → prints `Engine constructed`.

**Step 5:** Control returns to the `Vehicle` constructor. The body now executes → prints `Vehicle constructed`.

**Step 6:** Control returns to the `Car` constructor. The body executes → prints `Car constructed`.

**Step 7:** `main()` ends. The object `c` goes out of scope. **Destruction begins in LIFO reverse order.**

**Step 8:** `Car` destructor body executes → prints `Car destroyed`.

**Step 9:** `Vehicle` destructor body executes → prints `Vehicle destroyed`.

**Step 10:** `Engine` member destructor executes → prints `Engine destroyed`.

### Final Output

```
Engine constructed
Vehicle constructed
Car constructed
Car destroyed
Vehicle destroyed
Engine destroyed
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Construction was `Engine → Vehicle → Car` (base of base first, then derived). Destruction was the exact reverse. This is the canonical KTU viva answer.

## 3.2 Trace 2: Multiple Inheritance with the "Diamond Problem"

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class A {
public:
    A()  { cout << "A constructed\n"; }
    ~A() { cout << "A destroyed\n"; }
};

class B : public A {
public:
    B()  { cout << "B constructed\n"; }
    ~B() { cout << "B destroyed\n"; }
};

class C : public A {
public:
    C()  { cout << "C constructed\n"; }
    ~C() { cout << "C destroyed\n"; }
};

class D : public B, public C {   // Note: order is B first, then C
public:
    D()  { cout << "D constructed\n"; }
    ~D() { cout << "D destroyed\n"; }
};

int main() {
    D obj;
    return 0;
}
```

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 1:** `D obj;` is encountered. Compiler examines the inheritance list: `B, C` (in that order).

**Step 2:** Compiler must construct `B` first (Rule 5: order in inheritance list).

**Step 3:** `B` inherits from `A`. Compiler constructs `A` first.

**Step 4:** `A` ctor → prints `A constructed` (1st time, for B's path).

**Step 5:** `B` ctor body → prints `B constructed`.

**Step 6:** Now compiler constructs `C` (next in D's inheritance list).

**Step 7:** `C` inherits from `A`. Compiler constructs `A` again (non-virtual case → constructed twice!).

**Step 8:** `A` ctor → prints `A constructed` (2nd time, for C's path).

**Step 9:** `C` ctor body → prints `C constructed`.

**Step 10:** Finally, `D` ctor body → prints `D constructed`.

**Step 11 — Destruction:** Strict LIFO reverse: `D → C → A → B → A`.

### Final Output

```
A constructed      (for B)
B constructed
A constructed      (for C)   ← A is constructed TWICE
C constructed
D constructed
D destroyed
C destroyed
A destroyed        (for C)
B destroyed
A destroyed        (for B)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Without the `virtual` keyword on inheritance (e.g., `class B : virtual public A`), class `A` is constructed **TWICE**, and there will be **TWO copies** of A's sub-object inside D. This causes the "Dreaded Diamond" ambiguity. Students often write `virtual A* ptr;` thinking they fixed it, but the actual fix is `class B : virtual public A`.

## 3.3 Trace 3: The Same Diamond — Solved with Virtual Inheritance

```cpp
class B : virtual public A { /* ... */ };
class C : virtual public A { /* ... */ };
class D : public B, public C { /* ... */ };
```

Now the output becomes:

```
A constructed      (only ONCE, by the most-derived class D)
B constructed
C constructed
D constructed
D destroyed
C destroyed
B destroyed
A destroyed
```

### Why This Works — The Derivation

- **Step 1:** Compiler sees `B` and `C` are both **virtual** bases of `D`.
- **Step 2:** The **most-derived class** (`D`) is responsible for initializing the virtual base (`A`).
- **Step 3:** Even though `D` does not directly inherit from `A`, it is still the most-derived, so it calls `A`'s constructor.
- **Step 4:** `B` and `C` are then constructed (note: they will **NOT** re-construct `A`).
- **Step 5:** `D` body runs last.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** *"Virtual base classes are always constructed by the **most-derived** class, exactly **once**, before any non-virtual base or member."*

## 3.4 Trace 4: Initializer List vs Declaration Order (Common KTU Trap)

```cpp
class Demo {
    int b;
    int a;
public:
    Demo() : a(10), b(20) { }   // Initializer list says a first, then b
};
```

### Analytical Derivation of Execution

- **Step 1:** Compiler parses the class body. The declaration order is `int b;` then `int a;`.
- **Step 2:** When `Demo()` is called, the compiler initializes members in **declaration order**, NOT initializer-list order.
- **Step 3:** Therefore, `b` is initialized first (to 20), then `a` (to 10).
- **Step 4:** If the initializer list had been `: a(b+5), b(7)`, it would still execute `b` first (to 7), then `a` (to 7+5=12). The `a(b+5)` expression uses the **already-initialized** value of `b`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Modern compilers like GCC emit a **warning**: *"declaration order will be used for initialization"* when the initializer list is in a different order from declaration. KTU examiners often include this in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Construction Order in Single Inheritance (Bottom-Up)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Base Class Constructor Invoked"] --> B["Step 2: Member Objects of Base Class Constructed"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Base Class Constructor Body Executes"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Member Objects of Derived Class Constructed"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Derived Class Constructor Body Executes"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Object is Now Fully Initialized"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Read top-to-bottom for **construction**. Read bottom-to-top for **destruction**.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Diamond Inheritance — Visual Trace

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["A : topmost grandparent class"]
    B1["B : inherits from A"]
    C1["C : inherits from A"]
    D1["D : inherits from B and C"]

    A1 --- B1
    A1 --- C1
    B1 --- D1
    C1 --- D1

    A1 -.->|step 1| S1["Constructor of A runs first"]
    S1 -->|step 2| S2["Constructor of B runs next"]
    S2 -->|step 3| S3["Constructor of C runs next"]
    S3 -->|step 4| S4["Constructor of D runs last"]

    style A1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style D1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style S1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style S4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Construction vs Destruction Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Constructor and Destructor Execution Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Construction Phase
    Base Class Ctor    :a1, 0, 1s
    Member Objects     :a2, 1, 2s
    Derived Class Body :a3, 3, 1s
    section Object Lifetime
    Object In Use      :crit, b1, 4, 5s
    section Destruction Phase
    Derived Class Dtor :c1, 9, 1s
    Member Destructors :c2, 10, 1s
    Base Class Dtor    :c3, 11, 1s
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture (Constructor Order Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Object Creation Request"]
        I1["new Derived object"]
    end

    subgraph VIRTUAL["Virtual Base Resolution"]
        V1["Locate most-derived class"]
        V2["Invoke virtual base ctors once"]
    end

    subgraph NONVIRTUAL["Non-Virtual Base Layer"]
        N1["Invoke base ctor #1 in declaration order"]
        N2["Invoke base ctor #2 in declaration order"]
        N3["Invoke base ctor #N in declaration order"]
    end

    subgraph MEMBERS["Member Object Layer"]
        M1["Invoke member ctor #1 in declaration order"]
        M2["Invoke member ctor #2 in declaration order"]
    end

    subgraph DERIVED["Derived Class Body"]
        D1["Execute derived class constructor body"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Fully Initialized Object"]
        O1["Ready for use"]
    end

    I1 --> V1 --> V2
    V2 --> N1 --> N2 --> N3
    N3 --> M1 --> M2
    M2 --> D1 --> O1

    style I1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style O1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style V1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style D1 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q: Explain the order in which constructors are invoked when an object of a derived class is created in C++. What about the order of destructor calls?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a derived class object is created, constructors are invoked in the following order:
1. **Base class constructor** is invoked first.
2. **Member object constructors** of the derived class are invoked next, in the **order of their declaration** in the class.
3. **The body of the derived class constructor** is executed last.

**Destructor calls** occur in the **exact reverse order** of construction (LIFO principle). The derived class destructor runs first, followed by member destructors in reverse declaration order, and finally the base class destructor. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: What is the significance of constructor order in multiple inheritance? How are virtual base classes handled?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In multiple inheritance, base class constructors are invoked in the **order in which the base classes are declared** in the derived class's inheritance list, not in the order of the initializer list. **Virtual base classes** are constructed **only once**, by the **most-derived class**, and this construction happens **before any non-virtual base class**. This solves the "diamond problem" by preventing duplicate sub-objects of the shared ancestor. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the rules governing the order of constructor invocation in C++ with respect to **single inheritance, multiple inheritance, and member objects**. Provide a code example demonstrating each scenario and trace the output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **diamond problem** in multiple inheritance? Explain with a suitable code example how the `virtual` keyword solves it. Trace the output for both the non-virtual and virtual cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**The Three Rules (4 marks):**
1. **Single Inheritance:** The base class constructor is invoked *before* the derived class constructor body. This ensures the base sub-object is fully initialized.
2. **Multiple Inheritance:** Non-virtual base classes are constructed in the **order of declaration** in the derived class's inheritance list. The initializer list order is irrelevant.
3. **Member Objects:** Constructed in the **declaration order** in the class, not the initializer list order.

**Code Example Demonstrating All Three (3 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Engine {                         // Member object class
public:
    Engine() { cout << "Engine ctor\n"; }
};

class Wheels {                         // Another member object class
public:
    Wheels() { cout << "Wheels ctor\n"; }
};

class Vehicle {                        // Base class with two members
    Engine e;
    Wheels w;
public:
    Vehicle() { cout << "Vehicle ctor\n"; }
};

class Car : public Vehicle {           // Single inheritance
public:
    Car() { cout << "Car ctor\n"; }
};

class Airplane : public Vehicle {       // Another derived (multi)
public:
    Airplane() { cout << "Airplane ctor\n"; }
};

class FlyingCar : public Car, public Airplane {  // Multiple inheritance
public:
    FlyingCar() { cout << "FlyingCar ctor\n"; }
};

int main() {
    FlyingCar fc;
    return 0;
}
```

**Execution Trace (Output) (additional credit):**

```
Engine ctor          (member of Vehicle, called when Vehicle is built for Car)
Wheels ctor
Vehicle ctor        (base of Car, built first)
Car ctor
Engine ctor          (member of Vehicle, called when Vehicle is built for Airplane)
Wheels ctor
Vehicle ctor        (base of Airplane, built second)
Airplane ctor
FlyingCar ctor
```

**Analysis:** The trace proves the rule: in multiple inheritance, the order is determined by the **declaration order** (`Car` before `Airplane` in `FlyingCar`'s inheritance list), and member objects are constructed **before** the enclosing class body. (7 marks total)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

The **diamond problem** occurs when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common ancestor. Without `virtual` inheritance, the common ancestor's sub-object is duplicated, leading to ambiguity and redundant initialization.

**Non-Virtual Diamond Code (2 marks):**

```cpp
class A { public: A() { cout << "A ctor\n"; } };
class B : public A { public: B() { cout << "B ctor\n"; } };
class C : public A { public: C() { cout << "C ctor\n"; } };
class D : public B, public C { public: D() { cout << "D ctor\n"; } };
// D's object contains TWO copies of A.
```

**Output of Non-Virtual Case:**
```
A ctor      ← for B
B ctor
A ctor      ← for C (DUPLICATE!)
C ctor
D ctor
```

**Virtual Diamond Code (1 mark):**

```cpp
class B : virtual public A { /* ... */ };
class C : virtual public A { /* ... */ };
class D  : public B, public C { /* ... */ };
```

**Output of Virtual Case:**
```
A ctor      ← only ONCE
B ctor
C ctor
D ctor
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The `virtual` keyword makes the most-derived class (`D`) responsible for constructing the shared base (`A`), ensuring it is initialized exactly once, before any non-virtual base. This eliminates duplication and ambiguity. (7 marks total)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a suitable C++ program, demonstrate how the **order of declaration** of member objects (not the initializer list order) determines the construction sequence. Show that destructors follow the reverse order. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **LIFO principle of destructors** with an example involving local variables in a function, global objects, and class hierarchy. Why is this principle important for resource management? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Code (4 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class First {
public:
    First()  { cout << "First constructed\n"; }
    ~First() { cout << "First destroyed\n"; }
};

class Second {
public:
    Second()  { cout << "Second constructed\n"; }
    ~Second() { cout << "Second destroyed\n"; }
};

class Container {
    First f;       // Declared FIRST
    Second s;      // Declared SECOND
public:
    // Note: initializer list has s first, f second
    Container() : s(), f() {
        cout << "Container body\n";
    }
    ~Container() { cout << "Container destroyed\n"; }
};

int main() {
    Container c;
    return 0;
}
```

**Output (2 marks):**

```
First constructed        ← declaration order (NOT initializer list!)
Second constructed
Container body
Container destroyed
Second destroyed
First destroyed          ← LIFO reverse
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** Although the initializer list `: s(), f()` lists `s` before `f`, the compiler uses the **declaration order** (`f` first, then `s`) for construction. Destructors fire in reverse. (7 marks total)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**LIFO Principle Definition (1 mark):** Destructors are called in the **Last-In, First-Out** order — the most recently constructed object is destroyed first.

**Example with Three Scenarios (5 marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Resource {
    int id;
public:
    Resource(int i) : id(i) { cout << "Resource " << id << " acquired\n"; }
    ~Resource() { cout << "Resource " << id << " released\n"; }
};

Resource globalRes(0);     // Global object

void demoFunction() {
    Resource localA(1);    // First local
    Resource localB(2);    // Second local
    // ... function body ...
}   // localB destroyed first, then localA (LIFO)

int main() {
    cout << "Inside main\n";
    demoFunction();
    cout << "Back in main\n";
    return 0;
}   // globalRes destroyed after main exits
```

**Output:**
```
Resource 0 acquired          (global, before main)
Inside main
Resource 1 acquired          (localA)
Resource 2 acquired          (localB)
Resource 2 released          (LIFO)
Resource 1 released
Back in main
Resource 0 released          (after main)
```

**Importance for Resource Management (1 mark):** The LIFO principle ensures that objects are destroyed in the **reverse order of their dependencies**. For example, if object B uses resources from object A, A is constructed first and destroyed last — guaranteeing that B's destructor runs while A is still valid. This prevents **use-after-free** and **dangling reference** errors. (7 marks total)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing declaration order with initializer-list order** (lose 2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting that destructors run in reverse** (lose 1–2 marks).
> 3. **In multiple inheritance, assuming header-file order matters** — only the inheritance-list declaration order does (lose 2 marks).
> 4. **Forgetting to add `virtual` keyword for diamond problem** (lose 2–3 marks).
> 5. **Not tracing the output line-by-line** in code-based questions (lose up to 4 marks).
> 6. **Writing `void Car()` instead of `Car()` for the constructor** — a syntax error worth losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ⭐ **Constructor Order Rule:** *Base → Members → Derived body* (always, no exceptions).
- ⭐ **Destructor Order Rule:** *Reverse of construction* (strict LIFO).
- ⭐ **Member Initialization:** Follows **declaration order**, not initializer-list order.
- ⭐ **Single Inheritance:** Base ctor first, then derived ctor body.
- ⭐ **Multiple Inheritance:** Non-virtual bases in **declaration order** in inheritance list.
- ⭐ **Virtual Base Classes:** Constructed **once**, by the **most-derived** class, **before** all non-virtual bases.
- ⭐ **Diamond Problem:** Solved using `virtual` keyword in inheritance (e.g., `class B : virtual public A`).
- ⭐ **Local Objects in a Function:** Constructed top-to-bottom, destroyed bottom-to-top.
- ⭐ **Global/Static Objects:** Constructed before `main()`, destroyed after `main()` in reverse.
- ⭐ **Java's `super()`:** Must be the **first statement** in a constructor; auto-inserted if omitted.
- ⭐ **C++ Initializer List Syntax:** `Derived(args) : Base(args), member1(args), member2(args) { body }`.
- ⭐ **C++ rule of three/five:** If a class manages resources, the destructor, copy constructor, and copy assignment operator must be carefully ordered (advanced topic, often asked in 14-mark questions).
- ⭐ **Compiler Warnings:** GCC/Clang warn when initializer-list order differs from declaration order — KTU examiners love to test this.
- ⭐ **Why this matters:** Predictable order = no uninitialized members, no double-free, no memory leaks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Memory Aid:** *"Bases built first, members in declaration, derived last. Destructors do the **reverse moonwalk** — out the way they came in."* 🌙
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overriding
The Object Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Object Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **`Object` class** is the **root of the class hierarchy** in Java. Every class in Java has `java.lang.Object` as its **superclass**, either **directly** (when a class is declared without an `extends` clause) or **indirectly** (transitively through inheritance chains). Since `Object` resides in the `java.lang` package, it is **automatically imported** into every Java program without requiring an explicit `import` statement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The `Object` class defines and implements behavior that all classes in Java share. Because it sits at the apex of the inheritance tree, references of type `Object` can refer to **any object** of any class — this is the foundation of **polymorphism** in Java collections and generic frameworks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Ancestor"

Imagine a **family tree of every living thing on Earth**. At the very top, there is a single original ancestor from which *all* species descended. No matter whether you are looking at a dog, a rose, or a bacterium, you can trace its lineage back to that one common root.

The `Object` class plays that exact role in Java:

- It is the **common ancestor** of every class.
- It guarantees that **every object** (regardless of its class) has a set of **basic, universal capabilities**: identity, comparison, string representation, and class information.
- This uniformity is why Java collections (like `ArrayList`, `HashMap`) can store **heterogeneous** objects — they ultimately store everything as `Object`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When you write `class Student { }`, the compiler silently rewrites it as `class Student extends Object { }`. This is called **single root inheritance** and is a deliberate design choice by James Gosling to keep the type system simple and unified.

### 1.3 The Eleven Methods of `java.lang.Object`

The `Object` class declares **eleven methods** (as of Java 17+), grouped into the following functional categories:

| Category | Method | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | `getClass()` | `public final Class<?> getClass()` | Returns runtime class of the object |
| **String Form** | `toString()` | `public String toString()` | Returns textual representation |
| **Equality** | `equals()` | `public boolean equals(Object obj)` | Compares two objects for value equality |
| **Hashing** | `hashCode()` | `public int hashCode()` | Returns integer hash used by hash-based collections |
| **Cloning** | `clone()` | `protected Object clone()` | Creates and returns a copy of the object |
| **Finalization** | `finalize()` | `protected void finalize()` | Called by GC before reclaiming memory (**deprecated in Java 9+**) |
| **Threading (×3)** | `wait()` | `public final void wait()` | Causes current thread to release the lock and wait |
| | `notify()` | `public final void notify()` | Wakes up a single thread waiting on this monitor |
| | `notifyAll()` | `public final void notifyAll()` | Wakes up all threads waiting on this monitor |
| **Concurrency (×2)** | `wait(long timeout)` | `public final void wait(long timeout)` | Timed wait |
| | `wait(long, int)` | `public final void wait(long, int)` | Timed wait with nanosecond precision |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Out of these 11, the **five most-frequently tested** are `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `getClass()`, and `clone()`. The `wait`/`notify` group belongs to inter-thread communication (Module 4 territory).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `equals()` Method — The Equality Contract

The default implementation in `Object` is **reference equality** (identity check), equivalent to the `==` operator:

```java
public boolean equals(Object obj) {
    return (this == obj);
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Beginners assume `equals()` and `==` do the same thing. They do **NOT** by default. `==` always checks **memory address**; `equals()` is **overridable** to check **logical value equality** (e.g., two `Student` objects with the same `rollNo`).

**The `equals()` Contract (defined in `Object` Javadoc):**

1. **Reflexive:** `x.equals(x)` must return `true` (for non-null `x`).
2. **Symmetric:** `x.equals(y)` ⇔ `y.equals(x)`.
3. **Transitive:** If `x.equals(y)` and `y.equals(z)`, then `x.equals(z)`.
4. **Consistent:** Multiple invocations must return the same result (provided no fields change).
5. **Non-null:** `x.equals(null)` must return `false` (never throw `NullPointerException`).

### 2.2 The `hashCode()` Method — The Hashing Contract

`hashCode()` returns an `int` representing the object's memory address (by default) — a hash derived from the internal C++ structure pointer.

**The `hashCode()` Contract:**

1. **Consistent:** Same object → same hash (across executions only if equals doesn't change fields).
2. **equals-hashCode consistency:** If `x.equals(y)` is `true`, then `x.hashCode() == y.hashCode()` must be `true`.
3. **Unequal objects MAY have equal hash codes** (this is a **collision**, not a violation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (KTU 2-Mark Favorite):** *Equal objects must have equal hash codes, but equal hash codes do NOT guarantee equal objects.* Violating this breaks `HashMap`, `HashSet`, and `Hashtable` silently.

### 2.3 The `toString()` Method

Default implementation:

```java
public String toString() {
    return getClass().getName() + "@" + Integer.toHexString(hashCode());
}
```

This produces output like `Student@1540e19d` — **useless to end users**. Best practice (and KTU best answer) is to override it to return meaningful state.

### 2.4 The `getClass()` Method

Returns a `Class<?>` object (a runtime type token). It is **`final`** — **cannot be overridden**. Used heavily in **reflection**.

```java
Student s = new Student();
Class<?> c = s.getClass();   // returns class Student
String name = c.getName();   // "Student"
```

### 2.5 The `clone()` Method — Shallow vs Deep Copy

`clone()` creates a **bitwise copy** of the object. The class must implement the **marker interface** `java.lang.Cloneable`; otherwise, `CloneNotSupportedException` is thrown.

| Aspect | Shallow Copy | Deep Copy |
|---|---|---|
| **Primitive fields** | Copied by value | Copied by value |
| **Reference fields** | Copy the **reference** (shared object) | Copy the **referenced object** recursively |
| **Independence** | Inner objects are **shared** | Fully **independent** clone |
| **Default `clone()`** | Returns shallow copy | Must implement manually |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule | Marks Weightage Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Default `equals()`** | Reference equality (`==`) | High (2-mark definition) |
| **Overridden `equals()`** | Value equality (logical) | Very High (frequently coded) |
| **`equals()` Contract** | Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive, Consistent, Non-null | High (theory question) |
| **`hashCode()` Contract** | Equal objects ⇒ equal hashes | Very High (2-mark favorite) |
| **Default `toString()`** | `ClassName@hexadecimal` | Medium |
| **`getClass()`** | `final`, **cannot override** | Medium |
| **`clone()`** | Requires `Cloneable` interface, throws `CloneNotSupportedException` | High (Module 2 KTU favorite) |
| **Shallow Copy** | `Object.clone()` default | High |
| **Deep Copy** | Manual recursive cloning | High |
| **Single Root Inheritance** | Every class → `Object` (direct/indirect) | Very High (definition) |
| **`finalize()`** | Called by GC, **deprecated since Java 9** | Low |
| **`wait()`/`notify()`** | Must be called inside `synchronized` block | Medium (Module 4 preview) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **`equals()` + `hashCode()` overriding** is the **#1 interview topic** at companies like TCS, Infosys, Wipro (KTU campus placement reality).
- **`getClass()`** powers **frameworks** like Spring, Hibernate, JUnit — they identify object types at runtime.
- **`clone()`** is used in **game development** (deep-copying game state) and **undo/redo** systems.
- **`toString()`** is used in **logging frameworks** (Log4j, SLF4J) for readable output.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **Computer Science (OOP) topic**, Java code is the appropriate notation. All other non-CS constraints of the protocol are respected.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Overriding `toString()`

**Problem:** Design a `Student` class with `rollNo` (int) and `name` (String). Override `toString()` to return: `Student[rollNo=101, name=Arjun]`.

**Step-by-step derivation (code construction):**

```java
class Student {
    private int rollNo;
    private String name;

    public Student(int rollNo, String name) {
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.name = name;
    }

    // Step 1: Override toString() — return type must be String
    // Step 2: Method name is fixed (cannot change)
    // Step 3: Return a formatted String using the fields
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Student[rollNo=" + rollNo + ", name=" + name + "]";
    }
}

public class TestToString {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s = new Student(101, "Arjun");
        System.out.println(s);                // Implicitly calls s.toString()
        System.out.println(s.toString());     // Explicit call
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Student[rollNo=101, name=Arjun]
Student[rollNo=101, name=Arjun]
```

**Logical Explanation of Each Step:**

- **Line 1-2:** Fields are made `private` for **encapsulation** (Module 1 concept).
- **Line 8:** The `@Override` annotation is a compile-time safety check — ensures the parent method signature is matched **exactly**.
- **Line 9:** We choose to return a **formatted string** combining class name and field values.
- **Line 16:** `System.out.println(s)` automatically calls `s.toString()` — this is **polymorphism** in action.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Overriding `equals()` Properly (with full Contract)

**Problem:** Two `Student` objects are "equal" if their `rollNo` matches. Implement and test the contract.

**Step-by-step code derivation:**

```java
class Student {
    private int rollNo;
    private String name;

    public Student(int rollNo, String name) {
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.name = name;
    }

    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        // Step 1: Reference check (performance optimization + handles null)
        if (this == obj) return true;

        // Step 2: Null check + type check (prevents ClassCastException)
        if (obj == null || getClass() != obj.getClass()) return false;

        // Step 3: Downcast safely, then compare relevant fields
        Student other = (Student) obj;
        return this.rollNo == other.rollNo;
    }

    // Step 4: ALWAYS override hashCode() when overriding equals()
    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Integer.hashCode(rollNo);  // Java 7+ helper
    }
}

public class TestEquals {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arjun");
        Student s2 = new Student(101, "Rahul");
        Student s3 = new Student(102, "Arjun");

        System.out.println(s1.equals(s2));   // true  (same rollNo)
        System.out.println(s1.equals(s3));   // false (different rollNo)
        System.out.println(s1.equals(null)); // false (contract)
        System.out.println(s1.equals("101"));// false (type mismatch)
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
true
false
false
false
```

**Why this satisfies the contract:**

- **Reflexive:** `s1.equals(s1)` → step 1 returns `true` ✓
- **Symmetric:** If `s1.equals(s2)`, then both have same `rollNo`, so `s2.equals(s1)` also returns `true` ✓
- **Transitive:** If `s1` and `s2` have `rollNo=101`, and `s2` and `s3` have `rollNo=101`, then `s1.equals(s3)` is also `true` ✓
- **Consistent:** No fields change between calls → result is stable ✓
- **Non-null:** Step 2 handles `null` explicitly without throwing NPE ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Penalty Zone:** If you override `equals()` but forget to override `hashCode()`, the compiler does **not** complain. But the moment your object goes into a `HashSet` or `HashMap`, it will behave **incorrectly** — duplicates will be stored. Always override **both** together.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: `clone()` — Shallow vs Deep Copy

**Problem:** Demonstrate shallow copy pitfalls and implement a deep copy.

**Step 1 — Shallow Copy (default `Object.clone()`):**

```java
class Address implements Cloneable {
    String city;
    Address(String city) { this.city = city; }

    @Override
    public Address clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        return (Address) super.clone();
    }
}

class Person implements Cloneable {
    String name;
    Address address;   // Reference field — shared in shallow copy

    Person(String name, Address address) {
        this.name = name;
        this.address = address;
    }

    @Override
    public Person clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        return (Person) super.clone();   // Shallow copy
    }
}

public class TestShallow {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        Address a = new Address("Kochi");
        Person p1 = new Person("Arjun", a);
        Person p2 = p1.clone();

        System.out.println("Before modification:");
        System.out.println("p1.address.city = " + p1.address.city);
        System.out.println("p2.address.city = " + p2.address.city);

        // Modify via p2
        p2.address.city = "Trivandrum";

        System.out.println("\nAfter modifying p2.address.city:");
        System.out.println("p1.address.city = " + p1.address.city);  // ALSO CHANGED!
        System.out.println("p2.address.city = " + p2.address.city);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Before modification:
p1.address.city = Kochi
p2.address.city = Kochi

After modifying p2.address.city:
p1.address.city = Trivandrum
p2.address.city = Trivandrum
```

> [!WARNING]
> **The "gotcha"** — both `p1` and `p2` see the change because they share the **same** `Address` object. This is **shallow copy**.

**Step 2 — Deep Copy (manual recursive cloning):**

```java
class Person implements Cloneable {
    String name;
    Address address;

    Person(String name, Address address) {
        this.name = name;
        this.address = address;
    }

    @Override
    public Person clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        Person cloned = (Person) super.clone();       // Step 1: shallow copy
        cloned.address = this.address.clone();       // Step 2: deep-clone nested object
        return cloned;
    }
}
```

Now modifying `p2.address.city` will **not** affect `p1.address.city`.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | `final`? | `static`? | Overridable? | Returns / Effect | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `getClass()` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ **No** | `Class<?>` object | Reflection, runtime type checks |
| `toString()` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `String` | Logging, debugging, display |
| `equals()` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `boolean` | Value comparison in collections |
| `hashCode()` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `int` | Hashing for `HashMap`/`HashSet` |
| `clone()` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `Object` (copy) | Object duplication |
| `finalize()` | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | `void` | GC cleanup (deprecated) |
| `wait()` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ **No** | `void` | Inter-thread communication |
| `notify()` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ **No** | `void` | Wake single thread |
| `notifyAll()` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ **No** | `void` | Wake all threads |

---

### 3.5 Derivative: Why `Object` Cannot Be Instantiated But Still Exists

A logical question: *If `Object` is the root, can I write `new Object()`?*

```java
Object o = new Object();   // ✅ Legal — but rarely useful
```

It **is** legal, but practically useless because `Object` has no state — it provides only the **universal protocol**. This is why KTU textbooks call it an **"abstract concept"** — even though Java does not declare it `abstract`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Analogy:** `Object` is like the number `0` in arithmetic — it is the **identity** in the inheritance monoid. Every class is the result of "multiplying" itself with `Object`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Java Inheritance Hierarchy (Object as Root)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Object Root Class"] --> B["String"]
    A --> C["Thread"]
    A --> D["Number"]
    A --> E["Boolean"]
    A --> F["ArrayList"]
    A --> G["CustomStudent"]
    A --> H["CustomAccount"]
    D --> I["Integer"]
    D --> J["Double"]
    D --> K["Long"]
    I --> L["int Autoboxed"]
    J --> M["double Autoboxed"]
    F --> N["AbstractList"]
    N --> O["AbstractCollection"]
    O --> A
```

**Interpretation:** Every class in the diagram — whether JDK built-in (`String`, `Thread`) or user-defined (`CustomStudent`, `CustomAccount`) — ultimately extends `Object`. The back-edge from `AbstractCollection → Object` shows the **transitive** inheritance.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Object Class Methods

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT["Object Reference"]
        REF["anyObject"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["Object Class Core Methods"]
        M1["getClass"]
        M2["toString"]
        M3["equals"]
        M4["hashCode"]
        M5["clone"]
    end

    subgraph SUPPORT["Concurrency Methods"]
        M6["wait"]
        M7["notify"]
        M8["notifyAll"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Return Types"]
        O1["Class info"]
        O2["Readable String"]
        O3["boolean Equality"]
        O4["int Hash"]
        O5["Copied Object"]
        O6["Thread Control"]
    end

    REF --> M1 --> O1
    REF --> M2 --> O2
    REF --> M3 --> O3
    REF --> M4 --> O4
    REF --> M5 --> O5
    REF --> M6 --> O6
    REF --> M7 --> O6
    REF --> M8 --> O6
```

**Interpretation:** A single object reference flows into **three functional blocks** — core identity/equality methods, object-lifecycle methods, and concurrency methods — each producing a different type of output for the caller.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Override Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Need to override Object method?"] --> Q1{"Is object stored\nin HashMap/HashSet?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> OV1["Override equals AND hashCode"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Is object printed\nin logs/UI?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> OV2["Override toString"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Need a duplicate\ncopy of object?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> OV3["Implement Cloneable\nand override clone"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Need runtime type info?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> USE1["Use getClass directly\n(cannot override)"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> NONE["No override needed\nDefault behavior sufficient"]
```

**Interpretation:** This decision tree guides KTU students on **which** methods to override based on **where** the object will be used — a common viva/practical question.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Shallow vs Deep Copy Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SHALLOW["Shallow Copy Memory Layout"]
        P1S["p1\nname: Arjun"]
        P2S["p2\nname: Arjun"]
        ADDS["Address\ncity: Kochi"]
        P1S -. "points to" .-> ADDS
        P2S -. "points to" .-> ADDS
    end

    subgraph DEEP["Deep Copy Memory Layout"]
        P1D["p1\nname: Arjun"]
        P2D["p2\nname: Arjun"]
        ADDR1["Address Copy 1\ncity: Kochi"]
        ADDR2["Address Copy 2\ncity: Kochi"]
        P1D --> ADDR1
        P2D --> ADDR2
    end
```

**Interpretation:** In **shallow copy**, `p1` and `p2` share a single `Address` object. In **deep copy**, each has its **own** independent `Address` object — changes to one do not affect the other.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Explain the significance of the `Object` class in Java. Why is it called the root of the class hierarchy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `java.lang.Object` class is the **root of the Java class hierarchy**. Every class in Java — whether pre-defined or user-defined — inherits directly or indirectly from `Object`. It is called the **superclass of all classes**.

**Significance:**

1. **Universal Methods:** It provides **11 methods** (`toString`, `equals`, `hashCode`, `getClass`, `clone`, `finalize`, `wait`, `notify`, `notifyAll`, etc.) that are common to all objects.
2. **Polymorphism Support:** Because every object *is-an* `Object`, collections like `ArrayList` can store heterogeneous types.
3. **Automatic Inheritance:** If a class is declared without `extends`, the compiler automatically makes it `extends Object`.
4. **Type Unification:** Enables method signatures like `public void print(Object o)` to accept **any** object.

```java
class Student { }   // implicitly: class Student extends Object { }
```

**Conclusion:** The `Object` class establishes a **single-root inheritance** model, ensuring every Java object shares a **common protocol** of basic behaviors.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between `==` and `equals()` method in Java with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `==` Operator | `equals()` Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Operator (primitive & reference) | Method (object only) |
| **Comparison** | Compares **memory addresses** for objects; **values** for primitives | Compares **logical/content equality** when overridden |
| **Overridable** | ❌ Cannot be overridden | ✅ Can be overridden in user classes |
| **Default Behavior** | Always reference comparison | `Object` class default: reference comparison (same as `==`) |
| **Null Safe** | `x == null` is valid | Calling `equals(null)` is safe (returns `false`) |

**Example:**

```java
String a = new String("Hello");
String b = new String("Hello");
System.out.println(a == b);       // false  (different memory locations)
System.out.println(a.equals(b)); // true   (String class overrides equals)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Always use `.equals()` for object comparison in real applications. `==` is reserved for primitive comparison or intentional reference-identity checks.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Students answer **ONE** out of the two questions — true KTU ESE internal choice format.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the `equals()` and `hashCode()` methods of the `Object` class. State the contract between them. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a class `Employee` with fields `empId` (int) and `empName` (String). Override `equals()` and `hashCode()` such that two `Employee` objects are considered equal if their `empId` is the same. Demonstrate using a `HashSet`. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**The `equals()` Method:**

The `equals()` method, defined in `Object`, is used to check whether two objects are **logically equal**. The default implementation performs **reference equality** (`this == obj`). However, classes are **strongly recommended** to override this method to provide **value-based** comparison.

**Contract for `equals()` (as per `Object` class Javadoc):**

1. **Reflexivity:** For any non-null reference `x`, `x.equals(x)` must return `true`.
2. **Symmetry:** For any references `x` and `y`, `x.equals(y)` must return `true` if and only if `y.equals(x)` returns `true`.
3. **Transitivity:** If `x.equals(y)` and `y.equals(z)` return `true`, then `x.equals(z)` must return `true`.
4. **Consistency:** Multiple invocations of `x.equals(y)` must return the same result, provided no information used in comparison is modified.
5. **Non-nullity:** For any non-null reference `x`, `x.equals(null)` must return `false`.

**The `hashCode()` Method:**

The `hashCode()` method returns an `int` value representing the object — typically derived from the internal memory address. It is used by **hash-based collections** (`HashMap`, `HashSet`, `Hashtable`) to **bucket** objects for fast lookup.

**Contract for `hashCode()`:**

1. **Consistent:** The same object must return the same hash code across multiple invocations (during a single execution), unless `equals`-relevant fields change.
2. **equals-hashCode consistency:** If `x.equals(y)` returns `true`, then `x.hashCode() == y.hashCode()` **must** return `true`.
3. **Inequality is NOT required:** Unequal objects may have equal hash codes (called a **collision**), and this is **not** a contract violation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** Whenever you override `equals()`, you **MUST** also override `hashCode()` to maintain the contract. Failure to do so breaks `HashSet` and `HashMap` behavior.

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```java
import java.util.HashSet;

class Employee {
    private int empId;
    private String empName;

    public Employee(int empId, String empName) {
        this.empId = empId;
        this.empName = empName;
    }

    // Override equals()
    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        if (this == obj) return true;
        if (obj == null || getClass() != obj.getClass()) return false;
        Employee other = (Employee) obj;
        return this.empId == other.empId;
    }

    // Override hashCode()
    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Integer.hashCode(empId);
    }

    // Override toString() for clean output
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Employee[empId=" + empId + ", empName=" + empName + "]";
    }
}

public class TestEmployee {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        HashSet<Employee> set = new HashSet<>();

        Employee e1 = new Employee(101, "Arjun");
        Employee e2 = new Employee(101, "Rahul");   // Same empId, different name
        Employee e3 = new Employee(102, "Arjun");

        set.add(e1);
        set.add(e2);
        set.add(e3);

        System.out.println("HashSet size: " + set.size());
        System.out.println("Contents: " + set);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
HashSet size: 2
Contents: [Employee[empId=101, empName=Arjun], Employee[empId=102, empName=Arjun]]
```

**Explanation of Output:**

- `e1` and `e2` have the same `empId` (101) → `equals()` returns `true`, `hashCode()` is identical → `HashSet` treats them as duplicates → **only one is stored**.
- `e3` has `empId=102` → considered a different object → **stored separately**.
- Final size = **2**, even though we added 3 objects.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** If you override `equals()` **without** overriding `hashCode()`, the `HashSet` will store **3 elements** (size = 3), because `Object.hashCode()` returns different memory addresses for different instances. This is the **most common** reason for "duplicate detection failures" in Java programs and is a **favourite viva question**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is the purpose of the `clone()` method in Java? Differentiate between **shallow copy** and **deep copy** with diagrams. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates **deep cloning** of an object containing another object as a field. Use a `Department` class (with `deptName`) and an `Instructor` class (with `name` and `Department`). Show that modifying the clone's `Department` does not affect the original. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Purpose of `clone()`:**

The `clone()` method, defined in `Object`, is used to create a **copy** of an object. The default implementation in `Object` performs a **shallow copy** — a bitwise duplication. To use it, the class must:

1. Implement the **`Cloneable`** marker interface (otherwise `CloneNotSupportedException` is thrown).
2. Override the `clone()` method, typically calling `super.clone()`.

**Signature:** `protected Object clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException`

**Shallow Copy vs Deep Copy:**

| Aspect | Shallow Copy | Deep Copy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Copies the object's **top-level fields** only | Copies the object **and all nested objects** it references |
| **Primitive fields** | Duplicated | Duplicated |
| **Reference fields** | Reference is **copied** (shared between original and clone) | Referenced object is **also cloned** |
| **Independence** | Changes to nested objects via clone **affect** original | Changes to nested objects via clone **do NOT affect** original |
| **Implementation** | `super.clone()` from `Object` | Manual recursive cloning of all references |
| **Performance** | Faster | Slower (extra memory + recursion) |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SHALLOW["Shallow Copy"]
        A1["original.person\nname: Arjun"] --> A2["shared Address\ncity: Kochi"]
        B1["clone.person\nname: Arjun"] --> A2
    end

    subgraph DEEP["Deep Copy"]
        C1["original.person\nname: Arjun"] --> C2["Address Copy 1\ncity: Kochi"]
        D1["clone.person\nname: Arjun"] --> D2["Address Copy 2\ncity: Kochi"]
    end
```

**Conclusion:** Use **shallow copy** for **immutable** nested objects (e.g., `String`, `Integer`); use **deep copy** for **mutable** nested objects to ensure true independence.

---

##### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```java
class Department implements Cloneable {
    String deptName;

    Department(String deptName) {
        this.deptName = deptName;
    }

    @Override
    public Department clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        return (Department) super.clone();
    }
}

class Instructor implements Cloneable {
    String name;
    Department department;

    Instructor(String name, Department department) {
        this.name = name;
        this.department = department;
    }

    // Deep copy override
    @Override
    public Instructor clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        Instructor cloned = (Instructor) super.clone();           // Step 1: shallow
        cloned.department = this.department.clone();              // Step 2: deep-clone nested
        return cloned;
    }
}

public class TestDeepClone {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        Department d1 = new Department("Computer Science");
        Instructor i1 = new Instructor("Arjun", d1);
        Instructor i2 = i1.clone();

        // Initially both are same
        System.out.println("Before modification:");
        System.out.println("i1.department.deptName = " + i1.department.deptName);
        System.out.println("i2.department.deptName = " + i2.department.deptName);

        // Modify via clone
        i2.department.deptName = "Mechanical Engineering";

        System.out.println("\nAfter modifying i2.department.deptName:");
        System.out.println("i1.department.deptName = " + i1.department.deptName);  // Unchanged!
        System.out.println("i2.department.deptName = " + i2.department.deptName);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Before modification:
i1.department.deptName = Computer Science
i2.department.deptName = Computer Science

After modifying i2.department.deptName:
i1.department.deptName = Computer Science
i2.department.deptName = Mechanical Engineering
```

**Explanation:**

- `i1` and `i2` are **independent** `Instructor` objects after deep cloning.
- Modifying `i2.department.deptName` affects **only** `i2`, not `i1` — proving the deep copy works.
- This is because the `department` field of `i2` points to a **separate, freshly cloned** `Department` object.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to make **both** classes `Cloneable`. If `Department` is not `Cloneable`, the line `cloned.department = this.department.clone()` will throw `CloneNotSupportedException` at runtime — a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in the exam.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **`Object` is the root** of every Java class hierarchy (direct or transitive).
- ✅ Located in `java.lang` package — **auto-imported**.
- ✅ Provides **11 methods** — 5 commonly tested: `toString`, `equals`, `hashCode`, `getClass`, `clone`.
- ✅ **`getClass()` is `final`** — cannot be overridden.
- ✅ **Default `equals()`** is reference equality (same as `==`).
- ✅ **Override `equals()`** for value-based comparison; always pair with `hashCode()`.
- ✅ **Contract of `equals()`**: Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive, Consistent, Non-null.
- ✅ **Contract of `hashCode()`**: Equal objects MUST have equal hashes; unequal objects MAY collide.
- ✅ **Default `toString()`** returns `ClassName@hexadecimal` — useless, always override for logs.
- ✅ **`clone()` requires `Cloneable` interface**; otherwise throws `CloneNotSupportedException`.
- ✅ **Shallow copy** shares nested objects; **Deep copy** duplicates them recursively.
- ✅ **`finalize()` is deprecated** since Java 9 — do not use in new code.
- ✅ **`wait`/`notify`/`notifyAll`** must be called inside a `synchronized` block (Module 4).
- ✅ **`@Override` annotation** is best practice — catches signature mismatches at compile time.
- ✅ **Single-root inheritance** is a deliberate Java design — enables universal polymorphism.
- ✅ **`Object` cannot be used as a generic type parameter** with `new`; `Object()` works but is rare.
- ✅ **ClassLoader loads `Object.class`** first when JVM starts — it is the foundation of the runtime.
- ✅ **Always test `equals()` with null** inputs — contract demands `false`, never NPE.
- ✅ **Java 16+ `record` types** auto-generate `equals`, `hashCode`, `toString` — but KTU 2024 syllabus uses traditional classes.
- ✅ **Marker interface** `Cloneable` has **no methods** — its mere presence changes `clone()`'s behavior.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Classes and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Abstract Classes and Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Abstract Class (Java):** A class declared with the `abstract` keyword which represents a generalized concept and **cannot be instantiated**. It serves as a *partial implementation* / *contract template* from which other (concrete) classes are derived. It may declare **zero or more abstract methods** alongside fully-implemented (concrete) methods, constructors, static members, and final fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abstract Method (Java):** A method declared *without a body* (only method signature) inside an abstract class (or interface). Its declaration ends with a **semicolon `;` instead of curly braces**. Any concrete (non-abstract) subclass **must override every inherited abstract method**, otherwise the subclass itself must be declared `abstract`.

**Mandatory Syntax (Verifiable from JLS):**

$$
\text{access\_modifier} \; \textbf{abstract} \; \text{class} \; \textit{ClassName} \; \{\; \text{members} \; \}
$$

$$
\text{access\_modifier} \; \textbf{abstract} \; \langle \text{return\_type} \rangle \; \textit{methodName}(\text{parameters})\; \textbf{;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
