> **Memory Anchor for Exams:** The two indispensable keywords governing this topic in Java are `abstract class` and `abstract method`. The single most important rule is: **You cannot write `new AbstractClassName()` in Java** — the compiler rejects it with *"AbstractClassName is abstract; cannot be instantiated"*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **generic "Vehicle Manufacturing Blueprint"** stored at a central R&D office of an automobile company.

* The blueprint itself (the *abstract class*) is **not a real car** — you cannot drive a blueprint, just as you cannot instantiate an abstract class.
* The blueprint **defines what every vehicle must do** — for example, it leaves blank boxes saying *"Specify Engine Type"*, *"Specify Brake System"*. These are **abstract methods** — they declare *what must be done*, but the blueprint does not say *how*.
* Different factories (the *concrete subclasses* like `Car`, `Bike`, `Truck`) **take the same blueprint and fill in the blanks** with their own engine, brakes, etc. — this is **method overriding**.
* The blueprint *also* provides some common, fully-filled details that all vehicles share, e.g., a standard *"Chassis Number Location"* — these are **concrete methods** living inside the abstract class.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam One-Liner Analogy:** *"An abstract class is like a partially filled form — it has some filled fields and some empty fields that the applicant (subclass) must complete before submission (instantiation)."*

## 1.3 Why Abstract Classes Exist in OOP

| Engineering Need | How Abstract Class Solves It |
|---|---|
| Enforce a common protocol across related classes | Declares abstract method signatures every subclass must implement |
| Share common code among siblings | Holds concrete methods, fields, constructors reused by all subclasses |
| Prevent creation of incomplete / generic objects | Forbids direct instantiation, forcing meaningful specialized objects |
| Achieve runtime polymorphism through dynamic dispatch | Reference type is abstract, object type is concrete — late binding |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Rules — The "Why" and "How"

The following rules are **enforced by the Java compiler** and are the most frequently tested points in KTU Module 2 of PEMRT415.

1. **Declaration Rule** — An abstract class *must* be declared with the keyword `abstract`. Example: `public abstract class Shape { }`
   * **Why:** It is the syntactic flag the compiler uses to disable direct allocation of objects of that type.
2. **Instantiation Rule** — `new AbstractClass()` produces a **compile-time error**.
   * **Why:** Instantiating would create an object with potentially unimplemented (abstract) methods, leaving the object behaviorally incomplete.
3. **Abstract Method Rule** — A method is abstract *iff* it is declared with the `abstract` keyword *and* has **no body** (terminates with `;`).
   * **Why:** A method without a body defines only a contract — the *what* — and leaves the *how* to subclasses.
4. **Containment Rule** — An abstract class **may contain zero abstract methods**. Such a class is still abstract because it is declared `abstract`.
   * **Why:** Sometimes the goal is solely to prevent instantiation and force subclassing, not to declare incomplete behavior.
5. **Concrete Method Rule** — An abstract class *may* contain fully implemented methods, and subclasses inherit them as usual.
   * **How:** Code reuse is achieved through normal inheritance — no special dispatch mechanism is needed for inherited concrete methods.
6. **Constructor Rule** — An abstract class *can* declare constructors. They are **invoked implicitly through `super(...)` from the subclass constructor chain**.
   * **Why:** Even though you cannot `new AbstractClass()`, the abstract class's fields still need to be initialized when a concrete subclass object is constructed.
7. **Static and Final Rule** — Abstract classes can hold `static` methods, `static` fields, and `final` methods. They **cannot** be `abstract` and `final` simultaneously (the two keywords are semantically contradictory).
8. **Subclass Obligation Rule** — A first concrete (non-abstract) subclass **must override every inherited abstract method**; otherwise the compiler raises *"Class X is not abstract and does not override abstract method Y in Z"*.
9. **Transitive Abstractness Rule** — A subclass that does *not* wish to implement some abstract methods may itself be declared `abstract`, propagating the obligation further down the hierarchy.
10. **Reference-Type Polymorphism Rule** — A reference of an abstract class type **can** legally refer to an object of any concrete subclass. Method calls on this reference resolve at runtime via dynamic dispatch (virtual method invocation).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Rule Sheet

The table below is your **exam-day cheat sheet** for Abstract Classes. Every row is a potential 1–2 mark sub-question.

| \# | Rule / Fact | Verbatim Compiler Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| R1 | Instantiation forbidden | `Shape s = new Shape();` → compile error |
| R2 | Abstract method must be overridden | Subclass without override → must itself be `abstract` |
| R3 | Abstract class may have 0 abstract methods | Permitted; instantiation still forbidden |
| R4 | Abstract class can have constructors | Called via `super(...)` from subclass |
| R5 | `abstract` and `final` together | Compile error — contradictory modifiers |
| R6 | `abstract` and `static` together (on methods) | Compile error — static cannot be overridden |
| R7 | `abstract` and `private` together (on methods) | Compile error — private cannot be overridden |
| R8 | Abstract method signature must match in subclass | Access level may widen (e.g. `protected` → `public`) |
| R9 | Reference of abstract type, object of concrete type | Allowed; enables runtime polymorphism |
| R10 | Abstract class can implement interfaces | Must implement interface methods or stay abstract |
| R11 | Multiple inheritance of *type* via interfaces | An abstract class can `implements` many interfaces |
| R12 | A concrete subclass can be instantiated only when | All inherited abstract methods are implemented |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Forbidden Combinations** (memorize for viva + MCQ):  
> `abstract + final`, `abstract + static`, `abstract + private` — *all three raise a compile-time error when applied to a method*.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Abstract classes are foundational in frameworks where a **template-method design pattern** is used:

* **Java Servlet API** — `HttpServlet` is abstract; concrete servlets override `doGet()` and `doPost()`.
* **Java AWT** — `Component` is an abstract superclass; `Button`, `Label`, `TextField` are concrete subclasses.
* **Spring Framework** — `AbstractController`, `AbstractView` provide partial implementations.
* **JDBC** — `DriverManager`, `AbstractDataSource`-like adapters offer skeletons for database drivers.
* **Game Development** — An `Entity` abstract class defines `update()` and `render()` as abstract, leaving sprite-specific behavior to `Player`, `Enemy`, `NPC`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Code Logic Derivations

Because this topic is rooted in Java language semantics rather than algebraic physics, the "derivations" here are **compiler-level behavioral derivations**: given a piece of Java source, we derive what the JVM will permit, reject, or dispatch at runtime.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Verifying the Instantiation Rule

**Source Code Under Analysis:**

$$
\texttt{abstract class Shape} \; \{\; \texttt{void draw()\{ System.out.println("Drawing"); \}\;\}}
$$

$$
\texttt{class Circle extends Shape} \; \{\; \texttt{void draw()\{ System.out.println("Circle"); \}\;\}}
$$

**Step-by-Step Compiler Trace:**

* **Step 1:** Compiler reads `abstract class Shape`. It registers `Shape` in the class table with the flag `ACC_ABSTRACT = 0x0400`.
* **Step 2:** When the user writes `new Shape()`, the compiler checks the access flags of `Shape` in the constant pool. The `ACC_ABSTRACT` bit is set → instantiation rejected.
* **Step 3:** When the user writes `new Circle()`, the compiler first verifies that `Circle` does **not** carry `ACC_ABSTRACT` and that every inherited abstract method from `Shape` is implemented. Since `Circle.draw()` overrides `Shape.draw()`, the check passes.
* **Step 4:** During `new Circle()`, the JVM allocates heap memory, then invokes `<init>` of `Circle`, which chains to `super()` (the constructor of `Shape`) implicitly. Initialization of any fields in `Shape` completes before `Circle`'s body executes.

**Final Verdict:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{new Shape()} &\;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Compile-time error:} \\
&\text{"Shape is abstract; cannot be instantiated"} \\
\texttt{new Circle()} &\;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Success: object of concrete type created}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Method Resolution at Runtime (Dynamic Dispatch)

Consider a polymorphic call:

$$
\texttt{Shape s = new Circle();} \quad\quad \texttt{s.draw();}
$$

**Resolution Algorithm (What the JVM Does):**

* **Step 1 — Compile-time reference type check:** The compiler verifies that `draw()` exists in the reference type `Shape`. It is declared in `Shape`, so the call is *type-checked* successfully.
* **Step 2 — Bytecode emission:** The compiler emits an `invokevirtual` instruction (not `invokespecial`) with the symbolic reference `Shape.draw : ()V`.
* **Step 3 — Runtime lookup:** The JVM locates the actual object's class in the heap (`Circle`). It walks up the class hierarchy `Circle → Shape → Object` searching for the method `draw`. The first match is `Circle.draw()`.
* **Step 4 — Dispatch:** The JVM pushes the current `this` (the `Circle` object) onto the operand stack and jumps to `Circle.draw()`.
* **Result:** `Circle`'s version executes, even though the reference type was `Shape`.

**Symbolic form of the dispatch equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{method\_executed} &= \arg\min_{m \in M} \; \text{depth}\bigl(\text{class}(\text{this}), \; m\bigr) \\
&\quad \text{subject to} \; m.\text{signature} = s
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M$ is the set of all methods named $m$ discovered by walking the class chain upward, and the chosen $m$ is the *nearest* (smallest depth) match.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Access Modifier Widening in Override

A subtle rule tested frequently:

$$
\text{If } \texttt{Shape.draw() is declared} \; \texttt{public abstract}, \; \text{then} \; \texttt{Circle.draw()} \; \text{must be} \; \texttt{public} \; \text{or wider (none wider exists).}
$$

Conversely:

$$
\text{If } \texttt{Shape.draw() is declared} \; \texttt{protected abstract}, \; \texttt{Circle.draw()} \; \text{may be} \; \texttt{protected} \;\lor\; \texttt{public}.
$$

This is a strict *covariant widening* rule, enforced by the JLS §8.4.8.3.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Output Prediction Table

Given the snippet below, predict the output of `main()`. The matrix exposes the dynamic-dispatch behavior step by step.

$$
\texttt{Animal a = new Dog();} \; \texttt{a.sound();} \; \texttt{a.move();}
$$

| Reference type | Object type | Method `sound()` invoked? | Method `move()` invoked? | Actual Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Animal` | `Dog` | Yes — overridden in `Dog` | Yes — only declared in `Animal` (concrete) | "Bark" then "Animal moves" |
| `Animal` | `Cat` | Yes — overridden in `Cat` | Yes — inherited from `Animal` | "Meow" then "Animal moves" |

**Logical flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{a.sound()} &\;\xrightarrow{\text{invokevirtual}}\; \text{walk class chain Dog → Animal} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{execute Dog.sound()} \\
\texttt{a.move()} &\;\xrightarrow{\text{invokevirtual}}\; \text{walk class chain Dog → Animal} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{execute Animal.move()}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compiler Constraint that the Compiler Enforces (Step-by-step):**  
> (1) Does the method exist in the *reference type*? → Otherwise, **compile error**.  
> (2) Does the *object type* override that method? → If yes, that override is called; if no, the parent's version is called.  
> Steps (1) and (2) together yield the famous "compile-time reference, runtime object" rule.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Abstract Class Hierarchy

The diagram below models a typical KTU Module 2 question: an abstract `Shape` superclass with two abstract methods and one concrete method, extended by two concrete subclasses.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Shape {
        <<abstract>>
        -String color
        +area() double
        +perimeter() double
        +displayColor() void
    }
    class Circle {
        -double radius
        +Circle(String c, double r)
        +area() double
        +perimeter() double
    }
    class Rectangle {
        -double length
        -double width
        +Rectangle(String c, double l, double w)
        +area() double
        +perimeter() double
    }
    Shape <|-- Circle : extends
    Shape <|-- Rectangle : extends
    Circle ..> Shape : super constructor call
    Rectangle ..> Shape : super constructor call
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU exam-ready description):**

* `Shape` is shown in **italics-with-annotation `<<abstract>>`** denoting that it is abstract and cannot be instantiated.
* `area()` and `perimeter()` are listed **without parameters block body**, indicating they are abstract (no implementation).
* `displayColor()` is shown with parentheses and `void` return, indicating a concrete method.
* The arrows `Shape <|-- Circle` and `Shape <|-- Rectangle` denote inheritance (the "is-a" relationship).
* The dashed arrows `..>` represent the constructor `super(...)` call chain that runs when a `Circle` or `Rectangle` object is constructed.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Runtime Method Dispatch Flow

The second diagram captures the dynamic-dispatch mechanism at runtime when a polymorphic call is made.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Shape s equals new Circle] --> B[Compiler emits invokevirtual Shape dot draw]
    B --> C[JVM reads actual class of object on heap]
    C --> D{Does Circle override draw?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Execute Circle draw method body]
    D -- No --> F[Walk up to parent Shape]
    F --> G{Does Shape implement draw?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Execute Shape draw method body]
    G -- No --> I[AbstractMethodError at runtime]
    E --> J[Return to caller]
    H --> J[Return to caller]
```

**Walk-through for the examiner's answer key:**

* The reference `s` has static (compile-time) type `Shape`.
* The object referred to has dynamic (runtime) type `Circle`.
* The `invokevirtual` JVM instruction performs **late binding** — the method to invoke is decided at runtime, not at compile time.
* If neither `Circle` nor any ancestor up to `Object` provides a concrete `draw()`, a `java.lang.AbstractMethodError` is raised when the call is attempted.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Place of Abstract Class in a System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ApplicationLayer
        App[Main Application Code]
    end
    subgraph AbstractionLayer
        AbsCls[Abstract Class Shape]
    end
    subgraph ImplementationLayer
        Impl1[Concrete Class Circle]
        Impl2[Concrete Class Rectangle]
        Impl3[Concrete Class Triangle]
    end
    App -->|holds reference of type| AbsCls
    AbsCls -.defines contract.-> Impl1
    AbsCls -.defines contract.-> Impl2
    AbsCls -.defines contract.-> Impl3
    Impl1 -->|invokes super constructor| AbsCls
    Impl2 -->|invokes super constructor| AbsCls
    Impl3 -->|invokes super constructor| AbsCls
```

**Engineering interpretation:** The application layer is **decoupled** from concrete implementations. To add a new shape (e.g., `Pentagon`), only the `ImplementationLayer` is touched — the application code remains unchanged. This is the **Open/Closed Principle** in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q: Define an abstract class in Java. Can an abstract class be instantiated? Justify your answer with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Definition (1 mark):** An abstract class in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that may contain abstract methods (methods without a body) as well as concrete methods. It represents a generalized concept and serves as a base class for other classes.
* **Instantiation Rule (1 mark):** No, an abstract class cannot be instantiated. The Java compiler prohibits the use of the `new` operator on an abstract class because the object would be behaviorally incomplete.
* **Example (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{abstract class Animal \{ public abstract void sound(); \}} \\
&\texttt{// new Animal(); } \rightarrow \texttt{ compile-time error}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: Differentiate between an abstract class and a concrete class in Java. Mention any three differences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference):**

| \# | Abstract Class | Concrete Class |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declared using the `abstract` keyword | Declared without the `abstract` keyword |
| 2 | Cannot be instantiated; `new` is rejected | Can be instantiated freely |
| 3 | May contain abstract (unimplemented) methods | All methods must have a body |
| 4 | Used to provide a partial implementation / contract | Used to provide a complete implementation |
| 5 | A subclass must override all abstract methods or itself be abstract | No obligation of overriding |

*(Any three of the above are acceptable for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE WRITING**
> (1) When asked to "explain with an example", students often **forget to declare the subclass as concrete (no `abstract` keyword) and forget to override every abstract method** — both omissions are independent 1-mark deductions.  
> (2) Do **not** put a body on an abstract method — writing `{ }` after an `abstract` method signature costs you 1 full mark instantly.  
> (3) Access modifier must be **equal or wider** in the override (e.g., `protected abstract` in parent → `public` in child, NOT `private`).  
> (4) Constructors of the abstract class are **not inherited**, but **are called** via `super()` — confusing these two facts loses 1 mark.  
> (5) When drawing a class diagram, mark the abstract class with `<<abstract>>` *stereotype* — this is the KTU-expected UML convention.

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of abstract classes and abstract methods in Java with a suitable example. Mention the rules that must be followed while using abstract classes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** An abstract class is a partially implemented class declared with the `abstract` keyword. It can declare abstract methods (no body) and concrete methods (with body). It cannot be instantiated but can be subclassed. An abstract method defines the *what* (contract) and leaves the *how* (implementation) to subclasses.
2. **Example (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{abstract class Shape} &\;\{\; \texttt{String color;} \\
&\;\;\texttt{abstract double area();} \\
&\;\;\texttt{abstract double perimeter();} \\
&\;\;\texttt{void displayColor()\{ System.out.println(color); \}\;\}} \\
\texttt{class Circle extends Shape} &\;\{\; \texttt{double r;} \\
&\;\;\texttt{Circle(String c, double r)\{ color=c; this.r=r; \}} \\
&\;\;\texttt{double area()\{ return 3.14 * r * r; \}} \\
&\;\;\texttt{double perimeter()\{ return 2 * 3.14 * r; \}\;\}}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Rules (2 marks):**
   * Abstract class cannot be instantiated.
   * Abstract method has no body and ends with `;`.
   * Subclass must override all abstract methods or be declared abstract itself.
   * Abstract class can have constructors (called via `super()` from subclass).
   * An abstract class can have zero abstract methods.

**(b)** Write a Java program to create an abstract class `Employee` with attributes `name` and `id`, an abstract method `calculateSalary()`, and a concrete method `display()`. Derive two concrete classes `FullTimeEmployee` and `PartTimeEmployee` that override `calculateSalary()` appropriately. Demonstrate polymorphism using an array of `Employee` references. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
abstract class Employee {
    String name;
    int id;
    Employee(String name, int id) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
    }
    abstract double calculateSalary();
    void display() {
        System.out.println("ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name);
    }
}

class FullTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    double monthlySalary;
    FullTimeEmployee(String n, int i, double s) {
        super(n, i);
        this.monthlySalary = s;
    }
    double calculateSalary() {
        return monthlySalary;
    }
}

class PartTimeEmployee extends Employee {
    double hourlyRate;
    int hours;
    PartTimeEmployee(String n, int i, double r, int h) {
        super(n, i);
        this.hourlyRate = r;
        this.hours = h;
    }
    double calculateSalary() {
        return hourlyRate * hours;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee[] emps = new Employee[2];
        emps[0] = new FullTimeEmployee("Arun", 1, 50000);
        emps[1] = new PartTimeEmployee("Meera", 2, 300, 80);
        for (Employee e : emps) {
            e.display();
            System.out.println("Salary: " + e.calculateSalary());
        }
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step trace for the examiner (2 marks for correct structure, 2 marks for correct logic, 1 mark each for `FullTimeEmployee` and `PartTimeEmployee` overrides, 1 mark for polymorphic loop):**

* The reference type `Employee[]` allows storage of any subclass object.
* The call `e.calculateSalary()` is **dynamically dispatched** — the JVM resolves it based on the actual object type at runtime.
* Expected output: lines showing ID, Name, and the respective salary for both employees.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the various restrictions and rules associated with abstract classes and abstract methods in Java. Provide code snippets to illustrate at least four such rules. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Rule | Code Snippet (2 marks for 4 rules) | Explanation (remaining marks for description) |
|---|---|---|
| Cannot instantiate | `Shape s = new Shape(); // ERROR` | Compiler rejects because the class is abstract |
| Must override all abstract methods | Subclass without override → must be `abstract` | Otherwise the subclass itself is incomplete |
| `abstract` + `final` not allowed | `abstract final void show(); // ERROR` | Contradictory: abstract demands override, final forbids it |
| `abstract` + `static` not allowed on methods | `abstract static void show(); // ERROR` | Static methods are not overridden but hidden |
| `abstract` + `private` not allowed | `abstract private void show(); // ERROR` | Private methods are not visible to subclasses |
| Access modifier can be widened in override | `protected abstract` → `public` in child | JLS §8.4.8.3 — covariant widening permitted |

Each rule must be paired with a one-line justification for full marks.

**(b)** Design an abstract class `BankAccount` with fields `accountNumber`, `balance`, and abstract methods `deposit()` and `withdraw()`. Implement two subclasses `SavingsAccount` (minimum balance rule) and `CurrentAccount` (overdraft rule). Write a driver class to demonstrate runtime polymorphism. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
abstract class BankAccount {
    String accountNumber;
    double balance;
    BankAccount(String acc, double bal) {
        this.accountNumber = acc;
        this.balance = bal;
    }
    abstract void deposit(double amount);
    abstract void withdraw(double amount);
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Acc: " + accountNumber + ", Bal: " + balance);
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    final double MIN_BALANCE = 1000;
    SavingsAccount(String acc, double bal) { super(acc, bal); }
    void deposit(double amt) { balance += amt; }
    void withdraw(double amt) {
        if (balance - amt >= MIN_BALANCE) balance -= amt;
        else System.out.println("Insufficient balance (min violated)");
    }
}

class CurrentAccount extends BankAccount {
    double overdraftLimit;
    CurrentAccount(String acc, double bal, double od) {
        super(acc, bal);
        this.overdraftLimit = od;
    }
    void deposit(double amt) { balance += amt; }
    void withdraw(double amt) {
        if (balance + overdraftLimit >= amt) balance -= amt;
        else System.out.println("Overdraft exceeded");
    }
}

public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount[] accs = new BankAccount[2];
        accs[0] = new SavingsAccount("SA001", 5000);
        accs[1] = new CurrentAccount("CA001", 1000, 2000);
        for (BankAccount a : accs) {
            a.deposit(500);
            a.withdraw(2000);
            a.display();
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (per KTU 2024 scheme):**
* Abstract class `BankAccount` design: **1.5 marks**
* `SavingsAccount` with min-balance logic: **2 marks**
* `CurrentAccount` with overdraft logic: **2 marks**
* Polymorphic driver class with output trace: **1.5 marks**

**Key trace:**

* `accs[0].withdraw(2000)` → balance drops from 5500 to 3500 (still ≥ 1000 min). Output: `Acc: SA001, Bal: 3500.0`.
* `accs[1].withdraw(2000)` → balance 1500 → 1500 − 2000 = −500, but overdraft allows up to −2000, so it succeeds. Output: `Acc: CA001, Bal: -500.0`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before the exam**

* **Keyword:** `abstract` is the only keyword you write; no body on the abstract method, terminator is a semicolon.
* **Cannot instantiate:** `new AbstractClass()` is a **compile-time** error — know this cold.
* **Concrete subclass obligation:** Override *every* inherited abstract method **or** declare the subclass `abstract`.
* **Forbidden combos (memorize):** `abstract + final`, `abstract + static`, `abstract + private` — all on methods, all compile errors.
* **Constructors exist in abstract classes:** They are called via `super(...)` from the subclass constructor chain, never via direct `new`.
* **Static fields/methods are allowed** inside an abstract class — they are not instance-level.
* **Access modifier rule:** Subclass override can **widen** access (e.g., `protected` → `public`), but **cannot narrow** it.
* **Polymorphism equation:** *Reference type decides what is callable; object type decides which version runs.* This is the single most-tested line of reasoning in 14-mark questions.
* **Abstract class vs. interface (very high yield):** Abstract class can hold state (non-final fields) and constructors; an interface (pre-Java 8) cannot. Both can declare abstract methods.
* **UML convention:** Mark abstract class with `<<abstract>>` stereotype and abstract methods in *italics* in diagrams.
* **Runtime error if not overridden:** If a concrete subclass somehow misses an override (e.g., wrong signature), invoking that method raises `java.lang.AbstractMethodError` at runtime.
* **Use case for the exam:** Abstract class is the right tool when subclasses share **code (concrete methods)** and **state (fields)** AND must obey a **common contract (abstract methods)**. If there is no shared state, prefer an interface.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using final with Inheritance. Packages and Interfaces
Exception Handling - Checked Exceptions
Unchecked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unchecked Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unchecked Exceptions** (also called **Runtime Exceptions**) in Java are exceptions that **are not verified by the compiler at compile-time**. They belong to the `java.lang.RuntimeException` class hierarchy and any of its subclasses. The compiler does not force the programmer to either **catch** them using a `try-catch` block or **declare** them in the method's `throws` clause.

In formal Object Oriented Programming terminology (as per KTU 2024 Scheme):

> An unchecked exception is a subclass of `RuntimeException`, which itself is a subclass of `Exception`. Because these exceptions typically arise from **programming logic errors, faulty assumptions, or invalid client-supplied data**, the Java compiler assumes that such errors are *preventable* through better code and therefore does not enforce strict exception handling at the compilation stage.

The **Java Language Specification (JLS)** formally categorizes unchecked exceptions as:
- All instances of class `RuntimeException`
- All instances of class `Error`
- Both these classes are part of `java.lang` and are subclasses of `java.lang.Throwable`

```java
// Official Java Exception Hierarchy (simplified)
java.lang.Object
    └── java.lang.Throwable
            ├── java.lang.Error        (Unchecked - JVM/Serious failures)
            └── java.lang.Exception    (Most are Checked)
                    ├── java.io.IOException          (Checked)
                    ├── java.sql.SQLException        (Checked)
                    └── java.lang.RuntimeException   (Unchecked - our focus)
                            ├── ArithmeticException
                            ├── NullPointerException
                            ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
                            ├── ClassCastException
                            ├── IllegalArgumentException
                            │       └── NumberFormatException
                            ├── IndexOutOfBoundsException
                            │       ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
                            │       └── StringIndexOutOfBoundsException
                            └── UnsupportedOperationException
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy: The "Speed Breaker Without a Signboard"**

Imagine you are driving on a road in Kerala.

- **Checked Exception** = A *pothole* with a big yellow signboard saying "**POTHOLE AHEAD — SLOW DOWN**". The signboard is a *compile-time warning* — the traffic police (Java compiler) will **not allow** you to drive past without acknowledging it (i.e., without writing a `try-catch` or `throws`). You MUST declare a plan.

- **Unchecked Exception** = A **sudden speed breaker** that appears on your road **without any warning signboard**. There was no compile-time warning. If you hit it at high speed, your car gets damaged (the program crashes). The fix is **not** to put a signboard after the speed breaker appears — the fix is to **drive carefully** in the first place (i.e., write better logic, validate inputs, check `null` before using it).

In short:
- Checked = *“I can foresee this problem; handle it now.”*
- Unchecked = *“This problem only appears if your logic is buggy; fix the logic.”*

## 1.3 Why "Unchecked"?

The exception is called **unchecked** because:

1. The **compiler does not check** whether the code handles it.
2. You can **omit** both the `try-catch` block and the `throws` declaration.
3. The Java runtime detects and **throws** the exception automatically when a faulty condition is encountered.
4. The responsibility shifts from *"handling the error"* to *"preventing the error"*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Whenever asked "Why is it called unchecked?", write:
> *"Because the Java compiler does not perform a check at compile-time to verify whether the method either handles the exception using a try-catch block or declares it in the throws clause."*

## 1.4 Key Characteristics Summary

| Characteristic | Unchecked Exception | Checked Exception |
|----------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Base Class** | `RuntimeException` | `Exception` (not RuntimeException) |
| **Compiler Check** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Mandatory `try-catch`** | ❌ Optional | ✅ Mandatory |
| **Mandatory `throws`** | ❌ Optional | ✅ Mandatory |
| **Origin** | Programming logic errors | External resource failures |
| **Recoverable?** | Usually NO (fix the code) | Usually YES (retry, alternate flow) |
| **Examples** | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` | `IOException`, `SQLException` |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Exception Class Tree (Detailed View)

The **complete** understanding of unchecked exceptions requires tracing the **inheritance chain** in Java's exception framework:

```
java.lang.Throwable                     ← Ultimate superclass
│
├── java.lang.Error                     ← UNCHECKED (serious JVM problems)
│     ├── VirtualMachineError
│     │     ├── OutOfMemoryError
│     │     └── StackOverflowError
│     ├── LinkageError
│     │     ├── NoClassDefFoundError
│     │     └── IncompatibleClassChangeError
│     └── AssertionError
│
└── java.lang.Exception                 ← MOST are CHECKED
      ├── java.lang.RuntimeException    ← UNCHECKED ← OUR FOCUS
      │     ├── ArithmeticException
      │     ├── NullPointerException
      │     ├── ClassCastException
      │     ├── IllegalArgumentException
      │     │     └── NumberFormatException
      │     ├── IndexOutOfBoundsException
      │     │     ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
      │     │     └── StringIndexOutOfBoundsException
      │     ├── NegativeArraySizeException
      │     ├── UnsupportedOperationException
      │     ├── NoSuchElementException
      │     └── ConcurrentModificationException
      │
      ├── java.io.IOException           ← CHECKED
      │     ├── FileNotFoundException
      │     └── EOFException
      └── java.sql.SQLException         ← CHECKED
```

## 2.2 How Unchecked Exceptions Work — Step-by-Step Operational Flow

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding the **runtime throwing mechanism** is critical for KTU's 14-mark questions.

1. **JVM Monitors Execution:** During program execution, the JVM continuously watches for conditions that indicate programming errors (e.g., division by zero, null dereference, invalid array index).

2. **Detection of Faulty Condition:** When a faulty condition is encountered, the JVM **instantiates** the appropriate `RuntimeException` subclass with a descriptive message.

3. **Stack Unwinding Begins:** The exception object is **thrown**. The JVM then begins **stack unwinding** — moving up the call stack looking for a `catch` block that matches the exception type.

4. **Search for a Matching Handler:** Each method in the call stack is checked. If a `catch` block exists that handles this exception type (or its superclass), control transfers to that block.

5. **Program Termination (Default):** If **no** `catch` block is found all the way to `main()`, the default exception handler kicks in, prints the **stack trace**, and **terminates** the program abnormally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The "unchecked" nature means the compiler doesn't *force* you to write a `catch` block. But if a `catch` block IS present, it WILL catch the exception at runtime. The freedom is in *not being forced*, not in *not being able to catch*.

## 2.3 The Most Important Unchecked Exceptions (KTU High-Yield Catalog)

### 2.3.1 `ArithmeticException`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | An exceptional arithmetic condition occurs (e.g., integer division by zero) |
| **Thrown By** | JVM during division/modulo operations on integers |
| **Note** | Floating-point division by zero returns `Infinity`/`NaN`; it does NOT throw |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |

### 2.3.2 `NullPointerException` (NPE)

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | Application attempts to use `null` in a case where an object is required |
| **Common Triggers** | Calling method on null reference, accessing field of null, `null.length()`, `null[0]`, auto-unboxing null |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |

### 2.3.3 `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | Array accessed with an illegal index (negative or ≥ length) |
| **Parent Class** | `IndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |

### 2.3.4 `ClassCastException`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | Code attempts to cast an object to a subclass of which it is not an instance |
| **Example** | Casting `Object` containing `String` to `Integer` |
| **Note** | Compiler prevents obvious downcasts, but it can occur with generic/Object types at runtime |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐ (High) |

### 2.3.5 `NumberFormatException`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | Application tries to convert a string to a numeric type, but the string is not properly formatted |
| **Example** | `Integer.parseInt("abc")` |
| **Parent Class** | `IllegalArgumentException` |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐ (High) |

### 2.3.6 `IllegalArgumentException`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | A method has been passed an illegal or inappropriate argument |
| **Use Case** | Often thrown **manually** by programmers to validate arguments |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐ (High) |

### 2.3.7 `IndexOutOfBoundsException` (Parent Class)

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | Some type of indexing is out of range (general form) |
| **Subclasses** | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐⭐ (High) |

### 2.3.8 `UnsupportedOperationException`

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| **Thrown When** | The requested operation is not supported (e.g., modifying an unmodifiable collection) |
| **KTU Frequency** | ⭐ (Moderate) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Equation/Concept Table)

| # | Concept | Formula/Rule | When Applied |
|---|---------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | Compilation Check | `CompilerCheck(RuntimeException) = false` | All unchecked exceptions |
| 2 | Optional Handling | `try-catch` and `throws` are **optional** | All unchecked exceptions |
| 3 | NPE Trigger | `obj.method()` when `obj == null` | `NullPointerException` |
| 4 | ArithmeticException | `int x = a / b;` where `b == 0` | `ArithmeticException` |
| 5 | Array Bounds | `0 ≤ index < arr.length` | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| 6 | Cast Safety | `actualType instanceof TargetType` must be `true` | `ClassCastException` |
| 7 | NumberFormat | `str` must match `[-+]?\d+` for int | `NumberFormatException` |
| 8 | Float Div By Zero | `x / 0.0` where x is float/double | Returns `Infinity` (no exception) |
| 9 | Null Autoboxing | `Integer i = null; int x = i;` | `NullPointerException` |
| 10 | Error vs Exception | `Error` ⊂ Unchecked but indicates **JVM failure** | Out of KTU scope for handling |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember Unchecked Exceptions: "NAACU PIN"**
> - **N**ullPointerException
> - **A**rithmeticException
> - **A**rrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
> - **C**lassCastException
> - **U**nsupportedOperationException
> - **P**lain `RuntimeException`
> - **I**llegalArgumentException
> - **N**umberFormatException

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Usage of Unchecked Exception Concept |
|--------|--------------------------------------|
| **Software Testing** | JUnit/NUnit test frameworks throw `AssertionError` to indicate failed test conditions |
| **API Design** | Libraries throw `IllegalArgumentException` to signal invalid client inputs (e.g., Spring Framework, Guava) |
| **Big Data Systems** | Hadoop/Spark use unchecked exceptions extensively for fail-fast error reporting |
| **Android Development** | `NullPointerException` is the most common crash — Android lint tools specifically warn against potential NPEs |
| **Game Development** | Engine crashes due to `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` are common without proper bounds checking |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Demonstration 1: `ArithmeticException` — Division by Zero

**Problem Statement:** Show how an `ArithmeticException` is thrown and demonstrate its handling.

### Step 1 — Program Without Any Handling

```java
public class ArithmeticDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int numerator = 100;
        int denominator = 0;
        int result = numerator / denominator;   // ❌ Problem occurs HERE
        System.out.println("Result: " + result);
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — Runtime Trace (What Happens Step-by-Step)

```
Line 1: int numerator = 100;       →  numerator holds 100
Line 2: int denominator = 0;       →  denominator holds 0
Line 3: int result = 100 / 0;      →  JVM detects division by zero
                                      JVM creates new ArithmeticException("/ by zero")
                                      JVM begins stack unwinding
                                      No catch block found in main()
                                      Default handler runs:
                                          - Prints stack trace
                                          - Terminates program with non-zero exit code
Line 4: System.out.println(...);   →  NEVER REACHED
```

### Step 3 — Output Produced

```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
    at ArithmeticDemo.main(ArithmeticDemo.java:4)
```

### Step 4 — With Proper Handling (Defensive Programming)

```java
public class ArithmeticHandled {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int numerator = 100;
        int denominator = 0;
        try {
            int result = numerator / denominator;
            System.out.println("Result: " + result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Cannot divide by zero.");
            System.out.println("Details: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues normally...");
    }
}
```

### Step 5 — Output With Handling

```
Error: Cannot divide by zero.
Details: / by zero
Program continues normally...
```

### Step 6 — Why This Is Unchecked (Compiler Proof)

```java
// This code COMPILES SUCCESSFULLY even though there is no try-catch or throws!
public class ProvesUnchecked {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        causeError();      // ✅ No compile error!
    }

    // Note: NO "throws ArithmeticException" in the signature below.
    // If ArithmeticException were CHECKED, this would be a compile error.
    public static void causeError() {
        int x = 10 / 0;
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fact that `causeError()` compiles WITHOUT `throws ArithmeticException` is the literal proof that ArithmeticException is unchecked.** The compiler does not enforce any check.

---

## 3.2 Demonstration 2: `NullPointerException` — The Most Common Java Bug

**Problem Statement:** Trace all possible NPE triggers in a single program.

### Step 1 — Multiple NPE Triggers

```java
public class NPEDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s = null;                  // Step A: reference is null

        // Trigger 1: Method call on null
        System.out.println(s.length());   // ❌ NPE here

        // Trigger 2: Accessing field on null
        // (uncomment to test) - String s2 = null; s2.toUpperCase();

        // Trigger 3: Auto-unboxing null
        Integer i = null;                 // Integer can be null
        int j = i;                        // ❌ NPE during unboxing

        // Trigger 4: Accessing null array element
        String[] arr = null;
        System.out.println(arr[0]);       // ❌ NPE here

        // Trigger 5: Synchronizing on null
        // (uncomment to test) - synchronized(s) { }
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — NPE Stack Trace Anatomy

```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NullPointerException
    at NPEDemo.main(NPEDemo.java:6)
         ↑          ↑                  ↑
         |          |                  +-- The class & file location
         |          +-- The exception class name
         +-- The thread name
```

### Step 3 — Defensive Coding (KTU Best Practice)

```java
public class SafeNPEHandling {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s = null;

        // Strategy 1: Explicit null check
        if (s != null) {
            System.out.println(s.length());
        } else {
            System.out.println("String is null, defaulting to 0");
        }

        // Strategy 2: Try-catch (less preferred but valid)
        try {
            System.out.println(s.length());
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught NPE: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Tip:** When comparing check vs unchecked, remember that handling NPE with try-catch is generally considered **anti-pattern**. The recommended approach is **defensive programming** with null checks (`if (x != null)`) or **Optional** class (Java 8+).

---

## 3.3 Demonstration 3: `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` — Detailed Trace

**Problem Statement:** Show how array bounds are violated and what message is generated.

### Step 1 — Code With Violation

```java
public class ArrayBoundsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] marks = new int[5];         // Valid indices: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
        // marks has length 5

        // Trigger 1: Index = length (off-by-one error)
        // marks[5] → ❌ AIOOBE

        // Trigger 2: Negative index
        // marks[-1] → ❌ AIOOBE

        // Trigger 3: Loop runs one too many
        for (int i = 0; i <= marks.length; i++) {   // Bug: should be i < length
            System.out.println(marks[i]);           // ❌ Throws on i = 5
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — The Boundary Condition Formula

$$\text{Valid Index Range} = [0, \text{array.length} - 1]$$

$$\text{For an array of length } n = 5: \text{Valid indices} = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$$

$$\text{Any access to index } i \text{ where } i < 0 \text{ OR } i \geq n \text{ throws AIOOBE}$$

### Step 3 — Corrected Code With Boundary Check

```java
public class SafeArrayAccess {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] marks = {85, 90, 78, 92, 88};

        // CORRECT: using marks.length as strict upper bound
        for (int i = 0; i < marks.length; i++) {   // ✅ i < length, not ≤
            System.out.println(marks[i]);
        }

        // CORRECT: explicit bounds check
        int index = 7;
        if (index >= 0 && index < marks.length) {
            System.out.println(marks[index]);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Index " + index + " is out of bounds!");
        }
    }
}
```

---

## 3.4 Demonstration 4: `ClassCastException` — The Type Mismatch

**Problem Statement:** Show when `ClassCastException` occurs at runtime despite the code compiling.

### Step 1 — The Setup (Code That Compiles Fine)

```java
public class ClassCastDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step A: Object reference holds a String
        Object obj = "Hello KTU";          // ✅ Compiles (String IS-A Object)

        // Step B: Try to cast to Integer
        Integer num = (Integer) obj;       // ✅ Compiles! But fails at runtime
        System.out.println(num);
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — Why Does This Compile?

The compiler uses **static type analysis**:
- `obj` is declared as `Object`
- `Integer` is a subclass of `Object`
- The compiler **assumes** the cast *might* be valid

The JVM uses **dynamic type checking**:
- At runtime, the actual object is a `String`
- `String` is **NOT** a subclass of `Integer`
- JVM throws `ClassCastException`

### Step 3 — Output and Exception Trace

```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ClassCastException:
    class java.lang.String cannot be cast to class java.lang.Integer
    (java.lang.String and java.lang.Integer are in module java.base of loader 'bootstrap')
    at ClassCastDemo.main(ClassCastDemo.java:7)
```

### Step 4 — The Safe Pattern: `instanceof` Check

```java
public class SafeCastDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Object obj = "Hello KTU";

        // GUARD the cast with instanceof
        if (obj instanceof Integer) {
            Integer num = (Integer) obj;
            System.out.println(num);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Cannot cast: obj is not an Integer.");
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 5 — The instanceof Truth Formula

$$\text{Cast } (TargetType) \text{ object is safe} \iff \text{object instanceof TargetType} = \text{true}$$

---

## 3.5 Demonstration 5: `NumberFormatException` — Invalid String to Number

**Problem Statement:** Convert strings to integers and observe the failure pattern.

### Step 1 — Code Demonstrating the Issue

```java
public class NumberFormatDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String valid = "123";
        String invalid = "abc";
        String mixed = "12abc";

        // Case 1: Valid string — works
        int a = Integer.parseInt(valid);
        System.out.println("a = " + a);    // Output: a = 123

        // Case 2: Pure letters — throws
        int b = Integer.parseInt(invalid);  // ❌ NumberFormatException
        System.out.println("b = " + b);
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — Exception Trace

```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NumberFormatException: For input string: "abc"
    at java.base/java.lang.NumberFormatException.forInputString(NumberFormatException.java:67)
    at java.base/java.lang.Integer.parseInt(Integer.java:668)
    at NumberFormatDemo.main(NumberFormatDemo.java:11)
```

### Step 3 — Defensive Solution

```java
public class SafeNumberParse {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String userInput = "abc123";

        try {
            int value = Integer.parseInt(userInput);
            System.out.println("Parsed value: " + value);
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid number format: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // Alternative: regex-based validation
        if (userInput.matches("-?\\d+")) {
            int value = Integer.parseInt(userInput);
            System.out.println("Safe-parsed: " + value);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Input contains non-numeric characters.");
        }
    }
}
```

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use What?

| Exception | Best Detection Strategy | Best Recovery Strategy | KTU Recommended Pattern |
|-----------|-------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| `ArithmeticException` | Pre-check divisor | Set default value | `if (divisor != 0) divide()` |
| `NullPointerException` | `if (obj != null)` | Use `Optional` | Null checks at boundaries |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Verify `index < length` | Return `Optional` | `for (i = 0; i < arr.length; i++)` |
| `ClassCastException` | `instanceof` check | Skip or default value | `if (obj instanceof Type)` |
| `NumberFormatException` | Regex validation | Use `try-catch` | `if (str.matches("\\d+"))` |
| `IllegalArgumentException` | Validate input first | Throw custom message | Manual `throw new IllegalArgumentException()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Whenever you see a runtime error in a question, your answer should mention: (1) what class it belongs to, (2) when it is thrown, (3) a code example, (4) how to prevent it. This 4-part structure scores full marks.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Runtime Flow When an Unchecked Exception Occurs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Program Execution] --> B[Method A Calls Method B]
    B --> C[Method B Calls Method C]
    C --> D[Method C Executes Faulty Code]
    D --> E{Faulty Condition<br/>Detected?}

    E -- No --> F[Continue Normal Execution]
    E -- Yes --> G[JVM Instantiates<br/>RuntimeException]

    G --> H[Stack Unwinding Begins<br/>Search Call Stack]

    H --> I{Method C has<br/>catch block<br/>for this exception?}

    I -- Yes --> J[Transfer Control to<br/>catch Block in C]
    I -- No --> K[Pop C, Move to B]

    K --> L{Method B has<br/>catch block?}
    L -- Yes --> J
    L -- No --> M[Pop B, Move to A]

    M --> N{Method A has<br/>catch block?}
    N -- Yes --> J
    N -- No --> O[Pop A, Move to main]

    O --> P{main has<br/>catch block?}
    P -- Yes --> J
    P -- No --> Q[Default Exception Handler<br/>Runs]

    Q --> R[Print Stack Trace<br/>to System.err]
    R --> S[Terminate Program<br/>with Non-Zero Exit Code]

    J --> T[Execute Recovery Code]
    T --> U[Continue Normal Flow<br/>After catch Block]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: What is an unchecked exception in Java? Give two examples.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> An **unchecked exception** is an exception that occurs at the time of program execution and is **not checked by the compiler** at compile-time. These exceptions are subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`. The compiler does not enforce the programmer to either catch them using a `try-catch` block or declare them in a `throws` clause.
>
> They typically occur due to **programming errors** like invalid arithmetic operations, null references, or invalid array indexing.
>
> **Two Examples:**
>
> 1. **`ArithmeticException`** — Thrown when an integer is divided by zero (e.g., `int x = 10 / 0;`).
>
> 2. **`NullPointerException`** — Thrown when a `null` reference is used to access a method or field (e.g., `String s = null; s.length();`).
>
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Two examples with explanation (1 mark each)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Base Class** | `java.lang.Exception` (not RuntimeException) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` |
| **Compiler Check** | Compiler enforces handling | Compiler does NOT enforce handling |
| **Occurrence** | At runtime (but predictable) | At runtime (due to programming errors) |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |
| **Mandatory Handling** | Must use `try-catch` or `throws` | Optional; can be left unhandled |
| **Recovery** | Usually recoverable | Usually indicates code bug |

> **Mark Distribution:** Table with 5-6 rows (1 mark) + 1-2 sentence summary (1 mark) + Examples for each type (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1): 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **unchecked exceptions** in Java with a neat diagram of the exception hierarchy. Mention any **five** important unchecked exception classes. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition (2 marks):**
Unchecked exceptions in Java are exceptions that belong to the `RuntimeException` class hierarchy and are **not verified at compile-time**. The Java compiler does not require the programmer to handle them using `try-catch` or declare them using `throws`. They typically represent programming logic errors.

**Exception Hierarchy Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Object
  └── Throwable
        ├── Error (Unchecked)
        └── Exception
              ├── IOException (Checked)
              └── RuntimeException (Unchecked)
                      ├── ArithmeticException
                      ├── NullPointerException
                      ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
                      ├── ClassCastException
                      └── NumberFormatException
```

**Five Important Unchecked Exceptions (3 marks — 1 each, with brief description):**

1. **`ArithmeticException`** — Thrown for exceptional arithmetic conditions, e.g., integer division by zero.
2. **`NullPointerException`** — Thrown when an application attempts to use `null` where an object is required.
3. **`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`** — Thrown to indicate that an array has been accessed with an illegal index.
4. **`ClassCastException`** — Thrown when an object cannot be cast to the specified type.
5. **`NumberFormatException`** — Thrown when a string cannot be parsed into a numeric format.

---

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the handling of `ArithmeticException` and `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. Explain how the program works. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

```java
public class ExceptionHandlingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // ============ ArithmeticException Demo ============
        System.out.println("--- ArithmeticException Demo ---");
        int a = 50, b = 0;
        try {
            int result = a / b;                    // Risky operation
            System.out.println("Result: " + result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
            System.out.println("You cannot divide by zero!");
        }

        // ============ ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException Demo ============
        System.out.println("\n--- ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException Demo ---");
        int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
        try {
            for (int i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) {  // Bug: i <= length
                System.out.println("arr[" + i + "] = " + arr[i]);
            }
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
            System.out.println("Array index exceeded the valid range!");
        }

        System.out.println("\nProgram execution completed normally.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**

- The first `try-catch` block performs integer division `a / b` where `b = 0`. The JVM detects this and throws an `ArithmeticException`, which is caught and handled gracefully. The program does NOT crash.
- The second `try-catch` block iterates from `i = 0` to `i <= arr.length` (off-by-one error). When `i = 3`, the array has only 3 elements (indices 0, 1, 2), so `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is thrown and caught.
- After both exception handlers execute, the program continues normally and prints the final message.

**Output (1 mark):**
```
--- ArithmeticException Demo ---
Caught: / by zero
You cannot divide by zero!

--- ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException Demo ---
arr[0] = 10
arr[1] = 20
arr[2] = 30
Caught: Index 3 out of bounds for length 3
Array index exceeded the valid range!

Program execution completed normally.
```

> **Mark Distribution:** Code (3 marks) + Output (1 mark) + Explanation (3 marks)

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain `NullPointerException` and `ClassCastException` in Java. For each, provide: (i) when it is thrown, (ii) a code example, and (iii) how to prevent it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**1. NullPointerException (NPE)**

- **(i) When Thrown (1 mark):** `NullPointerException` is thrown when the program attempts to use an object reference that has the value `null`. Common scenarios include:
  - Invoking a method on a `null` reference
  - Accessing or modifying a field of a `null` object
  - Taking the length of a `null` array
  - Accessing elements of a `null` array
  - Auto-unboxing a `null` wrapper object

- **(ii) Code Example (1 mark):**
```java
String name = null;
int length = name.length();   // ❌ Throws NullPointerException
```

- **(iii) Prevention (1 mark):**
```java
String name = null;
if (name != null) {                       // Defensive null check
    System.out.println(name.length());
} else {
    System.out.println("Name is null");
}
```

**2. ClassCastException**

- **(i) When Thrown (0.5 mark):** `ClassCastException` is thrown when the code attempts to cast an object to a subclass of which it is not an instance. It happens at runtime when the actual type of the object is incompatible with the target type of the cast.

- **(ii) Code Example (1 mark):**
```java
Object obj = "Hello World";
Integer num = (Integer) obj;    // ❌ ClassCastException at runtime
```

- **(iii) Prevention (0.5 mark):**
```java
Object obj = "Hello World";
if (obj instanceof Integer) {              // instanceof guard
    Integer num = (Integer) obj;
} else {
    System.out.println("Cannot cast: not an Integer");
}
```

**Why Both Are Unchecked (1 mark):**
Both `NullPointerException` and `ClassCastException` extend `RuntimeException`. The compiler cannot determine at compile-time whether a reference is `null` or what the actual runtime type of an `Object` is, so it does not enforce their handling.

---

**(b)** With a suitable Java program, demonstrate how `NumberFormatException` is generated when converting a string to an integer. Show both the **faulty code** (without handling) and the **corrected code** (with handling). Explain the output. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Faulty Code (Without Handling) — 2 marks:**

```java
public class NumberFormatFaulty {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String data = "Kerala123";          // Contains letters
        int value = Integer.parseInt(data); // ❌ Throws NumberFormatException
        System.out.println("Value: " + value);
    }
}
```

**Output (1 mark):**
```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NumberFormatException: For input string: "Kerala123"
    at java.base/java.lang.Integer.parseInt(Integer.java:652)
    at NumberFormatFaulty.main(NumberFormatFaulty.java:5)
```

**Corrected Code (With Handling) — 2 marks:**

```java
public class NumberFormatCorrected {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String data = "Kerala123";

        // Method 1: Using try-catch
        try {
            int value = Integer.parseInt(data);
            System.out.println("Parsed value: " + value);
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid number format!");
            System.out.println("Error message: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // Method 2: Using regex pre-validation (BEST PRACTICE)
        if (data.matches("\\d+")) {                    // Checks all digits
            int value = Integer.parseInt(data);
            System.out.println("Valid number: " + value);
        } else {
            System.out.println("'" + data + "' contains non-numeric characters.");
        }

        System.out.println("Program ended gracefully.");
    }
}
```

**Output of Corrected Code (1 mark):**
```
Invalid number format!
Error message: For input string: "Kerala123"
'Kerala123' contains non-numeric characters.
Program ended gracefully.
```

**Explanation (1 mark):**
`NumberFormatException` is thrown by methods like `Integer.parseInt()` when the input string is not a valid representation of a number. The first `try-catch` block safely catches the exception and prints a friendly error. The second approach uses regex `\d+` to validate the string BEFORE attempting the conversion, which is the recommended approach in real-world applications.

> **Mark Distribution:** Faulty code (2) + Output (1) + Corrected code (2) + Output (1) + Explanation (1)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the base class:** Students often write "`ArithmeticException` is an unchecked exception" without mentioning that it **extends `RuntimeException`**. This loses 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing `Error` with `Exception`:** Students sometimes list `OutOfMemoryError` as an unchecked *exception*. It is actually an unchecked `Error`, not an `Exception`. Be precise.
>
> 3. **Writing only the catch block:** When asked to "demonstrate" an unchecked exception, students often jump straight to `try-catch` and skip the original unhandled version. The KTU key wants BOTH — first show it crashes, then show it handled.
>
> 4. **Wrong inheritance in diagrams:** Drawing `IOException` under `RuntimeException` is a common mistake. Always verify the tree before drawing.
>
> 5. **Mixing up `IndexOutOfBoundsException` and its subclasses:** `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is a SUBCLASS of `IndexOutOfBoundsException`, not the other way around.
>
> 6. **Missing `instanceof` in `ClassCastException` answers:** The standard prevention always involves the `instanceof` operator. Writing just `try-catch` for prevention loses 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Using float for division by zero:** `5.0 / 0.0` does NOT throw `ArithmeticException` — it returns `Infinity`. Only **integer** division by zero throws it.
>
> 8. **Forgetting to throw `IllegalArgumentException` manually:** In KTU 14-mark questions, mentioning that `IllegalArgumentException` is often **thrown manually by the programmer** (not just by JVM) earns extra credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Unchecked Exceptions**

- 🔑 **Definition:** Unchecked exceptions are subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException` and are **not checked by the compiler** at compile-time.

- 🔑 **Compiler Behavior:** The compiler does NOT require `try-catch` or `throws` for them.

- 🔑 **When They Occur:** At **runtime** (during program execution).

- 🔑 **Root Cause:** Almost always a **programming error** (logic bug, not handled input).

- 🔑 **Most Common Unchecked Exceptions (Top 6):**
  1. `ArithmeticException` — integer division by zero
  2. `NullPointerException` — null dereference
  3. `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` — invalid array index
  4. `ClassCastException` — invalid type cast
  5. `NumberFormatException` — invalid string to number
  6. `IllegalArgumentException` — bad argument to method

- 🔑 **Float Division by Zero:** Does **NOT** throw — returns `Infinity` (Double) or `NaN`.

- 🔑 **`ClassCastException` Prevention:** Always use `instanceof` before casting.

- 🔑 **`NullPointerException` Prevention:** Use null checks (`if (x != null)`) or `Optional` class.

- 🔑 **`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` Formula:** Valid indices are `[0, length - 1]`.

- 🔑 **`NumberFormatException` is a subclass** of `IllegalArgumentException`.

- 🔑 **Best Practice:** Prevent unchecked exceptions with **defensive programming** rather than catch them — they indicate bugs that should be fixed, not handled.

- 🔑 **Errors vs Exceptions:** `Error` and `RuntimeException` are both unchecked, but `Error` represents JVM failures (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) that applications should NOT try to catch.

- 🔑 **Mnemonic:** **"NAACU PIN"** for top 8 unchecked exceptions.

- 🔑 **KTU Keyword Triggers:** When the question says "compiler does not check" → answer "unchecked". When it says "I/O operation failed" → answer "checked".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: try Block and catch Clause
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** try Block and catch Clause

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> In Java's exception handling framework, a **`try` block** is a guarded region of code that monitors statements capable of throwing runtime exceptions, while a **`catch` clause** is an exception handler that intercepts and processes the thrown exception object. Together they form the fundamental two-component syntactic unit of structured error handling in OOP.

The general syntax mandated by the Java Language Specification (JLS §14.20) is:

```java
try {
    // Protected code: statements that may throw an exception
} catch (ExceptionType1 e1) {
    // Handler for ExceptionType1
} catch (ExceptionType2 e2) {
    // Handler for ExceptionType2
}
```

### Key Technical Terms (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Guarded Region**: The lexical scope enclosed by `{ }` immediately following the `try` keyword.
- **Exception Object**: A subclass instance of `java.lang.Throwable` propagated up the call stack.
- **Exception Handler**: The executable body of a `catch` block bound to a specific exception type.
- **Polymorphic Dispatch**: The JVM's mechanism for matching a thrown exception to the appropriate `catch` parameter based on type compatibility.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Trampoline & Paramedic" Model**
> 
> Imagine you are learning to perform a **somersault** on a high platform:
> 
> | Element | Real-World Analogy | Java Equivalent |
> |---|---|---|
> | Platform jump (risky action) | Attempting the somersault | `try { }` block |
> | Trampoline below | Safety net that catches you if you fall | `catch (ExceptionType e) { }` |
> | Paramedic on trampoline | The medic decides *what to do* next | Code inside `catch` body |
> | Missing trampoline | Unhandled exception → program crashes | `Stack trace & abrupt termination` |
> 
> **Plain English**: The `try` block says, *"I am about to do something risky."* The `catch` clause says, *"If something goes wrong of THIS type, here's my recovery plan."*

### First-Read Summary for Beginners

- **Without try-catch**: One bad input → entire program crashes (like a phone switching off on a single error).
- **With try-catch**: One bad input → program detects it, handles gracefully, and continues running (like a phone showing "Invalid PIN, try again").

---

## 1.3 Academic Context Block

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mapping:**
> - **Course Outcome (CO) Addressed**: CO3 — *Implement robust Java programs using exception handling, multithreading, and I/O streams.*
> - **Bloom's Taxonomy Level**: L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze)
> - **Module Weightage**: Exception handling typically carries **15–20%** marks in KTU ESE for OOP.
> - **Prerequisite Knowledge**: Inheritance hierarchy, `Throwable` class, method calling stack.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How `try-catch` Actually Works

The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) executes a `try-catch` construct through a precise **6-step runtime protocol**:

1. **Entry into `try` block**: JVM pushes a special *exception table entry* onto the runtime stack mapping the `try` block's program counter (PC) range to its associated `catch` handlers.
2. **Sequential execution**: Statements inside the `try` block execute top-to-bottom in normal flow.
3. **Exception detection**: If a statement throws an exception object (either explicitly via `throw` or implicitly by JVM — e.g., `/ by zero`), normal control flow **aborts immediately**.
4. **Stack unwinding begins**: The JVM walks back up the call stack, popping frames until it finds a frame whose exception table contains a matching handler.
5. **Type matching**: The thrown exception's class is compared (using `instanceof` semantics) against each `catch` parameter type in source order. **The first match wins**.
6. **Handler execution**: The matching `catch` block's body executes. After it completes, control resumes **after the entire try-catch construct** (not inside the `try`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule**: Once an exception is thrown inside a `try` block, **the remaining statements in that `try` block are skipped** — execution never returns to that `try` block for the current invocation.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Component

| Component | Purpose | Why It Exists |
|---|---|---|
| `try { }` | Marks risky code | Tells compiler/JVM where to install exception handlers |
| `catch (Type e)` | Catches & binds the exception object | Allows access to exception details (`e.getMessage()`) |
| Exception parameter `e` | Reference to thrown object | Enables inspection, logging, or rethrowing |
| Curly braces `{ }` | Define block scope | Localizes variable declarations & limits `e`'s lifetime |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Rules You Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

| # | Rule | Example / Exception |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | A `try` block **must** be followed by at least one `catch` **or** a `finally` | `try{} catch{}` ✔, `try{} finally{}` ✔, `try{}` alone ✘ |
| 2 | Multiple `catch` blocks are allowed (multi-catch since Java 7) | `catch(IOException \| SQLException e)` |
| 3 | `catch` types must be ordered **subclass → superclass** | `catch(NullPointerException)` before `catch(Exception)` |
| 4 | The exception parameter `e` is **block-scoped** — cannot be used outside `catch` | Compile error if referenced outside |
| 5 | A `try` block **cannot** appear without a method or static initializer | Standalone `try{}` outside method is illegal |
| 6 | If no exception occurs, `catch` blocks are **completely skipped** | Zero overhead in normal path |
| 7 | Only one `catch` block executes per exception (first match wins) | Remaining catches are bypassed |
| 8 | After `catch` completes, execution resumes **after the try-catch** | Not back inside `try` |
| 9 | `catch` can be **empty** (silently swallow exception — *bad practice*) | `catch(Exception e) { }` — KTU penalizes this |
| 10 | `Throwable`, `Exception`, `RuntimeException` are catchable | `Error` and its subclasses generally not caught |

---

## 2.4 Multi-Catch Clause (Java 7+ Feature — High KTU Yield)

```java
try {
    // risky I/O and DB operations
} catch (IOException | SQLException ex) {
    logger.error("Operation failed: " + ex.getMessage());
    throw ex;  // rethrow is allowed; effectively final
}
```

**Rules:**
- Types separated by `|` must **not** have an inheritance relationship (no `IOException | Exception`).
- The parameter `ex` is **effectively final** — cannot be reassigned.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Banking Software** | Validate withdrawal amount; catch `InsufficientFundsException` |
| **Network Programming** | Handle dropped connections via `SocketException` |
| **Database Apps** | Recover from `SQLException` (duplicate keys, deadlock) |
| **Embedded Systems (IoT)** | Catch sensor-read failures to keep main loop alive |
| **Web Servers (Tomcat/Spring)** | Catch per-request exceptions without crashing the whole server |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Programming Note**: Since PEMRT415 is a *Computer Science* course (Object Oriented Programming), Java code examples are integral and pedagogically necessary — this is an explicit exception to the no-code rule for non-CS subjects.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Arithmetic Exception (Division by Zero)

### Problem Statement
Write a Java program that attempts to divide two integers entered by the user. The program must handle the case where the denominator is zero without terminating abnormally.

### Complete Annotated Solution

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class DivideDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        
        try {
            // ----- Protected (risky) region -----
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int a = sc.nextInt();
            
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int b = sc.nextInt();
            
            int result = a / b;          // ⚠ Throws ArithmeticException if b == 0
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
            
            System.out.println("This line is SKIPPED if exception occurs");
            
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            // ----- Exception Handler -----
            System.out.println("❌ Error: Division by zero is not allowed.");
            System.out.println("Exception class : " + e.getClass().getName());
            System.out.println("Message         : " + e.getMessage());
        }
        
        System.out.println("✅ Program continues after try-catch...");
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

**Case A: Input `10` and `2` (no exception)**

| Step | Action | JVM State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enter `try` block | Exception table active |
| 2 | Read `a = 10` | Normal flow |
| 3 | Read `b = 2` | Normal flow |
| 4 | Compute `10 / 2 = 5` | No exception thrown |
| 5 | Print `Result = 5` | Normal flow |
| 6 | Print *"This line is SKIPPED..."* | Normal flow |
| 7 | Exit `try` → skip all `catch` blocks | First-match not triggered |
| 8 | Print *"Program continues..."* | Resumes after construct |

**Case B: Input `10` and `0` (exception thrown)**

| Step | Action | JVM State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enter `try` block | Exception table active |
| 2 | Read `a = 10` | Normal flow |
| 3 | Read `b = 0` | Normal flow |
| 4 | Evaluate `10 / 0` | **JVM throws `new ArithmeticException("/ by zero")`** |
| 5 | Statement at line `result = a/b` aborts | Stack unwinding begins |
| 6 | "This line is SKIPPED..." is **NOT printed** | Control jumps out of `try` |
| 7 | Match `catch (ArithmeticException e)` | Type match ✔ (first and only) |
| 8 | Execute `catch` body | `e` bound to thrown object |
| 9 | Print error messages | Normal flow inside handler |
| 10 | Exit `catch` | Resumes after construct |
| 11 | Print *"Program continues..."* | Program survives ✔ |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Multiple Catch Blocks (Array Index Out of Bounds)

### Problem Statement
Demonstrate the use of multiple `catch` clauses to handle different exception types separately.

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
        
        try {
            System.out.println("Length of args = " + args.length);
            int n = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);          // ⚠ NumberFormatException
            int value = arr[n];                          // ⚠ ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            int result = 100 / value;                    // ⚠ ArithmeticException
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
            
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler 1: Invalid array index → " + e.getMessage());
            
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler 2: Input is not a number → " + e.getMessage());
            
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler 3: Math error → " + e.getMessage());
            
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Handler 4 (Generic): " + e.getMessage());
        }
        
        System.out.println("Program ended normally.");
    }
}
```

### KTU-Style Analytical Matrix — Trace Table

Suppose the program is run with `java MultiCatchDemo hello`.

| Statement | Throws? | Which Handler? | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| `args.length` | No | — | Safe operation |
| `Integer.parseInt("hello")` | **Yes** | `NumberFormatException` | "hello" not parseable as int |
| `arr[n]` | No | — | Never reached |
| `100/value` | No | — | Never reached |
| `System.out.println("Result...")` | No | — | Never reached |
