**Execution Outcome**: Control jumps to **Handler 2**, executes it, then resumes after the try-catch printing *"Program ended normally."*

### Critical Ordering Rule Demonstrated

The hierarchy of these exceptions is:

$$
\text{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} \rightarrow \text{IndexOutOfBoundsException} \rightarrow \text{RuntimeException} \rightarrow \text{Exception} \rightarrow \text{Throwable}
$$

If we wrote `catch(Exception e)` **before** `catch(ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)`, the compiler would reject it because `Exception` is a superclass → the subclass catch would become **unreachable code** (compile-time error).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: try-catch with Method Call Stack

```java
class StackDemo {
    static void methodA() {
        methodB();          // Call chain: A → B → C
    }
    
    static void methodB() {
        methodC();
    }
    
    static void methodC() {
        int x = 10 / 0;     // ⚠ ArithmeticException thrown HERE
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            methodA();
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught in main(): " + e.getMessage());
            e.printStackTrace();   // Prints full stack trace
        }
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Stack Unwinding

```
Call Stack at moment of throw:
┌──────────────────────────┐
│ main()                   │ ← catch block located HERE
├──────────────────────────┤
│ methodA()                │
├──────────────────────────┤
│ methodB()                │
├──────────────────────────┤
│ methodC()  ← 10/0 here   │ ← Exception originates HERE
└──────────────────────────┘
```

1. `methodC()` throws `ArithmeticException`.
2. `methodB()` has **no try-catch** → frame popped, exception propagates up.
3. `methodA()` has **no try-catch** → frame popped, exception propagates up.
4. `main()` has matching `catch` → exception caught, bound to `e`.

The `printStackTrace()` output will show all three frames:
```
java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
    at StackDemo.methodC(StackDemo.java:9)
    at StackDemo.methodB(StackDemo.java:5)
    at StackDemo.methodA(StackDemo.java:2)
    at StackDemo.main(StackDemo.java:14)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Control Flow of try-catch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start] --> B[Enter try block]
    B --> C{Execute statement}
    C --> D{Exception thrown?}
    D -- No --> E[Next statement]
    E --> F{More statements?}
    F -- Yes --> C
    F -- No --> G[Exit try normally]
    G --> H[Skip all catch blocks]
    H --> I[Continue after try-catch]
    D -- Yes --> J[Identify exception type]
    J --> K{First matching catch?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Bind exception to parameter e]
    L --> M[Execute catch body]
    M --> I
    K -- No --> N[Propagate to caller / terminate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Multi-Catch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRY[try block - Protected Region]
        T1[Statement 1]
        T2[Statement 2 - Risky]
        T3[Statement 3]
        T1 --> T2 --> T3
    end
    TRY -->|exception flows out| DECISION{Exception Type}
    DECISION -->|Type A| CA[catch Type A handler]
    DECISION -->|Type B| CB[catch Type B handler]
    DECISION -->|Type C| CC[catch Type C handler]
    DECISION -->|None match| ERR[Unhandled - stack trace dump]
    CA --> RESUME[Resume after try-catch]
    CB --> RESUME
    CC --> RESUME
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Stack Unwinding Visualization

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main()
    participant MethA as methodA()
    participant MethB as methodB()
    participant MethC as methodC()
    Main->>MethA: invoke
    MethA->>MethB: invoke
    MethB->>MethC: invoke
    MethC-->>MethB: throw ArithmeticException
    Note over MethB: No handler - propagate up
    MethB-->>MethA: throw ArithmeticException
    Note over MethA: No handler - propagate up
    MethA-->>Main: throw ArithmeticException
    Note over Main: catch matched - bind to e
    Main->>Main: execute catch body
    Main->>Main: resume after try-catch
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Exception Object Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Instantiated: throw new XException
    Instantiated --> Propagating: stack unwinding begins
    Propagating --> Caught: catch matches type
    Propagating --> Propagating: no match in current frame
    Caught --> Handled: catch body executing
    Handled --> GarbageCollected: scope exits
    Propagating --> UncaughtReachedMain: reaches main without match
    UncaughtReachedMain --> [*]: JVM prints trace and terminates
    GarbageCollected --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the purpose of the `try` block in Java? Can a `try` block exist without a `catch` block?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The `try` block in Java is used to **enclose a section of code that may throw an exception** during runtime. It defines a *guarded region* monitored by the JVM, so that if any statement inside it throws an exception, the normal flow of control is interrupted and the exception is transferred to an associated handler.
>
> A `try` block **must** be followed by either at least one `catch` block **or** a `finally` block. A `try` block **cannot exist alone**.
>
> - ✔ `try { } catch(Exception e) { }` — Valid
> - ✔ `try { } finally { }` — Valid (used for cleanup)
> - ✘ `try { }` alone — **Compile-time error**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Explain the role of the `catch` clause. What happens if no matching `catch` is found?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The `catch` clause acts as an **exception handler** — it intercepts a thrown exception object, binds it to its parameter (e.g., `ExceptionType e`), and contains the recovery code.
>
> The JVM matches the thrown exception against each `catch` parameter in **source order** using `instanceof` semantics. The **first matching catch** executes; remaining catches are skipped.
>
> If **no matching catch** is found in the current method, the exception **propagates up the call stack** to the caller. If it reaches the `main` method without being caught, the JVM **prints the stack trace** to `System.err` and **terminates the program abnormally**.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the syntax and execution flow of the `try-catch` mechanism in Java with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that reads two integers from the user and performs division. Use a `try-catch` block to handle `ArithmeticException` and `InputMismatchException` separately. Display appropriate error messages. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Syntax & Flow (7 marks)

**Syntax:**

```java
try {
    // code that may throw exception
} catch (ExceptionType1 ref1) {
    // handler 1
} catch (ExceptionType2 ref2) {
    // handler 2
}
```

**Execution Flow Description:**

1. Statements inside `try` execute sequentially under JVM monitoring.
2. If an exception occurs:
   - The remaining `try` body is **skipped**.
   - The JVM identifies the exception's runtime type.
   - It searches for the **first `catch` whose parameter type is a superclass of (or equal to) the exception**.
   - The matched `catch` executes; others are bypassed.
   - After `catch`, control resumes **after the entire construct**.
3. If no exception occurs, all `catch` blocks are skipped.

**Flow Diagram (reproduced for answer script):**

```
        ┌─────────────────────┐
        │ Enter try block     │
        └──────────┬──────────┘
                   ▼
        ┌─────────────────────┐
        │ Execute statement 1 │
        └──────────┬──────────┘
                   ▼
            ┌──────────────┐
            │ Exception?   │
            └──┬───────┬───┘
              No      Yes
               │       │
               ▼       ▼
        (continue)  (match catch)
                       │
                       ▼
                ┌─────────────────┐
                │ Run catch body  │
                └────────┬────────┘
                         ▼
              ┌──────────────────────┐
              │ Resume after try-catch│
              └──────────────────────┘
```

#### Part (b) — Java Program (7 marks)

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.InputMismatchException;

public class SafeDivide {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int a = sc.nextInt();        // may throw InputMismatchException
            
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int b = sc.nextInt();        // may throw InputMismatchException
            
            int result = a / b;          // may throw ArithmeticException
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
            
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("❌ Arithmetic Error: Cannot divide by zero.");
            System.out.println("Details: " + e.getMessage());
            
        } catch (InputMismatchException e) {
            System.out.println("❌ Input Error: Please enter integers only.");
            System.out.println("Details: " + e.getMessage());
            
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("❌ Unexpected Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        
        System.out.println("✅ Program completed safely.");
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Runs:**

| Input (numerator, denominator) | Output |
|---|---|
| `10`, `2` | `Result = 5`, then "Program completed safely." |
| `10`, `0` | "Arithmetic Error: Cannot divide by zero." |
| `abc`, `5` | "Input Error: Please enter integers only." |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong catch order**: Placing `catch(Exception e)` before specific catches — causes **compilation error** (unreachable code). Marks deducted: **−2**
> 2. **Empty catch block**: Writing `catch(...) {}` without any handling — KTU deducts **−1** for bad practice.
> 3. **Forgetting to import** `java.util.InputMismatchException` — though auto-resolved sometimes, safer to import.
> 4. **Placing `e.getMessage()` output without explanation** — examiner expects demonstration of *what* you understood about the exception object.
> 5. **Not closing Scanner in a finally block** (in production code) — minor deduction for resource leak awareness.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions. Give two examples of each and show how each is handled using `try-catch`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that accepts a string from the user and converts it to an integer. Handle `NumberFormatException` using `try-catch`. If conversion fails, prompt the user again until a valid integer is entered. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Checked vs Unchecked (7 marks)

| Feature | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|---|---|---|
| **Checked by** | Compiler (at compile-time) | JVM (at runtime) |
| **Inheritance** | Subclass of `Exception` (but not `RuntimeException`) | Subclass of `RuntimeException` |
| **Handling mandatory?** | **Yes** — must use `try-catch` or `throws` | No — optional |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **Cause** | External resources (files, DB, network) | Programming logic errors |

**Examples with try-catch:**

```java
// CHECKED — FileNotFoundException
try {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
} catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
    System.out.println("File not found: " + e.getMessage());
}

// UNCHECKED — NullPointerException
try {
    String s = null;
    System.out.println(s.length());
} catch (NullPointerException e) {
    System.out.println("Null reference accessed: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

#### Part (b) — Repeat Input Until Valid (7 marks)

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class RobustInput {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        int number = 0;
        boolean valid = false;
        
        while (!valid) {
            try {
                System.out.print("Enter an integer: ");
                number = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
                valid = true;                          // success — exit loop
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                System.out.println("❌ Invalid input! '" 
                    + e.getMessage().substring(
                        e.getMessage().indexOf('"') + 1,
                        e.getMessage().lastIndexOf('"')) 
                    + "' is not a valid integer. Try again.");
            }
        }
        
        System.out.println("✅ You entered: " + number);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Simpler Alternative (preferred for clarity in exam):**

```java
while (true) {
    try {
        System.out.print("Enter an integer: ");
        number = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
        break;                                       // breaks loop on success
    } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
        System.out.println("❌ Not a valid integer. Try again.");
    }
}
```

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — `try` Block and `catch` Clause**

- ☐ **Definition**: `try` = risky code region; `catch` = handler for thrown exception.
- ☐ **Mandatory Pairing**: `try` must be followed by `catch` and/or `finally`.
- ☐ **Block Scope**: Exception parameter `e` is local to its `catch` block.
- ☐ **Order Matters**: `catch` blocks ordered **subclass → superclass** (most specific first).
- ☐ **First Match Wins**: Only one `catch` executes per exception.
- ☐ **No Return to `try`**: After exception, the remaining `try` body is skipped permanently for that execution.
- ☐ **Stack Unwinding**: Uncaught exceptions propagate up the call stack until matched.
- ☐ **Multi-Catch (Java 7+)**: `catch (A | B e)` — types must be unrelated by inheritance.
- ☐ **Don't Swallow Exceptions**: Avoid empty `catch` blocks — always at least log the error.
- ☐ **Checked vs Unchecked**: Only checked exceptions *require* `try-catch` or `throws` declaration.
- ☐ **Common Methods on `e`**: `e.getMessage()`, `e.toString()`, `e.printStackTrace()`, `e.getClass().getName()`.
- ☐ **KTU Exam Tip**: Always include a `printStackTrace()` or `getMessage()` call in your `catch` body to demonstrate understanding of the exception object — earns full marks.
- ☐ **Hierarchy to Remember**:
  $$\text{ArithmeticException} \rightarrow \text{RuntimeException} \rightarrow \text{Exception} \rightarrow \text{Throwable}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple catch Clauses
Nested try Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nested try Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Nested try Statement** in Java is an exception-handling construct in which one (or more) `try` block(s) is placed *inside* another `try` block (or `catch`/`finally` block). It allows the programmer to handle different categories of exceptions at different logical scopes of execution, providing fine-grained, hierarchical error recovery.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT415 – Object Oriented Programming)** syllabus, Module 3 (Exception Handling & Multithreading), nested `try` blocks are a sub-topic under "Exception Handling Fundamentals." They directly support the Course Outcome **CO3**: *“Implement robust Java programs using exception handling and multithreading mechanisms.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Matryoshka (Russian Doll) of Safety Nets**

Imagine a trapeze artist performing high above the ground.

* **Outer `try` Block:** The large circus net spread across the entire arena — it catches *any* fall that escapes inner protections.
* **Inner `try` Block:** A smaller, specialized harness tied directly to the artist — designed to catch a *specific* kind of slip (e.g., grip failure) before it ever reaches the net.
* **`catch` Blocks:** Trained safety personnel standing beside each net. If the inner harness catches the problem, the inner personnel react. Only if it slips past both does the outer personnel respond.

Just as the artist has **layered protection**, a Java method with nested `try` blocks has **layered exception recovery**. Inner blocks handle local exceptions first; outer blocks handle broader, unhandled exceptions.

### 1.3 Standard Java Keywords in Play

| Keyword | Role | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| `try` | Monitored region where exceptions may arise | Yes |
| `catch` | Handler for a specific exception type | At least one per `try` (or `finally`) |
| `throw` | Manually trigger an exception | Optional |
| `throws` | Declare exceptions in method signature | Optional |
| `finally` | Cleanup block executed always | Optional |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Java Exception Class Hierarchy Anchor:**  
> `Throwable` → `Exception` → `RuntimeException` (unchecked) / `IOException` (checked).  
> All Java exceptions ultimately inherit from `java.lang.Throwable`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Nested `try` Blocks?

Nested `try` blocks are required in scenarios where:

1. **Different Exception Types Require Different Scopes** — A inner block may need to recover from a specific exception (e.g., `ArithmeticException`) while the outer block catches a broader one (e.g., `Exception`).
2. **Localized Resource Management** — Inner `try` may declare and close a resource; outer `try` may handle failures at a higher logical level.
3. **Method Calls Throwing Multiple Exceptions** — A method invoked inside a `try` may itself be wrapped in another `try` to handle exceptions thrown from *its* internal operations.
4. **Partial Recovery** — A recoverable error is handled locally; a catastrophic error is allowed to propagate upward.

## 2.2 Rules of Nested `try` Statements (Board-Exam Gold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rules — Memorize for 3-Mark Short Questions**

* **Rule 1:** A `try` block can be nested directly inside another `try`, `catch`, or `finally` block.
* **Rule 2:** If an exception is thrown in the **inner** `try`, the **inner** `catch` clauses are searched **first**.
* **Rule 3:** If the inner `catch` does **not match** the exception type, the exception propagates to the **outer** `catch`.
* **Rule 4:** If **no** matching `catch` is found in the entire call stack, the **JVM default handler** terminates the program with a stack trace.
* **Rule 5:** Syntax forbids writing a `catch` (or `finally`) block immediately after a `try` *without* an intervening nested `try`. The compiler enforces proper pairing.
* **Rule 6:** Local variables declared inside a `try` block are **out of scope** outside that block — including inside the outer `try`.

## 2.3 Execution Flow Algorithm

For nested `try-catch-finally` blocks, the JVM follows this deterministic flow:

1. Enter the **outer** `try` block.
2. Enter the **inner** `try` block.
3. Execute statements sequentially.
4. **If exception occurs:**
   - Search inner `catch` blocks in order.
   - If matched → execute handler → run inner `finally` → resume after outer `try`.
   - If not matched → search outer `catch` blocks.
   - If matched → execute handler → run outer `finally` → resume after outer `try`.
   - If still not matched → execute any pending `finally` → propagate to caller / JVM default handler.
5. **If no exception occurs:**
   - Run inner `finally` (if present).
   - Continue outer `try` statements.
   - Run outer `finally` (if present).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Construct / Rule | Syntax Pattern | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple nested `try` | `try { try { ... } catch(X e) { ... } } catch(Y e) { ... }` | Inner `X` caught locally; outer `Y` catches leaks |
| 2 | Inner `try` inside outer `catch` | `try { ... } catch(X e) { try { ... } catch(Y e2) { ... } }` | Recovery logic is also protected |
| 3 | Inner `try` inside outer `finally` | `try { ... } finally { try { ... } catch(Y e) { ... } }` | Cleanup protected against its own failures |
| 4 | Exception propagation rule | Throw → Inner `catch` → Outer `catch` → JVM | Only one matching `catch` ever executes |
| 5 | `finally` execution guarantee | Always runs unless `System.exit()` is called | Cleanup is guaranteed |
| 6 | Re-throwing from catch | `throw e;` from inside `catch` | Allows outer handler to refine response |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In enterprise Java (Spring, Hibernate, JDBC), nested `try` patterns are everywhere. For example, **database transactions** use an outer `try` to roll back on failure, while an inner `try` handles specific SQL exceptions (`SQLException`). The pattern also appears in **file I/O** where an inner `try` may parse a file line-by-line, and an outer `try` catches `IOException` if the file itself is missing.

## 2.6 Exception Class Hierarchy (Quick-Reference)

$$
\text{Throwable} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Error} \;\;(\text{unchecked, JVM fatal}) \\
\text{Exception} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{RuntimeException (unchecked)} \\
\text{IOException, SQLException, ... (checked)}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Below are **exhaustive, line-by-line code traces** of canonical nested-`try` programs. Each transition is explained. This is the kind of structured output KTU examiners expect for full-mark 7-mark and 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Inner Catch Handles, Outer Continues

**Question:** Demonstrate a nested `try` where the inner block throws and catches `ArithmeticException`, and execution continues normally afterwards.

```java
class NestedTryDemo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // OUTER try
        try {
            System.out.println("Outer try begins");
            int a = 10, b = 0;

            // INNER try
            try {
                System.out.println("Inner try begins");
                int c = a / b;  // Line X: triggers ArithmeticException
                System.out.println("Inner try ends (skipped)"); // not reached
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch: " + e);
            }

            System.out.println("Outer try resumes");
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
        }

        System.out.println("Main ends normally");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Execution enters } \texttt{main}. \text{ JVM pushes a new stack frame.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Outer } \texttt{try} \text{ opens. Prints: } \texttt{"Outer try begins"}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Inner } \texttt{try} \text{ opens. Prints: } \texttt{"Inner try begins"}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{At Line X: } 10/0 \text{ is evaluated. JVM throws } \texttt{ArithmeticException}. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Normal flow halts inside inner } \texttt{try}. \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{JVM searches inner } \texttt{catch (ArithmeticException e)} \rightarrow \textbf{MATCH}. \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Inner } \texttt{catch} \text{ executes. Prints: } \texttt{"Inner catch: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero"}. \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Control passes out of inner } \texttt{try-catch}. \text{ Resumes outer } \texttt{try}. \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{Outer } \texttt{try} \text{ continues. Prints: } \texttt{"Outer try resumes"}. \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad & \text{Outer } \texttt{try} \text{ ends normally. Outer } \texttt{catch} \text{ is skipped.} \\
\text{Step 11:} \quad & \text{Program prints: } \texttt{"Main ends normally"}. \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Expected Output

```
Outer try begins
Inner try begins
Inner catch: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
Outer try resumes
Main ends normally
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Inner Mismatch, Outer Catches (Exception Propagation)

**Question:** Demonstrate a nested `try` where the inner `catch` does **not** match, and the exception propagates to the outer `catch`.

```java
class NestedTryDemo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            System.out.println("Outer try begins");
            try {
                int[] arr = new int[3];
                arr[5] = 100;   // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException e) {  // mismatch!
                System.out.println("Inner catch (Arithmetic) - not reached");
            }
            System.out.println("After inner try (skipped)");
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
        }
        System.out.println("Main ends");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Outer } \texttt{try} \text{ opens. Prints: } \texttt{"Outer try begins"}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Inner } \texttt{try} \text{ opens. Allocates } \texttt{int[3]}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{arr[5] = 100} \text{ triggers } \texttt{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{JVM searches inner } \texttt{catch (ArithmeticException)} \rightarrow \textbf{NO MATCH}. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Exception propagates upward through inner } \texttt{try} \text{ block boundary.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{JVM searches outer } \texttt{catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException)} \rightarrow \textbf{MATCH}. \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Outer } \texttt{catch} \text{ executes. Prints: } \texttt{"Outer catch: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3"}. \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Outer } \texttt{try} \text{ terminates after } \texttt{catch}. \text{ Statement } \texttt{"After inner try (skipped)"} \text{ is never reached.} \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{Program prints: } \texttt{"Main ends"}. \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Expected Output

```
Outer try begins
Outer catch: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Main ends
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice in Step 8 that the statement `"After inner try (skipped)"` is **NOT** printed. When an exception escapes the inner `try` (unmatched), control jumps **past** all subsequent statements in the outer `try` block until a matching `catch` is found.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Use of `finally` in Nested `try`

```java
class NestedTryFinally {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            System.out.println("Outer try");
            try {
                System.out.println("Inner try");
                String s = null;
                System.out.println(s.length()); // NullPointerException
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch A");
            }
            finally {
                System.out.println("Inner finally");
            }
        }
        catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally");
        }
        System.out.println("Done");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Prints: } \texttt{"Outer try"}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Prints: } \texttt{"Inner try"}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{s.length()} \text{ throws } \texttt{NullPointerException}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{JVM searches inner } \texttt{catch (ArithmeticException)} \rightarrow \textbf{NO MATCH}. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{JVM runs inner } \texttt{finally} \text{ before propagating. Prints: } \texttt{"Inner finally"}. \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Exception propagates to outer scope.} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{JVM searches outer } \texttt{catch (NullPointerException)} \rightarrow \textbf{MATCH}. \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Prints: } \texttt{"Outer catch: java.lang.NullPointerException"}. \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{JVM runs outer } \texttt{finally}. \text{ Prints: } \texttt{"Outer finally"}. \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad & \text{Prints: } \texttt{"Done"}. \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Expected Output

```
Outer try
Inner try
Inner finally
Outer catch: java.lang.NullPointerException
Outer finally
Done
```

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Inner `catch` matches? | Inner `finally` runs? | Outer `catch` runs? | Outer `finally` runs? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| No exception anywhere | — | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Exception caught locally | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Exception escapes inner (mismatch) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Inner `catch` re-throws | — | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (if matches) | ✅ Yes |
| `System.exit(0)` called | — | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control-Flow Diagram of Nested `try-catch-finally`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Program Start]) --> B[Outer try begin]
    B --> C[Inner try begin]
    C --> D{Exception in inner try?}
    D -- No --> E[Continue inner statements]
    D -- Yes --> F{Inner catch matches?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Execute inner catch block]
    F -- No --> H[Inner finally runs]
    G --> I[Inner finally runs]
    I --> J[Resume outer try]
    H --> K{Outer catch matches?}
    E --> J
    J --> L{Exception in outer try?}
    L -- No --> M[Outer finally runs]
    L -- Yes --> K
    K -- Yes --> N[Execute outer catch]
    K -- No --> O[Propagate to JVM default]
    M --> P([Program continues normally])
    N --> Q[Outer finally runs]
    Q --> P
    O --> R([JVM terminates program])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OuterScope["OUTER SCOPE — Broad Protection"]
        OT[Outer try block]
        OF[Outer finally block]
        OC1[Outer catch block A]
        OC2[Outer catch block B]
    end

    subgraph InnerScope["INNER SCOPE — Localized Protection"]
        IT[Inner try block]
        IF[Inner finally block]
        IC1[Inner catch block A]
        IC2[Inner catch block B]
    end

    OT --> IT
    IT --> IC1
    IT --> IC2
    IC1 --> IF
    IC2 --> IF
    IF --> OT
    OT --> OC1
    OT --> OC2
    OC1 --> OF
    OC2 --> OF
    OF --> Z([Resume Program])
```

## 4.3 Exception Propagation Path (Stack-Style View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[main outer try] --> S2[methodA inner try]
    S2 --> S3[Statement throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    S3 --> S4{Inner catch handles?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Recover locally]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Bubble to methodA outer catch]
    S6 --> S7{Outer catch handles?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Recover at methodA level]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Bubble to main outer catch]
    S9 --> S10{Main catch handles?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Recover at main level]
    S10 -- No --> S12[JVM default handler prints stack trace and terminates]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Model answers use the same board-evaluation rubric: concept (3), code/diagram (3), execution trace (1) for 7-mark; with proportional expansion for 14-mark questions.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a nested `try` block. Give one situation where it is preferred over a single `try-catch` block.**

**Model Answer:**

A nested `try` block is a `try` statement placed within another `try`, `catch`, or `finally` block. It allows different exceptions at different scopes to be handled independently.

**Situation:** When one section of code can throw a *specific* exception (e.g., division by zero in an inner calculation) that should be recovered locally, while the *outer* block may encounter *different* exceptions (e.g., file-not-found while reading input). Using nested `try` keeps recovery logic isolated, readable, and avoids over-broad exception handlers.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What happens if an exception thrown in an inner `try` block is not caught by its inner `catch` clause?**

**Model Answer:**

The exception **propagates outward** — it leaves the inner `try-catch` boundary (running any inner `finally` block along the way) and is offered to the **outer** `catch` clauses in sequence. If matched, the outer `catch` handles it. If no matching `catch` is found anywhere in the call stack, the JVM default handler prints the stack trace and **terminates** the program.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of nested `try` statements in Java with a suitable real-world analogy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates a nested `try` where:
* The inner block throws an `ArithmeticException` (handled by inner `catch`).
* The outer block throws an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` (handled by outer `catch`).
* Show the complete execution output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Nested `try` statements are a hierarchical exception-handling construct where one `try` block is placed inside another. **Analogy:** Think of a building with multiple fire-escape routes. The innermost `try` is like a fire extinguisher inside a server room — it handles small, localized fires (specific exceptions). The outer `try` is the building's main sprinkler system and fire exit — it handles larger emergencies that escape the inner layer. Only one level of defense catches the fire; if the extinguisher fails, the sprinkler activates.

Key advantages:
* **Localized recovery** for specific exceptions.
* **Layered safety** — catastrophic errors still caught at outer level.
* **Cleaner code** — handlers don't need to be over-broad (`catch (Exception e)`).

**(b)** Java program:

```java
class NestedTryFullDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            System.out.println("Outer try started");
            try {
                int a = 10, b = 0;
                int c = a / b;   // ArithmeticException
                System.out.println("Result = " + c);
            }
            catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch: " + e);
            }

            int[] arr = new int[3];
            arr[5] = 50;  // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            System.out.println("After array assign");
        }
        catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally executed");
        }
        System.out.println("Program ended normally");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Outer try started
Inner catch: java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
Outer catch: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Outer finally executed
Program ended normally
```

**Trace Explanation (Board-Rubric Style):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phase 1:} \quad & \text{Outer try opens. Prints: "Outer try started".} \\
\text{Phase 2:} \quad & \text{Inner try opens. } 10/0 \text{ throws ArithmeticException.} \\
\text{Phase 3:} \quad & \text{Inner catch matches. Prints: "Inner catch: ...".} \\
\text{Phase 4:} \quad & \text{Inner try-catch exits normally. Outer try continues.} \\
\text{Phase 5:} \quad & \texttt{arr[5]=50} \text{ throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.} \\
\text{Phase 6:} \quad & \text{Outer catch matches. Prints: "Outer catch: ...".} \\
\text{Phase 7:} \quad & \text{Outer finally runs: "Outer finally executed".} \\
\text{Phase 8:} \quad & \text{Program continues: "Program ended normally".} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Supplementary]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, illustrate the control flow of nested `try-catch-finally` blocks. State any four rules of nested `try` blocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following code. Predict the output and justify each line.

```java
class Exam {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            System.out.println("1");
            try {
                System.out.println("2");
                int x = Integer.parseInt("abc");
                System.out.println("3");
            }
            catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                System.out.println("4: " + e.getMessage());
            }
            finally {
                System.out.println("5");
            }
            System.out.println("6");
        }
        catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("7");
        }
        finally {
            System.out.println("8");
        }
        System.out.println("9");
    }
}
```
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Control-flow diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Enter outer try] --> B[Enter inner try]
    B --> C{Exception?}
    C -- No --> D[Inner finally]
    C -- Yes --> E{Inner catch match?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Inner catch executes]
    E -- No --> D
    F --> D
    D --> G[Outer finally]
    G --> H[Resume program]
```

**Four Rules:**
1. Inner `catch` is searched **first** for any exception thrown in the inner `try`.
2. If unmatched, the exception propagates to the **outer** `catch`.
3. A `finally` block executes whether or not an exception is thrown/handled.
4. Variables declared inside a `try` block are **local** to that block.

**(b)** Predicted Output:
```
1
2
4: For input string: "abc"
5
6
8
9
```

**Justification (line by line):**
* `1` → Printed on entering outer try.
* `2` → Printed on entering inner try.
* `Integer.parseInt("abc")` → Throws `NumberFormatException`.
* Line `3` is **skipped** (exception).
* Inner `catch` matches → Prints `4: For input string: "abc"`.
* Inner `finally` always runs → Prints `5`.
* Outer `try` resumes → Prints `6`.
* No exception escapes outer try → Outer `catch` (7) **skipped**.
* Outer `finally` runs → Prints `8`.
* Program continues → Prints `9`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common mark-loss area:** Students forget that **inner `finally` runs even when inner `catch` mismatches**. Always trace `finally` placement before answering.
> 2. **Second-most common error:** Confusing **compile-time vs runtime** behavior. Nested `try` itself never throws a compile error — only mismatched checked exceptions do.
> 3. **Output order trap:** When inner `catch` handles the exception, students mistakenly print *both* inner and outer catch outputs. **Only one catch executes** for any single thrown exception.
> 4. **Variable-scope trap:** A `String s` declared inside an inner `try` **cannot** be used inside the outer `catch`. This causes *compile-time* errors worth full negative marking.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Definition:** A nested `try` is a `try` placed inside another `try`/`catch`/`finally` for hierarchical exception handling.
- [x] **Search order:** Inner `catch` first → outer `catch` next → JVM default handler last.
- [x] **`finally` always runs** (unless `System.exit()` is invoked) — both inner and outer.
- [x] **Re-throwing** from a `catch` (`throw e;`) lets outer handlers refine the response.
- [x] **Scope rule:** Inner-block variables are inaccessible outside that block.
- [x] **Real-world use:** Database transactions, file I/O, multi-layer validation, network retries.
- [x] **Common exam pitfall:** Forgetting that after an exception escapes inner `try`, statements *between* inner and outer `catch` are **skipped**.
- [x] **Mnemonic for output trace:** **T-C-F** — Try → Catch → Finally (apply this sequence at every nesting level).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throw. Java Library - String Handling - String Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** throw. Java Library - String Handling - String Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The `throw` Keyword in Java

### Formal Academic Definition
The `throw` keyword in Java is an **explicit exception-throwing mechanism** used to programmatically generate and dispatch an exception object to the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) runtime stack. It is syntactically classified as a *unary operator* that takes a single operand — an instance of `Throwable` (or any of its subclasses such as `Exception`, `RuntimeException`, `Error`, or custom user-defined exception classes).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of `throw` as a **fire alarm button** inside a building. The alarm doesn't fix the fire, but it *signals* the authorities (the JVM) that something has gone wrong, so the appropriate emergency protocol (the `catch` block) can be activated. Just as you can press the alarm at any moment (programmatically) rather than waiting for automatic smoke detection, `throw` lets a programmer raise an exception at any logical point in the code, not just when a system error occurs naturally.

### Syntax Grammar
```java
throw ThrowableInstance;
```
Where `ThrowableInstance` must be an object of class `Throwable` or any subclass (typically `Exception` or `RuntimeException`).

### Key Operational Rules

| Rule No. | Operational Rule | Explanation |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|
| **R1** | **Single Exception Rule** | Only ONE exception object can be thrown per `throw` statement. |
| **R2** | **Instantiation Requirement** | The operand must be a reference to an existing object; `null` can technically be thrown but results in `NullPointerException`. |
| **R3** | **Checked Exception Propagation** | If a checked exception is thrown, the method must declare it using `throws` clause, OR the throw must be enclosed within a `try-catch` block. |
| **R4** | **Flow Control Disruption** | Execution of the current method is **immediately terminated**; control transfers to the nearest enclosing `catch` handler. |
| **R5** | **Unreachable Code Error** | Any code written after a `throw` statement in the same block becomes **unreachable** and causes a compile-time error. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The `throw` keyword is **distinct** from the `throws` keyword. `throw` *creates and dispatches* an exception, while `throws` *declares* that a method may propagate exceptions to its caller. This distinction is a high-frequency question in KTU university examinations.

---

## 1.2 Java Library — String Handling (Overview)

### Formal Academic Definition
**String Handling** in Java refers to the set of operations, classes, and methods provided by the `java.lang` package for creating, manipulating, comparing, and processing sequences of characters. The cornerstone class is `java.lang.String`, which represents an **immutable** sequence of UTF-16 encoded characters.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A Java `String` is like a **tattoo on your skin** — once inscribed (created), it cannot be altered. If you want a "different" string, you must get a new tattoo (create a new `String` object). The original tattoo remains unchanged underneath. This is what we mean by **String immutability**.

### The String Constant Pool
> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a string literal is created (e.g., `String s = "Hello";`), it is stored in a special memory region called the **String Constant Pool** within the JVM heap. This pooling mechanism enables **string interning**, which optimizes memory usage by reusing identical string literals.

```java
String a = "KTU";          // Stored in String Constant Pool
String b = "KTU";          // References SAME object in pool
String c = new String("KTU"); // New object created in heap (NOT in pool)
System.out.println(a == b);  // true  (same reference)
System.out.println(a == c);  // false (different objects)
System.out.println(a.equals(c)); // true  (same content)
```

---

## 1.3 String Constructors — Formal Definition

### Formal Academic Definition
A **String constructor** is a special method of the `java.lang.String` class that initializes a new `String` object with a character sequence derived from a specified source — which may be a `char` array, `byte` array, `StringBuffer`, `StringBuilder`, another `String`, or Unicode code points.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** String constructors are like **different molds** in a factory that produce plastic bottles (String objects). The **same final product** (a String) can be created from **different raw materials** (char arrays, byte arrays, other strings, StringBuffers, etc.). Each constructor defines a different "mold" — a different way of shaping the same final product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Java `String` class provides **13 overloaded constructors** (as per Java 17 / 21 LTS versions). Mastering the parameter signatures of these constructors is **mandatory** for KTU Module 3 examination questions on String Handling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: The `throw` Keyword

### Operational Logic Flow
1. **Step 1 — Instantiation:** A `Throwable` subclass object is created (either explicitly via `new` or obtained from a method).
2. **Step 2 — Dispatch:** The `throw` statement dispatches the object to the JVM runtime stack.
3. **Step 3 — Stack Unwinding:** The JVM begins *unwinding* the call stack, searching for a matching `catch` block.
4. **Step 4 — Handler Matching:** The first `catch` block whose parameter type is **assignment-compatible** with the thrown exception type is selected.
5. **Step 5 — Resolution:** If no matching handler is found, the **default exception handler** terminates the program with a stack trace.

### Why and How — Theoretical Justification
The `throw` mechanism exists because **certain exceptional conditions cannot be detected automatically** by the JVM. For example, a banking application may need to throw a custom `InsufficientFundsException` when a withdrawal amount exceeds the account balance — this is a *business logic* condition, not a system error.

### Code Demonstration
```java
class AgeValidator {
    static void validateAge(int age) {
        if (age < 18) {
            throw new ArithmeticException("Age " + age + " is not eligible for voting.");
        }
        System.out.println("Welcome! You are eligible to vote.");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            validateAge(15);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Exception Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Exception Caught: Age 15 is not eligible for voting.
```

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Java String Handling

### Key Characteristics of Java Strings

| Characteristic | Description | Engineering Significance |
|----------------|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Immutability** | String objects cannot be modified after creation. | Thread-safe by default; enables caching in String Pool. |
| **Final Class** | `String` class is declared `public final class String`. | Prevents subclassing; guarantees immutability contract. |
| **Backed by `char[]`** | Internally stored as `char[] value` (pre-Java 9) or `byte[]` (compact strings, Java 9+). | Memory-efficient storage. |
| **Serializable** | Implements `java.io.Serializable`. | Can be transmitted across networks. |
| **Comparable** | Implements `java.lang.Comparable<String>`. | Supports natural ordering. |
| **CharSequence** | Implements `java.lang.CharSequence`. | Foundation for `StringBuilder`, `StringBuffer`. |

### The `String` Class Hierarchy
```
java.lang.Object
    └── java.lang.String (final class, implements Serializable, Comparable<String>, CharSequence)
```

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive: String Constructors — Complete Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table:** Memorize these constructor signatures — they appear frequently in KTU Module 3 questions.

| # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | Example Usage |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------|
| **1** | `String()` | Creates an **empty** string. | `String s = new String();` |
| **2** | `String(String original)` | Creates a **copy** of the given string. | `String s = new String("Hello");` |
| **3** | `String(char[] value)` | Creates a string from a **complete char array**. | `String s = new String(ch);` |
| **4** | `String(char[] value, int offset, int count)` | Creates a string from a **subarray** of chars. | `String s = new String(ch, 1, 3);` |
| **5** | `String(byte[] bytes)` | Creates a string from a **byte array** using platform default charset. | `String s = new String(bytes);` |
| **6** | `String(byte[] bytes, int offset, int length)` | Creates a string from a **subarray of bytes**. | `String s = new String(bytes, 0, 5);` |
| **7** | `String(byte[] bytes, Charset charset)` | Creates a string from bytes using **specified charset**. | `String s = new String(bytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);` |
| **8** | `String(byte[] bytes, String charsetName)` | Creates a string from bytes using **named charset**. | `String s = new String(bytes, "UTF-8");` |
| **9** | `String(byte[] bytes, int offset, int length, Charset charset)` | Subarray of bytes with **specified charset**. | `String s = new String(bytes, 0, 5, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);` |
| **10** | `String(byte[] bytes, int offset, int length, String charsetName)` | Subarray of bytes with **named charset**. | `String s = new String(bytes, 0, 5, "UTF-8");` |
| **11** | `String(StringBuffer buffer)` | Creates a string from a **thread-safe** `StringBuffer`. | `String s = new String(sb);` |
| **12** | `String(StringBuilder builder)` | Creates a string from a **non-synchronized** `StringBuilder`. | `String s = new String(sb);` |
| **13** | `String(int[] codePoints, int offset, int count)` | Creates a string from a **Unicode code point array**. | `String s = new String(codepoints, 0, 2);` |

### Formula Sheet — String Constructor Parameter Semantics

| Parameter | Meaning | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|
| `offset` | Starting index (0-based) into the source array | Must satisfy $\text{offset} \geq 0$ |
| `count` / `length` | Number of characters/bytes to read | Must satisfy $\text{count} \geq 0$ |
| `offset + count` | Must NOT exceed `array.length` | $\text{offset} + \text{count} \leq \text{array.length}$ |
| `charset` | Character encoding scheme | `null` throws `NullPointerException` |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Networking Protocols:** HTTP request/response bodies are received as `byte[]` and converted to `String` using constructors #5–#10.
- **File I/O Operations:** File contents read as bytes are decoded using charset-specific constructors for multi-language support.
- **Database Connectivity:** `StringBuffer` is used internally by JDBC drivers; constructor #11 is used to extract final SQL strings.
- **Internationalization (i18n):** Unicode code points (constructor #13) are essential for handling **emoji** and **non-BMP characters** (e.g., Indic scripts).

---

## 2.4 KTU-Style Conceptual Comparison Tables

### `throw` vs `throws` (Most Asked KTU Question)

| Feature | `throw` | `throws` |
|---------|---------|----------|
| **Keyword Type** | Used to *explicitly throw* an exception | Used to *declare* exceptions a method may propagate |
| **Number of Exceptions** | Only **one** exception per `throw` statement | **Multiple** exceptions can be declared, comma-separated |
| **Location** | Inside method **body** | In method **signature** (declaration) |
| **Syntax** | `throw new Exception("msg");` | `void method() throws IOException, SQLException` |
| **Checked Exception** | Must be caught or declared in enclosing method's `throws` | Declares that caller must handle it |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: The `throw` Statement Execution

### Example Problem
```java
public class ThrowDemo {
    static void demoMethod(int number) {
        if (number < 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Negative numbers not allowed: " + number);
        }
        System.out.println("Valid number: " + number);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            demoMethod(-5);
            System.out.println("This line will NOT execute.");
        } catch (IllegalArgumentException e) {
            System.out.println("Exception Handled: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues normally.");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Step | Action | Stack State | Output So Far |
|------|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **1** | JVM invokes `main()`. | `main` frame pushed. | *(empty)* |
| **2** | `main` calls `demoMethod(-5)`. | `demoMethod` frame pushed on top. | *(empty)* |
| **3** | Condition `number < 0` evaluates to `true`. | — | *(empty)* |
| **4** | `new IllegalArgumentException(...)` instantiates exception object. | Exception object created in heap. | *(empty)* |
| **5** | `throw` dispatches exception; `demoMethod` terminates abnormally. | `demoMethod` frame **popped**. | *(empty)* |
| **6** | Stack unwinding — JVM searches for matching `catch`. | `main` frame inspected. | *(empty)* |
| **7** | `catch(IllegalArgumentException e)` matches. | Handler entered. | `Exception Handled: Negative numbers not allowed: -5` |
| **8** | After `try-catch`, next line executes. | `main` continues. | `Program continues normally.` |

### Final Output
```
Exception Handled: Negative numbers not allowed: -5
Program continues normally.
```

### Mathematical Verification of the Termination Property
Let $S$ be the set of executable statements remaining in a block $B$ after a `throw` statement at position $i$. Then:

$$S = \left\{ s_j \in B \;\middle|\; j > i \right\} = \emptyset$$

$$S = \emptyset \implies \text{Unreachable code error at compile-time}$$

This means **all statements after `throw` in the same block are unreachable**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: String Constructor Mechanics

### Example Problem
```java
public class StringConstructorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Constructor 1: Empty String
        String s1 = new String();
        System.out.println("s1 length: " + s1.length());  // Output: 0

        // Constructor 2: Copy Constructor
        String original = "KTU B.Tech";
        String s2 = new String(original);
        System.out.println("s2: " + s2);                   // Output: KTU B.Tech

        // Constructor 3: char[] to String
        char[] charArray = {'J', 'a', 'v', 'a'};
        String s3 = new String(charArray);
        System.out.println("s3: " + s3);                   // Output: Java

        // Constructor 4: Partial char[] to String
        char[] data = {'H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', ' ', 'W', 'o', 'r', 'l', 'd'};
        String s4 = new String(data, 6, 5);
        System.out.println("s4: " + s4);                   // Output: World

        // Constructor 5: byte[] to String
        byte[] bytes = {72, 101, 108, 108, 111};  // ASCII for "Hello"
        String s5 = new String(bytes);
        System.out.println("s5: " + s5);                   // Output: Hello

        // Constructor 11: StringBuffer to String
        StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Mutable");
        String s6 = new String(sb);
        System.out.println("s6: " + s6);                   // Output: Mutable
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Analytical Trace for Constructor 4

Given:
```java
char[] data = {'H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', ' ', 'W', 'o', 'r', 'l', 'd'};
String s4 = new String(data, 6, 5);
```

**Step 1:** Identify the source array:
$$\text{data} = [\text{'H'}, \text{'e'}, \text{'l'}, \text{'l'}, \text{'o'}, \text{' '}, \text{'W'}, \text{'o'}, \text{'r'}, \text{'l'}, \text{'d'}]$$

**Step 2:** Identify the parameter values:
$$\text{offset} = 6, \quad \text{count} = 5$$

**Step 3:** Compute the valid range:
$$\text{Valid indices} = [6, 6 + 5) = [6, 11) = \{6, 7, 8, 9, 10\}$$

**Step 4:** Extract elements at those indices:
$$\text{data}[6] = \text{'W'}, \quad \text{data}[7] = \text{'o'}, \quad \text{data}[8] = \text{'r'}, \quad \text{data}[9] = \text{'l'}, \quad \text{data}[10] = \text{'d'}$$

**Step 5:** Form the resulting String:
$$s_4 = \text{"World"}$$

**Step 6:** Verify boundary condition:
$$\text{offset} + \text{count} = 6 + 5 = 11 = \text{data.length} = 11 \quad \checkmark$$

### Analytical Matrix: Constructor Behavior Summary

| Constructor Call | Input Source | Extracted Substring | Length | Notes |
|------------------|--------------|---------------------|--------|-------|
| `new String()` | *(none)* | `""` | **0** | Empty string |
| `new String("KTU")` | String literal | `"KTU"` | **3** | Copy semantics |
| `new String({'a','b','c'})` | char array | `"abc"` | **3** | Full array |
| `new String({'a','b','c'}, 1, 2)` | char array | `"bc"` | **2** | Subarray from index 1, length 2 |
| `new String({65, 66, 67})` | byte array (ASCII) | `"ABC"` | **3** | Platform default charset |
| `new String(sb)` | StringBuffer | `"Mutable"` | **7** | Snapshot of mutable content |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Exercise: `throw` vs `throw new` Patterns

### Pattern A: Throw an existing exception object
```java
Exception e = new RuntimeException("Custom error");
throw e;  // Throws the SAME object referenced by 'e'
```

### Pattern B: Throw a freshly created exception object
```java
throw new RuntimeException("Custom error");  // Throws a NEW object
```

### Pattern C: Rethrow an exception inside a catch block
```java
try {
    riskyOperation();
} catch (SQLException e) {
    System.out.println("Logging error: " + e);
    throw e;  // RETHROWS the SAME object to the caller
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When rethrowing, the *original* exception object is propagated with its *original* stack trace. This is critical for debugging in production systems.

### Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Throw Pattern

| Scenario | Recommended Pattern | Justification |
|----------|--------------------|--------------|
| Simple validation failure | `throw new XxxException("msg")` | Concise; one-line idiom. |
| Pre-existing exception object | `throw existingException;` | Preserves the original object identity. |
| Rethrowing after partial handling | `throw e;` inside `catch` | Maintains original stack trace. |
| Wrapping a low-level exception | `throw new HighLevelException("context", cause)` | Adds semantic meaning; preserves root cause. |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Code: Charset-Specific Byte Array Constructor

```java
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;

public class CharsetDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        String original = "Café ₹";  // Contains non-ASCII characters

        // Convert to bytes using UTF-8
        byte[] utf8Bytes = original.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
        System.out.println("UTF-8 byte length: " + utf8Bytes.length);

        // Reconstruct using constructor #9
        String reconstructed = new String(utf8Bytes, 0, utf8Bytes.length, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
        System.out.println("Reconstructed: " + reconstructed);
    }
}
```

### Analytical Trace

**Step 1:** Source string:
$$s = \text{"Café ₹"}$$

**Step 2:** UTF-8 encoding (variable-width, 1–4 bytes per character):

| Character | Unicode Code Point | UTF-8 Bytes | Byte Count |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------|------------|
| `C` | U+0043 | `0x43` | **1** |
| `a` | U+0061 | `0x61` | **1** |
| `f` | U+0066 | `0x66` | **1** |
| `é` | U+00E9 | `0xC3 0xA9` | **2** |
| ` ` | U+0020 | `0x20` | **1** |
| `₹` | U+20B9 | `0xE2 0x82 0xB9` | **3** |
| **Total** | — | — | **9 bytes** |

**Step 3:** Constructor signature: `String(byte[] bytes, int offset, int length, Charset charset)`

**Step 4:** Parameters:
$$\text{bytes} = \text{utf8Bytes}, \quad \text{offset} = 0, \quad \text{length} = 9, \quad \text{charset} = \text{UTF-8}$$

**Step 5:** The constructor decodes 9 bytes from index 0 using UTF-8 rules, producing:
$$s_{\text{reconstructed}} = \text{"Café ₹"}$$

**Step 6:** Verification:
$$s_{\text{original}} = s_{\text{reconstructed}} \implies \text{Round-trip SUCCESS} \quad \checkmark$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: `throw` Keyword Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Method execution starts"] --> nodeB{"Exception condition<br/>detected?"}
    nodeB -- "No" --> nodeC["Continue normal<br/>execution"]
    nodeB -- "Yes" --> nodeD["Instantiate<br/>Throwable object"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Execute throw<br/>statement"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Terminate current<br/>method abnormally"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"Matching catch<br/>block in call stack?"}
    nodeG -- "Yes" --> nodeH["Transfer control<br/>to catch handler"]
    nodeG -- "No" --> nodeI["Default handler:<br/>print stack trace<br/>and terminate JVM"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Resume execution<br/>after try-catch"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK["Program ends<br/>abnormally"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Program ends<br/>normally"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: String Class Hierarchy and Constructor Sources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["java.lang.Object"] --> nodeB["java.lang.String<br/>(final class)"]
    nodeB -.implements.-> nodeC["Serializable"]
    nodeB -.implements.-> nodeD["Comparable String"]
    nodeB -.implements.-> nodeE["CharSequence"]
    
    nodeF["String literal"] -- "Constructor 2" --> nodeB
    nodeG["char array"] -- "Constructor 3, 4" --> nodeB
    nodeH["byte array"] -- "Constructor 5 to 10" --> nodeB
    nodeI["StringBuffer"] -- "Constructor 11" --> nodeB
    nodeJ["StringBuilder"] -- "Constructor 12" --> nodeB
    nodeK["int codePoints"] -- "Constructor 13" --> nodeB
    nodeL["empty source"] -- "Constructor 1" --> nodeB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: String Memory Layout — Pool vs Heap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JVMHeap["JVM Heap Memory"]
        subgraph StringPool["String Constant Pool"]
            nodeA["ref: s1 -> KTU"]
            nodeB["ref: s2 -> KTU<br/>(same object as s1)"]
        end
        subgraph RegularHeap["Regular Heap Area"]
            nodeC["ref: s3 -> new String KTU<br/>(separate object)"]
        end
    end
    
    nodeA -. "== comparison" .-> nodeB
    nodeA -. "== comparison (false)" .-> nodeC
    nodeA -. ".equals comparison (true)" .-> nodeC
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of String Constructor Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Programmer intent:<br/>create a new String"] --> nodeB{"What is the<br/>source data?"}
    nodeB -- "Literal value" --> nodeC["Use string literal<br/>or new String literal"]
    nodeB -- "Character data" --> nodeD["Use char array<br/>constructors 3 or 4"]
    nodeB -- "Raw network/file bytes" --> nodeE{"Encoding<br/>known?"}
    nodeB -- "Mutable buffer" --> nodeF{"Thread-safe<br/>needed?"}
    nodeB -- "Unicode code points" --> nodeG["Use code point<br/>constructor 13"]
    nodeB -- "No initial data" --> nodeH["Use no-arg<br/>constructor 1"]
    
    nodeE -- "Yes" --> nodeI["Use charset-aware<br/>byte constructors 7 to 10"]
    nodeE -- "No" --> nodeJ["Use default-charset<br/>byte constructors 5 or 6"]
    
    nodeF -- "Yes" --> nodeK["Use StringBuffer<br/>constructor 11"]
    nodeF -- "No" --> nodeL["Use StringBuilder<br/>constructor 12"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Exception Propagation via `throw`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Level1["Caller Method (main)"]
        nodeM1["try block"]
        nodeM2["catch block"]
    end
    subgraph Level2["Callee Method (validate)"]
        nodeN1["if condition true"]
        nodeN2["throw new Exception"]
    end
    subgraph Level3["JVM Runtime Stack"]
        nodeP1["Stack frame: main"]
        nodeP2["Stack frame: validate"]
        nodeP3["Exception object in heap"]
    end
    
    nodeM1 -->|"invokes"| nodeN1
    nodeN1 -->|"condition met"| nodeN2
    nodeN2 -->|"creates"| nodeP3
    nodeN2 -->|"unwinds"| nodeP2
    nodeP2 -->|"pops frame"| nodeP1
    nodeP1 -->|"matches type"| nodeM2
    nodeM2 -->|"handler executes"| nodeR["Recovery code"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Explain the `throw` keyword in Java with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The `throw` keyword in Java is used to **explicitly throw an exception** from a program. It can throw either a checked or unchecked exception, but only **one exception at a time**. The syntax is:
```java
throw new ArithmeticException("Division by zero");
```
When a `throw` statement is executed, the **current method execution is terminated** and the exception is transferred to the nearest enclosing `try-catch` block. If no such handler exists, the JVM's default handler prints the stack trace and terminates the program. The `throw` keyword is typically used for **custom validation logic**, such as rejecting invalid input parameters in a business application.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**List any six constructors of the `String` class in Java with their purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Six important constructors of the `java.lang.String` class are:
1. **`String()`** — Creates an empty string object.
2. **`String(String original)`** — Creates a copy of the given string.
3. **`String(char[] value)`** — Creates a string from a character array.
4. **`String(char[] value, int offset, int count)`** — Creates a string from a subarray of characters.
5. **`String(byte[] bytes)`** — Creates a string from a byte array using the platform's default charset.
6. **`String(StringBuffer buffer)`** — Creates a string from a thread-safe StringBuffer object.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**Q. (a)** Explain the `throw` keyword in Java with syntax and example. Differentiate between `throw` and `throws`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The `throw` keyword is used in Java to **explicitly raise** an exception during program execution. It is followed by an instance of `Throwable` (or its subclass) which is dispatched to the JVM runtime.

**Syntax:**
```java
throw new ExceptionClass("Error message");
```

**Example Program:**
```java
class ThrowExample {
    public static void checkNumber(int n) {
        if (n < 0) {
            throw new ArithmeticException("Negative value: " + n);
        }
        System.out.println("Positive number: " + n);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            checkNumber(-10);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Caught: Negative value: -10
```

**Differentiation Table: `throw` vs `throws`**

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Purpose | Used to explicitly throw an exception | Used to declare exceptions a method can propagate |
| Number of Exceptions | Only one at a time | Multiple can be declared (comma-separated) |
| Location | Inside method body | In method signature |
| Syntax | `throw new Exception("msg")` | `void m() throws IOException` |
| Compile-time check | The thrown object's class must be compatible with enclosing `throws` clause or `try-catch` | Forces the caller to handle the declared exception |

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the different ways of creating String objects in Java. Explain at least five constructors of the `String` class with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Two Ways of Creating String Objects in Java:**

1. **Using String Literals:**
```java
String s = "Hello";
```
Strings created this way are stored in the **String Constant Pool** for memory efficiency. Identical literals share the same object reference.

2. **Using the `new` Keyword:**
```java
String s = new String("Hello");
```
This creates a **new object in the heap memory** outside the String Pool, even if an identical literal already exists in the pool.

**Five Important String Constructors with Examples:**

```java
public class StringConstructors {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // (i) String() - Empty string
        String s1 = new String();
        System.out.println("s1 = \"" + s1 + "\", length = " + s1.length());

        // (ii) String(String original) - Copy constructor
        String s2 = new String("KTU 2024");
        System.out.println("s2 = " + s2);

        // (iii) String(char[] value) - From char array
        char[] ch = {'P', 'E', 'M', 'R', 'T'};
        String s3 = new String(ch);
        System.out.println("s3 = " + s3);

        // (iv) String(char[] value, int offset, int count) - Subarray
        char[] letters = {'J', 'A', 'V', 'A', ' ', 'C', 'O', 'D', 'E'};
        String s4 = new String(letters, 5, 4);  // "CODE"
        System.out.println("s4 = " + s4);

        // (v) String(StringBuffer sb) - From StringBuffer
        StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Java Programming");
        String s5 = new String(sb);
        System.out.println("s5 = " + s5);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
s1 = "", length = 0
s2 = KTU 2024
s3 = PEMRT
s4 = CODE
s5 = Java Programming
```

**Key Observation:** In example (iv), the constructor extracts characters from index 5 to 8 (4 characters) from the `letters` array, producing the substring `"CODE"`.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q. (a)** What are the different constructors provided by the `String` class? Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of `String(char[] value, int offset, int count)` and `String(byte[] bytes, Charset charset)` constructors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `java.lang.String` class provides the following categories of constructors:

| Category | Constructor Form |
|----------|------------------|
| **Default** | `String()` |
| **String-based** | `String(String original)` |
| **Char array** | `String(char[])`, `String(char[], int, int)` |
| **Byte array** | `String(byte[])`, `String(byte[], int, int)`, charset variants |
| **Mutable buffer** | `String(StringBuffer)`, `String(StringBuilder)` |
| **Unicode** | `String(int[], int, int)` |

**Java Demonstration Program:**
```java
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;

public class StringConstructorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Demonstration of String(char[] value, int offset, int count)
        char[] alphabet = {'A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F', 'G', 'H', 'I', 'J'};
        String sub1 = new String(alphabet, 2, 5);  // Starts at index 2, length 5
        System.out.println("From char array: " + sub1);  // CDEFG

        // Demonstration of String(byte[] bytes, Charset charset)
        String greeting = "Hello KTU";
        byte[] utf8Bytes = greeting.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
        
        String reconstructed = new String(utf8Bytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
        System.out.println("From byte array (UTF-8): " + reconstructed);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
From char array: CDEFG
From byte array (UTF-8): Hello KTU
```

**Boundary Validation for `new String(alphabet, 2, 5)`:**
- `offset = 2` (must be $\geq 0$) ✓
- `count = 5` (must be $\geq 0$) ✓
- `offset + count = 2 + 5 = 7 \leq 10$ (alphabet.length) ✓

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the concept of immutability of Strings in Java. How does the String Constant Pool work? Write a program to demonstrate the difference between `==` and `.equals()` when comparing strings. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**String Immutability:**
In Java, once a `String` object is created, its content **cannot be modified**. Any operation that appears to modify a string (such as `concat()`, `replace()`, `toUpperCase()`) actually **creates a new String object** and returns it, leaving the original string unchanged. This is because the `String` class is declared as `final` and its internal `value` array is also final and private.

**String Constant Pool:**
The String Constant Pool is a **special memory area in the JVM heap** that stores unique string literals. When a string literal is created, the JVM first checks the pool:
- If the literal already exists, the **same reference** is returned.
- If not, a new string object is created and added to the pool.

This mechanism **optimizes memory usage** by avoiding duplicate string objects.

**Demonstration Program:**
```java
public class StringPoolDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Using literals - both refer to same pool object
        String s1 = "PEMRT415";
        String s2 = "PEMRT415";

        // Using new keyword - creates new object in heap
        String s3 = new String("PEMRT415");

        // Using intern() - forces pool reference
        String s4 = s3.intern();

        System.out.println("s1 == s2 : " + (s1 == s2));    // true  (same pool object)
        System.out.println("s1 == s3 : " + (s1 == s3));    // false (different objects)
        System.out.println("s1 == s4 : " + (s1 == s4));    // true  (after intern)
        System.out.println("s1.equals(s3) : " + s1.equals(s3));  // true (same content)
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
s1 == s2 : true
s1 == s3 : false
s1 == s4 : true
s1.equals(s3) : true
```

**Comparison: `==` vs `.equals()`**

| Comparison Method | Checks | Result for Same Content |
|-------------------|--------|------------------------|
| `==` (Reference equality) | Whether two references point to the **same memory address** | May be `false` even for identical content |
| `.equals()` (Value equality) | Whether two strings have the **same character sequence** | Always `true` for identical content |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `throw` with `throws`:** Students frequently interchange these two keywords. Remember: `throw` is a **statement** (inside the method body); `throws` is a **clause** (in the method signature). Marks are heavily deducted for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting `throws` clause for checked exceptions:** When using `throw new IOException()`, the enclosing method MUST declare `throws IOException` or wrap it in `try-catch`. Missing this causes a **compilation error** and full mark deduction.
> 3. **Off-by-one errors in `offset` and `count`:** For `new String(char[], int offset, int count)`, the `count` parameter specifies the **number of characters**, NOT the ending index. Writing `new String(ch, 0, 5)` reads 5 characters starting from index 0 — not up to index 5.
> 4. **Using `==` instead of `.equals()` for string comparison:** This is the most common string-handling mistake in KTU exams and results in **incorrect output** for heap-allocated strings.
> 5. **Forgetting to import `StandardCharsets`:** When using `new String(bytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8)`, students must add `import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;` at the top of the file.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **`throw` keyword** is used to **explicitly raise** an exception inside a method body; it can throw only **ONE exception per statement**.
- ✅ The operand of `throw` must be an instance of `Throwable` or its subclass (e.g., `Exception`, `RuntimeException`, `Error`).
- ✅ Code following a `throw` statement in the **same block** is **unreachable** and causes a compile-time error.
- ✅ **`throws` keyword** is used in the **method signature** to declare that the method may propagate one or more checked exceptions to the caller.
- ✅ Checked exceptions thrown via `throw` must be either **caught** in a `try-catch` block or **declared** in the enclosing method's `throws` clause.
- ✅ Java `String` class is declared as **`public final class String extends Object implements Serializable, Comparable, CharSequence`**.
- ✅ Strings in Java are **immutable** — once created, the character sequence cannot be changed.
- ✅ The **String Constant Pool** is a special heap region that stores unique string literals for memory optimization.
- ✅ **Two ways to create strings:** (1) String literals (stored in pool) and (2) `new String(...)` constructor (creates new heap object).
- ✅ The `String` class has **13 overloaded constructors** accepting inputs like empty parameters, another `String`, `char[]`, `byte[]` (with various charset options), `StringBuffer`, `StringBuilder`, and Unicode `int[]` code points.
- ✅ For **subarray constructors**, the syntax is `new String(array, offset, count)` where `count` is the **number of elements** to read (not the ending index).
- ✅ Boundary condition: $\text{offset} \geq 0$, $\text{count} \geq 0$, and $\text{offset} + \text{count} \leq \text{array.length}$.
- ✅ **`==` compares references** (memory addresses); **`.equals()` compares content** (character sequences). Always use `.equals()` for string content comparison.
- ✅ The `intern()` method forces a string to be added to the String Constant Pool, enabling reference equality with pool-resident literals.
- ✅ For multi-language support, use **charset-specific constructors** like `new String(bytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8)` to avoid encoding-related bugs.
- ✅ Real-world applications of String constructors: HTTP request body parsing, file I/O, database query construction, and internationalization (i18n).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: String Length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** String Length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Object Oriented Programming (OOP), **String Length** is a fundamental accessor operation (often implemented as a member function or method) that returns the total number of characters present within a string object, excluding the terminating null character (`\0`).
