According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEMRT415, string length operations are introduced as part of the **Standard Template Library (STL)** in C++ and the **`java.lang.String`** class in Java, both of which are core OOP implementations.

Formally, for a string $S$ consisting of characters $c_0, c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_{n-1}$, the string length function is defined as:

$$\text{length}(S) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 = n$$

where $n$ is the cardinality of the string, representing the count of valid characters preceding the sentinel null byte `\0`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** String length is a **Read-Only Operation** (Const Member Function). It does NOT modify the original string object. This adheres to the OOP principle of **Encapsulation** where internal state is protected from external mutation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **train with connected compartments**:
- Each **compartment** is a single character.
- The **train** is the string.
- The **String Length** is simply the **count of compartments** in the train, counted from the **engine (first character)** to the **last passenger car (last character before the brake van / null terminator)**.

You don't need to physically open every compartment — you just need to know the total number. In modern OOP languages like C++ and Java, the string object internally *remembers* its length, so getting it is **instantaneous** (like a train's pre-printed manifest), unlike in C where you had to walk through every compartment to count them.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Language | Method/Function | Header/Class | Return Type |
|----------|----------------|--------------|-------------|
| **C++ (C-style)** | `strlen(s)` | `<cstring>` | `size_t` |
| **C++ (STL)** | `s.length()` / `s.size()` | `<string>` | `size_t` |
| **Java** | `s.length()` | `java.lang.String` | `int` |
| **Python** | `len(s)` | Built-in | `int` |

> [!NOTE]
> In C++ STL, both `length()` and `size()` are **synonymous** and have identical implementation. The `size()` method was added for STL container consistency, while `length()` is more semantically meaningful for strings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — C++ `string::length()`

The C++ Standard Template Library (STL) `std::string` class maintains an internal member variable (typically `_M_length` or `_Size`) that stores the current string length. The `length()` method simply returns this stored value.

**Logic Steps:**

1. **Object Creation:** A `std::string` object is instantiated, allocating dynamic heap memory.
2. **Internal Tracking:** The class constructor initializes an internal size counter.
3. **Mutation Operations:** Functions like `push_back()`, `append()`, `+` operator, etc., update the internal counter automatically.
4. **Query Operation:** Calling `length()` is a direct memory read from the internal counter — **O(1) time complexity**.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    string greeting = "Hello, KTU!";
    cout << "Length: " << greeting.length() << endl;  // Output: 12
    cout << "Size:   " << greeting.size() << endl;    // Output: 12
    return 0;
}
```

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Java `String.length()`

In Java, strings are **immutable objects** backed by an internal `char[]` array (since Java 9, a `byte[]` with a coder flag for UTF-16/ISO-8859-1 optimization). The `length()` method is `final` and returns the array's `length` field.

**Logic Steps:**

1. **String Literal Pooling:** Java interns string literals in a constant pool.
2. **Immutable Storage:** Once created, the underlying `char[]` (or `byte[]`) cannot be resized.
3. **Length Storage:** The `String` class stores a `private final int hash` and uses the array's intrinsic `.length` property.
4. **Query Operation:** `s.length()` returns the array length in **O(1)**.

```java
public class StringLengthDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String dept = "Computer Science";
        int len = dept.length();
        System.out.println("Length: " + len);  // Output: 16
    }
}
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Method | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Notes |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|------------------|-------|
| **C++ STL `length()`** | Returns internal `_M_length` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Constant time, no null terminator counted |
| **C++ C-style `strlen()`** | Loops until `\0` encountered | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | **Includes** null check overhead |
| **Java `String.length()`** | Returns `value.length` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | Counts UTF-16 code units, NOT code points |
| **C++ C-style Length Formula** | $n = \text{count of chars before } \backslash 0$ | — | — | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \cup \{0\}$ |
| **Total Memory Footprint (C++)** | $\text{sizeof}(char) \times (n+1) \text{ bytes}$ | — | — | `+1` accounts for null terminator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** In C++ STL, the `length()` function returns the **logical length** (number of characters), NOT the memory size. The string stores the null terminator for compatibility with C-style functions, but `length()` does not count it.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Text Processing Pipelines:** Word counters, character limit validators (e.g., Twitter's 280-character cap), input sanitization.
- **Database Query Optimization:** Bounding string size before indexing operations.
- **Cryptography:** Verifying message block alignment (AES requires 16-byte blocks).
- **Network Protocols:** HTTP headers have strict length fields; incorrect `Content-Length` values cause transmission errors.
- **Compiler Design:** Lexical analyzers track token lengths for symbol table entries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Manual Length Computation — Analytical Walkthrough

Let us derive the length of the string $S = \text{"KTU 2024"}$ manually, character by character. This mirrors the C-style `strlen()` algorithm.

**Given String:** $S = \text{'K', 'T', 'U', ' ', '2', '0', '2', '4'}$

We iterate over each character with a counter variable $i$ starting at $0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & S[0] = \text{'K'} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 0 + 1 = 1 \\
\text{Step 2: } & S[1] = \text{'T'} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
\text{Step 3: } & S[2] = \text{'U'} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
\text{Step 4: } & S[3] = \text{' '} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 3 + 1 = 4 \\
\text{Step 5: } & S[4] = \text{'2'} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
\text{Step 6: } & S[5] = \text{'0'} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 5 + 1 = 6 \\
\text{Step 7: } & S[6] = \text{'2'} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 6 + 1 = 7 \\
\text{Step 8: } & S[7] = \text{'4'} \neq \backslash 0 \implies \text{count} = 7 + 1 = 8 \\
\text{Step 9: } & S[8] = \backslash 0 \text{ (Null Terminator)} \implies \textbf{STOP} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** $\text{length}(\text{"KTU 2024"}) = 8$ characters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whitespace Inclusion Rule:** The space character `' '` (ASCII 32) is **counted** as a valid character. Only the null terminator `\0` (ASCII 0) terminates the count.

### 3.2 Time Complexity Derivation for `strlen()`

For a string of length $n$, the C-style `strlen()` function performs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) & = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1 \\
     & = n \text{ iterations} \\
\therefore T(n) & = O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **linear time complexity** because in the worst case, the function must examine every character to locate the null terminator.

In contrast, for the C++ STL `string::length()`, the implementation simply returns a stored integer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) & = 1 \text{ (single memory read operation)} \\
\therefore T(n) & = O(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents a fundamental **performance improvement** of the OOP STL approach over the procedural C-style approach.

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: C-style `strlen()` vs C++ STL `length()`

| Parameter | C-style `strlen(const char*)` | C++ STL `string::length()` |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Algorithm** | Linear scan for `\0` | Direct field access |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Underlying Storage** | Raw `char[]` array | `std::string` object with internal counter |
| **Null Safety** | Undefined behavior if `nullptr` passed | No null issue (object-managed) |
| **Header Required** | `<cstring>` / `<string.h>` | `<string>` |
| **Return Type** | `size_t` (unsigned) | `size_t` (unsigned) |
| **OOP Compliance** | ❌ Procedural | ✅ Encapsulated |
| **Thread Safety** | ✅ (read-only on immutable memory) | ✅ (const method) |

### 3.4 Unicode Length Pitfall (Advanced)

For a Java string containing an emoji like `"😀KTU"`:

- The emoji 😀 is encoded in **UTF-16** as a **surrogate pair** (two `char` values: `\uD83D` and `\uDE00`).
- `s.length()` returns **5** (2 for emoji + 3 for "KTU"), NOT the visual character count of 4.
- For true Unicode code point counting, Java provides `s.codePointCount(0, s.length())`.

$$
\text{codePointCount} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \text{isHighSurrogate}(S[i]) \text{ ?} \text{ pair} : \text{single}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not assume `length()` always returns the number of *visual* characters. It returns the number of UTF-16 code **units**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — C++ STL String Length Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User calls s.length on std::string object] --> B{Is string object valid?}
    B -- No --> C[Undefined Behavior / Compile Error]
    B -- Yes --> D[Access internal _M_length member variable]
    D --> E[Return value as size_t]
    E --> F[Display or store result]
    F --> G[End of Operation]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — C-style `strlen()` Linear Scan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Call strlen with char array] --> B[Initialize counter = 0]
    B --> C[Access character at index counter]
    C --> D{Is character == null terminator?}
    D -- No --> E[Increment counter by 1]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Return counter as size_t]
    F --> G[End of Operation]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Java String Class Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "java.lang.String Object"
        A[hash field: int]
        B[value field: byte array since Java 9]
        C[ coder field: byte ]
    end
    D[Method: length] --> B
    B --> E[Returns value array length]
    E --> F[Result: int]

    style A fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison: Procedural vs OOP Length Query

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC["Procedural C Approach"]
        P1[Raw char array] --> P2[Pass to strlen]
        P2 --> P3[Linear scan: On]
    end

    subgraph OOP["OOP C++ STL Approach"]
        O1[String object with internal counter] --> O2[Call length method]
        O2 --> O3[Direct read: O1]
    end

    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "String Length" in the context of Object Oriented Programming. How is it determined in C++ STL `std::string`?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** String length is the number of characters present in a string object, excluding the null terminator (`\0`). It represents the size of the character sequence stored in the string object.

**C++ STL Determination (1.5 Marks):** In C++ STL, the `std::string` class maintains an internal member variable that tracks the current string size. The `length()` (or equivalently `size()`) member function returns this stored value directly, achieving $O(1)$ time complexity without scanning the character array.

```cpp
string s = "Hello";
cout << s.length();  // Output: 5
```

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Compare the time complexity of C-style `strlen()` function and C++ STL `string::length()` method. Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Function | Time Complexity | Reason |
|----------|----------------|--------|
| C-style `strlen()` | $O(n)$ | Linear scan until `\0` is encountered |
| C++ STL `length()` | $O(1)$ | Returns pre-stored internal counter |

**Justification (1 Mark):** The C-style function must iterate character-by-character because it has no metadata about the string's length. In contrast, the STL `std::string` object encapsulates the length as a private data member updated during every mutation, enabling constant-time queries.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of String Length in C++ STL with a suitable code example. Discuss how the `length()` and `size()` methods differ, if at all. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a C++ program that accepts a full sentence from the user (including spaces) and counts:
   (i) The total number of characters.
   (ii) The number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u — case insensitive).
   (iii) The number of words (separated by single spaces). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Concept (3 Marks):** In C++ STL, `std::string` is a class that encapsulates a dynamic character sequence. The `length()` and `size()` methods are **public member functions** that return the number of characters in the string object. Both functions have **identical implementations** and differ only in nomenclature — `size()` was introduced for consistency with other STL containers (like `vector`, `list`), while `length()` is semantically specific to strings.

**Code Example (4 Marks):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    string course = "Object Oriented Programming";
    
    cout << "Course: " << course << endl;
    cout << "Length(): " << course.length() << endl;  // 27
    cout << "Size():   " << course.size() << endl;    // 27
    
    // Demonstrating empty string
    string empty = "";
    cout << "Empty string length: " << empty.length() << endl;  // 0
    
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Course: Object Oriented Programming
Length(): 27
Size():   27
Empty string length: 0
```

**Model Solution (b):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    string sentence;
    cout << "Enter a sentence: ";
    getline(cin, sentence);
    
    int totalChars = sentence.length();
    
    // Count vowels
    int vowelCount = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < sentence.length(); i++) {
        char ch = tolower(sentence[i]);
        if (ch == 'a' || ch == 'e' || ch == 'i' || ch == 'o' || ch == 'u') {
            vowelCount++;
        }
    }
    
    // Count words (assuming words separated by single spaces)
    int wordCount = 0;
    bool inWord = false;
    for (int i = 0; i < sentence.length(); i++) {
        if (sentence[i] != ' ' && !inWord) {
            wordCount++;
            inWord = true;
        } else if (sentence[i] == ' ') {
            inWord = false;
        }
    }
    
    cout << "Total characters: " << totalChars << endl;
    cout << "Number of vowels: " << vowelCount << endl;
    cout << "Number of words:  " << wordCount << endl;
    
    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**
```
Enter a sentence: Object Oriented Programming is fun
Total characters: 35
Number of vowels: 11
Number of words:  5
```

**Mark Distribution (b):**
- Correct input handling with `getline()`: **1 Mark**
- Character counting using `.length()`: **1 Mark**
- Vowel detection logic with case handling: **2 Marks**
- Word counting state machine logic: **2 Marks**
- Output formatting: **1 Mark**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe how string length is implemented in Java. Explain the immutability aspect and its effect on length computation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to:
   (i) Find the length of a string entered by the user.
   (ii) Check whether the string is a palindrome (case insensitive, ignoring spaces).
   (iii) Display the reversed string. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Java Implementation (3 Marks):** In Java, strings are objects of the `java.lang.String` class. The class internally stores the character data in a `char[]` array (or `byte[]` since Java 9 with a `coder` field for compact strings). The `length()` method is declared as `public int length()` and simply returns the `length` property of the underlying array, achieving $O(1)$ time complexity.

**Immutability (2 Marks):** Java strings are **immutable** — once a `String` object is created, its content cannot be modified. This means:
- The `length` value is computed once at construction and never changes.
- Any modification creates a **new** `String` object.
- This guarantees that `length()` always returns a consistent value, supporting thread safety.

**Effect on Length Computation (2 Marks):** Because the string is immutable, the JVM can safely cache and optimize the length. There is no need to re-compute or synchronize. The `length()` method is implicitly thread-safe due to the read-only nature of immutable objects.

**Model Solution (b):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class StringAnalyzer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a string: ");
        String input = sc.nextLine();
        
        // (i) Length
        int len = input.length();
        System.out.println("Length: " + len);
        
        // (ii) Palindrome check (case insensitive, ignoring spaces)
        String cleaned = input.replaceAll("\\s+", "").toLowerCase();
        String reversed = new StringBuilder(cleaned).reverse().toString();
        boolean isPalindrome = cleaned.equals(reversed);
        System.out.println("Is Palindrome: " + isPalindrome);
        
        // (iii) Display reversed string
        System.out.println("Reversed: " + reversed);
        
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Enter a string: A man a plan a canal Panama
Length: 30
Is Palindrome: true
Reversed: amanaplanacanalpanama
```

**Mark Distribution (b):**
- Scanner input and length computation: **1 Mark**
- String cleaning logic (remove spaces + lowercase): **2 Marks**
- Reversal using `StringBuilder`: **2 Marks**
- Palindrome comparison logic: **1 Mark**
- Proper output formatting: **1 Mark**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing `length()` with `length` property (Java):** `arr.length` is an array property (no parentheses), but `str.length()` is a String method (with parentheses). Mixing these up results in **compilation errors** and **zero marks**.
> 2. **Counting the null terminator in C++:** Students often incorrectly add `+1` to the STL `length()` result. Remember: STL `length()` does **NOT** count `\0`. Only the underlying `c_str()` returned array would include it.
> 3. **Forgetting `getline()` in C++:** Using `cin >> str` for sentences with spaces will **truncate** at the first space, leading to wrong character and word counts. KTU examiners specifically test this edge case.
> 4. **Unicode Assumption:** In Java, `length()` returns UTF-16 code units, not visual characters. For emojis and certain Asian scripts, this gives surprising results. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Off-by-one errors in word counting:** A naive `count = number of spaces + 1` fails when there are multiple consecutive spaces. Use a **state-based** approach (inWord flag) for robustness.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "String Length":**
> - **C++ STL:** `s.length()` and `s.size()` are **identical**; both return `size_t` in **O(1)**.
> - **C++ C-style:** `strlen(s)` is a **function** (not method) that scans linearly in **O(n)** until `\0`.
> - **Java:** `s.length()` is a **method** (note the parentheses!) returning `int` in **O(1)**.
> - **Java Immutability:** Strings are immutable; length is set at creation and never changes.
> - **Null Terminator:** Counted by `strlen()` indirectly (as termination marker), **NOT counted** by STL `length()`.
> - **Whitespace:** Spaces are valid characters and ARE included in the length.
> - **Return Types:** C++ returns unsigned `size_t`; Java returns signed `int` — this matters for very large strings (>2³¹ in Java would overflow).
> - **Time Complexity Edge:** Both `s.length()` (C++) and `s.length()` (Java) are O(1). Do NOT mark them as O(n) — this is a frequent conceptual error.
> - **UTF-16 Surrogate Pairs:** Java's `length()` counts surrogate pairs as 2, not 1. Use `codePointCount()` for true Unicode character count.
> - **Encapsulation Principle:** OOP strings hide internal buffer management; you interact only via methods like `length()`, `substr()`, `append()`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special String Operations. Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Special String Operations. Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Special String Operations in Java

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Special String Operations** in Java refer to the advanced, mutable manipulation of character sequences using the `java.lang.StringBuffer` and `java.lang.StringBuilder` classes, along with tokenization via `java.util.StringTokenizer` and parsing of command-line arguments to the `main()` method. Unlike the immutable `String` class, these classes allow in-place modification of character data, providing efficient memory utilization and O(1) amortized append operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Java's `String` objects are **immutable** (cannot be changed once created), while `StringBuffer` and `StringBuilder` objects are **mutable** (their content can be modified without creating new objects).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are writing on a sheet of paper:
- **String** = A printed page in a book. If you want to change a word, you must reprint the entire page.
- **StringBuffer** = A whiteboard with a lock. You can erase and rewrite, but only one person (thread) can use it at a time.
- **StringBuilder** = A whiteboard without a lock. Multiple people can write simultaneously, but they may collide (no synchronization).

This analogy immediately clarifies why Java offers three string-handling classes — each tuned for a specific concurrency and performance scenario.

### Key Distinctions Table

| Class | Mutability | Thread-Safety | Performance | Storage Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `String` | Immutable | Yes (read-only) | Slow for concatenation | String Constant Pool |
| `StringBuffer` | Mutable | Yes (synchronized) | Moderate | Heap |
| `StringBuilder` | Mutable | No (non-synchronized) | Fast | Heap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Capacity:** Both `StringBuffer` and `StringBuilder` are created with an initial capacity of **16 characters**. When exceeded, the new capacity becomes `(oldCapacity * 2) + 2`.

### The Main Thread
Every Java application has at least one thread — the **main thread** — which is created by the JVM automatically when the program starts. The `main()` method runs on this thread, and additional threads are spawned from it.

---

## 1.2 Multithreaded Programming & The Java Thread Model

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
**Multithreaded Programming** is a paradigm in which a single process contains multiple lightweight concurrent execution contexts called **threads**, each sharing the same memory space (heap) but maintaining its own stack, program counter, and local variables. **The Java Thread Model** is Java's built-in, language-level support for multithreading, defined primarily by the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` interface, providing built-in keywords like `synchronized`, `volatile`, and methods like `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` for thread coordination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Core Concept:** A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Java inherently supports multithreading — it was the first mainstream language to integrate threading primitives directly into the language specification (James Gosling, 1995).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **kitchen (process)** preparing a multi-course meal:
- **Single-threaded** = One chef doing everything sequentially (boil pasta → cook sauce → bake bread → ...). Slow!
- **Multithreaded** = Multiple chefs (threads) working in the same kitchen. They share the pantry (heap memory) and appliances (class resources), but each has their own cutting board and recipe notes (stack + local variables). The head chef (main thread) coordinates the others.

This perfectly illustrates three core properties of threads:
1. **Shared memory** (pantry) — threads in the same process share heap memory.
2. **Independent stacks** (cutting boards) — each thread has its own call stack.
3. **Coordination needed** (head chef) — synchronization prevents resource conflicts.

### Key Constants & Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> - **Thread priority range:** `MIN_PRIORITY = 1`, `NORM_PRIORITY = 5`, `MAX_PRIORITY = 10` (constants defined in `Thread` class).
> - **Default main thread priority:** `5` (NORM_PRIORITY).
> - **Java does NOT guarantee thread scheduling order** — priorities are merely *hints* to the OS thread scheduler (especially on Windows; on Linux they are largely ignored).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: `StringBuffer` & `StringBuilder` Operations

Both classes inherit from `Object` and implement `java.io.Serializable`, `java.lang.CharSequence`. Their API surface is nearly identical; the only difference is **synchronization** (present in `StringBuffer`, absent in `StringBuilder`).

### Core Methods — Structured Logic Steps

**Step 1: Construction**
- `new StringBuffer()` → initial capacity = **16**
- `new StringBuffer(int capacity)` → user-defined capacity
- `new StringBuffer(String str)` → capacity = `str.length() + 16`

**Step 2: Appending Data (`append()`)**
- Overloaded for `int`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`, `Object`, `String`, `char[]`, etc.
- Returns a reference to **the same object** (chainable).
- If the new length exceeds capacity → internal array is grown to `(oldCapacity * 2) + 2`.

**Step 3: Insertion (`insert(int offset, ...)`)**
- Inserts data at a specified index, shifting existing characters right.
- Throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if `offset < 0` or `offset > length()`.

**Step 4: Deletion (`delete(int start, int end)` and `deleteCharAt(int index)`)**
- `delete(start, end)` removes characters in the half-open range `[start, end)`.
- `deleteCharAt(index)` removes a single character.

**Step 5: Reversal (`reverse()`)**
- Reverses the character sequence in place.

**Step 6: Replacement (`replace(int start, int end, String str)`)**
- Replaces the substring `[start, end)` with the given string.

**Step 7: Capacity Management**
- `capacity()` returns current allocated size.
- `length()` returns actual character count.
- `ensureCapacity(int minCapacity)` guarantees at least the given capacity.
- `setLength(int newLength)` truncates or pads with null characters (`'\u0000'`).

**Why these methods matter in engineering:**
- Building dynamic SQL queries
- Constructing JSON/XML payloads in server responses
- Real-time log aggregation
- Stream processing in data pipelines

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: `StringTokenizer`

### Formal Definition
`java.util.StringTokenizer` is a legacy class that **splits a string into tokens** (substrings) based on a set of delimiter characters. It is simpler than `String.split()` but less powerful (no regex support).

### Key Constructors & Methods
- `StringTokenizer(String str)` — uses default delimiters: space, tab, newline, carriage return.
- `StringTokenizer(String str, String delimiters)` — custom delimiter set.
- `StringTokenizer(String str, String delimiters, boolean returnDelims)` — optionally returns delimiters as tokens.
- `hasMoreTokens()` / `hasMoreElements()` — returns `true` if more tokens exist.
- `nextToken()` / `nextToken(String delim)` / `nextElement()` — returns next token.
- `countTokens()` — number of remaining tokens.

### Why and How
- **Why use it?** Simpler and faster than regex for simple delimiter-based parsing (e.g., CSV files with fixed delimiters).
- **How it works internally:** Maintains a cursor position and a `String` reference; `nextToken()` uses `String.indexOf()` to find the next delimiter.

---

## 2.3 Command Line Arguments

### Formal Definition
The `main(String args[])` method receives **command-line arguments** as a `String` array. `args.length` indicates the number of arguments; `args[0]` is the first argument (no argument contains the program name in Java — unlike C/C++).

### Why and How
- **Why?** Enables runtime configuration without recompilation (e.g., file paths, server ports, flags).
- **How?** The JVM parses the command line, splits by whitespace, and passes the array to `main()`. Quoted strings preserve embedded spaces: `java MyApp "Hello World" 42`.

---

## 2.4 The Java Thread Model — Deep Theory

### Two Ways to Create a Thread

**Method A: Extend `java.lang.Thread`**
1. Subclass `Thread`.
2. Override the `run()` method (this is the thread's entry point).
3. Create an instance and call `start()` — `start()` invokes `run()` in a new call stack.

**Method B: Implement `java.lang.Runnable`**
1. Implement the `Runnable` interface (single method: `void run()`).
2. Pass the `Runnable` object to a `new Thread(runnableObj)` constructor.
3. Call `start()` on the `Thread` object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Implementing `Runnable` is **preferred** over extending `Thread` because:
> 1. Java does not support multiple inheritance — implementing `Runnable` leaves the class free to extend another.
> 2. It separates the **task** (`Runnable`) from the **thread mechanism** (`Thread`) — better OO design (favor composition over inheritance).
> 3. The same `Runnable` instance can be passed to multiple `Thread` objects (task reuse).

### Thread Life Cycle (5 States)

| State | Description | Entry Transition | Exit Transition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **New** | Thread object created, `start()` not yet called | `new Thread()` | `start()` |
| **Runnable** | Ready to run, waiting for CPU | `start()` called | Scheduler picks it |
| **Running** | Actively executing in CPU | Scheduler dispatches | Time slice expired / `yield()` / blocked |
| **Blocked/Waiting/Sleeping** | Suspended (I/O, `sleep()`, `wait()`, lock contention) | `sleep()`, `wait()`, I/O, `join()` | Sleep timeout / `notify()` / I/O done |
| **Terminated (Dead)** | `run()` returned or exception escaped | `run()` completes | Never re-enters |

### Thread Priority Constants

$$ \text{MIN\_PRIORITY} = 1 $$
$$ \text{NORM\_PRIORITY} = 5 $$
$$ \text{MAX\_PRIORITY} = 10 $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Setting a high priority **does not guarantee** execution order. The JVM maps Java priorities to OS-native priorities, and on many systems (especially Linux with NPTL), this mapping is coarse. Do not rely on priority for program correctness — use synchronization primitives.

### Critical Thread Methods

| Method | Purpose | Static? |
|---|---|---|
| `start()` | Spawns new call stack, invokes `run()` | No |
| `run()` | Entry point of the thread | No |
| `sleep(ms)` | Pauses current thread for `ms` milliseconds (does NOT release locks) | Yes |
| `yield()` | Hints scheduler to give other threads a chance | Yes |
| `join()` | Current thread waits for this thread to die | No |
| `interrupt()` | Sets interrupt flag; interrupts `sleep`/`wait`/`join` | No |
| `isAlive()` | Returns `true` if thread started and not yet dead | No |
| `setPriority(int)` / `getPriority()` | Sets/gets priority (1–10) | No |
| `setName(String)` / `getName()` | Sets/gets thread name | No |
| `currentThread()` | Returns reference to currently executing thread | Yes |
| `wait()` | Releases lock, waits for `notify()` (must be in `synchronized` block) | No |
| `notify()` | Wakes one waiter on the same object's monitor | No |
| `notifyAll()` | Wakes all waiters on the same object's monitor | No |

### Synchronization

**The Problem:** When multiple threads access shared mutable data, **race conditions** occur — results depend on unpredictable scheduling order.

**The Solution:** Use the `synchronized` keyword.

**Two forms:**
1. **Synchronized method:** `synchronized void increment() { ... }` — locks `this` (the current object).
2. **Synchronized block:** `synchronized(obj) { ... }` — locks the specified object.

$$ \text{Intrinsic Lock (Monitor)} = \text{Every Java object has one implicit lock} $$

### Inter-Thread Communication: `wait()` / `notify()`

These methods (inherited from `Object`) allow coordinated communication between threads. They **must** be called inside a `synchronized` context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Rule:** `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` are instance methods of `Object`, NOT `Thread`. This is because they operate on the **monitor of the calling object**, not on the thread itself.

**The "Producer–Consumer" pattern** is the canonical use case:
1. Consumer calls `wait()` → releases lock, blocks.
2. Producer produces data, calls `notify()` → wakes consumer.
3. Consumer reacquires lock, consumes data.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| StringBuffer growth | $C_{new} = (C_{old} \times 2) + 2$ | Characters |
| Default capacity | $C_0 = 16$ | Characters |
| String + String cost | $O(n^2)$ for `n` concatenations | Use `StringBuilder` |
| StringBuilder append | $O(1)$ amortized | n/a |
| Thread priority range | $1 \le p \le 10$ | Integer |
| Main thread priority | $p_{main} = 5$ | Integer |
| `sleep(ms)` precision | OS-dependent (typically 10–50 ms) | ms |
| Monitor per object | 1 lock per Java object | Binary semaphore |
| `wait()` releases lock | Yes | Must hold lock first |
| `sleep()` releases lock | No | Holds lock |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** Multithreading powers web servers (handling 10,000+ concurrent HTTP requests), real-time trading systems, GUI responsiveness (keep UI thread free for rendering), and parallel data processing (MapReduce, Spark). StringBuffer/StringBuilder underpin every JSON/HTTP response builder in frameworks like Spring and Netty.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: `StringBuffer` Capacity Growth Function

**Problem:** Prove that after $k$ growth events, the StringBuffer capacity follows a geometric progression.

**Step 1: Initial state.** Let $C_0 = 16$ (default initial capacity).

**Step 2: First growth event.** When content exceeds 16, the new capacity is:

$$ C_1 = (C_0 \times 2) + 2 = (16 \times 2) + 2 = 34 $$

**Step 3: Second growth event.**

$$ C_2 = (C_1 \times 2) + 2 = (34 \times 2) + 2 = 70 $$

**Step 4: General recurrence relation.**

$$ C_{n+1} = 2C_n + 2 $$

**Step 5: Solve the recurrence** by adding the constant to convert to homogeneous form. Let $C_n + 2 = D_n$:

$$ D_{n+1} = C_{n+1} + 2 = (2C_n + 2) + 2 = 2(C_n + 2) = 2D_n $$

So $D_n = D_0 \cdot 2^n = (16 + 2) \cdot 2^n = 18 \cdot 2^n$.

**Step 6: Back-substitute.**

$$ C_n + 2 = 18 \cdot 2^n $$
$$ C_n = 18 \cdot 2^n - 2 $$

**Step 7: Verification.**

$$ C_0 = 18 \cdot 2^0 - 2 = 18 - 2 = 16 \quad \checkmark $$
$$ C_1 = 18 \cdot 2^1 - 2 = 36 - 2 = 34 \quad \checkmark $$
$$ C_2 = 18 \cdot 2^2 - 2 = 72 - 2 = 70 \quad \checkmark $$

**Conclusion:** The capacity grows as $C_n = 18 \cdot 2^n - 2$. This exponential growth ensures **amortized O(1) cost** per `append()` call.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Time Complexity of String Operations

| Operation | `String` (immutable) | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` |
|---|---|---|---|
| `length()` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| `charAt(i)` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| `concat(s)` / `+` | $O(n+m)$ (new object) | n/a | n/a |
| `append(s)` | n/a | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(1)$ amortized |
| `insert(i, s)` | n/a | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| `delete(s, e)` | n/a | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| `reverse()` | n/a | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| `substring(s, e)` | $O(n)$ (new object) | $O(n)$ (new String) | $O(n)$ (new String) |

> **Rule of Thumb:** For concatenating strings in a loop, always use `StringBuilder` (single-threaded) or `StringBuffer` (multi-threaded). The `+` operator with `String` creates a new object on every iteration, leading to the infamous $O(n^2)$ behavior.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: `StringTokenizer` Walkthrough

**Problem:** Parse the CSV string `"apple,banana,cherry,dates"` using `,` as delimiter.

**Step-by-step execution:**

```java
StringTokenizer st = new StringTokenizer("apple,banana,cherry,dates", ",");
System.out.println("Total tokens: " + st.countTokens());  // 4

while (st.hasMoreTokens()) {
    System.out.println(st.nextToken());
}
```

**Output trace:**

| Iteration | `hasMoreTokens()` | `nextToken()` returns | `countTokens()` after |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | true | `"apple"` | 3 |
| 2 | true | `"banana"` | 2 |
| 3 | true | `"cherry"` | 1 |
| 4 | true | `"dates"` | 0 |
| 5 | false | (loop exits) | 0 |

**Conversion logic:**
- Constructor: scans the input string, building an internal delimiter index.
- `hasMoreTokens()`: checks if cursor is past the end.
- `nextToken()`: scans from current cursor to next delimiter, returns substring, advances cursor.
- `countTokens()`: re-scans from current cursor to count remaining delimiters + 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** `countTokens()` is **O(n)** in the remaining string length because it re-scans. If you need a quick token count, use `String.split()` and check `arr.length`.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Demonstrating Multithreading with `Runnable`

**Full Program (with line-by-line explanation):**

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyTask task = new MyTask();          // Step 1: create shared task
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "A");   // Step 2: wrap in Thread object
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "B");   // Step 3: same task, different thread
        t1.start();                          // Step 4: start thread A
        t2.start();                          // Step 5: start thread B
    }
}
```

**Expected Output (interleaved, non-deterministic):**

```
A : 1
B : 1
A : 2
B : 2
A : 3
B : 3
A : 4
B : 4
A : 5
B : 5
```

> [!WARNING]
> The exact interleaving is **not guaranteed**. The OS scheduler decides the order. This non-determinism is the central challenge in concurrent programming.

---

## 3.5 Demonstrating `synchronized` — Solving a Race Condition

**The problem (unsafe):**

```java
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    public void increment() {
        count++;   // NOT atomic: read-modify-write
    }
}
```

**Analysis of the race condition:**

$$ \text{count++} \equiv \text{read } count \rightarrow \text{add 1} \rightarrow \text{write } count $$

If two threads execute this concurrently, both might read $count = 5$, both write $count = 6$ — losing one increment.

**The fix:**

```java
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    public synchronized void increment() {  // acquires 'this' lock
        count++;
    }
}
```

**Result:** Only one thread can execute `increment()` at a time per `Counter` instance. Race condition eliminated.

---

## 3.6 Producer–Consumer Using `wait()` / `notify()`

```java
class SharedBuffer {
    private int data;
    private boolean available = false;

    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (available) {
            wait();   // buffer full → wait for consumer
        }
        data = value;
        available = true;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + value);
        notify();    // wake up consumer
    }

    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!available) {
            wait();   // buffer empty → wait for producer
        }
        available = false;
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + data);
        notify();    // wake up producer
        return data;
    }
}
```

**Why `while` instead of `if`?** Spurious wakeups and multiple-waiter scenarios can cause a thread to wake up even when the condition isn't met. The `while` loop re-checks — this is a KTU-favorite interview question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Java Thread Life Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew : new Thread()
    stateNew --> stateRunnable : start()
    stateRunnable --> stateRunning : scheduler dispatch
    stateRunning --> stateRunnable : yield() / time slice
    stateRunning --> stateBlocked : sleep() / wait() / I/O / lock
    stateBlocked --> stateRunnable : timeout / notify() / I/O done
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated : run() returns
    stateTerminated --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `[*]` denotes the start (no thread exists).
- `stateNew` → object created, `start()` not called.
- `stateRunnable` → ready queue, waiting for CPU.
- `stateRunning` → actively executing on a CPU core.
- `stateBlocked` → suspended (sleep, wait, I/O, monitor contention).
- `stateTerminated` → `run()` method has returned; thread cannot be restarted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Calling `start()` on a terminated thread throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. Each `Thread` object can be started **exactly once**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: String Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    objA["Object"] --> charSeq["CharSequence Interface"]
    charSeq --> strCls["String Immutable"]
    charSeq --> strBuf["StringBuffer Mutable Synchronized"]
    charSeq --> strBld["StringBuilder Mutable Non Synchronized"]
    strBuf -.->. implA["Serializable Appendable"]
    strBld -.->. implB["Serializable Appendable"]
    strCls --> tok["StringTokenizer Utility"]
    tok -.->. utilA["Enumeration of Tokens"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All three classes implement `CharSequence`, exposing `charAt()`, `length()`, `subSequence()`.
- `StringBuffer` and `StringBuilder` additionally implement `Appendable` and `Serializable`.
- `StringTokenizer` is a utility class (not a `CharSequence`) that produces tokens from a `String`.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Multithreaded Server Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    clientA["Client 1"] --> listener["ServerSocket accept"]
    clientB["Client 2"] --> listener
    clientC["Client 3"] --> listener
    listener --> pool["Thread Pool Executor"]
    pool --> th1["Worker Thread 1"]
    pool --> th2["Worker Thread 2"]
    pool --> th3["Worker Thread 3"]
    th1 --> heap["Shared Heap Memory"]
    th2 --> heap
    th3 --> heap
    heap --> dbConn["Shared DB Connection Pool"]
    dbConn -. synchronized .- th1
    dbConn -. synchronized .- th2
    dbConn -. synchronized .- th3
```

**Engineering context:** A web server (Tomcat, Netty) accepts incoming connections, dispatches each to a worker thread from a thread pool, all sharing the heap (loaded classes, static data) and a synchronized connection pool. This is the architectural pattern behind every multi-user web application.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: `synchronized` Monitor Lock Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Thread 1
    participant OBJ as Shared Object Monitor
    participant T2 as Thread 2

    T1->>OBJ: acquire lock
    OBJ-->>T1: granted (enters synchronized block)
    Note over T1: executes critical section
    T2->>OBJ: acquire lock (BLOCKED)
    Note over T2: queued, waiting
    T1->>OBJ: release lock (exit block)
    OBJ-->>T2: granted (enters critical section)
    Note over T2: executes critical section
    T2->>OBJ: release lock
```

**Interpretation:**
- Only **one thread** can hold a given object's monitor at a time.
- Other threads attempting to enter a `synchronized` block on the same object are parked in the **entry set** (waiting queue).
- When the holder exits, one waiter is unblocked and granted the lock.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Explain any three methods of `StringBuffer` class with example.*

**Model Answer:**

**Method 1: `append()`** — Concatenates data to the end of the buffer.

```java
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Hello");
sb.append(" World");
System.out.println(sb);  // Hello World
```

**Method 2: `insert(int offset, String s)`** — Inserts string `s` at index `offset`, shifting existing characters right.

```java
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Hello World");
sb.insert(6, "Java ");
System.out.println(sb);  // Hello Java World
```

**Method 3: `reverse()`** — Reverses the character sequence in place.

```java
StringBuffer sb = new StringBuffer("Java");
System.out.println(sb.reverse());  // avaJ
```

All three methods return the same `StringBuffer` object reference, enabling method chaining like `sb.append("a").insert(0, "X").reverse();`.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any four differences between `String` and `StringBuffer`.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | `String` | `StringBuffer` |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Immutable — content cannot be changed | Mutable — content can be modified |
| 2 | Stored in String Constant Pool | Stored in Heap memory |
| 3 | Concatenation creates new object (`+` operator) | `append()` modifies the same object |
| 4 | Thread-safe by design (read-only) | Thread-safe via `synchronized` methods |
| 5 | Slower for repeated modifications | Faster for repeated modifications |
| 6 | Overrides `equals()` for content comparison | Inherits `equals()` from `Object` (reference comparison) |

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the Java Thread Life Cycle with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of `start()`, `run()`, `sleep()`, `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to create two threads where one thread prints even numbers from 1 to 20 and the other prints odd numbers from 1 to 20. Use the `Runnable` interface. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Thread Life Cycle

A thread in Java transitions through the following states:

1. **New**: The `Thread` object is created using `new Thread()`, but `start()` has not been called. The thread is not yet scheduled.

2. **Runnable**: After `start()` is called, the thread enters the runnable state and waits in the ready queue for CPU allocation. *Diagram:* (See SECTION 4.1)

3. **Running**: The thread scheduler picks the thread and it begins executing its `run()` method on a CPU core.

4. **Blocked / Waiting / Sleeping**: The thread is temporarily suspended due to:
   - `sleep(ms)` — pauses for given milliseconds.
   - `wait()` — releases the lock and waits for `notify()`.
   - I/O operations or lock contention.

5. **Terminated (Dead)**: The `run()` method returns or an uncaught exception escapes. The thread cannot be restarted.

**Roles of key methods:**

- `start()`: Creates a new call stack and invokes `run()`. Calling `run()` directly executes it in the current thread (no new thread created).
- `run()`: The entry point of the thread's execution. Override this to define the task.
- `sleep(ms)`: Static method; pauses the current thread for `ms` milliseconds. **Does NOT release the lock**. Throws `InterruptedException` if interrupted.
- `wait()`: Instance method of `Object`; releases the lock on the calling object and blocks until `notify()`/`notifyAll()` is called by another thread on the same object. Must be called inside a `synchronized` block.
- `notify()`: Wakes **one** arbitrary thread waiting on the same object's monitor.
- `notifyAll()`: Wakes **all** threads waiting on the same object's monitor. They compete for the lock; only one wins.

#### Model Solution (b) — Two Threads Using `Runnable`

```java
class EvenPrinter implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 2; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Even : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}

class OddPrinter implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Odd  : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new EvenPrinter(), "EvenThread");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new OddPrinter(), "OddThread");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- Two `Runnable` implementations (`EvenPrinter`, `OddPrinter`) define separate tasks.
- Each is wrapped in a `Thread` object with a descriptive name.
- `start()` (not `run()`) is called to spawn new threads.
- `Thread.sleep(100)` slows the output so interleaving is observable; without it one thread may finish entirely before the other starts (depending on OS scheduling).

**Sample Output (interleaved, order not guaranteed):**

```
Even : 2
Odd  : 1
Even : 4
Odd  : 3
...
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the differences between `String`, `StringBuffer`, and `StringBuilder`. When would you prefer one over the others? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of `StringTokenizer` for splitting a sentence into words and counting the total number of words. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Comparison

| Feature | `String` | `StringBuffer` | `StringBuilder` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutability | Immutable | Mutable | Mutable |
| Thread Safety | Yes (inherently) | Yes (synchronized) | No |
| Performance | Slowest for concatenation | Moderate | Fastest |
| Storage | String Pool | Heap | Heap |
| Introduced In | Java 1.0 | Java 1.0 | Java 1.5 |
| Memory | New object per modification | Grows on demand | Grows on demand |

**When to prefer each:**

- **Use `String`** when the value is constant or rarely changes (e.g., error messages, configuration keys, log tags). Compiler optimizations like string interning make these efficient.

- **Use `StringBuilder`** in **single-threaded** contexts where you perform many modifications — building SQL queries, constructing JSON, formatting strings in loops. It is the **fastest** of the three.

- **Use `StringBuffer`** in **multi-threaded** contexts where multiple threads modify the same string buffer. The synchronization overhead is the price of safety.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse `String` concatenation (which creates a new object) with `StringBuffer.append()` (which modifies in place). In `for` loops, repeated `s = s + x` is **O(n²)** and creates **n+1 garbage objects** — a guaranteed mark deduction if not handled correctly.

#### Model Solution (b) — `StringTokenizer` Word Counter

```java
import java.util.StringTokenizer;

public class WordCounter {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String sentence = "Java is a powerful object oriented programming language";
        StringTokenizer st = new StringTokenizer(sentence);
        
        int wordCount = st.countTokens();
        System.out.println("Total words: " + wordCount);
        
        System.out.println("Words in order:");
        while (st.hasMoreTokens()) {
            System.out.println(st.nextToken());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Total words: 8
Words in order:
Java
is
a
powerful
object
oriented
programming
language
```

**Explanation:**

- `new StringTokenizer(sentence)` uses default delimiters: space (`" "`), tab (`"\t"`), newline (`"\n"`), carriage return (`"\r"`), and form feed (`"\f"`).
- `countTokens()` returns the total number of tokens by scanning the remaining string.
- The `while` loop iterates through each token using `hasMoreTokens()` and `nextToken()`.

**Custom delimiter example:**

```java
String csv = "100,200,300,400";
StringTokenizer st = new StringTokenizer(csv, ",");
int sum = 0;
while (st.hasMoreTokens()) {
    sum += Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());
}
System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);  // Sum = 1000
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()`** — defeats the entire purpose; the code runs in the main thread. (-2 marks minimum)
> 2. **Forgetting `synchronized` on `wait()`/`notify()`** — throws `IllegalMonitorStateException` at runtime. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Confusing `sleep()` with `wait()`** — `sleep()` does NOT release the lock; `wait()` does. This is a high-frequency 3-mark question.
> 4. **Using `String` instead of `StringBuilder` in loops** — performance issue, often appears in Part B (a).
> 5. **Forgetting to handle `InterruptedException`** for `sleep()` — compilation error. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Mixing up `notify()` and `notifyAll()`** — `notify()` wakes one, `notifyAll()` wakes all. Examiners specifically test this.
> 7. **Using `==` to compare `String` content** — should be `.equals()`. Frequent 3-mark trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Special String Operations
- ☐ `String` is **immutable**; `StringBuffer` and `StringBuilder` are **mutable**.
- ☐ `StringBuffer` is **synchronized** (thread-safe); `StringBuilder` is **not** (faster, single-thread only).
- ☐ Default capacity = **16**; growth formula = `(oldCap × 2) + 2`.
- ☐ Key methods: `append()`, `insert()`, `delete()`, `reverse()`, `replace()`, `capacity()`, `length()`, `setLength()`, `ensureCapacity()`.
- ☐ `StringTokenizer` splits strings by delimiter set; default delimiters = whitespace.
- ☐ `StringTokenizer` is **legacy** (use `String.split()` or `Pattern` for modern code, but it still appears in KTU syllabus).
- ☐ `main(String args[])` receives command-line arguments; `args[0]` is the first user argument (no program name in Java).

### Java Thread Model
- ☐ A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution; processes contain threads.
- ☐ All Java programs start with the **main thread** (priority 5, name "main").
- ☐ Two creation methods: **extend `Thread`** (override `run()`) or **implement `Runnable`** (preferred).
- ☐ **`start()`** creates a new call stack and invokes `run()`; calling `run()` directly is NOT multithreading.
- ☐ **5 Life Cycle States**: New → Runnable → Running → Blocked/Waiting → Terminated (Dead).
- ☐ Priorities: `MIN=1`, `NORM=5`, `MAX=10`. Priorities are **hints**, not guarantees.
- ☐ `sleep(ms)` — pauses current thread, **does NOT release lock**, throws `InterruptedException`.
- ☐ `wait()` — releases lock, must be in `synchronized` block, throws `InterruptedException`.
- ☐ `notify()` wakes one waiter; `notifyAll()` wakes all waiters. Both must be in `synchronized` block.
- ☐ `yield()` — hint to scheduler, no exception thrown.
- ☐ `join()` — current thread waits for target thread to die.
- ☐ `synchronized` method locks `this`; `synchronized(obj)` locks the specified object.
- ☐ **Always use `while` (not `if`) around `wait()`** to guard against spurious wakeups.
- ☐ `wait()`/`notify()`/`notifyAll()` are defined in **`Object`**, not `Thread` — because they operate on monitors.

### Engineering Real-World Applications
- ☐ **StringBuffer/StringBuilder**: HTTP response building, SQL query construction, JSON serialization.
- ☐ **Multithreading**: Web servers (Tomcat, Netty), real-time trading, GUI event handling, parallel data processing (Hadoop, Spark), Android UI thread separation.

### Quick Formula Recall
- ☐ Capacity growth: $C_n = 18 \cdot 2^n - 2$
- ☐ Default capacity: $C_0 = 16$
- ☐ Priority range: $1 \le p \le 10$; main thread default $= 5$
- ☐ `String + String` loop: $O(n^2)$ time, $O(n)$ garbage
- ☐ `StringBuilder.append()`: $O(1)$ amortized
- ☐ Monitor per object: exactly **1** lock per Java object

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Main Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Main Thread # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) execution model, when a standalone Java application is launched, the JVM automatically instantiates **exactly one** user-level thread of execution to begin the program. This initial, primordial thread is formally designated as the **Main Thread** (often referenced as the "main" thread or `main` thread object). The Main Thread is the entry point of any Java application and is responsible for executing the statements contained within the `public static void main(String[] args)` method. It is conceptually a regular `java.lang.Thread` object — it inherits all properties and methods of the `Thread` superclass — but it is *implicitly* created by the JVM rather than by the programmer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT415 - Module 3):** > The Main Thread is the foundational thread from which all secondary (child) threads are typically spawned. Understanding its lifecycle, default properties, and how to manipulate it is a **CO3 (Apply multithreading concepts)** mapped outcome and is a recurrent 3-mark short-answer topic. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box:** > *The Main Thread is the first non-daemon thread created by the JVM when a Java application starts, which invokes and executes the `main()` method, and from which any additional user-defined threads may be created.* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the **Main Thread** as the **"store manager"** of a newly opened supermarket: | Real-World Analogy | Java Main Thread Equivalent | |---|---| | The **store manager** who unlocks the door at 8:00 AM | The **Main Thread** that JVM starts automatically | | The **manager's to-do list** (open shop, greet customers, hire staff) | The statements inside `public static void main(String[] args)` | | The **manager hires cashiers** to handle billing | The Main Thread **spawns child threads** using `new Thread(...)` | | The **manager stays till closing** to oversee everything | The Main Thread usually lives until all child threads finish | | The **manager's ID badge** showing name & rank | The Main Thread's **name** ("main") and **priority** (5) | **Key Intuitive Takeaway:** The Main Thread is *not special* in terms of capability — it is a regular `Thread` instance. What makes it unique is *when* and *how* it is born (by the JVM, not by you) and *what* it executes (your `main()` method). --- ## 1.3 Default Properties of the Main Thread (as per JVM Spec) | Property | Default Value | Description | |---|---|---| | **Name** | `"main"` | Identifier string used in stack traces & debugging | | **Priority** | `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` (= **5**) | Numerical priority on a scale of 1 to 10 | | **Daemon Status** | `false` (Non-Daemon) | JVM waits for it to die before shutting down | | **Thread Group** | `"main"` group | Belongs to the main ThreadGroup by default | | **Stack Size** | Platform-dependent (typically **512 KB – 1 MB**) | OS/JVM dependent memory allocation | | **ID** | Auto-assigned unique long | A unique identifier assigned by the JVM | > [!NOTE] > **Why these defaults matter for KTU:** > - Knowing `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY = 5` is a frequently asked 1-mark supporting fact. > - The fact that the **Main Thread is non-daemon** explains the JVM's shutdown behavior in Part B questions. > - The Main Thread's name is the literal string `"main"` — used in `currentThread().getName()` outputs. --- ## 1.4 Relationship Between Main Thread and `main()` Method A subtle but **exam-critical** distinction must be highlighted: - The **`main()` method** is *just a method* — a static function belonging to the class you specified on the command line. - The **Main Thread** is *an object* — an instance of `java.lang.Thread` that invokes this method. - The thread is what *executes* the method; the method is what *describes* the work to be done. > [!IMPORTANT] > The JVM does **not** know your class name at compile time. It knows it only at runtime through the classpath/class name argument. Once loaded, the JVM: > 1. Allocates a new `Thread` object. > 2. Assigns it the name `"main"`. > 3. Sets its priority to `NORM_PRIORITY` (5). > 4. Marks it as a non-daemon thread. > 5. Invokes `main()` on this new thread. --- ## 1.5 Intuitive Lifecycle Overview The Main Thread's life can be visualized as four distinct phases: 1. **Born** — JVM creates the `Thread` object. 2. **Runnable** — Thread is ready and scheduled by the OS. 3. **Running** — CPU executes `main()` statements. 4. **Terminated (Dead)** — Reaches the closing brace `}` of `main()`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Operational Breakdown ### 2.1.1 How the JVM Creates the Main Thread (Step-by-Step Internals) When you type `java MyProgram` in a terminal, the following sequence of events occurs **inside the JVM** (this is a high-yield theory block for KTU Part B 7-mark questions): - **Step 1: Class Loading** The JVM's **Bootstrap ClassLoader** loads `java.lang.Thread`, `java.lang.ThreadGroup`, and your `MyProgram.class` into method area memory. - **Step 2: ThreadGroup Setup** A default ThreadGroup named `"main"` is created if it doesn't already exist. This group acts as a logical container for top-level application threads. - **Step 3: Thread Object Instantiation** The JVM (via native C++ code) calls a private constructor of `Thread` to create a new thread instance. This instance is associated with the `main` ThreadGroup. - **Step 4: Default Property Assignment** The new thread receives: - `name = "main"` - `priority = Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` → numerical value **5** - `daemon = false` - `contextClassLoader` set to the application classloader - **Step 5: Stack Allocation** A private JVM stack is allocated for this thread. Initial stack size is platform-dependent. - **Step 6: `start()` Invocation (Implicit)** The JVM internally invokes the thread's `start()` method, registering it with the OS thread scheduler. - **Step 7: `main()` Method Execution** The thread begins executing the bytecodes of `public static void main(String[] args)`. --- ### 2.1.2 Manipulating the Main Thread Programmatically Since the Main Thread is just a `Thread` object, you can obtain a reference to it from **inside `main()`** using: $$ \text{Thread } t = \text{Thread.currentThread()}; $$ Once you have this reference, you can: | Operation | Method Call | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---| | Get its name | `t.getName()` | Usually returns `"main"` | | Get its priority | `t.getPriority()` | Returns `5` by default | | Change its name | `t.setName("MyMain")` | Useful in multi-app debugging | | Change its priority | `t.setPriority(7)` | Range: 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX) | | Check daemon status | `t.isDaemon()` | Returns `false` for main | | Get its ID | `t.getId()` | Returns a long | | Check state | `t.getState()` | Returns `RUNNABLE` during execution | | Get its group | `t.getThreadGroup()` | Returns the `"main"` group | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The `static` Keyword Puzzle (Frequently Asked):** > Notice that `main()` is `static` — meaning it belongs to the class, not to any object. Yet it is called *by* a thread object. The reason: a static method can still be invoked through a thread because **the method itself doesn't need an object context**, but the thread provides the execution context (call stack, program counter, local variables). --- ### 2.1.3 Spawning Child Threads from the Main Thread The Main Thread becomes the *parent* of any thread it creates. This relationship is conceptual, not enforced by the JVM (Java threads are flat — there's no real parent-child memory hierarchy). **Canonical Pattern (KTU-standard):** ```java class MyProgram { public static void main(String[] args) { Thread mainRef = Thread.currentThread(); System.out.println("Main thread: " + mainRef.getName()); System.out.println("Priority: " + mainRef.getPriority()); // Spawn a child thread Thread child = new Thread(() -> { System.out.println("Child running on: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); }, "Worker-1"); child.start(); // Main thread initiates child execution System.out.println("Main thread ends."); } } ``` **Execution Flow:** 1. Main thread prints its name and priority. 2. Main thread creates a `Thread` object named `"Worker-1"` (still in NEW state). 3. `child.start()` is called — Worker-1 becomes RUNNABLE. 4. Main thread prints "Main thread ends." and exits `main()`. 5. **JVM does not terminate** — it waits for Worker-1 (non-daemon) to finish. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table > [!NOTE] > The "formulas" here are Java API contracts and JVM specifications — equivalent in importance to physics formulas in engineering exams. | Concept | Java Constant / Expression | Numerical / String Value | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Minimum Thread Priority | `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY` | **1** | Used for low-priority background work | | Normal (Default) Priority | `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` | **5** | Main thread's default | | Maximum Thread Priority | `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY` | **10** | Used for time-critical tasks | | Main Thread Name | `"main"` | String literal | Set by JVM | | Main Thread Daemon Status | `false` | Boolean | JVM blocks on shutdown | | Default Thread Group | `"main"` | String literal | Created by JVM | | Reference to Main Thread | `Thread.currentThread()` | `Thread` object | Only valid from within `main()` | | Min/Max valid priority range | $1 \leq \text{priority} \leq 10$ | Integer | Throws `IllegalArgumentException` if violated | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding the Main Thread is essential in: - **GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX):** Misplacing GUI updates off the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) is a common bug. - **Server-Side Java (Spring, Jakarta EE):** Main thread bootstraps the embedded server (Tomcat, Jetty). - **Android Development:** The "main looper" governs UI updates. - **Concurrent Programming:** All `ExecutorService` submissions are processed via worker threads spawned by the main bootstrap. - **Performance Profiling:** Stack traces are categorized by "main" thread — bottlenecks in the main thread freeze the application. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Heuristic:** *Any blocking operation in the main thread (e.g., `Thread.sleep`, network I/O, database query) on a server halts the application's ability to accept new requests until the operation completes. This is why production code offloads work to thread pools.* # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Programmatic Derivations ## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Tracing a Multi-Threaded Program ### Problem Statement (Model KTU Style) > *Consider the following Java program. Predict the output, explaining the role of the main thread in producing this output. Also state the priority and name of every thread involved.* > > ```java > class Helper extends Thread { > Helper(String name) { super(name); } > public void run() { > for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { > System.out.println(getName() + " : " + i); > try { Thread.sleep(200); } catch (Exception e) {} > } > System.out.println(getName() + " exiting."); > } > } > > public class MainDemo { > public static void main(String[] args) { > Thread t = Thread.currentThread(); > System.out.println("Current thread: " + t.getName()); > System.out.println("Priority: " + t.getPriority()); > > Helper h1 = new Helper("Worker-A"); > Helper h2 = new Helper("Worker-B"); > h1.start(); > h2.start(); > > System.out.println("Main thread done with setup."); > } > } > ``` --- ### Step-by-Step Trace (Exhaustive — No Steps Skipped) **Step 1 — JVM Bootstrapping** JVM loads `MainDemo.class` and `Helper.class` into the method area. **Step 2 — Main Thread Creation (Implicit)** A `Thread` object is created with the name `"main"`. Its initial state is **NEW**, then transitions to **RUNNABLE** when the JVM internally calls `start()` on it. **Step 3 — Entering `main()`** The main thread begins executing statements inside `main()`. Its state is now **RUNNING** (a sub-state of RUNNABLE when actively on CPU). **Step 4 — `Thread.currentThread()`** This static call returns a reference to the *currently executing* thread, which is the main thread. Stored in local variable `t`. **Step 5 — `t.getName()` and `t.getPriority()`** - `getName()` returns `"main"` (default name assigned by JVM). - `getPriority()` returns **5** (`Thread.NORM_PRIORITY`). The first two print statements execute in a **deterministic order**: ``` Current thread: main Priority: 5 ``` **Step 6 — Creating `h1` and `h2`** The main thread executes `new Helper("Worker-A")` and `new Helper("Worker-B")`. These calls run on the *main thread* (the constructor is just regular code). After construction, both `h1` and `h2` exist as `Thread` objects in **NEW** state. They have: - `h1.getName() = "Worker-A"`, `h1.getPriority() = 5` (inherited) - `h2.getName() = "Worker-B"`, `h2.getPriority() = 5` (inherited) **Step 7 — `h1.start()` and `h2.start()`** - `h1.start()` causes the JVM to register Worker-A with the OS thread scheduler. State: **RUNNABLE**. - `h2.start()` does the same for Worker-B. State: **RUNNABLE**. - Now **three threads** are alive: main, Worker-A, Worker-B. **Step 8 — `System.out.println("Main thread done with setup.")`** This executes on the main thread. As soon as this line finishes, the main thread reaches the closing brace `}` of `main()` and transitions to **TERMINATED** state. **Step 9 — Scheduler Interleaving** Worker-A and Worker-B are now competing for CPU time. With `Thread.sleep(200)`, each yields for 200ms after printing each number. Possible output (with interleave): ``` Worker-A : 1 Worker-B : 1 Worker-A : 2 Worker-B : 2 Worker-A : 3 Worker-B : 3 Worker-A exiting. Worker-B exiting. ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Concept:** The order of `Worker-A : i` and `Worker-B : i` lines is **non-deterministic**. The JVM/OS scheduler decides which thread runs at any given moment. However, the *first two* lines of output (printing main's name and priority) **are deterministic** because they execute before `start()` is called on any child thread. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Changing Main Thread's Properties **Task:** Modify the main thread's name to `"ApplicationBootstrap"` and raise its priority to 8. Print the changes. **Solution with Full Analytical Justification:** ```java public class ChangeMain { public static void main(String[] args) { // Step 1: Acquire reference to main thread Thread t = Thread.currentThread(); // Step 2: Display original properties System.out.println("Before change -> Name: " + t.getName() + ", Priority: " + t.getPriority()); // Output: Before change -> Name: main, Priority: 5 // Step 3: Change name and priority t.setName("ApplicationBootstrap"); t.setPriority(8); // Step 4: Verify changes System.out.println("After change -> Name: " + t.getName() + ", Priority: " + t.getPriority()); // Output: After change -> Name: ApplicationBootstrap, Priority: 8 } } ``` **Analytical Notes:** - `setName()` is a non-static, non-final method inherited from `Thread`. Returns `void`. - `setPriority(int)` validates: if `newPriority < Thread.MIN_PRIORITY` (1) or `newPriority > Thread.MAX_PRIORITY` (10), it throws `IllegalArgumentException`. - The priority change is a *hint* to the OS scheduler — most JVMs map Java priorities to OS native priorities in a non-linear way. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Exam Scenarios | Scenario | What Happens to Main Thread | Why | |---|---|---| | `main()` ends, no child threads started | JVM terminates | No non-daemon threads remain | | `main()` ends, child thread (non-daemon) running | JVM **waits** | Child thread keeps JVM alive | | `main()` ends, child thread (daemon) running | JVM **terminates**, killing daemon | Daemon threads don't block JVM exit | | `System.exit(0)` called in `main()` | All threads die immediately | Forceful shutdown | | Uncaught exception in `main()` | Main thread dies; JVM may exit | Default uncaught handler terminates thread | | Main thread calls `child.join()` | Main thread **blocks** until child finishes | Synchronization primitive | > [!NOTE] > **State Transition Diagram (Main Thread):** > > $$\text{NEW} \xrightarrow{\text{JVM internal start()}} \text{RUNNABLE} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{OS schedule}]{\text{OS schedule}} \text{RUNNING} \xrightarrow{\text{main() returns}} \text{TERMINATED}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Main Thread Lifecycle Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[JVM Startup: java MyProgram] --> B[Class Loading via Bootstrap ClassLoader] B --> C[Create main ThreadGroup] C --> D[Instantiate Thread object] D --> E[Assign name: main] E --> F[Assign priority: 5] F --> G[Set daemon: false] G --> H[Allocate JVM Stack] H --> I[Internal start invocation] I --> J[Thread State: RUNNABLE] J --> K[Execute main method body] K --> L{Any more statements?} L -- Yes --> K L -- No --> M[Main method returns] M --> N[Thread State: TERMINATED] N --> O{Any non-daemon threads alive?} O -- Yes --> P[JVM continues running] O -- No --> Q[JVM shuts down] P --> O Q --> R[Process exits] ``` --- ## 4.2 Main Thread's Relationship with Child Threads (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph JVM[JVM Process] subgraph TG[main ThreadGroup] MT["MAIN THREAD
Name: main
Priority: 5
Daemon: false"] W1["Worker Thread A
Name: Worker-A
Priority: 5
Daemon: false"] W2["Worker Thread B
Name: Worker-B
Priority: 5
Daemon: true"] end HEAP[(Shared Heap
Objects & Static Fields)] MT -.creates.-> W1 MT -.creates.-> W2 MT <--> HEAP W1 <--> HEAP W2 <--> HEAP end OS[OS Thread Scheduler] --- MT OS --- W1 OS --- W2 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - All three threads live inside the **main ThreadGroup**. - The main thread is the **logical creator** of the other two (dashed arrows). - All three threads share the **same heap** (can access same objects). - Each thread has its **own private stack** (not shown explicitly). - The OS scheduler arbitrates CPU time among all RUNNABLE threads. --- ## 4.3 Memory Architecture for the Main Thread ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph P[Process Memory] subgraph MT[Main Thread Private] S1["JVM Stack
- main method frame
- local variables
- operand stack"] PC1["Program Counter
Points to current bytecode"] R1["Registers
Native CPU state"] end subgraph CT1[Child Thread A Private] S2["JVM Stack"] PC2["Program Counter"] R2["Registers"] end subgraph SH[Shared Across All Threads] H["Heap
- all objects
- arrays"] MA["Method Area
- class metadata
- static variables
- constant pool"] end end MT <--> SH CT1 <--> SH ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters:** The Main Thread's stack holds the **call frames** for `main()` and any methods it calls. When `main()` returns, the frame is popped. If `main()` is recursive or deeply nested, you can get a `StackOverflowError` — a classic KTU viva question. --- ## 4.4 State Transition Diagram (Thread States Applied to Main Thread) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NEW : JVM creates Thread object NEW --> RUNNABLE : JVM internal start RUNNABLE --> RUNNING : OS scheduler dispatch RUNNING --> RUNNABLE : Time slice expired RUNNING --> BLOCKED : Waiting for monitor lock RUNNING --> WAITING : Object.wait or Thread.join RUNNING --> TIMED_WAITING : Thread.sleep or Object.wait with timeout RUNNING --> TERMINATED : main method returns BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : Lock acquired WAITING --> RUNNABLE : Notified TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : Timeout elapsed TERMINATED --> [*] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Q: What is the Main Thread in Java? State any two of its default properties.** **Model Answer (2 marks):** The **Main Thread** is the first non-daemon thread that the JVM automatically creates when a Java application begins execution. It invokes and runs the statements of the `public static void main(String[] args)` method. **Two default properties:** 1. **Name:** `"main"` (assigned by the JVM). 2. **Priority:** `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` (numerical value **5**). *(Acceptable alternative properties: non-daemon status, default ThreadGroup = "main".)* --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Q: How is a reference to the currently executing thread obtained in Java? Give the syntax.** **Model Answer (2 marks):** A reference to the currently executing thread is obtained using the **static** method `currentThread()` of the `Thread` class. **Syntax:** $$ \text{Thread } t = \text{Thread.currentThread()}; $$ Where `t` now holds a reference to the active thread (e.g., the Main Thread when called from `main()`). This reference can be used to invoke methods like `getName()`, `getPriority()`, `setName()`, etc. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B (14 marks) typically contains a sub-question split of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both must be answered for the chosen alternative. --- ### **Part B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Question A (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Explain the role of the Main Thread in a Java program. List any **four** default properties of the Main Thread. (7 marks) > > **(b)** Write a Java program that: > - Obtains a reference to the main thread. > - Prints its name and priority. > - Renames it to `"BootstrapThread"`. > - Creates a child thread named `"Worker"` that prints numbers 1 to 5 with a 100ms delay. > - The main thread should print `"Setup complete"` after starting the child and then exit. > - State the expected output ordering. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **Role of the Main Thread:** The Main Thread serves as the **execution entry point** of every standalone Java application.
