Its roles include: 1. **Bootstrap Execution:** The JVM automatically creates it to invoke `public static void main(String[] args)`, which is the first user code executed. 2. **Thread Factory:** The Main Thread typically acts as the *creator* of additional user-defined threads by calling `new Thread(...)` and invoking their `start()` methods. 3. **Lifecycle Anchor:** Since the Main Thread is **non-daemon**, its presence keeps the JVM alive. The JVM exits only when all non-daemon threads (including the main one) have terminated. 4. **Default Execution Context:** All initial class loading, static variable initialization, and `main()`-level logic occur on this thread. **Four Default Properties:** | Property | Value | |---|---| | Name | `"main"` | | Priority | `5` (`Thread.NORM_PRIORITY`) | | Daemon status | `false` (non-daemon) | | Thread Group | `"main"` (default) | *(Optionally: ID — auto-assigned long; Stack size — platform-dependent.)* **Mark Allocation:** Definition (2m) + Roles (3m) + 4 properties (2m). --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Java Program:** ```java class Worker extends Thread { Worker(String name) { super(name); } public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(getName() + " : " + i); try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (Exception e) {} } } } public class MainThreadDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // (i) Reference to main thread Thread t = Thread.currentThread(); // (ii) Print name and priority System.out.println("Original name: " + t.getName()); System.out.println("Priority: " + t.getPriority()); // (iii) Rename the main thread t.setName("BootstrapThread"); System.out.println("New name: " + t.getName()); // (iv) Create and start child thread Thread w = new Worker("Worker"); w.start(); // (v) Main thread's final action System.out.println("Setup complete"); } } ``` **Expected Output (Deterministic Portion):** The first **four** lines are guaranteed in order: ``` Original name: main Priority: 5 New name: BootstrapThread Setup complete ``` After this, the Worker thread's output (`Worker : 1` to `Worker : 5`) is interleaved with the OS scheduler's decisions. The Main Thread exits `main()` after printing `"Setup complete"`, but the JVM stays alive because Worker is a non-daemon thread. **Mark Allocation:** Code structure (3m) + Correct API usage (2m) + Output explanation (2m). --- ### **Part B — Option B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Question B (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Differentiate between the **Main Thread** and a **Child Thread** in Java. Discuss how the Main Thread's daemon status affects JVM termination. (7 marks) > > **(b)** Consider the following code. Identify and correct the errors. Predict the output. (7 marks) > > ```java > public class Test { > public static void main(String[] args) { > Thread t = currentThread(); > t.setPriority(15); > System.out.println(t.getName() + " " + t.getPriority()); > } > } > ``` --- #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** | Aspect | Main Thread | Child Thread | |---|---|---| | **Creation** | Implicitly by the JVM | Explicitly by programmer using `new Thread()` | | **Name** | Default `"main"` | Programmer-defined or auto (`Thread-0`, `Thread-1`, ...) | | **Starting** | JVM calls `start()` internally | Programmer calls `start()` explicitly | | **Priority** | Default 5 | Can be set before `start()` | | **Daemon** | Always non-daemon | Can be set to daemon or non-daemon | | **Count per app** | Exactly one | Zero or more | **JVM Termination & Daemon Status:** - The JVM continues running as long as **at least one non-daemon thread is alive**. - The Main Thread is **non-daemon**, so the JVM will not terminate until it finishes `main()` AND all other non-daemon threads finish. - If a programmer creates a child thread and sets `t.setDaemon(true)` *before* calling `start()`, then that thread becomes a **daemon thread**. - Daemon threads are abruptly terminated when the JVM decides to exit (when only daemons remain). - The Main Thread **cannot** be converted to a daemon — calling `setDaemon(true)` on it throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. **Mark Allocation:** Comparison table (4m) + Daemon impact explanation (3m). --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Error Identification:** | # | Line | Error | Correction | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `Thread t = currentThread();` | `currentThread()` is a static method; must be qualified. | `Thread t = Thread.currentThread();` | | 2 | `t.setPriority(15);` | Priority 15 is out of valid range [1, 10]. Throws `IllegalArgumentException`. | `t.setPriority(8);` *(or any value 1-10)* | **Corrected Code:** ```java public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Thread t = Thread.currentThread(); // Fixed t.setPriority(8); // Fixed: valid range System.out.println(t.getName() + " " + t.getPriority()); } } ``` **Predicted Output:** ``` main 8 ``` **Explanation:** After correction, `getName()` returns `"main"` (default JVM-assigned name), and `getPriority()` returns `8` (the new value just set). **Mark Allocation:** Identifying 2 errors (4m) + Corrections (1m) + Output prediction (2m). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting `Thread.` prefix on `currentThread()`:** This is the most common error in KTU scripts — losing 2 marks. Always remember it's a **static** method. > > 2. **Confusing `setDaemon()` with `setPriority()`:** Daemon status must be set **before** `start()`. Setting it after throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. > > 3. **Output Order Misconception:** Students often write deterministic output for child threads. **Mark deduction:** -2 marks. Always clarify which lines are deterministic and which are non-deterministic. > > 4. **Priority Range Forgetting:** Setting priority to 15 or 0 throws `IllegalArgumentException`. Many students miss this — losing 1-2 marks. > > 5. **Not stating that main thread is non-daemon:** A 1-mark detail that examiners specifically look for. Always mention `daemon = false`. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — The Main Thread** ### **Core Concepts (Must Memorize)** - [ ] Main Thread is the **first non-daemon thread** created by the **JVM** (not by the programmer). - [ ] It executes the `public static void main(String[] args)` method. - [ ] Default name = **"main"**; default priority = **5** (`NORM_PRIORITY`); daemon = **false**. - [ ] It belongs to the default ThreadGroup named **"main"**. - [ ] A reference is obtained via `Thread.currentThread()` from inside `main()`. - [ ] The Main Thread **cannot be made a daemon** — `setDaemon(true)` throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. ### **API Methods (Frequently Tested)** - [ ] `Thread.currentThread()` — static, returns currently running Thread reference. - [ ] `getName()` / `setName(String)` — read/write the thread's name. - [ ] `getPriority()` / `setPriority(int)` — read/write priority (valid range: **1 to 10**). - [ ] `isDaemon()` / `setDaemon(boolean)` — must be called **before** `start()`. - [ ] `getId()` — returns unique long identifier. - [ ] `getThreadGroup()` — returns the parent ThreadGroup. - [ ] `getState()` — returns one of `NEW`, `RUNNABLE`, `BLOCKED`, `WAITING`, `TIMED_WAITING`, `TERMINATED`. ### **Priority Constants (Magic Numbers to Remember)** - [ ] `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY = 1` - [ ] `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY = 5` *(Main Thread's default)* - [ ] `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY = 10` ### **Lifecycle States (Mandatory for Diagrams)** - [ ] NEW → RUNNABLE → RUNNING → TERMINATED (basic path) - [ ] RUNNING ⇄ BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING (waiting states) ### **Common Exam Traps** - [ ] Output of child threads is **non-deterministic** unless explicitly synchronized. - [ ] JVM **waits** for non-daemon child threads to finish before exiting. - [ ] Daemon threads are killed abruptly when JVM exits. - [ ] Setting priority outside [1, 10] throws `IllegalArgumentException`. - [ ] The `static` keyword in `main()` doesn't mean it runs without a thread — a thread *calls* it. ### **One-Line Definition (For 1-mark questions)** > *"The Main Thread is the JVM-created, non-daemon thread of execution that invokes the `main()` method and serves as the default parent for all programmatically created threads."*

topics.title: Creating Thread
Creating Multiple Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Multiple Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In the Java programming model, a **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process. **Creating Multiple Threads** refers to the programmatic mechanism by which a single Java application spawns two or more concurrent execution paths, each running independently within the same process space but sharing the same heap memory. According to the KTU 2024 OOP syllabus, thread creation is achieved primarily through two standard approaches defined in the `java.lang` package:

1. **Extending the `java.lang.Thread` class** (Inheritance-based approach).
2. **Implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` interface** (Interface-based approach).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The ability to create, start, prioritize, and synchronize multiple threads is a **Core Course Outcome (CO3)** mapped competency. Students are expected to write, trace, and debug multi-threaded Java programs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a restaurant kitchen:

- **Single-Threaded Program** = One chef working alone, taking one order, cooking it, serving it, then taking the next. Customers wait in a long queue.
- **Multi-Threaded Program** = Multiple chefs working in parallel in the same kitchen. One chef takes orders, another grills, another plates — all sharing the same kitchen (heap memory), the same refrigerator (shared resources), but each handling their own task independently.

Each "chef" is a **thread**. The kitchen itself is the **process**. The chefs share ingredients (shared objects), but each chef has their own cutting board and knife (thread-local stack).

### 1.3 Why Multiple Threads? — The Engineering Justification

| Use Case | Real-World Benefit |
|----------|-------------------|
| Web Servers | Handle **1000+ simultaneous client requests** without blocking |
| GUI Applications | Keep the UI responsive while a background task runs |
| Scientific Computing | Parallelize matrix multiplication across CPU cores |
| Real-Time Systems | Sensor reading + display update + alarm monitoring concurrently |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Concurrency vs Parallelism:**
> - **Concurrency** = Multiple threads making progress in overlapping time periods (single-core illusion).
> - **Parallelism** = Multiple threads executing *literally at the same instant* on multiple CPU cores.
> Java threads support both, depending on the underlying OS and hardware.

### 1.4 The Java Memory Model for Threads (Foundational)

When multiple threads are created inside one JVM process:

- **Shared Memory Region (Heap):** All threads access the same objects, static variables, and arrays. ⚠️ This is the root cause of **race conditions**.
- **Private Memory Region (Stack):** Each thread has its own **call stack**, **local variables**, and **program counter (PC)**.

$$\text{Memory}_{\text{process}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Stack}_i \;\; \cup \;\; \text{Heap}_{\text{shared}}$$

where $n$ is the number of active threads.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Standard Approaches — Decomposed

#### Approach A: Extending the `Thread` Class

The class `java.lang.Thread` itself implements `Runnable`. By subclassing it, we override the `run()` method, which acts as the **entry point** for the new thread.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Declare a class extending `Thread`.
2. Override the public method `public void run()` — write the task logic here.
3. In the main method, instantiate the subclass object.
4. Call `start()` on the object — **never call `run()` directly** for true multithreading.
5. The JVM invokes a new OS thread, which executes `run()` concurrently.

#### Approach B: Implementing the `Runnable` Interface

This is the **preferred** approach in industry because:
- Java does not support multiple class inheritance, so extending `Thread` consumes the only inheritance slot.
- Implementing `Runnable` leaves the class free to extend another class.
- It supports the **"separation of concerns"** principle — the *task* (Runnable) is decoupled from the *worker* (Thread).

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Declare a class implementing `Runnable`.
2. Implement `public void run()`.
3. Create a `Thread` object, passing the `Runnable` instance to its constructor.
4. Call `start()` on the `Thread` object.

### 2.2 Critical Methods of the `Thread` Class (Cheat Sheet)

| Method | Signature | Purpose | Static? |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------|
| `start()` | `void start()` | Spawns a new OS thread; calls `run()` internally | No |
| `run()` | `void run()` | Entry point of the new thread's task | No |
| `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | Returns reference to the executing thread | **Yes** |
| `getName()` | `String getName()` | Returns the thread's name (e.g., `Thread-0`) | No |
| `setName(String)` | `void setName(String name)` | Sets a custom name | No |
| `getId()` | `long getId()` | Returns the unique thread ID | No |
| `getPriority()` | `int getPriority()` | Returns priority (1 to 10) | No |
| `setPriority(int)` | `void setPriority(int p)` | Sets priority (1=MIN, 5=NORM, 10=MAX) | No |
| `sleep(long ms)` | `static void sleep(long)` | Pauses current thread for `ms` milliseconds | **Yes** |
| `join()` | `void join()` | Caller waits for this thread to finish | No |
| `join(long ms)` | `void join(long ms)` | Caller waits at most `ms` ms | No |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints scheduler to give other threads a chance | **Yes** |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean isAlive()` | True if thread has started and not yet died | No |
| `interrupt()` | `void interrupt()` | Sets the interrupt flag on the thread | No |
| `isDaemon()` | `boolean isDaemon()` | True if thread is a daemon (background) | No |
| `setDaemon(boolean)` | `void setDaemon(boolean on)` | Marks thread as daemon | No |

### 2.3 The `start()` vs `run()` Distinction — The Most-Tested Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Calling `run()` directly does **NOT** create a new thread. It executes the `run()` method in the *current* (calling) thread, just like any ordinary method call. Only `start()` triggers the JVM to allocate a fresh stack and invoke `run()` on a brand-new OS thread.

$$\text{call } t.start() \;\Rightarrow\; \text{New OS Thread spawned, run() invoked concurrently}$$
$$\text{call } t.run() \;\Rightarrow\; \text{No new thread, executes in caller's stack as normal method}$$

### 2.4 Thread Constructors

| Constructor | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `Thread()` | Default thread, auto-named `Thread-N` |
| `Thread(String name)` | Creates a thread with the given name |
| `Thread(Runnable target)` | Wraps a `Runnable` task |
| `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` | Wraps a task with a name |
| `Thread(ThreadGroup group, Runnable target, String name)` | For thread grouping (legacy) |

### 2.5 Thread Priority Constants

| Constant | Value | Meaning |
|----------|-------|---------|
| `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY` | **1** | Lowest |
| `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` | **5** | Default |
| `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY` | **10** | Highest |

$$\text{Priority range: } \text{MIN\_PRIORITY} \leq p \leq \text{MAX\_PRIORITY}, \quad p \in \mathbb{Z}^+$$

> [!NOTE]
> Priority is a **hint** to the OS thread scheduler. It does **not guarantee** execution order. On some platforms (e.g., older Windows), priority is even ignored. KTU questions frequently test this misconception.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Distributed Systems:** Apache Kafka, Hadoop use thread pools to process millions of messages.
- **Mobile Development:** Android's main thread vs worker threads for UI vs network.
- **Embedded Systems (IoT):** Sensor polling thread + communication thread running concurrently.
- **Financial Systems:** Trade execution threads with strict priority management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Execution Flow

### 3.1 Approach A — Extending `Thread` Class (Complete Traceable Program)

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    private String threadLabel;

    public MyThread(String label) {
        this.threadLabel = label;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(threadLabel + " - Count: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(500);   // Pause 500 ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(threadLabel + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(threadLabel + " finished execution.");
    }
}

public class ThreadDemo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Thread-A");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Thread-B");
        MyThread t3 = new MyThread("Thread-C");

        t1.start();   // Spawns new OS thread #1
        t2.start();   // Spawns new OS thread #2
        t3.start();   // Spawns new OS thread #3
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Step | Action | State of `t1` | State of `t2` | State of `t3` |
|------|--------|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Objects constructed in main | NEW | NEW | NEW |
| 2 | `t1.start()` invoked | RUNNABLE | NEW | NEW |
| 3 | `t2.start()` invoked | RUNNABLE | RUNNABLE | NEW |
| 4 | `t3.start()` invoked | RUNNABLE | RUNNABLE | RUNNABLE |
| 5 | Each thread loops; `sleep(500)` causes TIMED_WAITING | TIMED_WAITING | TIMED_WAITING | TIMED_WAITING |
| 6 | After 5 iterations each | TERMINATED | TERMINATED | TERMINATED |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output **interleaving order is non-deterministic**. The OS scheduler decides which thread runs at each instant. A KTU exam may ask: *"What is the possible output?"* — students must list an *interleaving*, not a fixed sequence.

### 3.3 Approach B — Implementing `Runnable` (Industry-Preferred Pattern)

```java
class PrintTask implements Runnable {
    private char targetChar;

    public PrintTask(char c) {
        this.targetChar = c;
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
            System.out.print(targetChar);
            if (i % 50 == 0) System.out.println();
        }
    }
}

public class RunnableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Runnable a = new PrintTask('A');
        Runnable b = new PrintTask('B');

        Thread t1 = new Thread(a, "Printer-A");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(b, "Printer-B");

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Execution Logic — Row by Row:**

1. `new PrintTask('A')` → Creates a task object (not a thread yet).
2. `new Thread(a, "Printer-A")` → Wraps the task into a Thread instance named `"Printer-A"`.
3. `t1.start()` → JVM requests the OS to spawn a new thread; the OS scheduler will call `a.run()` on the new thread.
4. Similarly for `t2`.
5. Output shows interleaved `A`s and `B`s — true concurrent execution.

### 3.4 Lambda-Based Thread Creation (Java 8+ — Modern KTU Trend)

```java
public class LambdaThread {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Anonymous Runnable via lambda
        Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
            for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
                System.out.println("Lambda Thread: " + i);
            }
        }, "LambdaWorker");

        t.start();
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The lambda `() -> { ... }` is implicitly compiled to a `Runnable` instance by the JVM. This is the **most concise** syntax for thread creation in modern Java and may appear in KTU 2024 questions.

### 3.5 The `join()` Method — Coordinating Threads

```java
public class JoinDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Thread worker = new Thread(() -> {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
                System.out.println("Worker: step " + i);
                try { Thread.sleep(400); } catch (Exception e) {}
            }
        });

        worker.start();
        worker.join();   // main BLOCKS until worker terminates

        System.out.println("Main resumes after worker finished.");
    }
}
```

**Logic Flow:**

$$\text{main thread} \xrightarrow{\text{calls } worker.join()} \text{WAITING state} \xrightarrow{\text{worker terminates}} \text{main resumes}$$

**State Transition Equation:**

$$S_{\text{main}}: \text{RUNNABLE} \;\xrightarrow{\;worker.join()\;}\; \text{WAITING} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{worker dies}\;}\; \text{RUNNABLE}$$

### 3.6 Daemon vs User Threads

A **daemon thread** is a background service thread (e.g., garbage collector) that does not prevent the JVM from exiting. A **user thread** keeps the JVM alive.

```java
Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    while (true) {
        System.out.println("Daemon running...");
        try { Thread.sleep(1000); } catch (Exception e) {}
    }
});
t.setDaemon(true);   // Must be called BEFORE start()
t.start();
System.out.println("Main ends.");
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Calling `setDaemon(true)` *after* `start()` throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. This is a guaranteed mark-loser if missed in an exam.

### 3.7 Multiple Threads Sharing a Common Object (Race Condition Setup)

```java
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    public void increment() { count++; }
}

public class SharedDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Counter c = new Counter();

        Runnable task = () -> {
            for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++) c.increment();
        };

        Thread t1 = new Thread(task);
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task);
        t1.start(); t2.start();
        t1.join();  t2.join();

        System.out.println("Final count = " + c.count);  // Expected: 2000, but often < 2000
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The final value is **often less than 2000** because `count++` is *not atomic* — it involves **read-modify-write**. Two threads may read the same value, increment locally, and write back the same result. This is the classic setup for the `synchronized` keyword, covered in the next topic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread()
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : lock unavailable
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() / join() no timeout
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms)
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / join target dies
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() returns
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

### 4.2 Two Approaches to Thread Creation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Approach A Inheritance]
        A1[User Class] -->|extends| A2[java.lang.Thread]
        A2 -->|override| A3[public void run]
    end
    subgraph B[Approach B Composition Preferred]
        B1[User Class] -->|implements| B2[java.lang.Runnable]
        B2 -->|implements| B3[public void run]
        B3 -->|passed to| B4[new Thread target]
    end
    A3 -->|calls start| S1[OS Thread spawned]
    B4 -->|calls start| S1
    S1 --> C[JVM invokes run concurrently]
    C --> D[Concurrent Execution]
```

### 4.3 Memory Architecture of Multiple Threads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P[JVM Process]
        H[Shared Heap]
        subgraph T1[Thread 1 Stack]
            T1PC[PC]
            T1LV[Local Variables]
        end
        subgraph T2[Thread 2 Stack]
            T2PC[PC]
            T2LV[Local Variables]
        end
        subgraph T3[Thread 3 Stack]
            T3PC[PC]
            T3LV[Local Variables]
        end
    end
    H -.shared.- T1
    H -.shared.- T2
    H -.shared.- T3
```

### 4.4 Method Call Sequence for `t.start()`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Thread
    participant JVM
    participant OS as OS Scheduler
    participant NewT as New Thread
    Main->>JVM: t1 = new MyThread()
    JVM-->>Main: Thread object in NEW state
    Main->>JVM: t1.start()
    JVM->>OS: request_new_native_thread
    OS-->>JVM: thread_handle
    JVM->>NewT: allocate fresh stack
    NewT->>NewT: run() begins execution
    NewT-->>Main: concurrent progress
    NewT->>JVM: run() returns
    JVM->>JVM: state = TERMINATED
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Explain two different ways of creating a thread in Java. Which one is preferred and why?*

**Model Answer:**

Java provides two standard mechanisms to create a thread:

**Method 1: Extending the `Thread` class.**
The user class inherits from `java.lang.Thread` and overrides the `run()` method. The thread is started by invoking `start()` on an object of the subclass.

**Method 2: Implementing the `Runnable` interface.**
The user class implements `java.lang.Runnable` and provides the `run()` method. A `Thread` object is created by passing the `Runnable` instance to the `Thread` constructor, and then `start()` is called on the `Thread` object.

**Preferred Approach:** Implementing the `Runnable` interface is preferred because:
- Java does not support multiple inheritance. Extending `Thread` blocks the class from extending any other class.
- It separates the **task** (what to do) from the **worker** (who does it), adhering to cleaner OO design.
- It is suitable for use with thread pools and executors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the difference between calling `start()` and `run()` on a Thread object?*

**Model Answer:**

Calling `start()` on a `Thread` object instructs the JVM to create a **new OS-level thread** with its own stack and call the `run()` method on that new thread. This results in **true concurrent execution**.

Calling `run()` directly does **not** create a new thread. It is treated as a normal method invocation, executed on the **caller's thread**, with no concurrency.

$$\boxed{\;start() \;\to\; \text{New thread spawned, run() invoked concurrently}\;}$$
$$\boxed{\;run() \;\to\; \text{No new thread, ordinary method call in caller's stack}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently write "start() calls run()". While technically true, the **vital part** is that start() does so on a *new* thread. Marks are awarded specifically for mentioning the **new thread creation**. Simply stating "start() calls run()" gets only partial credit.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the thread lifecycle in Java with a neat state diagram. Discuss the role of the `sleep()`, `join()`, and `yield()` methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that creates three threads. Each thread should print numbers from 1 to 5 along with its name. Demonstrate the use of the `sleep()` method to make the output interleaved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

A thread in Java moves through the following states during its lifetime:

1. **New (Born):** The thread object is created using `new Thread()`, but `start()` has not been called yet. The thread is not considered alive.
2. **Runnable:** After `start()` is invoked, the thread becomes eligible for execution. The JVM makes it available to the thread scheduler, which may run it immediately or defer it.
3. **Blocked / Waiting / Timed-Waiting:** A running thread may enter a non-runnable state due to:
   - `sleep(long ms)` → moves to **TIMED_WAITING** for the specified duration.
   - `join()` → caller moves to **WAITING** until the target thread finishes.
   - `wait()` / `notify()` → inter-thread communication.
   - I/O blocking or lock unavailability.
4. **Terminated (Dead):** The `run()` method returns normally or throws an uncaught exception. The thread cannot be restarted.

**Role of Key Methods:**
- `sleep(ms)`: Pauses the **current** thread for `ms` milliseconds; releases no locks.
- `join()`: Caller thread waits for the target thread to die.
- `yield()`: Hints the scheduler to give other threads of equal priority a chance; the thread remains in RUNNABLE state.

(Include the state diagram from Section 4.1 here.)

**Model Solution for (b):**

```java
class NumberThread extends Thread {
    public NumberThread(String name) { super(name); }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " prints: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(300);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " finished.");
    }
}

public class ThreeThreads {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        NumberThread t1 = new NumberThread("Thread-1");
        NumberThread t2 = new NumberThread("Thread-2");
        NumberThread t3 = new NumberThread("Thread-3");

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t3.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Code:**
- `getName()` is inherited from `Thread` and returns the name passed to the constructor.
- `Thread.sleep(300)` forces the thread into TIMED_WAITING for 300 ms, allowing the other threads to print.
- The exact output **interleaving** is OS-dependent; sample output:

```
Thread-1 prints: 1
Thread-3 prints: 1
Thread-2 prints: 1
Thread-1 prints: 2
...
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare extending the `Thread` class and implementing the `Runnable` interface. Write a Java program using the `Runnable` interface to print "Hello" from two threads concurrently. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are daemon threads? Explain with a Java program how daemon threads differ from user threads. What happens if a daemon thread is still running when all user threads finish? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Aspect | Extends `Thread` | Implements `Runnable` |
|--------|------------------|------------------------|
| Inheritance | Consumes the single inheritance slot | Leaves inheritance open |
| Flexibility | Less flexible | More flexible, supports multiple interfaces |
| Reusability | Task is bound to the thread class | Task object can be passed to multiple threads/thread pools |
| OO Design | Violates "favor composition over inheritance" | Clean separation of task and worker |
| Used In | Simple academic examples | Industry frameworks (Executors, ForkJoinPool) |

**Code:**

```java
class HelloTask implements Runnable {
    private int id;
    public HelloTask(int id) { this.id = id; }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println("Hello from Thread " + id + " - message " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class HelloRunnable {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Runnable r1 = new HelloTask(1);
        Runnable r2 = new HelloTask(2);

        Thread t1 = new Thread(r1, "Worker-1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(r2, "Worker-2");

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Model Solution for (b):**

A **daemon thread** is a low-priority background thread that provides services to user threads. The JVM does not wait for daemon threads to finish before shutting down — once all user threads terminate, the JVM kills all remaining daemon threads and exits.

**Code:**

```java
public class DaemonDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Thread daemon = new Thread(() -> {
            while (true) {
                System.out.println("Daemon: running background task...");
                try { Thread.sleep(500); }
                catch (InterruptedException e) { break; }
            }
        });

        daemon.setDaemon(true);   // MUST be before start()
        daemon.start();

        Thread userThread = new Thread(() -> {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
                System.out.println("User Thread: step " + i);
                try { Thread.sleep(700); }
                catch (InterruptedException e) {}
            }
            System.out.println("User Thread finished.");
        });

        userThread.start();
        userThread.join();
        System.out.println("Main exits. Daemon will be killed automatically by JVM.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The daemon thread prints indefinitely but is **killed by the JVM** as soon as `main` and `userThread` terminate.
- Examples of daemon threads: **Garbage Collector**, Finalizer, Signal Dispatcher.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Daemon Thread:**
> 1. Forgetting `setDaemon(true)` *before* `start()` → throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. Examiners specifically check for the order.
> 2. Writing "daemon thread runs forever" — **WRONG**. Daemon threads are killed when all user threads exit. This misconception costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. Calling `setDaemon(true)` on a thread that is *already running* → unchecked exception, no recompilation rescue.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Creating Multiple Threads**

- A **thread** = independent path of execution within a process, sharing heap memory but having its own stack.
- **Two creation methods:** (i) `extends Thread` + override `run()`, (ii) `implements Runnable` + override `run()` then pass to `new Thread(task)`.
- **`start()` creates a new OS thread**; `run()` is just a method call — never call `run()` for multithreading.
- **Preferred approach = `Runnable`** (preserves inheritance, supports thread pools, cleaner OO design).
- **Thread states:** NEW → RUNNABLE → (BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING) → TERMINATED.
- **Key static methods:** `Thread.sleep(ms)`, `Thread.yield()`, `Thread.currentThread()`.
- **Key instance methods:** `start()`, `run()`, `join()`, `getName()`, `setName()`, `getPriority()`, `setPriority()`, `isAlive()`, `interrupt()`, `setDaemon()`.
- **Priority range:** 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX); default is 5 (NORM). Priority is a **hint, not a guarantee**.
- **Daemon threads** = background service threads; JVM does not wait for them. `setDaemon(true)` MUST be called *before* `start()`.
- **`join()`** = caller waits for target thread to die; used for thread coordination.
- **Multiple threads sharing objects** → potential race conditions (foundation for synchronization).
- **Lambda syntax:** `new Thread(() -> { ... }).start();` is the modern concise form.
- **Interleaving of output is non-deterministic** — exams test understanding, not memorization of output order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synchronization** in Object Oriented Programming (specifically in Java/KTU syllabus context) is the capability to control the access of multiple threads to any shared resource. It is the mechanism that ensures that **only one thread** can access the shared resource (critical section) at any given point in time, thereby preventing **race conditions**, **data inconsistency**, and **thread interference**.

Synchronization is built on the concept of a **monitor** (also called a lock or mutex). Every object in Java has an intrinsic lock (monitor lock) associated with it. When a thread enters a synchronized block or method, it automatically acquires this lock, and when it exits (normally or via exception), the lock is released.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, synchronization is the cornerstone of multi-threaded programming and is essential for achieving **mutual exclusion** and **inter-thread communication** in concurrent OOP applications.

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy: The Single-Person Washroom

Imagine a public washroom with a single toilet and an **occupancy lock** on the door.

1. **Without the lock (No Synchronization)**: Two people walk in simultaneously because the door is unlocked. They collide, fight for the toilet, and the situation becomes chaotic — privacy is violated, hygiene is compromised, and resources are misused.
2. **With the lock (Synchronization)**: The first person enters and **locks the door** from inside. The second person, seeing the red "Occupied" indicator, must **wait outside** until the first person finishes and unlocks the door. Only then can the second person enter and lock it for themselves.

In this analogy:
- The **toilet** = the **shared resource** (a variable, file, database record, or object)
- The **person** = the **thread**
- The **lock** = the **synchronized monitor**
- The **waiting area outside** = the **thread pool's wait queue**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Synchronization is fundamentally about **mutual exclusion** — ensuring that critical sections of code (regions that manipulate shared data) are executed by exactly one thread at a time. This is the same principle that operating systems apply to kernel resources.

## 1.3 The Problem: Race Condition

A **race condition** occurs when two or more threads access a shared resource concurrently, and the final outcome depends on the unpredictable timing or interleaving of their execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Favorite Question**: "What is a race condition? How does synchronization prevent it?" — This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU exams.

**Example without synchronization (Vulnerable Code):**
```java
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    public void increment() {
        count++; // This is NOT atomic! It's: read -> modify -> write
    }
}
```

If Thread A and Thread B both call `increment()` simultaneously, both might read `count = 5`, both compute `6`, and both write `6` — losing one increment. This is the **lost update problem**.

## 1.4 Types of Synchronization in KTU Syllabus

| Type | Description | KTU Weightage |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Thread Synchronization** | Multiple threads within the same process coordinate access to shared objects | **High** (Core topic) |
| **Process Synchronization** | Different processes coordinate (uses message passing) | Low (Mentioned) |
| **Mutual Exclusion** | Ensuring only one thread enters the critical section at a time | **High** |
| **Inter-Thread Communication** | Threads communicate via `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` | **High** (Frequently asked) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Internal Locking Mechanism (How It Works)

Every Java object has an **intrinsic lock** (also called **monitor lock** or simply **monitor**). Here is the step-by-step logic of how synchronization operates:

1. **Lock Acquisition**: When a thread invokes a `synchronized` method, it automatically attempts to acquire the intrinsic lock of the object.
2. **Mutual Exclusion**: If the lock is held by another thread, the invoking thread **waits** (blocks) until the lock is released.
3. **Critical Section Execution**: Once acquired, the thread enters the critical section. No other thread can enter any synchronized block on the same object.
4. **Lock Release**: When the thread exits the method (either normally or via an exception), the lock is **automatically released**.
5. **Wake-up**: One of the waiting threads (if any) acquires the lock and proceeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reentrancy Rule**: Java intrinsic locks are **reentrant** — if a thread already holds the lock, it can re-enter other synchronized methods on the same object without deadlocking itself. The lock maintains a **hold count**.

## 2.2 Ways to Achieve Synchronization in Java

### A. Synchronized Method (Instance Method)
```java
class SharedResource {
    synchronized void printData(String name) {
        // Only one thread can execute this at a time
        for(int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
            System.out.println("Hello " + name);
        }
    }
}
```

### B. Synchronized Block (Fine-grained Locking)
```java
class SharedResource {
    void printData(String name) {
        synchronized(this) { // Lock on current object
            // Critical section
        }
    }
}
```

### C. Static Synchronization (Class-level Lock)
```java
class SharedResource {
    static synchronized void printData(String name) {
        // Lock is on the Class object, not the instance
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Exam Favorite)**:
> - **Instance synchronized method** → Lock is on the **current object** (`this`).
> - **Static synchronized method** → Lock is on the **Class object** (e.g., `SharedResource.class`).
> These are **two different locks**, so a thread holding the instance lock can still enter a static synchronized method, and vice versa.

## 2.3 Inter-Thread Communication (The `Object` Class Methods)

Threads can communicate with each other using three final methods of the `Object` class:

| Method | Purpose | Effect on Caller |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| `wait()` | Releases the lock and waits until another thread calls `notify()` | Thread goes to **Waiting State** |
| `notify()` | Wakes up **one** waiting thread on the same object's monitor | Random selection |
| `notifyAll()` | Wakes up **all** waiting threads on the same object's monitor | All threads compete for lock |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap**: `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` **must** be called from within a synchronized context (i.e., from a synchronized method or block). Otherwise, they throw `IllegalMonitorStateException`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | KTU Exam Significance |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| Synchronized Method | `synchronized void method()` | **3 marks** definition question |
| Synchronized Block | `synchronized(object) { ... }` | **7 marks** code explanation |
| Static Synchronization | `static synchronized void method()` | Different lock than instance |
| Inter-thread method | `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` | Must be in synchronized context |
| Lock type | Intrinsic/Monitor lock (reentrant) | Per-object lock |
| Deadlock condition | Two threads hold one lock each, waiting for the other | **Frequently asked** |
| IllegalMonitorStateException | Calling `wait()` outside synchronized block | Common pitfall |
| Lost Update Problem | Concurrent read-modify-write | Motivates synchronization |
| Mutual Exclusion | Only one thread in critical section | Core principle |
| Producer-Consumer Pattern | Uses `wait()` and `notifyAll()` | **14-mark** coding question |
| `volatile` keyword | Lightweight synchronization (visibility only) | Distinguish from `synchronized` |
| Daemon threads | Background service threads (e.g., GC) | Low-priority topic |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Database Connection Pools**: Synchronized access to limit concurrent connections
- **Banking Systems**: Atomic transactions to prevent double-withdrawal
- **GUI Frameworks**: Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) synchronization (Swing/AWT)
- **Real-time Systems**: Sensor data buffers with synchronized access
- **Logging Frameworks**: Concurrent log writers using synchronized blocks
- **Cache Implementations**: Thread-safe caches in web servers (e.g., ConcurrentHashMap)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Demonstrating the Race Condition (Before Synchronization)

Let us trace through a counter increment problem to **derive** why synchronization is mathematically necessary.

### The Counter Problem

```java
class Counter {
    int count = 0;
    public void increment() {  // NOT synchronized
        count++;
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace with Two Threads

The expression `count++` is decomposed by the JVM into **three atomic operations**:

1. **READ**: Load the current value of `count` into a register.
2. **MODIFY**: Increment the register's value by 1.
3. **WRITE**: Store the new value back to `count`.

Let the initial value of `count` be **5**. Threads **T1** and **T2** both call `increment()`.

### Interleaving Scenario 1: Interleaved (Lost Update)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } t_0 &: \text{Initial state: } count = 5 \\
\text{Time } t_1 &: T_1 \text{ reads } count \rightarrow \text{register}_1 = 5 \\
\text{Time } t_2 &: T_2 \text{ reads } count \rightarrow \text{register}_2 = 5 \\
\text{Time } t_3 &: T_1 \text{ computes } 5 + 1 = 6 \\
\text{Time } t_4 &: T_2 \text{ computes } 5 + 1 = 6 \\
\text{Time } t_5 &: T_1 \text{ writes } count = 6 \\
\text{Time } t_6 &: T_2 \text{ writes } count = 6 \\
\text{Final state} &: count = 6 \text{ (Expected: 7)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Lost Update**: We expected the final value to be **7** (since 2 increments were performed), but we got **6**. One increment was **lost** due to the race condition. This is the **KTU classic race condition example**.

### Interleaving Scenario 2: Non-interleaved (Correct)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time } t_0 &: count = 5 \\
\text{Time } t_1 &: T_1 \text{ reads } count \rightarrow \text{register}_1 = 5 \\
\text{Time } t_2 &: T_1 \text{ computes } 6 \\
\text{Time } t_3 &: T_1 \text{ writes } count = 6 \\
\text{Time } t_4 &: T_2 \text{ reads } count \rightarrow \text{register}_2 = 6 \\
\text{Time } t_5 &: T_2 \text{ computes } 7 \\
\text{Time } t_6 &: T_2 \text{ writes } count = 7 \\
\text{Final state} &: count = 7 \text{ (Correct)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The non-determinism of thread scheduling makes the outcome unpredictable. **Synchronization eliminates this non-determinism** by enforcing atomicity of the critical section.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Synchronized vs Unsynchronized Execution

| Execution Step | Unsynchronized | Synchronized |
|----------------|----------------|--------------|
| T1 enters `increment()` | ✅ Allowed | ✅ Allowed (acquires lock) |
| T2 enters `increment()` (while T1 is in) | ✅ Allowed (corrupts data) | ❌ Blocked (waits for lock) |
| T1 reads `count` | Possible overlap | Exclusive access |
| T1 writes `count` | Possible overlap | Exclusive access |
| T1 exits method | Lock NOT released | Lock automatically released |
| T2 proceeds | Race condition occurs | Resumes safely |
| Final result | **Non-deterministic** | **Deterministic & correct** |

## 3.3 Derivation: Producer-Consumer with `wait()` and `notifyAll()`

This is a **14-mark KTU classic**. Let us derive the solution.

### Problem Statement
A producer thread produces items and places them in a shared buffer (size 1). A consumer thread consumes them. Synchronization is required so that:
- Producer waits if buffer is full
- Consumer waits if buffer is empty

### Solution (Step-by-Step Logic)

```java
class Buffer {
    private int data;
    private boolean available = false;

    // Producer calls this
    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        // Step 1: Check if buffer is full
        while (available == true) {
            wait(); // Release lock and wait
        }
        // Step 2: Produce
        data = value;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + data);
        available = true;
        // Step 3: Notify the consumer
        notifyAll();
    }

    // Consumer calls this
    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        // Step 1: Check if buffer is empty
        while (available == false) {
            wait(); // Release lock and wait
        }
        // Step 2: Consume
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + data);
        available = false;
        // Step 3: Notify the producer
        notifyAll();
        return data;
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial} &: available = false, data = 0 \\
\text{T1 (Producer)} &: \text{Checks } available == false \rightarrow \text{OK to produce} \\
\text{T1} &: data = 10, available = true, notifyAll() \\
\text{T2 (Consumer)} &: \text{Wakes up, checks } available == true \rightarrow \text{OK to consume} \\
\text{T2} &: \text{Consumes } 10, available = false, notifyAll() \\
\text{T1} &: \text{Wakes up, loops back, sees } available == false \rightarrow \text{Produces again} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule**: Always use `while` loop with `wait()`, not `if`. This is because of **spurious wakeups** — a thread can wake up without being notified. The `while` loop re-checks the condition to ensure correctness.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: `wait()` vs `sleep()` (Common KTU Confusion)

| Feature | `wait()` | `sleep()` |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| Class | `java.lang.Object` | `java.lang.Thread` |
| Releases lock? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Must be in synchronized? | **Yes** | No |
| Wakes up by | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` | Timeout completion |
| Purpose | Inter-thread communication | Pausing execution |
| State transition | WAITING → RUNNABLE | TIMED_WAITING → RUNNABLE |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Synchronization Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread T1 invokes synchronized method] --> B{Lock on object available?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Acquire intrinsic lock]
    B -->|No| D[Add T1 to Entry Set wait queue]
    D --> E[T1 enters BLOCKED state]
    C --> F[Execute critical section]
    F --> G[Exit method / exception]
    G --> H[Release lock automatically]
    H --> I{Entry Set has waiting threads?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Pick one thread from Entry Set]
    I -->|No| K[Lock becomes free]
    J --> C
    K --> L[Thread execution complete]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Producer-Consumer Wait/Notify Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Producer Thread
    participant B as Shared Buffer Object
    participant C as Consumer Thread

    Note over P,C: Initial State: available = false

    P->>B: produce(10) acquires lock
    B-->>P: Checks while(available) -> false, proceeds
    P->>B: data = 10, available = true
    P->>B: notifyAll() releases lock
    Note over C: Consumer was waiting
    C->>B: consume() acquires lock
    B-->>C: Checks while(!available) -> false, proceeds
    C->>B: reads data, available = false
    C->>B: notifyAll() releases lock
    Note over P: Producer resumes, sees available=false, produces again
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of Synchronization Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Heap["JVM Heap Memory"]
        Obj[Shared Object]
        Mon[Intrinsic Monitor / Lock]
        ES[Entry Set Queue]
        WS[Wait Set Queue]
        Obj --- Mon
        Mon --- ES
        Mon --- WS
    end

    subgraph Threads["Thread States"]
        T1[Thread T1 RUNNABLE]
        T2[Thread T2 BLOCKED]
        T3[Thread T3 WAITING]
    end

    T1 -->|Acquires lock| Mon
    T2 -->|Waiting for lock| ES
    T3 -->|Called wait| WS
    WS -->|Notified| ES
    ES -->|Lock free| Mon
    Mon -->|Releases| T1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Deadlock Scenario (Tight Circular Wait)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread T1 holds Resource A] -->|Waits for| B[Resource B]
    B[Thread T2 holds Resource B] -->|Waits for| A[Resource A]
    A -.->|BLOCKED forever| B
    B -.->|BLOCKED forever| A
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style B fill:#ffcccc
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Deadlock Conditions (Coffman Conditions)** — All four must hold simultaneously:
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion**: Resources cannot be shared
> 2. **Hold and Wait**: Thread holds one resource while waiting for another
> 3. **No Preemption**: Resources cannot be forcibly taken
> 4. **Circular Wait**: T1 waits for T2's resource, and T2 waits for T1's resource
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define synchronization. Mention its types. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Synchronization** is the process of controlling the access of multiple threads to shared resources to prevent data inconsistency and race conditions. It ensures that only one thread can access a shared resource (critical section) at a time by using locks/monitors.

**Types of Synchronization:**
1. **Process Synchronization**: Coordination between separate processes (achieved via message passing).
2. **Thread Synchronization**: Coordination between threads within the same process (achieved via `synchronized` keyword, locks, and `wait/notify`).

(1 mark definition + 2 marks for types)

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between `wait()` and `sleep()` in Java? `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `wait()` | `sleep()` |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| Belongs to | `java.lang.Object` class | `java.lang.Thread` class |
| Releases lock? | **Yes**, releases the monitor lock | **No**, holds the lock |
| Must be in synchronized? | **Yes**, else `IllegalMonitorStateException` | No |
| Wake-up mechanism | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` from another thread | Timeout expiry |
| Purpose | Inter-thread communication | Pausing execution for a duration |

(1 mark purpose each + 1 mark for the lock-release distinction)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: Producer-Consumer Problem `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Producer-Consumer problem in Java. Discuss how inter-thread communication is achieved using `wait()` and `notify()` methods. Mention the role of the `synchronized` keyword.

**Model Answer:**

**The Producer-Consumer Problem** is a classic synchronization problem where a **Producer thread** generates data items and places them in a shared buffer, while a **Consumer thread** removes them for processing. The challenge is to synchronize them so that:
- The Producer must wait if the buffer is **full**.
- The Consumer must wait if the buffer is **empty**.

**Inter-Thread Communication in Java** is achieved using three methods of the `java.lang.Object` class:
1. **`wait()`**: The current thread releases the lock and enters the **WAITING** state until another thread calls `notify()` or `notifyAll()` on the same object.
2. **`notify()`**: Wakes up **one** waiting thread that is waiting on the same object's monitor.
3. **`notifyAll()`**: Wakes up **all** threads waiting on the same object's monitor.

**Role of `synchronized` keyword:**
- Ensures **mutual exclusion** — only one thread can execute the critical section at a time.
- Provides the **monitor lock** on which `wait()` and `notify()` operate.
- Without `synchronized`, calling `wait()` throws `IllegalMonitorStateException`.

(3 marks problem + 2 marks wait/notify + 2 marks synchronized role)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to implement the Producer-Consumer problem using `wait()` and `notifyAll()` for a shared buffer of size 5.

**Model Answer:**

```java
class Buffer {
    private int[] data;
    private int count = 0;
    private int in = 0, out = 0;
    
    Buffer(int size) {
        data = new int[size];
    }
    
    public synchronized void produce(int item) throws InterruptedException {
        // Wait if buffer is full
        while (count == data.length) {
            wait();
            System.out.println("Buffer FULL, Producer waiting...");
        }
        data[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) % data.length;
        count++;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + item);
        notifyAll(); // Wake up consumers
    }
    
    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        // Wait if buffer is empty
        while (count == 0) {
            wait();
            System.out.println("Buffer EMPTY, Consumer waiting...");
        }
        int item = data[out];
        out = (out + 1) % data.length;
        count--;
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + item);
        notifyAll(); // Wake up producers
        return item;
    }
}

class Producer extends Thread {
    Buffer b;
    Producer(Buffer b) { this.b = b; }
    public void run() {
        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) b.produce(i);
        } catch (Exception e) {}
    }
}

class Consumer extends Thread {
    Buffer b;
    Consumer(Buffer b) { this.b = b; }
    public void run() {
        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) b.consume();
        } catch (Exception e) {}
    }
}

public class PCMain {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Buffer buffer = new Buffer(5);
        Producer p = new Producer(buffer);
        Consumer c = new Consumer(buffer);
        p.start();
        c.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The `synchronized` keyword on `produce()` and `consume()` ensures only one thread accesses the buffer at a time.
- The `while` loop with `wait()` handles **spurious wakeups** — threads re-check the condition after waking up.
- `notifyAll()` is preferred over `notify()` to avoid **missed signals** in multi-producer/consumer scenarios.
- The producer waits when `count == size`, and the consumer waits when `count == 0`.

(2 marks correct structure + 2 marks wait/notify logic + 2 marks synchronized usage + 1 mark for execution explanation)

---

### Question B: Synchronization Mechanisms and Deadlock `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different ways to achieve synchronization in Java with suitable examples. Compare synchronized methods and synchronized blocks.

**Model Answer:**

**Synchronization in Java** can be achieved in the following ways:

**1. Synchronized Method (Instance Level)**
```java
class Table {
    synchronized void printTable(int n) { // synchronized method
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n * i));
            try { Thread.sleep(400); } catch (Exception e) {}
        }
    }
}
```
- The lock is acquired on the **current object** (`this`).
- The entire method becomes a critical section.

**2. Synchronized Block (Fine-grained Control)**
```java
class Table {
    void printTable(int n) {
        synchronized(this) { // synchronized block on 'this'
            for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
                System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n * i));
            }
        }
        // Non-critical code can run concurrently
        System.out.println("End of table");
    }
}
```
- Lock can be acquired on **any object**, not just `this`.
- Allows non-critical code to run in parallel → better performance.

**3. Static Synchronization**
```java
class Table {
    static synchronized void printTable(int n) {
        // Lock is on Table.class, not on the instance
    }
}
```
- Lock is on the **Class object**, affecting all instances.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Synchronized Method | Synchronized Block |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Scope | Entire method | Selected lines |
| Lock object | `this` (current object) | Any specified object |
| Granularity | Coarse | Fine |
| Performance | Lower (more blocking) | Higher (less blocking) |
| Flexibility | Less | More |

(2 marks methods + 2 marks block + 2 marks static + 1 mark comparison)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is deadlock? Write a Java program that demonstrates deadlock between two threads. Explain the necessary conditions for deadlock.

**Model Answer:**

**Deadlock** is a situation in multithreading where two or more threads are **permanently blocked**, each waiting for a lock held by another thread in the circular wait chain. The threads never proceed, and the program hangs.

**Java Program Demonstrating Deadlock:**

```java
public class DeadlockDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final String resourceA = "ResourceA";
        final String resourceB = "ResourceB";
        
        // Thread 1 tries to lock resourceA then resourceB
        Thread t1 = new Thread() {
            public void run() {
                synchronized (resourceA) {
                    System.out.println("Thread 1: Locked Resource A");
                    try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (Exception e) {}
                    System.out.println("Thread 1: Waiting for Resource B...");
                    synchronized (resourceB) {
                        System.out.println("Thread 1: Locked Resource B");
                    }
                }
            }
        };
        
        // Thread 2 tries to lock resourceB then resourceA
        Thread t2 = new Thread() {
            public void run() {
                synchronized (resourceB) {
                    System.out.println("Thread 2: Locked Resource B");
                    try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (Exception e) {}
                    System.out.println("Thread 2: Waiting for Resource A...");
                    synchronized (resourceA) {
                        System.out.println("Thread 2: Locked Resource A");
                    }
                }
            }
        };
        
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace:**
- T1 acquires `resourceA` lock.
- T2 acquires `resourceB` lock.
- T1 tries to acquire `resourceB` → blocks (T2 holds it).
- T2 tries to acquire `resourceA` → blocks (T1 holds it).
- **Both threads are stuck forever → DEADLOCK!**

**Necessary Conditions for Deadlock (Coffman Conditions):**
1. **Mutual Exclusion**: At least one resource is held in a non-shareable mode.
2. **Hold and Wait**: A thread holding a resource waits for another resource held by another thread.
3. **No Preemption**: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread.
4. **Circular Wait**: A circular chain of threads exists, where each thread holds a resource the next thread needs.

(2 marks definition + 2 marks program + 2 marks trace + 1 mark Coffman conditions)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `synchronized` around `wait()`**: Calling `wait()` outside a synchronized context throws `IllegalMonitorStateException` — **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Using `if` instead of `while` with `wait()`**: Causes issues with **spurious wakeups** — **lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing class-level and object-level locks**: Static `synchronized` uses the Class object lock, not instance lock — **lose 2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to call `notify()`**: Causes the program to hang forever — **lose 2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing `wait()` with `sleep()`**: Forgetting that `wait()` releases the lock but `sleep()` does not — **lose 1 mark**.
> 6. **Not using `synchronized` on the shared resource methods**: Race condition persists — **lose 3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save this for last-minute prep!)**

### Conceptual Definitions
- **Synchronization**: Coordinating thread access to shared resources to prevent data corruption.
- **Race Condition**: Outcome depends on the unpredictable interleaving of threads.
- **Critical Section**: The code segment accessing shared resources that must be protected.
- **Mutual Exclusion**: Only one thread executes the critical section at any time.
- **Monitor Lock**: The intrinsic lock associated with every Java object.
- **Deadlock**: Permanent block of two or more threads waiting for each other.
- **Spurious Wakeup**: A thread wakes up from `wait()` without being notified — re-check condition in `while` loop.
- **Lost Update Problem**: Concurrent read-modify-write leads to lost increments.

### Method Syntaxes (MUST memorize)
- `synchronized void method()` — Instance-level synchronization
- `static synchronized void method()` — Class-level synchronization
- `synchronized(obj) { ... }` — Block-level synchronization
- `obj.wait()` — Releases lock, enters WAITING state (must be in synchronized)
- `obj.notify()` — Wakes up ONE waiting thread on `obj`
- `obj.notifyAll()` — Wakes up ALL waiting threads on `obj`
- `Thread.sleep(ms)` — Pauses for duration, **does NOT release lock**

### Key Distinctions (Frequently Tested)
- `synchronized` vs `volatile`: `synchronized` provides mutual exclusion + visibility; `volatile` provides only visibility.
- `wait()` vs `sleep()`: `wait()` releases lock; `sleep()` does not.
- `notify()` vs `notifyAll()`: `notify()` wakes one; `notifyAll()` wakes all (safer for multiple waiters).
- Instance sync vs Static sync: Different locks (object vs class).
- `BLOCKED` state (waiting for monitor lock) vs `WAITING` state (after `wait()`).

### Producer-Consumer Pattern (14-Mark Template)
- Shared buffer with `synchronized` access methods.
- `while` condition check before `wait()`.
- `notifyAll()` after modifying the buffer.
- Always wrap `wait/notify` inside `synchronized` block.

### Deadlock Prevention Strategies (Bonus Points)
- **Lock Ordering**: Always acquire locks in the same global order.
- **Lock Timeout**: Use `tryLock()` with a timeout (java.util.concurrent).
- **Avoid Nested Locks**: Minimize holding multiple locks.
- **Use Higher-Level Concurrency**: `java.util.concurrent` classes (ConcurrentHashMap, etc.).

### KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Pattern
- **Definition questions** (Synchronization, Race Condition, Deadlock): 3 marks
- **Comparison questions** (`wait` vs `sleep`, synchronized method vs block): 7 marks
- **Code questions** (Producer-Consumer, Deadlock demo): 14 marks
- **Trace questions** (Lost Update, Lock acquisition): 7 marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suspending
Resuming and Stopping Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resuming and Stopping Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resuming and Stopping Threads** refers to the set of mechanisms provided by the `java.lang.Thread` API and the modern `java.util.concurrent` framework that allow a program to **suspend** the execution of a running thread, **restart** its execution after a pause, or **terminate** its execution permanently in a safe, predictable, and race-condition-free manner.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic sits inside **Module 3: Multithreading & Concurrency** and is a direct continuation of the **Thread Life Cycle** module. The `Thread` class in Java originally exposed three methods — `suspend()`, `resume()`, and `stop()` — but all three have been **deprecated since JDK 1.2** (1998) because they suffer from **intrinsic lock monopolization** and **asynchronous exception throwing** that can leave shared objects in an inconsistent state.

The current industry-accepted way (and what KTU examiners expect in 2024 Scheme answers) is:
1. **Stopping** a thread by making its `run()` method return naturally, usually via a `volatile boolean` flag or `Thread.interrupt()`.
2. **Resuming/Pausing** a thread by cooperative `wait()` / `notify()` (monitor-based) or by a guarded `volatile` flag.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Construction Worker

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Construction Worker on a High-Rise Site**
>
> Imagine a construction worker (the *Thread*) laying bricks on the 10th floor. The foreman (the *main thread / executor*) needs three controls:
> - **"Stop!"** — "Come down from the scaffolding immediately, the crane is moving overhead." (This is a *thread stop*.)
> - **"Pause!"** — "Hold your hand where it is, do not move the brick; I need to check the alignment." (This is a *thread suspend*.)
> - **"Resume!"** — "OK, place the brick." (This is a *thread resume*.)
>
> If the foreman simply *yanks the worker off the scaffold* without warning, the worker might drop a half-laid brick and damage the wall (a *corrupted shared object*). Java's deprecated `stop()` worked exactly like that — it threw a `ThreadDeath` exception *anywhere* in the running code, including inside a `synchronized` block while the worker was holding a hammer (the *monitor lock*). The new methods are like a polite, cooperative handshake: the worker keeps one ear open and **checks politely** whether they should stop.

## 1.3 The Three Deprecated Methods — At a Glance

| Method | Signature (Pre-JDK 1.2) | Modern Status | Reason for Deprecation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Thread.stop()` | `final void stop()` | **Deprecated** | Asynchronously throws `ThreadDeath` at any point, can leave locked monitors or shared resources in a corrupt state. |
| `Thread.suspend()` | `final void suspend()` | **Deprecated** | Does not release the monitor lock; the suspended thread holds the lock while paused, which can deadlock the whole program. |
| `Thread.resume()` | `final void resume()` | **Deprecated** | Pairs with `suspend()`; useless once `suspend()` is removed. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Any answer that proposes using `thread.stop()`, `thread.suspend()`, or `thread.resume()` directly in 2024 Scheme Java code will be marked as **conceptually incorrect** even if it compiles. The expected answer is the **cooperative-interruption model**.

## 1.4 Official Java SE 17+ Replacement Strategy

| Old Deprecated Call | Modern Replacement |
|---|---|
| `t.stop()` | A `volatile boolean running` flag checked inside `run()`, or `t.interrupt()`. |
| `t.suspend()` | `wait()` / `await()` from `java.util.concurrent.locks.Condition`. |
| `t.resume()` | `notify()` / `signal()` on the same monitor/condition. |
| `t.stop()` (from Executor) | `executor.shutdownNow()` + `Future.cancel(true)`. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying "Why" — The Three Reasons Deprecation Happened

### Reason 1 — `stop()` releases locks too aggressively
When `stop()` was called, it threw `ThreadDeath` *deep inside* the `run()` method. If the thread was inside a `synchronized` block, the **monitor lock was released as the stack unwound**, but any partial mutations of shared data (e.g., a half-transferred `BankAccount` balance) remained visible to other threads. This is the classic **atomicity violation**.

### Reason 2 — `suspend()` holds the lock while paused
`suspend()` froze the thread *in place*. If the thread was holding `Account.lock`, every other thread that tried to `synchronized(acc)` blocked **forever** (a *resource deadlock*). The thread had to be `resume()`d by *some other* thread that itself could not acquire the lock — a classic Catch-22.

### Reason 3 — `resume()` had no way to know *what* to resume
Since `suspend()` could be called multiple times on the same thread, `resume()` had an implicit "resume only one level" semantic that confused even experienced developers.

## 2.2 The Cooperative Interruption Model (The Modern Way)

The Java Memory Model (JMM, JSR-133) requires that thread-stop signalling be a **happens-before** relationship. The two canonical mechanisms are:

### Mechanism A — The `volatile` Flag Pattern

```java
class StoppableWorker extends Thread {
    private volatile boolean running = true;   // (1) 'volatile' is non-negotiable
    public void run() {
        while (running) {                     // (2) Read of 'running' on every iteration
            doOneUnitOfWork();
        }
        cleanup();                            // (3) Graceful exit
    }
    public void shutdown() { running = false; }   // (4) Write is visible to worker immediately
}
```

**Why `volatile`?**
Without `volatile`, the worker thread is free to cache `running` in a **CPU register or L1 cache** (per the JMM). The foreman's `running = false;` write would then sit in main memory and the worker would **never see it** — a textbook *visibility* failure. Marking it `volatile` inserts a **memory barrier** that flushes both the worker's read and the foreman's write across all caches, guaranteeing a happens-before edge.

### Mechanism B — The `interrupt()` Pattern

```java
class InterruptibleWorker extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        try {
            while (!Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted()) {
                doBlockingIO();           // throws InterruptedException
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            // Re-interrupt so the caller can still query the flag
            Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
        } finally {
            cleanup();
        }
    }
}
// Caller side:
worker.interrupt();  // sets the interrupt flag AND unblocks any wait/sleep/join
```

`interrupt()` does three distinct things:
1. Sets the thread's **internal interrupt status flag** to `true`.
2. **Unblocks** the thread if it is currently parked in `Object.wait()`, `Thread.sleep()`, `Thread.join()`, or any `InterruptibleChannel` I/O.
3. If the thread was unblocked, it throws `InterruptedException` from that blocking call.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Term | Definition | Exam-Relevant Detail |
|---|---|---|
| `Thread.stop()` | **Deprecated**. Throws `ThreadDeath`. | Marks deducted if used in 2024 answer. |
| `Thread.suspend()` | **Deprecated**. Holds monitor while paused. | Source of deadlocks; never use. |
| `Thread.resume()` | **Deprecated**. Pairs with `suspend()`. | Useless without `suspend()`. |
| `volatile` keyword | Guarantees **visibility** of writes across threads. | Required for the stop-flag pattern. |
| `Thread.interrupt()` | Sets interrupt flag, unblocks wait/sleep/join. | The modern "stop" signal. |
| `isInterrupted()` | Instance method; does **not clear** the flag. | Used inside `run()`. |
| `Thread.interrupted()` | Static method; **clears** the flag after reading. | Used at the call site. |
| `wait()` / `notify()` | Monitor-based pause/resume. | Must be called from `synchronized` block. |
| `await()` / `signal()` | `Lock`-condition-based pause/resume. | More flexible; multiple conditions per lock. |
| `executor.shutdownNow()` | Attempts to stop all running tasks. | Returns `List<Runnable>` of pending tasks. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Event Dispatch Threads (EDT):** A button "Cancel" must stop a long-running background task; the `volatile` flag pattern is used inside `SwingWorker.cancel()`.
- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** Each request-handler thread checks an `isRunning` flag during graceful shutdown so HTTP responses are flushed before the worker exits.
- **Database Connection Pools:** The pool's eviction thread is interrupted when the JVM shuts down, releasing sockets cleanly.
- **Embedded / IoT Devices:** A `volatile` boolean makes the main sensor-loop killable in <1 µs with no kernel syscall.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Logical Deduction & Analytical Matrix

> Since this is a Computer Science (Java) topic, the "derivation" is the **logical flow of state transitions and memory-visibility guarantees** in the cooperative stop model.

## 3.1 Deduction 1 — Why a Non-`volatile` Flag Fails (Memory-Visibility Proof)

Consider two threads executing on a typical multi-core x86 machine (each core has its own L1 cache). The foreman thread writes `running = false` to **L1 of Core 0**. The worker thread reads `running` from **L1 of Core 1**, which still has the stale value **`true`** cached.

The JMM allows this caching because the read and write have **no synchronization order** between them. The formal happens-before rule states:

$$
\text{Write}(r=\text{false}) \rightarrow_{hb} \text{Read}(r) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Program order, lock, volatile, or final write}
$$

Without any of those, the JMM permits the worker to **read `true` forever**, even after the foreman has long since set the flag.

### Step-by-Step Transition to a `volatile` Fix

1. Foreman writes `this.running = false;` — this is a write to a `volatile` field.
2. The JVM emits a **StoreStore barrier** after the write, flushing the value to main memory.
3. The JVM emits a **LoadLoad barrier** before every subsequent read of `running` by the worker.
4. The worker's read is therefore forced to **invalidate its L1 cache line** and fetch the latest value from main memory.
5. The worker sees `running == false`, exits the `while` loop, calls `cleanup()`, and `run()` returns.

Formally, the barrier sequence inside a `volatile` write is:

$$
\text{StoreStore} \;\Big|\; \text{Write to volatile} \;\Big|\; \text{StoreLoad}
$$

and the corresponding read is:

$$
\text{LoadLoad} \;\Big|\; \text{Read from volatile} \;\Big|\; \text{LoadStore}
$$

This is the JMM guarantee that makes the pattern **linearizable**.

## 3.2 Deduction 2 — The Three Outcomes of `worker.interrupt()`

Let $S$ be the worker's state at the moment `interrupt()` is called. The system behaves as:

$$
\text{interrupt}(S) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
S \rightarrow \text{RUNNABLE} \;\;+\;\; \text{flag}=\text{true} & \text{if } S \in \{\text{READY},\ \text{RUNNING}\} \\[4pt]
S \rightarrow \text{RUNNABLE} \;\;+\;\; \text{flag}=\text{true} \;\;+\;\; \text{throw InterruptedException} & \text{if } S \in \{\text{BLOCKED-on-sleep},\ \text{BLOCKED-on-wait},\ \text{BLOCKED-on-join}\} \\[4pt]
\text{No-op} & \text{if thread already terminated}
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Deduction 3 — The `wait()` / `notify()` Resume Algebra

The classic pause/resume on a shared monitor follows this invariant:

$$
\text{monitor.lock} = \text{acquired} \;\wedge\; \text{condition} = \text{false} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{thread waits, releases lock}
$$

$$
\text{condition} = \text{true} \;\wedge\; \text{notify()} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{waiting thread re-acquires lock, condition re-checked}
$$

The atomicity comes from Java requiring both `wait()` and `notify()` to be called from inside a `synchronized(monitor)` block. This guarantees the **monitor invariant** is preserved.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | `stop()` (Deprecated) | `volatile` flag | `interrupt()` | `Executor.shutdownNow()` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Asynchronous?** | Yes (unsafe) | No (cooperative) | No (cooperative) | No (cooperative) |
| **Releases locks safely?** | Unpredictably | Yes (worker frees them) | Yes (worker frees them) | Yes (tasks free them) |
| **Unblocks `wait/sleep`?** | N/A (dead) | No | **Yes** | Yes (via interrupt) |
| **Re-entrant / safe to call multiple times?** | N/A | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Forces a `finally` cleanup?** | No — *dangerous* | Yes (recommended) | Yes (recommended) | Yes (recommended) |
| **Preferred for new code (2024)?** | ❌ | ✅ (simple cases) | ✅ (blocking I/O) | ✅ (thread pools) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — The Modern Thread Pause/Resume/Stop Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED_WAIT : wait() or await()
    BLOCKED_WAIT --> RUNNABLE : notify() or signal()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED_IO : sleep() or blockingIO()
    BLOCKED_IO --> RUNNABLE : timeout or interrupt()
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : running flag false OR run() returns
    BLOCKED_WAIT --> RUNNABLE : interrupt() unblocks wait
```
