```mermaid
    BLOCKED_IO --> TERMINATED : interrupt() throws InterruptedException
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : interrupt() sets flag, run() returns
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stop-Request Propagation in `ExecutorService`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Thread] --> B[ExecutorService]
    B --> C[Worker Thread T1]
    B --> D[Worker Thread T2]
    C --> E{isRunning flag}
    D --> F{isInterrupted flag}
    A --> G[shutdownNow call]
    G --> C
    G --> D
    E -->|false| H[run returns, cleanup]
    F -->|true| I[catch InterruptedException]
    I --> H
    H --> J[Thread TERMINATED]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — `interrupt()` Crossing a `wait()` Boundary

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Thread
    participant Worker as Worker Thread
    participant Monitor as Shared Object (lock)

    Main ->> Monitor: synchronized(monitor) { monitor.wait(); }
    Note over Worker: Worker is BLOCKED inside wait
    Main ->> Worker: worker.interrupt()
    Worker ->> Monitor: Throws InterruptedException
    Monitor -->> Worker: Releases monitor lock atomically
    Worker ->> Worker: catch block runs cleanup
    Worker -->> Main: run() returns
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Three Layers of "Stop" Power

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1: Application-Level Flag] -->|Simplest| L2[Layer 2: Thread.interrupt]
    L2 -->|Unblocks I/O| L3[Layer 3: ExecutorService Lifecycle]
    L1 --> Use1[Spin-loop or polling tasks]
    L2 --> Use2[Network, file, sleep-bound tasks]
    L3 --> Use3[Thread pools, server shutdown]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why were `Thread.stop()`, `Thread.suspend()`, and `Thread.resume()` deprecated in Java?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) `Thread.stop()` was deprecated because it **asynchronously threw `ThreadDeath` at an arbitrary point** in the target thread, potentially leaving shared objects and monitor locks in an inconsistent state.
- (1 mark) `Thread.suspend()` was deprecated because it **froze the target thread while still holding its monitor lock**, leading to **deadlocks** when other threads tried to acquire the same monitor.
- (1 mark) `Thread.resume()` was deprecated in tandem with `suspend()` because, without a safe `suspend()`, it had no valid use-case and caused confusion in multi-suspend scenarios. Java's modern alternative is the **cooperative interruption model** using `volatile` flags or `Thread.interrupt()`.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the `volatile` keyword in the thread-stop flag pattern. What happens if you omit it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The `volatile` keyword **ensures that writes to the flag by one thread are immediately visible** to other threads by forcing a **memory barrier** (StoreStore + StoreLoad after writes; LoadLoad + LoadStore before reads).
- (1 mark) It also prevents the JIT compiler from caching the value in a CPU register, which is critical for the worker thread's `while(running)` loop.
- (1 mark) **If `volatile` is omitted**, the worker thread may **never observe** the foreman's `running = false` write due to per-core L1 caching, causing an **infinite loop** — a classic *visibility* failure. The `happens-before` relationship is broken.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Module in the End Semester Exam (ESE) contains one **14-mark question with internal choice (a) OR (b)**. Both sub-parts carry 7 marks each.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **cooperative interruption model** in Java. With a suitable code example, demonstrate how a thread can be safely stopped using a `volatile` flag. Highlight why `volatile` is essential.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the use of the `interrupt()`, `isInterrupted()`, and `Thread.interrupted()` methods. Show, with code, how `interrupt()` unblocks a thread sleeping inside `Thread.sleep(1000)`.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]

**The cooperative interruption model** (also called the *polite stop* model) is a design philosophy where the target thread is **not** stopped by force. Instead, a *signal* is sent (a flag, an interrupt, or a `Future.cancel()`), and the thread **periodically checks** for that signal and exits its own `run()` method gracefully. (1 mark)

**Code Example:**

```java
class FileProcessor extends Thread {
    private volatile boolean running = true;          // (2) volatile for visibility

    public void run() {
        int i = 0;
        while (running) {                            // (3) poll the flag
            System.out.println("Processing chunk " + i++);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { break; }
        }
        System.out.println("Cleanup: closing file handles.");
    }

    public void shutdown() {                          // (4) cooperative signal
        running = false;
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        FileProcessor t = new FileProcessor();
        t.start();
        Thread.sleep(3000);
        t.shutdown();       // asks politely
        t.join();           // wait for graceful exit
    }
}
```

**Why `volatile` is essential:** (2 marks) Without it, the JIT may hoist the `running` field into a register, so the worker **never re-reads** from main memory. The `volatile` modifier inserts a **StoreLoad fence** on every write and a **LoadLoad fence** on every read, guaranteeing a happens-before edge between `shutdown()` and the worker's loop check. The foreman's write is therefore guaranteed to be **globally visible** on the very next iteration.

**Cleanup is critical:** Because the thread returns from `run()` naturally, any `finally` block runs to completion — file handles, sockets, and locks are released safely. (1 mark)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]

The `java.lang.Thread` class provides three complementary methods for interruption handling: (1 mark)

| Method | Type | Clears Flag? | Typical Caller |
|---|---|---|---|
| `interrupt()` | Instance | No (sets it) | External thread signalling stop |
| `isInterrupted()` | Instance | **No** | Worker checking its own status |
| `Thread.interrupted()` | Static | **Yes** | Worker clearing after check |

**Code Example — Unblocking `Thread.sleep()`:** (5 marks)

```java
class Sleeper extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        try {
            System.out.println("Going to sleep...");
            Thread.sleep(60_000);             // long sleep
            System.out.println("Woke up naturally.");
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Woken up by interrupt!");
            // Re-interrupt to preserve status for the caller
            Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
        }
    }
}

public class InterruptDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Sleeper s = new Sleeper();
        s.start();
        Thread.sleep(1000);        // give Sleeper time to enter sleep
        System.out.println("Sending interrupt...");
        s.interrupt();             // unblocks sleep, throws InterruptedException
        s.join();
        System.out.println("Sleeper terminated. Interrupt flag: " + s.isInterrupted());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Going to sleep...
Sending interrupt...
Woken up by interrupt!
Sleeper terminated. Interrupt flag: false
```
(The flag is `false` because we called `Thread.currentThread().interrupt()` in the catch block — wait, the call re-sets it. The output is `true` unless you only call `Thread.interrupted()` which clears it.) 

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — `isInterrupted()` vs `Thread.interrupted()`:**
> Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by confusing these two. **`isInterrupted()` is an instance method that does NOT clear the flag**, while **`Thread.interrupted()` is a static method that DOES clear it after returning the current value**. If the question asks "check and clear", the answer is `Thread.interrupted()`. If the question asks "check without clearing", the answer is `this.isInterrupted()` or `worker.isInterrupted()`.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Java Thread Life Cycle** in detail. Show how `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` are used to pause and resume threads cooperatively.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a Java program that creates **two threads** — a `Producer` and a `Consumer` — sharing a bounded `BlockingQueue` (capacity = 5). Demonstrate how `interrupt()` can be used to gracefully shut down both threads.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]

**Java Thread Life Cycle (6 states, post-JDK 1.5):** (3 marks)

```
NEW → RUNNABLE ⇄ BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED
```

- **NEW:** Thread object is created but `start()` not yet called.
- **RUNNABLE:** Executing or ready to execute; the scheduler decides when.
- **BLOCKED:** Waiting to acquire a monitor lock (entered a `synchronized` block owned by another thread).
- **WAITING:** Called `Object.wait()` (no timeout), `Thread.join()` (no timeout), or `LockSupport.park()`. Released by `notify()` / `notifyAll()`.
- **TIMED_WAITING:** Called `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`, or `parkNanos()`. Released by timeout or `interrupt()`.
- **TERMINATED:** `run()` returned normally or threw an uncaught exception.

**Cooperative Pause/Resume using `wait()` / `notify()`:** (4 marks)

```java
class PauseResumeDemo {
    private final Object lock = new Object();
    private volatile boolean paused = false;

    public void doWork() throws InterruptedException {
        synchronized (lock) {
            while (paused) {                // (1) guarded wait
                lock.wait();                // (2) releases monitor
            }
            // ... do actual work ...
        }
    }

    public void pause() {
        synchronized (lock) { paused = true; }
    }

    public void resume() {
        synchronized (lock) {
            paused = false;
            lock.notifyAll();              // (3) wake all waiters
        }
    }
}
```

**Key Rules:** `wait()` and `notify()` *must* be called inside a `synchronized(lock)` block on the **same monitor object**. The `while` loop (not `if`) is required to guard against **spurious wakeups** — a JMM-permitted phenomenon where a thread can return from `wait()` without a `notify()` ever being called.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]

```java
import java.util.concurrent.*;

class Producer extends Thread {
    private final BlockingQueue<Integer> queue;
    Producer(BlockingQueue<Integer> q) { this.queue = q; }
    public void run() {
        try {
            int i = 0;
            while (!Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted()) {
                queue.put(i);                              // blocks if full
                System.out.println("Produced " + i++);
                Thread.sleep(200);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Producer shutting down.");
        }
    }
}

class Consumer extends Thread {
    private final BlockingQueue<Integer> queue;
    Consumer(BlockingQueue<Integer> q) { this.queue = q; }
    public void run() {
        try {
            while (!Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted()) {
                Integer item = queue.take();               // blocks if empty
                System.out.println("Consumed " + item);
                Thread.sleep(400);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Consumer shutting down.");
        }
    }
}

public class ProducerConsumerShutdown {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        BlockingQueue<Integer> q = new ArrayBlockingQueue<>(5);
        Producer p = new Producer(q);
        Consumer c = new Consumer(q);
        p.start(); c.start();

        Thread.sleep(3000);
        System.out.println("Initiating graceful shutdown...");
        p.interrupt();                                     // unblocks queue.put/sleep
        c.interrupt();                                     // unblocks queue.take/sleep
        p.join();  c.join();
        System.out.println("All threads terminated cleanly.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- `queue.put()` and `queue.take()` are **interruptible blocking calls** — they throw `InterruptedException` when the thread is interrupted.
- `Thread.sleep(200)` is also interruptible.
- The `isInterrupted()` check at the top of the `while` loop provides a **second line of defense** for the non-blocking code between I/O calls.
- `join()` ensures the main thread waits for the workers to finish their `finally` cleanup before printing the final message.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Marks Lost (3 marks total):**
> 1. **Using `stop()`/`suspend()`/`resume()` in code** — instant 2-mark deduction in 2024 Scheme.
> 2. **Forgetting `volatile`** on the stop flag — 1 mark deduction; the answer must explicitly mention the **happens-before / memory-visibility** guarantee.
> 3. **Using `if` instead of `while` with `wait()`** — 1 mark deduction; KTU expects the guarded-wait pattern and a one-line mention of **spurious wakeups**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ⛔ **Never** use `Thread.stop()`, `Thread.suspend()`, `Thread.resume()` in modern Java code; they are deprecated since JDK 1.2.
- ✅ **Stopping a thread** = making its `run()` method return naturally, via a `volatile` flag or `Thread.interrupt()`.
- ✅ **Pausing/Resuming a thread** = `wait()` / `notify()` on a monitor, or `await()` / `signal()` on a `Condition`.
- 🔑 **`volatile`** provides two guarantees: (1) **visibility** of writes across threads, (2) prevents the JIT from caching the value in a register.
- 🔑 **`interrupt()`** sets the flag, unblocks `wait/sleep/join/InterruptibleChannel`, but is **cooperative** — the target thread must respond.
- 🔑 **`isInterrupted()`** is an *instance* method that **does not clear** the flag; **`Thread.interrupted()`** is *static* and **clears** the flag.
- 🔑 **Guarded wait** must use `while(condition)` not `if(condition)` to defend against **spurious wakeups**.
- 🔑 **`executor.shutdownNow()`** interrupts all running tasks; **`executor.shutdown()`** allows in-flight tasks to complete.
- 🔑 **JMM formalisms**: `volatile` write inserts StoreStore + StoreLoad barriers; volatile read inserts LoadLoad + LoadStore barriers.
- 🔑 **Best practice**: always include a `finally` block in `run()` to release file handles, sockets, and locks before the thread terminates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Handling - Event Handling Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Handling - Event Handling Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Event Handling Mechanism** is a software paradigm in Java's AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and Swing frameworks that facilitates communication between a user (via input devices like mouse/keyboard) and a GUI (Graphical User Interface) component. It follows the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)**, wherein an *Event Source* generates an *Event*, which is dispatched to a registered *Event Listener* that contains the logic to process it.

The three principal actors in the Java Event Handling architecture are:

| Actor | Definition | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Event** | An object describing a state change in a GUI component (e.g., button click) | Carrier of information |
| **Event Source** | The GUI component that generates an event | Producer of events |
| **Event Listener** | An object that waits for events and reacts to them | Consumer / Handler |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Bell System 🍽️

Imagine a busy restaurant:

- The **Customer (Event Source)** presses a **call bell** — this represents a *button click* generating an event.
- The **bell signal (Event Object)** carries information — *which table? what was needed?*
- The **Waiter (Event Listener)** is registered specifically for that table. When the bell rings, the waiter responds with a specific action (bringing the menu, the bill, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this analogy, the customer does **not** know *which* waiter will come — the system **delegates** the task. This mirrors Java's **Delegation Event Model**, where the source does not know what action will be performed; it simply notifies all registered listeners.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

- **Event Source** → The component that **fires** the event (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`).
- **Event Object** → Subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`).
- **Event Listener Interface** → Interface defining the handler method (e.g., `ActionListener`).
- **Event Delegation Model** → Modern model where listeners are *registered* to sources via `addXxxListener()` methods.
- **Adapter Class** → Default empty implementation of a listener with multiple methods; used to override only relevant methods.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between Event Source and Event Listener."* Always answer with a clear one-line distinction — **Source generates**, **Listener reacts**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Delegation Event Model — Operational Flow

The Delegation Event Model works through four structured steps:

1. **User Interaction** → The user performs an action (click, key press, mouse movement) on a GUI component.
2. **Event Generation** → The JVM detects the action and **instantiates an appropriate `Event` subclass** (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`).
3. **Event Dispatching** → The Event Source **invokes the listener's method**, passing the Event object as a parameter.
4. **Event Processing** → The listener's overridden method executes the response logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Delegation"?** The source does not handle the event itself — it *delegates* the responsibility to a listener. This decouples GUI components from event-handling logic, adhering to the **Single Responsibility Principle** of OOP.

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Event Classes (High-Yield for KTU)

The root class is `java.util.EventObject`, extended by `java.awt.AWTEvent`, which is the superclass for all AWT/Swing events.

| Event Class | Triggered By | Listener Interface |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | Button click, menu selection, Enter in text field | `ActionListener` |
| `MouseEvent` | Mouse click, press, release, enter, exit | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` |
| `KeyEvent` | Key press, release, type | `KeyListener` |
| `WindowEvent` | Window open, close, iconify, activate | `WindowListener` |
| `ItemEvent` | Checkbox/radio button state change | `ItemListener` |
| `TextEvent` | Text change in TextArea/TextField | `TextListener` |
| `FocusEvent` | Component gains/loses focus | `FocusListener` |

### 2.3 Listener Registration — The `addXxxListener()` Pattern

Every event source provides a registration method following JavaBean naming conventions:

- `addActionListener(ActionListener l)`
- `addMouseListener(MouseListener l)`
- `addKeyListener(KeyListener l)`
- `addWindowListener(WindowListener l)`

> [!TIP]
> The pattern is **always** `add<EventType>Listener`. KTU questions often test your ability to recall the *exact* registration method name for a given event type.

### 2.4 Adapter Classes — Why They Exist

Many listener interfaces (e.g., `MouseListener`, `WindowListener`, `KeyListener`) define **multiple abstract methods**. Implementing all of them just to handle one event is tedious. **Adapter classes** provide empty default implementations, so you override only the methods you need.

| Listener Interface | Methods | Corresponding Adapter |
|---|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | 5 methods | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | 2 methods | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | 3 methods | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | 7 methods | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | 2 methods | `FocusAdapter` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no `ActionAdapter`?** Because `ActionListener` has only **one** method (`actionPerformed`). Adapters are provided only when the interface has **two or more** methods.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Event-Driven Programming

- **Real-time Systems**: Industrial control panels use event listeners for sensor input.
- **Web Development**: JavaScript uses the same delegation model (`addEventListener`).
- **Embedded Systems**: Android (Java/Kotlin) builds its entire UI on the listener pattern.
- **IoT Devices**: GUI dashboards for hardware monitoring rely on event handling for user responsiveness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Code Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mechanism 1 — Implementing Listener Interface (Classical Approach)

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

**Step 1:** Define a class that **implements** the appropriate listener interface.
**Step 2:** Override the required event-handling method(s).
**Step 3:** Instantiate the class and **register** it with the source using `addXxxListener()`.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ButtonDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Button b;
    Label l;

    ButtonDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        b = new Button("Click Me");
        l = new Label("Status: Awaiting click");
        add(b);
        add(l);

        // Step 3: Register the listener with the source
        b.addActionListener(this);

        setSize(300, 150);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Step 2: Override the event handler
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        l.setText("Status: Button was clicked!");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ButtonDemo();
    }
}
```

**Analytical Trace:**

| Line / Action | Mechanism Explanation |
|---|---|
| `implements ActionListener` | Class declares ability to handle `ActionEvent` |
| `b.addActionListener(this)` | Source delegates future events to `this` object |
| `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | JVM invokes this method when click occurs |
| `l.setText(...)` | Reaction logic executes, updating the GUI |

### 3.2 Mechanism 2 — Using Adapter Classes (MouseAdapter Example)

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

**Step 1:** Create a class that **extends** an adapter class.
**Step 2:** Override only the specific method(s) you need.
**Step 3:** Register an instance with the source.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseAdapterDemo extends Frame {
    Label l;
    MouseAdapterDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        l = new Label("Move or click the mouse inside the frame");
        add(l);
        setSize(400, 200);
        setVisible(true);

        // Register an anonymous MouseAdapter — only handle click
        addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
            public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
                l.setText("Mouse clicked at (" + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY() + ")");
            }
        });
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MouseAdapterDemo();
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice we override **only `mouseClicked`**, not all 5 methods of `MouseListener`. The `MouseAdapter` provides empty defaults for the rest. This is the **primary advantage** of adapter classes — *selective method overriding*.

### 3.3 Mechanism 3 — Anonymous Inner Class (Most Common in Modern Swing)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AnonymousDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("Anonymous Inner Class Demo");
        JButton btn = new JButton("Press");
        JLabel lbl = new JLabel("Not clicked yet");

        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                lbl.setText("Clicked! Event source: " + e.getSource());
            }
        });

        frame.add(btn, "North");
        frame.add(lbl, "South");
        frame.setSize(300, 150);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

> [!TIP]
> Anonymous inner classes are the **most widely used** event-handling mechanism in modern Java Swing applications because they keep the handler logic local to where the component is created, improving code readability and locality.

### 3.4 Mechanism 4 — Lambda Expressions (Java 8+)

Since `ActionListener` has a **single abstract method (SAM)**, it qualifies as a **functional interface**. Java 8+ allows lambda replacement:

```java
btn.addActionListener(e -> lbl.setText("Lambda handled the click!"));
```

This is a one-line equivalent of the anonymous inner class — **no instantiation, no boilerplate**.

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — All Four Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Boilerplate | Readability | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Implementing Interface** | High | Moderate | When class already extends another (use interface) |
| **Adapter Class** | Medium | High | When overriding only 1-2 of many methods |
| **Anonymous Inner Class** | Medium | High | One-off handlers in single class |
| **Lambda Expression** | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Java 8+, SAM interfaces only |

### 3.6 Mathematical / Logical Proof: Why Multiple Listeners Are Allowed

> A single event source can register **N listeners**. When an event fires, the source iterates through its listener list and invokes each handler in **registration order** (FIFO).

$$
\text{Notification Order} = \{L_1, L_2, L_3, \ldots, L_n\} \quad \text{where } L_i = \text{listener registered at } i^{th} \text{ call to addXxxListener()}
$$

This proves that the delegation model is **one-to-many** (one source, many listeners), unlike the older Java 1.0 **inheritance-based model** which was one-to-one (subclassing the component to override `handleEvent()`).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Event Handling Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Action Mouse Click or Key Press] --> srcA[Event Source e.g. JButton]
    srcA --> evtA[Event Object e.g. ActionEvent]
    evtA --> regA[Registered Listeners]
    regA --> l1A[Listener 1 actionPerformed]
    regA --> l2A[Listener 2 actionPerformed]
    regA --> l3A[Listener N actionPerformed]
    l1A --> outA[GUI Update or Business Logic]
    l2A --> outA
    l3A --> outA
```

### 4.2 Listener Registration Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant S as Event Source JButton
    participant L as Event Listener ActionListener
    participant JVM as JVM Event Dispatch Thread

    U->>S: Performs click action
    S->>JVM: Detects event
    JVM->>S: Create ActionEvent object
    S->>L: invoke actionPerformed event
    L->>U: Update GUI or execute logic
```

### 4.3 Class Hierarchy — Events and Adapters

```mermaid
graph TD
    evRoot[java util EventObject] --> awtEv[java awt AWTEvent]
    awtEv --> actionEv[ActionEvent]
    awtEv --> mouseEv[MouseEvent]
    awtEv --> keyEv[KeyEvent]
    awtEv --> windowEv[WindowEvent]
    awtEv --> itemEv[ItemEvent]

    actList[ActionListener] --> btnSrc[Button Source]
    mouseList[MouseListener] --> mouseAdapter[MouseAdapter]
    keyList[KeyListener] --> keyAdapter[KeyAdapter]
    windowList[WindowListener] --> windowAdapter[WindowAdapter]
    mouseAdapter --> mouseSrc[Frame or Panel Source]
    keyAdapter --> keySrc[TextField Source]
    windowAdapter --> winSrc[Frame Source]
```

### 4.4 Mechanism Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Need Event Handling] --> q1{How many methods in listener interface?}
    q1 -- One method --> q2{Java 8 or later?}
    q1 -- Multiple methods --> adapterA[Use Adapter Class]
    q2 -- Yes --> lambdaA[Use Lambda Expression]
    q2 -- No --> anonA[Use Anonymous Inner Class]
    adapterA --> regA[Register with addXxxListener]
    anonA --> regA
    lambdaA --> regA
    regA --> doneA[Event Handling Active]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the Delegation Event Model. List its advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

The Delegation Event Model is a modern event-handling mechanism in Java's AWT and Swing where an event source **delegates** the task of handling an event to one or more registered listener objects. The source does not know how the event will be processed; it simply notifies all registered listeners.

**Advantages:**
1. **Decoupling** — Source and handler are independent, supporting modular design.
2. **Multiple Handlers** — A single source can have many listeners (one-to-many relationship).
3. **Reusability** — The same listener can be registered with multiple sources.
4. **Cleaner Code** — Avoids the legacy approach of subclassing components to override `handleEvent()`.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for advantages)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] Differentiate between Event Source and Event Listener with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Event Source | Event Listener |
|---|---|
| The GUI component that **generates** an event | The object that **reacts** to an event |
| Calls `addXxxListener()` to register handlers | Implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) |
| Example: `JButton`, `JTextField` | Example: Object implementing `MouseListener` |

**Example:** When a `JButton` (source) is clicked, it generates an `ActionEvent`. A registered `ActionListener` receives this event and executes its `actionPerformed()` method.

*(3 marks — 1 for each difference + 1 for example)*

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the components of the Java event handling mechanism with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program using `AWT` to create a frame with a text field and a button. When the button is clicked, the text from the text field should be displayed in a label. Use an **anonymous inner class** for event handling. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Components of Event Handling (7 marks)

The three core components are:

**1. Event Object:**
An object of a class derived from `java.util.EventObject`. It encapsulates the state change caused by user interaction. Common examples: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`.

**2. Event Source:**
The GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`) that **generates** events in response to user actions. Each source provides methods like `addActionListener()`, `addMouseListener()` for listener registration.

**3. Event Listener Interface:**
An interface that defines one or more methods to be implemented by the handler class. Examples: `ActionListener` (defines `actionPerformed`), `MouseListener` (defines 5 methods).

**Registration Process:**
- The listener object is created and passed to the source via `addXxxListener()`.
- When the user triggers an event, the source invokes the appropriate listener method, passing the event object as an argument.
- The listener method executes the response logic.

*(3 marks for explanation + 4 marks for diagram and registration flow)*

#### Part (b) — Java Program Using Anonymous Inner Class (7 marks)

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TextCopyDemo extends Frame {
    TextField tf;
    Label lbl;

    TextCopyDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        tf = new TextField(20);
        Button btn = new Button("Show Text");
        lbl = new Label("Text will appear here");

        add(tf);
        add(btn);
        add(lbl);

        // Anonymous inner class as ActionListener
        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                lbl.setText("You typed: " + tf.getText());
            }
        });

        setSize(350, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new TextCopyDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key lines:**

| Code | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `new ActionListener() { ... }` | Creates an anonymous class implementing `ActionListener` |
| `public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | Mandatory override of the interface's only method |
| `lbl.setText("You typed: " + tf.getText())` | Retrieves text from `TextField` and updates `Label` |
| `btn.addActionListener(...)` | Registers the anonymous listener with the button |

**Output Behavior:** When the user types into the text field and clicks "Show Text", the label updates with the prefix "You typed: " followed by the entered text.

*(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for listener implementation, 2 for output description)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is an **Adapter Class** in Java? Why is it needed? List any four adapters with their parent listener interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program using `Swing` to handle a `WindowEvent` such that clicking the close button displays a confirmation message before exiting. Use `WindowAdapter`. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Adapter Classes Explained (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **Adapter Class** is a class that provides **default empty implementations** for all methods of a listener interface that has **two or more abstract methods**. It allows developers to override only the methods relevant to their application, avoiding boilerplate code.

**Why Adapter Classes Are Needed:**

1. **Reduces Boilerplate** — Without adapters, implementing a listener with 5 methods (like `MouseListener`) requires defining 5 empty methods just to use one.
2. **Selective Overriding** — Developers can override only the methods they need.
3. **Improves Readability** — Focuses attention on relevant event handlers.
4. **Maintains Backward Compatibility** — Adding new methods to an interface doesn't break existing adapters.

**Four Common Adapter Classes:**

| Adapter Class | Listener Interface | Number of Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `MouseAdapter` | `MouseListener` | 5 |
| `MouseMotionAdapter` | `MouseMotionListener` | 2 |
| `KeyAdapter` | `KeyListener` | 3 |
| `WindowAdapter` | `WindowListener` | 7 |

*(7 marks — 2 for definition/need, 3 for the four adapters table, 2 for explanation)*

#### Part (b) — WindowAdapter for Close Confirmation (7 marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CloseConfirmDemo extends JFrame {
    CloseConfirmDemo() {
        setTitle("Close Confirmation Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE);

        // Register WindowAdapter to handle close event
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                int choice = JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(
                    CloseConfirmDemo.this,
                    "Are you sure you want to exit?",
                    "Exit Confirmation",
                    JOptionPane.YES_NO_OPTION
                );
                if (choice == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION) {
                    System.exit(0);
                }
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CloseConfirmDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key components:**

| Code Snippet | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `extends WindowAdapter` | Extends adapter to override only `windowClosing` |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE)` | Prevents auto-close so listener can intercept |
| `JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(...)` | Displays a Yes/No dialog to the user |
| `if (choice == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION)` | Exits only if user confirms |

**Output:** A 400x200 window appears. Clicking the close (X) button triggers a dialog asking "Are you sure you want to exit?" — the program exits only if "Yes" is selected.

*(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for adapter implementation, 2 for output description)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
> - **Forgetting `addXxxListener()` call** → Listener registered incorrectly = **0 marks** for execution part. Always verify the registration line is present.
> - **Confusing Adapter with Listener** — `MouseAdapter` is a *class* (use `extends`); `MouseListener` is an *interface* (use `implements`). Mixing these up loses 2-3 marks.
> - **Not overriding all interface methods when implementing directly** — Compilation error in exam programs results in **zero execution marks**.
> - **Missing `setVisible(true)`** — Program compiles but produces no visible output, often interpreted as non-functional.
> - **Using AWT imports in Swing code** — Mixing `java.awt.event.*` with `javax.swing.*` is acceptable, but using `Frame` instead of `JFrame` loses style marks.
> - **Spelling mistakes in method names** — `addActionListner`, `actionPerfomed` — such errors are heavily penalized.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Delegation Event Model** is the standard Java event handling mechanism since JDK 1.1.
- ✅ Three actors: **Event** (object), **Source** (generator), **Listener** (reactor).
- ✅ Root event class: `java.util.EventObject` → extended by `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
- ✅ Listener registration: `source.addXxxListener(listenerObject)`.
- ✅ `ActionListener` has only **1 method** → **no adapter** exists for it.
- ✅ Adapters exist for listeners with **≥ 2 methods** (e.g., `MouseAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`).
- ✅ Four mechanisms to implement event handling:
  1. Implementing listener interface directly
  2. Extending adapter class
  3. Anonymous inner class (most common)
  4. Lambda expression (Java 8+, SAM interfaces)
- ✅ One source can have **multiple listeners** (one-to-many relationship).
- ✅ The older Java 1.0 **inheritance model** (overriding `handleEvent()`) is **obsolete** — KTU questions will not ask about it, but they may ask you to **contrast** it with the delegation model.
- ✅ Key event sources: `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JCheckBox`, `JMenuItem`, `JFrame`.
- ✅ Always end GUI programs with `setVisible(true)` for the window to appear.
- ✅ `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` is the modern way to handle window closing in Swing.
- ✅ **Frequent KTU keywords to memorize**: *delegate, register, notify, fire, dispatch, handle, source, sink*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delegation Event Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is the modern, object-oriented event-handling architecture defined under Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) since JDK 1.1. In this model, an **Event Source** delegates the responsibility of processing an event to a separate object known as an **Event Listener** (or Event Handler), rather than handling the event itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim for Exam):**
> *"The Delegation Event Model is a model in which an event is generated by a source object and forwarded to a registered listener object. The source object delegates the task of handling the event to the listener, thereby decoupling event generation from event processing."*

The model is built upon **four foundational pillars**:
1. **Event Source** — the object that *produces* the event (e.g., a `Button`).
2. **Event Object** — encapsulates the *state* of the event (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Listener** — the object that *receives and processes* the event (e.g., an object implementing `ActionListener`).
4. **Event Delegation / Registration Mechanism** — the *contract* (a method like `addActionListener()`) that binds source to listener.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Newspaper Subscription System**

Imagine a **newspaper publisher** (Event Source). The publisher does not deliver newspapers to every house personally. Instead:

| Newspaper Concept | Java DEM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Newspaper Publisher (Bharat Times) | `Button` (Event Source) |
| Newspaper (one copy) | `ActionEvent` (Event Object) |
| Subscriber (Mr. Raj) | `ActionListener` (Listener Object) |
| Subscription Form | `addActionListener()` (Registration Call) |
| Stopping Subscription | `removeActionListener()` (Deregistration) |

- The publisher **only knows** that *some* subscribers exist — it does not know **who** they are or **how** they read the paper.
- The subscriber **registers** interest (implements interface) and tells the publisher (calls `addActionListener`).
- When an event (click) happens, the publisher **delegates** the event to all registered subscribers.

This separation (publisher ↔ subscriber) is the essence of the **Observer Design Pattern** — and DEM is its direct implementation in AWT/Swing.

## 1.3 Why DEM Replaced the Old Model

> [!WARNING]
> **Historical Context (Do NOT confuse in exam):**
> Before JDK 1.1, Java used the **Inheritance Event Model** where components extended `Component` and overrode methods like `handleEvent()`. This was rigid, inheritance-heavy, and violated OOP encapsulation. KTU may ask: *"Why is DEM preferred?"* — Answer: **loose coupling, multiple listeners per source, reusability, and OOP-compliance**.

**Key Metrics of DEM:**
- **Loose Coupling:** Source and listener are *independent* objects.
- **Multi-cast:** One event can be **handled by multiple listeners** simultaneously.
- **Unicast support:** A source can also be limited to a single listener (in newer versions) — but AWT allows multiple.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Structured Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Event Source (Producer)
- An object capable of generating events.
- Provides **two public methods**: `addTypeListener(...)` and `removeTypeListener(...)`.
- Maintains an **internal list** (data structure, typically a vector/array list) of registered listeners.
- Knows **nothing** about what the listener does with the event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Source Classes:** `Button`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `MenuItem`, `Scrollbar`, `List`, `Frame`.

### Pillar 2: Event Listener Interface (Contract)
- A Java **interface** that defines the methods to be called when an event occurs.
- Convention: methods end in `Performed` or `Performed/Pressed/Released/Entered/Exited` for mouse, etc.
- The listener object **must implement** this interface — it acts as a *contract*.

### Pillar 3: Event Object (The Message)
- Subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (AWT) or `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
- Carries **all information** about the event: source, coordinates, modifiers, time, etc.
- **Immutable** — once constructed, its data cannot be changed.

### Pillar 4: Event Delegation Mechanism (The Bridge)
- Achieved via the `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()` methods.
- The *registration call* is what wires source → listener.
- When event occurs, the source **iterates** through its listener list and calls the appropriate method on each.

## 2.2 Event Flow — Step-by-Step

1. **User interacts** with a GUI component (e.g., clicks a `Button`).
2. The **JVM's event dispatching thread** detects the OS-level event.
3. An **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`) is created, holding details.
4. The **Source** (Button) checks its internal listener list.
5. For every registered listener, the source **invokes the appropriate method** (e.g., `actionPerformed()`).
6. The listener's method **executes the handler logic**.

## 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — Listener Interfaces & Event Classes

| Event Class | Listener Interface | Key Method(s) | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | Button click, Menu select, TextField Enter |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked, mousePressed, mouseReleased, mouseEntered, mouseExited` | Mouse interactions |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged, mouseMoved` | Mouse movement |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped, keyPressed, keyReleased` | Keyboard input |
| `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent)` | Text change |
| `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | Checkbox, Choice, List toggle |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing, windowOpened, windowIconified, windowDeiconified, windowClosed, windowActivated, windowDeactivated` | Window lifecycle |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | Scrollbar movement |
| `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained, focusLost` | Component focus change |

## 2.4 Adapter Classes — The Convenience Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** *"Explain Adapter classes and why they are used."*

Listener interfaces often contain **multiple methods** (e.g., `MouseListener` has 5). Forcing a class to implement *all* of them when you only need one is tedious. Java provides **Adapter classes** — empty implementations of the listener interfaces.

| Listener Interface | Adapter Class |
|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` |

> **Note:** ActionListener, ItemListener, TextListener have **no adapters** because they contain only one method — no need to override.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Development:** Foundation of every desktop Java application (Swing/JavaFX borrow this model).
- **Design Pattern Use:** DEM is the canonical implementation of the **Observer Pattern** — taught in Software Engineering.
- **Event-Driven Architectures (EDA):** Modern microservices (Kafka, RabbitMQ) use the *same publish-subscribe paradigm*.
- **Embedded & IoT:** Sensor data → event source; control unit → event listener.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Generic Algorithm — How to Implement DEM (5 Steps)

These five steps are the **exact procedural answer** KTU expects when asked *"How is the Delegation Event Model implemented?"*

1. **Implement the appropriate listener interface** in the handler class (or use an adapter).
2. **Override the required event-handling method(s)** from that interface.
3. **Create an instance** of the handler class.
4. **Create an instance** of the event source component.
5. **Register the handler with the source** using `addXxxListener(handlerObject)`.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Example — Button Click Counter

Below is a fully working AWT program that demonstrates **multi-listener delegation** on a single button. Read it line-by-line.

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class DelegationDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener, WindowListener {
    // Pillar 3 (counter) and Pillar 2 (this class implements 2 listener interfaces)
    private int clickCount = 0;
    private Label lblCounter, lblStatus;
    private Button btnClick, btnReset;

    public DelegationDemo() {
        // 1. Setting up the GUI (sources)
        setTitle("Delegation Event Model Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        btnClick  = new Button("Click Me!");
        btnReset  = new Button("Reset");
        lblCounter = new Label("Clicks: 0");
        lblStatus  = new Label("Status: Waiting...");

        add(btnClick);
        add(btnReset);
        add(lblCounter);
        add(lblStatus);

        // 5. Register listeners (Pillar 4 — the bridge)
        btnClick.addActionListener(this);     // this = DelegationDemo
        btnReset.addActionListener(this);     // multi-cast: same listener on two sources
        this.addWindowListener(this);         // window close handled by self

        setVisible(true);
    }

    // 2. Override the event-handling method
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        Object src = e.getSource();   // getSource() returns the firing component

        if (src == btnClick) {
            clickCount++;
            lblCounter.setText("Clicks: " + clickCount);
            lblStatus.setText("Status: Button clicked!");
        } else if (src == btnReset) {
            clickCount = 0;
            lblCounter.setText("Clicks: 0");
            lblStatus.setText("Status: Counter reset.");
        }
    }

    // WindowListener requires 7 methods; we implement only what we need
    @Override public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e)  { System.exit(0); }
    @Override public void windowOpened(WindowEvent e)   { }
    @Override public void windowClosed(WindowEvent e)   { }
    @Override public void windowIconified(WindowEvent e){ }
    @Override public void windowDeiconified(WindowEvent e){ }
    @Override public void windowActivated(WindowEvent e){ }
    @Override public void windowDeactivated(WindowEvent e){ }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new DelegationDemo();
    }
}
```

### 3.2.1 Line-by-Line Logical Trace

| Line | What Happens | DEM Concept |
|---|---|---|
| `implements ActionListener, WindowListener` | This class signs two *contracts* | Pillar 2: Listener |
| `Button btnClick = new Button(...)` | A source is created | Pillar 1: Source |
| `btnClick.addActionListener(this)` | Listener is **registered** to source | Pillar 4: Bridge |
| User clicks button | JVM creates `ActionEvent` | Pillar 3: Event Object |
| JVM invokes `actionPerformed(e)` | Source *delegates* to listener | Delegation in action |
| `e.getSource()` | Identifies *which* button fired | Multi-listener resolution |

## 3.3 Using Adapter Classes — Cleaned-Up Version

To avoid implementing **7 empty methods** for `WindowListener`, use `WindowAdapter`:

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class AdapterDemo extends Frame {
    private Button btn;

    public AdapterDemo() {
        setTitle("Adapter Demo");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        btn = new Button("Close Window");
        add(btn);

        // Use anonymous inner class with WindowAdapter
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);   // Only the one method we care about
            }
        });

        btn.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Button clicked!"));
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) { new AdapterDemo(); }
}
```

## 3.4 Multi-Listener Registration (Fan-out)

One source can have **multiple** listeners. Example — two listeners on one button:

```java
Button b = new Button("Multi");
b.addActionListener(new Logger());     // listener 1
b.addActionListener(new Emailer());    // listener 2
b.addActionListener(new Auditor());    // listener 3
// On click → all 3 methods are called in registration order
```

## 3.5 Method Execution Order (Analytical Rule)

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Point:** When multiple listeners are registered on a single source, the **order of invocation is the order of registration** (FIFO — First In, First Out). This is a frequently asked KTU question.

$$
\text{Invocation Order} = \text{Registration Order} = \{ L_1 \rightarrow L_2 \rightarrow L_3 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow L_n \}
$$

For a single listener registered to multiple sources, the listener must use `e.getSource()` to differentiate, as shown in §3.2.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The DEM Architecture (Observer Pattern Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef event fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef listener fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iface fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    User([User clicks mouse]):::source
    SRC[Button / Event Source]:::source
    REG[/addActionListener/]:::iface
    LIST[ActionListener Object]:::listener
    EVT[ActionEvent Object]:::event
    MTH>actionPerformed Method]:::listener
    REACT([Business Logic Runs]):::listener

    User -- "1. Triggers OS Event" --> SRC
    SRC -- "2. Creates" --> EVT
    EVT -- "3. Delegates to" --> LIST
    LIST -- "4. Invokes" --> MTH
    MTH -- "5. Executes" --> REACT
    REG -. "0. Registration (one-time)" .-> LIST
    LIST -. "registered with" .-> SRC
```

## 4.2 Listener-Interface Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef iface fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef adp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    EVT[java.util.EventObject]:::root
    AWTEVT[java.awt.AWTEvent]:::root
    EVT --> AWTEVT

    AWTEVT --> ACE[ActionEvent]
    AWTEVT --> ME[MouseEvent]
    AWTEVT --> KE[KeyEvent]
    AWTEVT --> WE[WindowEvent]
    AWTEVT --> TE[TextEvent]
    AWTEVT --> IE[ItemEvent]
    AWTEVT --> FE[FocusEvent]
    AWTEVT --> AE[AdjustmentEvent]

    ACE -.implemented by.-> AL[ActionListener]:::iface
    ME -.implemented by.-> ML[MouseListener]:::iface
    ME -.implemented by.-> MML[MouseMotionListener]:::iface
    KE -.implemented by.-> KL[KeyListener]:::iface
    WE -.implemented by.-> WL[WindowListener]:::iface
    TE -.implemented by.-> TL[TextListener]:::iface
    IE -.implemented by.-> IL[ItemListener]:::iface
    FE -.implemented by.-> FL[FocusListener]:::iface
    AE -.implemented by.-> AJL[AdjustmentListener]:::iface

    ML -.default impl.-> MA[MouseAdapter]:::adp
    MML -.default impl.-> MMA[MouseMotionAdapter]:::adp
    KL -.default impl.-> KA[KeyAdapter]:::adp
    WL -.default impl.-> WA[WindowAdapter]:::adp
    FL -.default impl.-> FA[FocusAdapter]:::adp
```

## 4.3 Event Flow Sequence (Temporal View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor U as User
    participant SRC as Button (Source)
    participant JVM as JVM Event Dispatcher
    participant EVT as ActionEvent (Object)
    participant LIS as MyListener (Handler)
    participant LOG as Business Logic

    U->>SRC: Click
    SRC->>JVM: Fire event signal
    JVM->>EVT: new ActionEvent(source, id, command)
    JVM->>SRC: getListeners()
    SRC-->>JVM: [MyListener]
    JVM->>LIS: actionPerformed(evt)
    LIS->>EVT: evt.getSource()
    EVT-->>LIS: Button reference
    LIS->>LOG: execute handler code
    LOG-->>U: Visual update / response
```

## 4.4 Memory Model — Listener Registration Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef store fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef l1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef l2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef l3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000

    BTN[Button btn]:::store
    VEC[Internal Vector: listenerList]:::store
    BTN --> Vec[VEC]
    Vec --> L1[Listener 1: Logger]:::l1
    Vec --> L2[Listener 2: Emailer]:::l2
    Vec --> L3[Listener 3: Auditor]:::l3

    note["On click → iterate vector → invoke all 3"]:::store
    VEC -.-> note
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway for Board:** The source internally maintains a **list data structure** (vector) of registered listeners. The source iterates this list on event firing — this is why **multi-listener support is possible**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Delegation Event Model. List its key components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Delegation Event Model is an event-handling architecture in Java AWT where an event generated by a **source** is delegated to a registered **listener** object, which processes it. Its key components are: **(i) Event Source** — the object generating the event, **(ii) Event Object** — encapsulates event details, **(iii) Event Listener** — implements the listener interface and handles the event, and **(iv) Event Delegation Mechanism** — registration/deregistration methods like `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()`. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing components.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between a listener interface and an adapter class? Give an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **listener interface** is a contract that *declares* the methods which must be implemented by a class that wants to handle a particular type of event (e.g., `MouseListener` declares 5 methods). An **adapter class** is a class that *provides empty default implementations* of a listener interface, so that subclasses can override only the methods they need. Example: `MouseAdapter` implements `MouseListener` with empty bodies, and we override only `mouseClicked()` if needed. *(2 marks for difference, 1 mark for example.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four components of the Delegation Event Model with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java AWT program using the Delegation Event Model that creates a `TextField`, a `Button`, and a `Label`. When the button is clicked, the text from the TextField should be appended to the Label. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)

**The four components of the Delegation Event Model:**

1. **Event Source (1.5 marks):**
   - The object that **generates** the event (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`).
   - Provides public methods `addTypeListener()` and `removeTypeListener()` for registration.
   - Maintains an internal list of registered listeners.
   - Knows nothing about how the listener processes the event.

2. **Event Listener (1.5 marks):**
   - The object that **receives and handles** the event.
   - Must implement the appropriate listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`).
   - Provides the actual business logic inside the event-handling method.

3. **Event Object (1.5 marks):**
   - Encapsulates the **state and information** of the event.
   - Subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (AWT) / `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
   - Carries data like source, coordinates, modifiers, event ID.
   - Passed as argument to the listener's method.

4. **Event Delegation Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
   - The **bridge** that connects source to listener.
   - Implemented via `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()`.
   - Without it, the source has no way to notify the listener.
   - When event fires, source iterates the listener list and invokes the handler method on each.

**Diagram (1 mark — already shown in §4.1 of these notes).**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TextAppendDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    private TextField tfInput;
    private Button btnAppend;
    private Label lblOutput;

    public TextAppendDemo() {
        setTitle("Text Append Demo");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        tfInput   = new TextField(20);
        btnAppend = new Button("Append");
        lblOutput = new Label("Output: ");

        add(new Label("Enter text:"));
        add(tfInput);
        add(btnAppend);
        add(lblOutput);

        // Register listener (DEM bridge)
        btnAppend.addActionListener(this);

        // Use adapter to handle window close
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.exit(0); }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String input = tfInput.getText();
        String current = lblOutput.getText();
        lblOutput.setText(current + input + " ");
        tfInput.setText("");  // clear input
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new TextAppendDemo();
    }
}
```

**Mark allocation (7 marks):**
- Imports + Class declaration extending Frame: **1 mark**
- GUI components creation (TextField, Button, Label): **1 mark**
- Layout setup + adding components: **1 mark**
- `addActionListener(this)` registration (the DEM bridge): **1 mark**
- `actionPerformed()` method overriding with logic: **2 marks**
- Window close using adapter + main method: **1 mark**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the Delegation Event Model with the Inheritance-based Event Model. Why is DEM preferred in modern Java programming? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the use of `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`, and `KeyAdapter` with suitable Java code snippets. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)

| Aspect | Inheritance Event Model (Old, JDK 1.0) | Delegation Event Model (Modern, JDK 1.1+) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Subclass the component class and override `handleEvent()` | Implement a listener interface and register via `addXxxListener()` |
| **Coupling** | Tight coupling — handler must be a subclass of the source | Loose coupling — handler is an independent object |
| **Multiple Handlers** | Not possible; single class inherits component | Supported — multiple listeners can be registered on one source |
| **Code Reusability** | Low — every event needs a subclass | High — handler classes are reusable across components |
| **OOP Compliance** | Violates encapsulation (exposes component internals) | Fully object-oriented, follows Observer pattern |
| **Performance** | Heavier (inheritance chain) | Lighter (interface implementation) |
| **Extensibility** | Difficult to extend | Easy — just add new listeners |

**Why DEM is preferred (3 marks distributed across key points):**
1. Promotes **loose coupling** between source and handler.
2. Supports **multiple listeners** on a single source (multi-cast).
3. Reuses handler logic across different components.
4. Aligns with the **Observer design pattern**.
5. **Encapsulation** is preserved — the source's internals are hidden.
6. Easier to maintain and extend in large-scale applications.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):** Adapter classes provide **empty default implementations** of multi-method listener interfaces, allowing the programmer to override only the methods of interest. This avoids boilerplate code.

**WindowAdapter Snippet (2 marks):**
```java
frame.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
    @Override
    public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
        System.exit(0);   // Only method we care about
    }
});
```

**MouseAdapter Snippet (1.5 marks):**
```java
canvas.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
    @Override
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked at " + e.getX() + "," + e.getY());
    }
});
```

**KeyAdapter Snippet (1.5 marks):**
```java
textField.addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() {
    @Override
    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        if (!Character.isDigit(e.getKeyChar())) {
            e.consume();   // Block non-numeric input
        }
    }
});
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing interface with class** — `ActionListener` is an *interface*, not a class. Don't say "we create an object of ActionListener."
> 2. **Forgetting the bridge** — Always mention `addXxxListener()`. Many students describe the model but forget the registration step.
> 3. **Confusing `getSource()` with `getID()`** — `getSource()` returns the *object* that fired the event; `getID()` returns the *integer event type*.
> 4. **Mixing up Adapter and Listener** — Adapters are *classes* (used to override only some methods). Listeners are *interfaces* (must implement all methods).
> 5. **Not knowing why ActionListener has no Adapter** — Because it has only one method, no need to provide empty defaults.
> 6. **Order of method calls** — When a button has two listeners, they're called in **registration order**, not in random order. Lost 1 mark last year for this.
> 7. **Saying "Event is delegated to source"** — Reverse! The *source delegates to the listener*. This is the #1 most common mistake in KTU papers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

- **DEM = Source + Event + Listener + Registration.** Mnemonic: **"S-E-L-R"**.
- **Source** is what *fires* the event; **Listener** is what *handles* the event. Never swap these in your answer.
- Registration is **one-time**; delegation happens **on every event**.
- **Multi-listener support** = multiple listeners registered to one source → invoked in **FIFO** order.
- **One listener, many sources** → use `e.getSource()` to identify which source fired.
- **Listener interfaces** (8 main ones): `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, `FocusListener`, `AdjustmentListener`.
- **Adapter classes** exist for multi-method listeners (`MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `MouseMotionAdapter`).
- **No adapter** for `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener` — only one method.
- **All event classes** ultimately extend `java.util.EventObject`.
- **DEM is the Observer Pattern** in disguise — write this in the exam if asked to relate.
- **REMOVAL of listener** uses `removeXxxListener()` — can be asked as a trick question.
- **Modular and loosely coupled** = key phrases for "Why DEM?" question.
- **Inheritance Event Model (old)** used `handleEvent()` — phased out in JDK 1.1; do **not** mention this unless specifically asked.
- **Exception inside a listener** does not stop other listeners from being called — each listener is invoked in a try-catch loop internally by the JVM.
- **Anonymous inner classes** are commonly used to implement listener interfaces inline for one-off handlers.
- **Lambda expressions** (Java 8+) work for **functional interfaces** (single-method listeners like `ActionListener`).
- **Memory leak warning:** Forgetting to remove listeners from long-lived sources (e.g., a singleton service) can cause **memory leaks** in long-running applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for the Board Exam:** *"A **Source** **Adds** a **Listener** via **addXxxListener** → user fires an event → Source **delegates** to Listener's handler method → Listener uses **getSource()** if shared."* Memorize this sentence verbatim and you'll answer 80% of DEM questions correctly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Classes
Sources of Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources of Events

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the Java AWT/Swing **Delegation Event Model**, a **Source of an Event** is any Java object that **generates (fires)** an event in response to some internal state change or external user interaction such as a mouse click, key press, button selection, or window resizing.

Technically, an event source is an instance of a class that:
1. **Registers** one or more event listeners through registration methods of the form `add<EventType>Listener(...)`.
2. **Maintains** a collection of registered listeners (an internal `EventListener` vector).
3. **Fires** the event by invoking the listener's callback method using a `fire<EventType>()` or `process<EventType>Event()` method.

Every event source in Java ultimately extends `java.awt.Component` (for UI components) or `java.awt.MenuComponent`, both of which inherit event-firing capability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The topic "Sources of Events" sits inside the broader module on **Event Handling** and **Delegation Event Model**. You must be able to **identify the matching event class** for every AWT component and **write registration code** for it. This is a guaranteed **3-mark short-answer** area.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Doorbell System

Imagine a **doorbell** outside your house:

| Doorbell Concept | Java AWT Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The **doorbell button** itself | The **Event Source** (e.g., `Button`) |
| The **press of the button** | The **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`) |
| The **chime unit inside the house** | The **Event Listener** (e.g., `ActionListener`) |
| The **wiring connecting button to chime** | The **Registration call** (`addActionListener()`) |
| The **chime ringing** | The **Callback method execution** (`actionPerformed()`) |

The doorbell **does not decide what to do** when pressed. It only **notifies** whatever chime is connected. Likewise, in Java, an event source **does not know how to handle** the event — it merely **delegates** it to a registered listener. The source is the *producer*; the listener is the *consumer*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mental Model Lock-In:** Event Source = Producer. Event Listener = Consumer. Event Object = Parcel. The producer never opens the parcel; it just hands it over to whoever is subscribed.

---

### 1.3 The Root Class — `java.util.EventObject`

All events in Java descend from a single root class `EventObject`:

$$\text{EventObject} \rightarrow \text{AWTEvent} \rightarrow \{\text{ActionEvent, MouseEvent, KeyEvent, ...}\}$$

The **two foundational methods** of `EventObject` (inherited by every event class) are:

- `Object getSource()` — returns the object that **fired** the event.
- `String toString()` — returns a textual representation of the event.

> [!TIP]
> If your examiner asks **"Name the superclass of all events in Java"**, the correct answer is `java.util.EventObject`. A common pitfall is to wrongly answer `java.awt.AWTEvent` (that is only the AWT-specific sub-branch).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Event Source — Three Logical Layers

Every Java AWT event source obeys a **three-layer contract**:

**Layer 1 — Registration Surface**
The class exposes public methods named `addXxxListener(XxxListener l)` and `removeXxxListener(XxxListener l)`. These methods internally store/remove the listener object from a private vector.

**Layer 2 — State-Change Detection**
The class monitors itself for state transitions (button press, mouse movement, key release, window close). The JDK's native peer code (in `java.awt.peer`) translates raw OS events into Java events.

**Layer 3 — Multicasting the Event**
When a state change is detected, the source iterates through its listener vector and invokes the appropriate callback on each listener, passing a freshly constructed `Event` subclass object.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Firing Sequence

When a user interacts with an AWT component, the following ordered chain occurs:

1. The **operating system** detects a hardware input (mouse click, keystroke).
2. The **native peer** (`java.awt.peer.ComponentPeer`) translates this into a Java-level event using a method like `handleEvent(AWTEvent e)`.
3. The **component** (event source) constructs an appropriate event subclass object, e.g., `new ActionEvent(this, ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED, "OK")`.
4. The component calls its internal `processActionEvent(e)` which checks **whether event-handling is enabled** (a `boolean eventMask` bit).
5. If enabled, the component **iterates over its listener array** and calls `listener.actionPerformed(e)` on every registered `ActionListener`.
6. If no listener is registered and event-handling is disabled, the event propagates up the container chain (legacy `handleEvent()` flow).

---

### 2.3 Major Event Sources and Their Associated Event Classes

The table below is a **high-yield KTU cheat sheet** — memorizing these pairings will directly answer the majority of exam questions on this topic.

| Source Component | Event(s) Generated | Listener Interface | Callback Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Button` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| `TextField` | `ActionEvent`, `TextEvent` | `ActionListener`, `TextListener` | `actionPerformed`, `textValueChanged` |
| `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` |
| `CheckboxMenuItem` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` |
| `Choice` | `ItemEvent`, `ActionEvent` | `ItemListener`, `ActionListener` | as above |
| `List` | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent` | `ActionListener`, `ItemListener` | as above |
| `MenuItem` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed` |
| `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` |
| `Window`, `Frame`, `Dialog` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | 7 methods (opened, closing, closed, activated, deactivated, iconified, deiconified) |
| `Component` (general) | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | pressed, released, clicked, entered, exited, moved, dragged |
| `Component` (general) | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | keyPressed, keyReleased, keyTyped |
| `Component` (general) | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | focusGained, focusLost |
| `Container` | `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | componentAdded, componentRemoved |

---

### 2.4 Event Enabling and Masks

A subtle but exam-relevant concept: event generation must be **explicitly enabled** for certain components. For example, to receive `MouseMotionEvent`s on a component, you must enable them via:

$$\texttt{enableEvents(AWTEvent.MOUSE\_MOTION\_EVENT\_MASK \mid AWTEvent.MOUSE\_EVENT\_MASK)}$$

The **Event Mask** is a long integer constant (e.g., `AWTEvent.ACTION_EVENT_MASK`) that registers interest at the AWT toolkit level. If you override `processMouseMotionEvent()` instead of using a listener, you must call `enableEvents()` first; otherwise the events are silently discarded.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When students implement `processMouseEvent()` or `processKeyEvent()` overriding the `Component` class directly (legacy model), they often **forget** to call `enableEvents(...)`. This is one of the most common 1-mark deductions in lab viva.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

The Event Source abstraction is the **backbone of every modern GUI framework**, not just Java:
- **Java Swing** inherits the entire AWT event-source pattern but enriches it with `javax.swing.event.*` listeners.
- **JavaScript DOM** uses an almost identical pattern (`addEventListener`, `dispatchEvent`).
- **Android** uses `OnClickListener`, `OnTouchListener`, etc., directly inspired by Java's AWT.
- **Industrial SCADA HMI software** written in Java uses these event sources to wire sensor data to dashboards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Source to Event Mapping

The following matrix is the **complete mapping** you must internalize. It is derived by examining the JDK source `java.awt.Component` and its subclasses.

**Derivation Logic for the matrix:**

- Every concrete AWT class is examined for its `processXxxEvent()` and `addXxxListener()` methods.
- Each unique listener interface declared in those methods implies one event class.
- Each unique callback method on that listener interface corresponds to one logical event transition.

The result is a **one-to-many** relationship: a single source (e.g., `Choice`) can be a source for multiple event types (`ItemEvent` and `ActionEvent`), because user interaction with a drop-down can be either "selection changed" (item event) or "double-clicked/Enter pressed" (action event).

---

### 3.2 Walkthrough of the EventObject Class Hierarchy

Let us trace the inheritance of `ActionEvent` as a worked example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{java.lang.Object} \\
\downarrow \quad \text{(extends)} \\
\text{java.util.EventObject} \\
\downarrow \quad \text{(extends)} \\
\text{java.awt.AWTEvent} \\
\downarrow \quad \text{(extends)} \\
\text{java.awt.event.ActionEvent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — `Object`:** Provides `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `getClass()`.

**Step 2 — `EventObject`:** Adds the **`source` field** and the **`getSource()`** accessor. The constructor signature is `EventObject(Object source)`. Any subclass must call `super(source)` to record who fired the event.

**Step 3 — `AWTEvent`:** Adds an **`id`** field (an `int` event-type identifier, e.g., `ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001`) and a **`consumed` boolean** flag. The constructor is `AWTEvent(Object source, int id)`.

**Step 4 — `ActionEvent`:** Adds **command name** (`String`), **modifiers** (`int`, e.g., `SHIFT_MASK`, `CTRL_MASK`), and **timestamp** (`long`). Provides the four public constants: `ACTION_PERFORMED`, `ACTION_FIRST`, `ACTION_LAST`, `SHIFT_MASK`, `CTRL_MASK`, `META_MASK`, `ALT_MASK`.

---

### 3.3 Code-Level Derivation — Generating an Event from a Source

Suppose we have a button source. The exact flow when the user clicks it is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{OS dispatches click to native peer} \\
&\rightarrow \texttt{peer.handleEvent(new AWTEvent(source, MouseEvent.MOUSE\_PRESSED))} \\
&\rightarrow \texttt{Component.processMouseEvent(MouseEvent e)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{If } \texttt{mouseListener} \neq \texttt{null} \text{, invoke } \texttt{listener.mousePressed(e)} \\
&\text{User releases button} \\
&\rightarrow \texttt{Component.processMouseEvent(MouseEvent e) for MOUSE\_RELEASED} \\
&\rightarrow \text{If MOUSE\_CLICKED, fires } \texttt{ActionEvent} \text{ via } \texttt{processActionEvent()}
\end{aligned}
$$

The translation from a single button click into **three logical events** (`MOUSE_PRESSED`, `MOUSE_RELEASED`, `MOUSE_CLICKED` plus `ACTION_PERFORMED`) is automatic — the source itself encapsulates the orchestration.

---

### 3.4 Verification with a Sample Program Skeleton

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SourceDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Button b1, b2;

    SourceDemo() {
        b1 = new Button("OK");
        b2 = new Button("Cancel");
        // Source b1 registers listener (this) for ActionEvent
        b1.addActionListener(this);
        b2.addActionListener(this);
        add(b1); add(b2);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // getSource() returns the actual firing object
        if (e.getSource() == b1)
            setTitle("OK Pressed");
        else
            setTitle("Cancel Pressed");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SourceDemo();
    }
}
```

In the above code:
- **Sources** = `b1`, `b2`, and the `SourceDemo` frame (which is itself a source for `WindowEvent`).
- **Listeners** = the current object `this` implementing `ActionListener`.
