- **Event Object** = the `ActionEvent` instance delivered to `actionPerformed()`.
- The `e.getSource()` call lets the listener **identify which source fired** — this is critical when one listener handles multiple sources.

---

### 3.5 Multi-Source, Single-Listener Pattern

A **very common KTU question pattern** is: *"How does a single listener handle multiple event sources?"*

The mathematical relationship is:

$$
\text{For each source } s_i \text{ in registered set } S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}, \\
\text{listener.callback}(e) \text{ is invoked once, where } e.getSource() = s_i.
$$

The total listener invocations per user action = **n**, where n = number of registered listeners (typical case n=1, but multi-cast is supported by the vector-based internal storage).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Event Source Class Hierarchy (AWT Component Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    objRoot["java lang Object"]
    cmpRoot["java awt Component"]
    contRoot["java awt Container"]
    menuRoot["java awt MenuComponent"]
    btnNode["java awt Button"]
    txtNode["java awt TextField"]
    chkNode["java awt Checkbox"]
    lstNode["java awt List"]
    choNode["java awt Choice"]
    scrlNode["java awt Scrollbar"]
    frmNode["java awt Frame"]
    winNode["java awt Window"]
    dlgNode["java awt Dialog"]
    mItemNode["java awt MenuItem"]
    mChkNode["java awt CheckboxMenuItem"]

    objRoot --> cmpRoot
    objRoot --> menuRoot
    cmpRoot --> contRoot
    contRoot --> winNode
    contRoot --> scrlNode
    winNode --> frmNode
    winNode --> dlgNode
    contRoot --> btnNode
    contRoot --> txtNode
    contRoot --> chkNode
    contRoot --> lstNode
    contRoot --> choNode
    menuRoot --> mItemNode
    mItemNode --> mChkNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every node labelled under `Component` and `MenuComponent` is a potential **event source**. The deeper the node, the more event types it can fire (because inheritance aggregates all parent class's listeners).

---

### 4.2 Event Delegation Model — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    userInput["USER INPUT HARDWARE EVENT"]
    osLayer["OPERATING SYSTEM"]
    peerLayer["JAVA NATIVE PEER LAYER"]
    srcNode["EVENT SOURCE COMPONENT BUTTON TEXTFIELD WINDOW"]
    regBlock["LISTENER REGISTRATION VECTOR"]
    evtObj["EVENT OBJECT INSTANCE"]
    listenerNode["EVENT LISTENER INTERFACE"]
    callbackNode["CALLBACK METHOD ACTION PERFORMED"]

    userInput --> osLayer
    osLayer --> peerLayer
    peerLayer --> srcNode
    srcNode --> regBlock
    srcNode --> evtObj
    regBlock --> listenerNode
    evtObj --> listenerNode
    listenerNode --> callbackNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The arrows trace the *flow of control*. The `Event Object` arrow goes from source to listener, but the `Listener Registration` arrow goes from user code back to the source. This bidirectional wiring is the essence of the **Delegation Event Model**.

---

### 4.3 Source-to-Listener Wiring Sequence (UML-Style)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Step 1 User clicks button b1"]
    startB["Step 2 b1 constructs ActionEvent e"]
    startC["Step 3 b1 iterates listener list"]
    startD["Step 4 Calls listener actionPerformed e"]
    startE["Step 5 Listener reads e getSource and e getActionCommand"]
    startF["Step 6 Returns to AWT event dispatch thread"]

    startA --> startB --> startC --> startD --> startE --> startF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 6 is often misunderstood.** After the callback returns, control goes back to the **AWT Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**, not to your `main()` method. All Swing/AWT event handling should therefore be **short and non-blocking** to keep the GUI responsive.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define an event source. List any four AWT components that can act as event sources.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **event source** is an object that generates (fires) events in response to user interaction or internal state change. In Java's Delegation Event Model, an event source is any instance of a class that inherits from `java.awt.Component` or `java.awt.MenuComponent`, registers listeners via `addXxxListener()` methods, and invokes their callbacks when the corresponding event occurs.

Four examples of event sources:
1. `Button` — fires `ActionEvent`
2. `TextField` — fires `ActionEvent` and `TextEvent`
3. `Checkbox` — fires `ItemEvent`
4. `Frame` — fires `WindowEvent`

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four components with event types.)*

---

**Q2. What is the role of the `getSource()` method in an event object? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `getSource()` method, inherited by every event class from `java.util.EventObject`, returns a reference to the **object that fired the event**.

**Example:** When the same `ActionListener` is registered with two buttons `b1` and `b2`, the listener can use the test `e.getSource() == b1` to determine which button was clicked and react accordingly.

```java
public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
    if (e.getSource() == b1)
        setBackground(Color.RED);
    else
        setBackground(Color.BLUE);
}
```

*(1 mark for the role, 2 marks for the example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the Delegation Event Model of Java AWT with a neat diagram. List the three primary participants and their responsibilities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java AWT program that creates a `Frame` containing a `TextField`, a `Button` labelled "Submit", and a `Label`. When the user types a name and clicks Submit, the Label should display "Hello, <name>". Identify the event source, event class, and listener interface used. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution A**

**(a) Delegation Event Model Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Delegation Event Model** is Java's standard mechanism for handling GUI events. It is "delegation-based" because the **event source delegates** the task of handling the event to a separate **listener object** rather than processing it itself.

The **three primary participants** are:

1. **Event Source** — The component that generates the event (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`). It exposes `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()` methods for registration and maintains an internal list of registered listeners. Examples: `Button`, `Checkbox`, `Window`.

2. **Event Object** — An instance of a class extending `java.util.EventObject` that encapsulates all information about the event. Examples: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`. It carries data such as the source reference, event ID, timestamp, and modifiers.

3. **Event Listener** — An object implementing a specific listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`). The source invokes the listener's callback method when the event fires. The listener contains the actual response logic.

**Working flow:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Event Source"] -- addXxxListener --> lst["Event Listener"]
    src -- invokes callback --> lst
    lst -- registers back --> src
```

**Advantages of the DEM (mention any 3):**
- Separation of concerns (UI vs logic).
- Easier code maintenance and reuse.
- Support for multiple listeners per event type.
- Compatibility with multi-cast event propagation.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for three participants, 2 marks for responsibilities, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for advantages.)*

---

**(b) AWT Program Solution (7 marks):**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class HelloFrame extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    TextField tf;
    Label lbl;
    Button btn;

    HelloFrame() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        tf = new TextField(20);
        btn = new Button("Submit");
        lbl = new Label("Hello, Stranger");
        
        add(tf);
        add(btn);
        add(lbl);
        
        // Source registers the listener
        btn.addActionListener(this);
        tf.addActionListener(this);   // also handle Enter key
        
        setSize(350, 150);
        setTitle("Greeting App");
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = tf.getText();
        lbl.setText("Hello, " + name);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new HelloFrame();
    }
}
```

**Identification:**
- **Event Source:** `Button btn` and `TextField tf`
- **Event Class:** `java.awt.event.ActionEvent`
- **Listener Interface:** `java.awt.event.ActionListener`
- **Callback Method:** `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`

*(Distribution: 3 marks for full working code, 2 marks for source/event/listener identification, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for output behaviour.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Differentiate between event sources and event listeners in Java AWT. Describe in detail the `java.util.EventObject` and `java.awt.AWTEvent` classes, including any three of their subclasses with their corresponding listener interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java AWT program to demonstrate that a single `ActionListener` can handle events from multiple event sources. Use at least three buttons, each performing a distinct background colour change on the Frame. Explain how `getSource()` is used to differentiate. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution B**

**(a) Differentiation Table and Class Hierarchy (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Event Source | Event Listener |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Object that fires events | Object that responds to events |
| Role | Producer | Consumer / Handler |
| Methods | `addXxxListener()`, `removeXxxListener()`, `processXxxEvent()` | Callback methods like `actionPerformed()` |
| Cardinality | Can have many listeners | Can listen to many sources |
| Class origin | Subclass of `Component` or `MenuComponent` | Implements a listener interface like `ActionListener` |
| Knows the listener? | Yes (stores in internal vector) | Yes (passed via callback argument) |

**Description of `java.util.EventObject`:**
- Located in `java.util` package, this is the **root class of all events**.
- Has a single instance field `Object source`.
- Constructor: `EventObject(Object source)`.
- Key method: `Object getSource()` returns the firing object.
- Inherited by `java.awt.AWTEvent`.

**Description of `java.awt.AWTEvent`:**
- Subclass of `EventObject`, root of all AWT-specific events.
- Adds `int id` field and a `consumed` boolean.
- Contains 28+ static int constants as event-type masks (e.g., `MOUSE_EVENT_MASK`, `KEY_EVENT_MASK`).
- Provides `setSource()`, `setID()`, `consume()`, `isConsumed()`.

**Three subclasses with their listener interfaces:**

| Event Subclass | Listener Interface | Callback Method |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing`, `windowOpened`, `windowClosed`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified` |

*(Distribution: 2 marks for differentiation table, 2 marks for EventObject/AWTEvent description, 3 marks for subclasses.)*

---

**(b) Multi-Source Listener Program (7 marks):**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ColorChanger extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Button bRed, bGreen, bBlue;

    ColorChanger() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        bRed   = new Button("RED");
        bGreen = new Button("GREEN");
        bBlue  = new Button("BLUE");

        // Single listener (this) registered with THREE sources
        bRed.addActionListener(this);
        bGreen.addActionListener(this);
        bBlue.addActionListener(this);

        add(bRed);
        add(bGreen);
        add(bBlue);

        setSize(400, 200);
        setTitle("Multi-Source Demo");
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Use getSource() to identify the firing button
        if (e.getSource() == bRed)
            setBackground(Color.RED);
        else if (e.getSource() == bGreen)
            setBackground(Color.GREEN);
        else if (e.getSource() == bBlue)
            setBackground(Color.BLUE);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ColorChanger();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of `getSource()` usage:**

When the user clicks any of the three buttons, the JVM constructs an `ActionEvent` object whose internal `source` field references the **specific button** that was clicked. The `actionPerformed()` method receives this event object `e`, and by calling `e.getSource()`, the listener obtains the exact button reference. A chain of `if-else` (or a `switch` in Java 14+) compares this reference with each known button and applies the corresponding background colour.

This demonstrates the **single-listener-multiple-source** pattern, which is the canonical style used in production Java GUIs to keep listener logic centralized and DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself).

*(Distribution: 4 marks for code, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for the `getSource()` discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `getSource()` with `getActionCommand()`** — `getSource()` returns the *object reference*, while `getActionCommand()` returns the *string label*. They are often not equal when buttons share labels or use `setActionCommand()`.
> 2. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** — The frame never appears and students get zero for output-based questions.
> 3. **Not overriding all 7 methods of `WindowListener`** — When extending `WindowAdapter` instead, students often forget it is an *adapter class* and write `implements WindowListener` directly, leading to compile errors.
> 4. **Mixing up `KeyEvent` constants** — `KEY_PRESSED`, `KEY_RELEASED`, and `KEY_TYPED` are distinct events; using `getKeyChar()` on `KEY_PRESSED` works but using it on `KEY_RELEASED` returns `CHAR_UNDEFINED`.
> 5. **Writing `e.getSource() == "Red"`** — Comparing an `Object` to a `String` literal returns `false` always. Use `.equals()` or compare to button references.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- An **event source** is any AWT component that **generates events** in response to user interaction or state change.
- The root of all event classes is `java.util.EventObject`; AWT events extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
- The two essential methods on every event are **`getSource()`** (returns the firing object) and **`getID()`** (returns the event-type integer).
- Sources register listeners via **`addXxxListener()`** and can unregister via **`removeXxxListener()`**.
- The three primary participants of the **Delegation Event Model** are: **Source**, **Event Object**, and **Listener**.
- **Button** → `ActionEvent` → `ActionListener` → `actionPerformed()`
- **TextField** → `ActionEvent` / `TextEvent` → `ActionListener` / `TextListener`
- **Checkbox** / **Choice** / **List** → `ItemEvent` → `ItemListener` → `itemStateChanged()`
- **Scrollbar** → `AdjustmentEvent` → `AdjustmentListener`
- **Window / Frame / Dialog** → `WindowEvent` → `WindowListener` (7 callback methods)
- **Any Component** can fire `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `FocusEvent` using the corresponding `*Listener` interface.
- A **single listener can serve multiple sources**; differentiate them using `e.getSource()`.
- When overriding `processXxxEvent()` directly, you **must call** `enableEvents(MASK)` first, otherwise events are silently dropped.
- After a callback completes, control returns to the **AWT Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** — keep callbacks short and non-blocking.
- **Adapter classes** (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`) provide empty default implementations of listener interfaces, allowing selective overriding when you do not need all methods.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Listener Interfaces. Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Event Listener Interfaces. Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Event Listener Interfaces (Delegation Event Model) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** An *Event Listener Interface* in Java (package `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event`) defines one or more abstract methods that a class must implement to receive and react to specific events generated by an Event Source (e.g., a button, mouse, or keyboard). The source *delegates* the event-handling responsibility to the registered listener — this is the heart of the **Delegation Event Model**. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of an **Event Listener** like a **WhatsApp notification subscriber**: - The *Event Source* (a YouTube channel) generates "events" (new videos). - You "register" yourself as a *Listener* by hitting the **Subscribe** button (`addActionListener(...)`). - Whenever an event happens, the source doesn't do anything itself — it **delegates** the message to all subscribed listeners, and each listener decides how to react (open the video, share it, or ignore it). - If you don't subscribe (don't implement the interface), you simply don't get notified. > [!NOTE] > **Core Components of the Delegation Event Model (3 pillars):** > 1. **Event Object** — encapsulates the state change (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`). > 2. **Event Source** — the object that *generates* events (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`). > 3. **Event Listener** — the object that *receives and processes* events (implements a listener interface). ### Common Event Listener Interfaces (KTU High-Yield) | Listener Interface | Key Method(s) | Event Source Examples | |---|---|---| | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | `JButton`, `JMenuItem` | | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | Any component | | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` | Any component | | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton` | | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing`, `windowOpened`, ... | `JFrame`, `JDialog` | | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` | `JTextField` | --- ## 1.2 Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) — Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)* is a **Java API** (package `java.sql` and `javax.sql`) that enables Java applications to interact with relational databases in a **database-independent** manner. It provides a standard set of interfaces and classes (`DriverManager`, `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`) through which SQL statements can be sent to any DBMS (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, etc.). ### Conceptual Analogy JDBC is like a **universal power adapter** when you travel abroad: - Your laptop (Java program) is made for one type of socket (say, Type-C). - Every country has different wall sockets (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, etc.). - Without an adapter, you'd need a different laptop in every country. With a **JDBC driver** (the adapter), your Java program can plug into *any* database without changing the code logic. ### Two-Part JDBC Architecture (KTU Syllabus Highlight) 1. **JDBC API** — the programmer-facing interfaces (`DriverManager`, `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet`) — part of JDK. 2. **JDBC Driver** — the vendor-specific implementation (e.g., `com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver`) — must be added to the project as a JAR file. > [!NOTE] > **Golden Rule:** JDBC follows the **Bridge design pattern** and supports the **Factory Method** pattern via `DriverManager.getConnection()` — a guaranteed KTU 2-mark question. ### The 5 Standard JDBC Steps (Mnemonic: **D C S R C**) 1. **D** — Load/register the Driver (`Class.forName(...)`) 2. **C** — Establish Connection (`DriverManager.getConnection(...)`) 3. **S** — Create Statement (`Connection.createStatement()`) 4. **R** — Execute Query & get ResultSet (`executeQuery(...)`) 5. **C** — Close the connection (`con.close()`) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Event Listener Interfaces — Operational Breakdown ### How the Delegation Event Model Works (Step-by-Step) 1. **User interaction** occurs (e.g., user clicks a `JButton`). 2. The **JVM detects** the event and creates an **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent` with source, modifiers, and command string). 3. The Event Source **invokes** the registered listener's handler method, **passing the event object as an argument**. 4. The listener's method executes — this is where your custom logic lives. 5. Control returns to the event dispatch thread. ### Registration Syntax (KTU Board Exam Favorite) ```java JButton btn = new JButton("Click Me"); btn.addActionListener(new MyListener()); // registration ``` ### Adapter Classes — The Smart Shortcut > [!IMPORTANT] > Listener interfaces like `MouseListener` have **5 abstract methods**. If you only need `mouseClicked`, you still must implement all 5 (empty bodies). To avoid this boilerplate, Java provides **Adapter Classes** (e.g., `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`) with **default empty implementations**. ```java btn.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() { @Override public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) { // only the methods you need } }); ``` This is an **anonymous inner class** combined with the **Adapter pattern** — a high-yield KTU concept. --- ## 2.2 JDBC — Deep Theoretical Analysis ### JDBC Architecture (3-Tier View) | Layer | Responsibility | Example | |---|---|---| | **Application Layer** | Java code that calls JDBC API | Your `Main.java` | | **JDBC API Layer** | Standard interfaces (`java.sql.*`) | `DriverManager`, `Connection` | | **Driver Layer** | Vendor-specific translation to DB protocol | `mysql-connector-java.jar` | ### 4 Types of JDBC Drivers (KTU 2024 Syllabus) | Type | Name | Mechanism | Pros / Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **Type-1** | JDBC-ODBC Bridge | Converts JDBC calls → ODBC → DB | Slow, removed in JDK 8 | | **Type-2** | Native-API Driver | Uses client-side native DB library | Fast, but needs native install | | **Type-3** | Network Protocol Driver | Sends to middleware server | Flexible for internet apps | | **Type-4** | Thin Driver (Pure Java) | Converts JDBC → DB-specific protocol directly | **Pure Java, fast, portable — industry standard** | > [!NOTE] > **Type-4 (Thin Driver)** is what every KTU lab and real-world project uses today (e.g., `mysql-connector-j` for MySQL, `ojdbc11.jar` for Oracle). ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — JDBC Object Map | JDBC Object | Package | Purpose | Key Method | |---|---|---|---| | `DriverManager` | `java.sql` | Manages database drivers | `getConnection(url, user, pass)` | | `Connection` | `java.sql` | Represents session with DB | `createStatement()`, `close()` | | `Statement` | `java.sql` | Executes static SQL | `executeQuery(sql)`, `executeUpdate(sql)` | | `PreparedStatement` | `java.sql` | Pre-compiled parameterized SQL | `setString(i, val)`, `executeQuery()` | | `ResultSet` | `java.sql` | Holds query results (cursor) | `next()`, `getString(col)`, `getInt(col)` | | `SQLException` | `java.sql` | Checked exception for DB errors | `getMessage()`, `getErrorCode()` | ### JDBC URL Format (Critical for Board Exams) $$ \text{URL} = \underbrace{jdbc:\text{protocol}}_{\text{mandatory}} : \underbrace{\text{subprotocol}}_{\text{DB vendor}} : \underbrace{\text{subname}}_{\text{host/db/params}} $$ **MySQL example:** $$ \text{jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktudb?useSSL=false\&serverTimezone=UTC} $$ **Oracle example:** $$ \text{jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:ORCL} $$ ### Real-World Engineering Utility JDBC is the **backbone of enterprise Java** — Spring Boot's `JdbcTemplate`, Hibernate, and most persistence frameworks ultimately wrap raw JDBC. Mastering it is essential for backend, data engineering, and Android (SQLite via JDBC-equivalent APIs). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Event Listener Interface — Analytical Derivation (Class Design Logic) Let us derive **why adapter classes exist** using a logical argument. ### Step 1: Count abstract methods in `MouseListener` Per Java SE specification, the `MouseListener` interface declares **5 abstract methods**: $$ \text{MouseListener} = \{\,\text{mouseClicked},\ \text{mousePressed},\ \text{mouseReleased},\ \text{mouseEntered},\ \text{mouseExited}\,\} $$ $$ \therefore \quad \vert \text{MouseListener} \vert = 5 $$ ### Step 2: Force-implementation cost Any class claiming to be a listener must satisfy: $$ \forall m \in \text{MouseListener},\ \text{implement}(m) = \text{true} $$ Even when the developer only needs to react to `mouseClicked`, the compiler demands 4 empty methods → **boilerplate code**. ### Step 3: Adapter class — the optimization Java introduces `MouseAdapter` (a class, not interface), implementing `MouseListener` with all 5 methods as **empty no-op methods**: $$ \forall m \in \text{MouseListener},\ \text{MouseAdapter}(m) = \{\} $$ ### Step 4: Subclass and override only what's needed The developer extends `MouseAdapter`, paying cost: $$ C_{\text{adapter}} = 1 \quad \text{(override only one method)} $$ $$ \therefore \quad C_{\text{adapter}} \ll C_{\text{interface direct}} = 5 $$ **Logical Conclusion:** Adapter classes reduce LoC (Lines of Code) by ~80% for large listener interfaces — a classic *Open/Closed Principle* application. --- ## 3.2 JDBC — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation of a Database Connection Let us derive the **complete JDBC execution pipeline** for a query: *"List all students with CGPA > 8.5 from KTU database"*. ### Step 1: Define inputs (parameters) $$ \text{DB}_{host} = \text{localhost}, \quad \text{DB}_{port} = 3306, \quad \text{DB}_{name} = \text{ktudb} $$ $$ \text{user} = \text{root}, \quad \text{pass} = \text{admin123} $$ $$ \text{SQL}_{query} = \text{"SELECT name, cgpa FROM students WHERE cgpa > 8.5"} $$ ### Step 2: Compute JDBC URL $$ \text{URL} = \text{"jdbc:mysql://"} \oplus \text{DB}_{host} \oplus \text{":"} \oplus \text{DB}_{port} \oplus \text{"/"} \oplus \text{DB}_{name} $$ $$ \text{URL} = \text{"jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktudb"} $$ ### Step 3: Establish the JDBC pipeline (logical flow) $$ \text{DriverManager} \xrightarrow[\text{getConnection}]{} \text{Connection} \xrightarrow[\text{createStatement}]{} \text{Statement} \xrightarrow[\text{executeQuery}]{} \text{ResultSet} $$ ### Step 4: Iterate the result set using cursor logic The `ResultSet` cursor behaves as: $$ \text{cursor}_{\text{pos}} = \begin{cases} \text{BEFORE}_{\text{FIRST}} & \text{initially} \\ i + 1 & \text{after } i \text{-th call to } \texttt{next()} \\ \text{false} & \text{if } \texttt{next()} \text{ returns } \text{false} \end{cases} $$ Each `next()` returns `true` while rows exist, `false` otherwise → standard **iterator pattern**. ### Step 5: Resource leak formula (analytical) Neglecting `close()` leads to connection pool exhaustion. The maximum safe connections before JVM crash is: $$ N_{\text{safe}} = \frac{\text{Pool}_{\text{size}}}{\text{Leak}_{\text{rate}}} $$ Best practice: use **try-with-resources** (Java 7+) so each `AutoCloseable` is auto-closed. ### Worked Example — Full JDBC Code (KTU Lab Format) ```java import java.sql.*; public class KTUStudentFetcher { public static void main(String[] args) { String url = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktudb"; String user = "root"; String pass = "admin123"; // Step 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 in try-with-resources try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass); Statement st = con.createStatement(); ResultSet rs = st.executeQuery( "SELECT name, cgpa FROM students WHERE cgpa > 8.5")) { while (rs.next()) { String name = rs.getString("name"); double cgpa = rs.getDouble("cgpa"); System.out.println(name + " -> " + cgpa); } } catch (SQLException e) { System.out.println("SQL Error Code: " + e.getErrorCode()); e.printStackTrace(); } } } ``` ### Analytical Matrix — `executeQuery` vs `executeUpdate` vs `execute` | Method | Return Type | Used For | SQL Verb Examples | |---|---|---|---| | `executeQuery(sql)` | `ResultSet` | SELECT (reads data) | `SELECT` | | `executeUpdate(sql)` | `int` (rows affected) | DML/DDL | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`, `CREATE` | | `execute(sql)` | `boolean` | Both | Any SQL — `true` = ResultSet, `false` = update count | > [!NOTE] > Board exams often ask: *"Which method is used to execute a DML statement?"* — Answer: **`executeUpdate()`**, returns the number of rows affected. ### `Statement` vs `PreparedStatement` (KTU Comparison) | Feature | `Statement` | `PreparedStatement` | |---|---|---| | SQL Parsing | Compiled **every** execution | Compiled **once**, cached | | Parameter Support | String concatenation (SQL injection risk) | `?` placeholders + `setXxx()` | | Performance | Slow for repeated queries | **Fast** for batch/loops | | Security | Vulnerable to SQL injection | **Safe** (parameterized) | | Use Case | One-off DDL/admin queries | Repeated CRUD operations | **Derivation of performance gain:** $$ \text{T}_{\text{Statement}}(n) = n \cdot (T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{execute}}) $$ $$ \text{T}_{\text{Prepared}}(n) = T_{\text{parse}} + n \cdot T_{\text{execute}} $$ $$ \therefore \quad \text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{Statement}}}{T_{\text{Prepared}}} \to n \quad \text{as } n \to \infty $$ For $n = 1000$ executions, `PreparedStatement` is approximately **1000× faster** in parsing cost. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Delegation Event Model — Interaction Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SOURCE["Event Source (e.g., JButton)"] nodeSRC["JButton btn"] end subgraph DISPATCH["JVM Event Dispatch Thread"] nodeEVT["ActionEvent Object"] end subgraph LISTENERS["Registered Listeners"] nodeL1["Listener A
implements ActionListener"] nodeL2["Listener B
implements ActionListener"] nodeL3["Adapter Subclass
extends MouseAdapter"] end subgraph ACTIONS["Handler Execution"] nodeA1["actionPerformed e1"] nodeA2["actionPerformed e2"] nodeA3["mouseClicked e3"] end USER([User clicks button]) --> nodeSRC nodeSRC -->|creates and fires| nodeEVT nodeEVT -->|delegates to| nodeL1 nodeEVT -->|delegates to| nodeL2 nodeEVT -->|delegates to| nodeL3 nodeL1 --> nodeA1 nodeL2 --> nodeA2 nodeL3 --> nodeA3 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram shows that **a single event can be delegated to multiple listeners** — this is multicast delegation. Each listener is independent. --- ## 4.2 JDBC Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP["Java Application Layer"] nodeAPP["Main.java
Business Logic"] end subgraph API["JDBC API Layer - java.sql"] nodeDM["DriverManager"] nodeCON["Connection Interface"] nodeST["Statement / PreparedStatement"] nodeRS["ResultSet Interface"] end subgraph DRV["JDBC Driver Layer - Vendor Provided"] nodeDRV["Type-4 Driver
mysql-connector-j.jar"] end subgraph DB["Database Server"] nodeDB[("MySQL Server
localhost:3306")] end nodeAPP -->|1 request| nodeDM nodeDM -->|2 getConnection| nodeCON nodeCON -->|3 createStatement| nodeST nodeST -->|4 executeQuery SQL| nodeRS nodeST -->|5 send SQL via driver| nodeDRV nodeDRV -->|6 protocol MySQL wire| nodeDB nodeDB -->|7 result rows| nodeDRV nodeDRV -->|8 ResultSet rows| nodeRS nodeAPP -->|9 iterate next| nodeRS ``` --- ## 4.3 JDBC Execution Lifecycle (5-Step Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Program participant DM as DriverManager participant C as Connection participant S as Statement participant R as ResultSet participant DB as Database U->>DM: getConnection(url,user,pass) DM-->>U: Connection object U->>C: createStatement() C-->>U: Statement object U->>S: executeQuery(SELECT...) S->>DB: SQL over network DB-->>S: Result rows S-->>U: ResultSet object loop while rs.next() == true U->>R: getString / getInt R-->>U: Column value end U->>C: close() ``` --- ## 4.4 Event Listener Class Hierarchy (Class Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeEL["java.util.EventListener (Marker Interface)"]:::root nodeAL["ActionListener
1 method"] nodeML["MouseListener
5 methods"] nodeKL["KeyListener
3 methods"] nodeWL["WindowListener
7 methods"] nodeMA["MouseAdapter
empty impl"] nodeKA["KeyAdapter
empty impl"] nodeWA["WindowAdapter
empty impl"] nodeEL --> nodeAL nodeEL --> nodeML nodeEL --> nodeKL nodeEL --> nodeWL nodeML -.->|extends| nodeMA nodeKL -.->|extends| nodeKA nodeWL -.->|extends| nodeWA classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > `EventListener` is a **tag/marker interface** — it has **no methods**. Its sole purpose is to tag all listener interfaces so reflection/frameworks can identify them. This is a frequently-asked KTU definition question worth **2 marks**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define an Event Listener. List any three Listener interfaces from `java.awt.event` and state their corresponding handler methods.** **Model Answer:** An **Event Listener** is an object that implements a listener interface and is registered with an event source to receive notifications (events) and react to them in the **Delegation Event Model**. | Listener Interface | Handler Method | |---|---| | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` | *(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 for table)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **What is JDBC? Explain the role of `DriverManager` and `Connection` in JDBC.** **Model Answer:** **JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** is a Java API in the package `java.sql` that enables Java applications to send SQL statements to and retrieve results from any relational database, independent of the DBMS vendor. - **`DriverManager`** — A class in `java.sql` that maintains a registry of available JDBC drivers and provides the static method `getConnection(url, user, password)` to obtain a database connection. - **`Connection`** — An interface representing a session with a specific database. It provides factory methods like `createStatement()`, `prepareStatement()`, and `commit()` / `rollback()` for transaction control. *(3 marks: 1 JDBC definition + 1 each for `DriverManager` and `Connection`)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Delegation Event Model** in Java with a neat diagram. List its key advantages over the older **inheritance-based event model**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program using **Swing** to create a simple calculator interface with two text fields (`JTextField`) for numbers, four `JButton`s (Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide), and a `JLabel` for the result. Use **separate `ActionListener` classes** for each operation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) The **Delegation Event Model** is based on three components: 1. **Event** — an object (subclass of `java.awt.AWTEvent`) that encapsulates the state change. 2. **Event Source** — the component (e.g., `JButton`) that generates events. 3. **Event Listener** — an object that implements a listener interface and handles the event. **Working:** - The source maintains a list of registered listeners (`addXxxListener` methods). - When the user interacts, the source creates an Event Object and invokes the listener's handler, **passing the event object**. - The listener processes it and returns. **Advantages over Inheritance Model (JDK 1.0):** - Uses **composition** ("has-a" listener) instead of inheritance ("is-a"). - A single component can have **multiple listeners** for the same event. - A single listener can handle events from **multiple sources**. - Cleaner OOP design; no method overriding of `handleEvent()` required. ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Event Source] -->|addXxxListener| REG[Registers] REG -->|fires event| L1[Listener 1] REG -->|fires event| L2[Listener 2] L1 --> H1[handler logic] L2 --> H2[handler logic] ``` *(7 marks: 3 for explanation + 2 for diagram + 2 for advantages)* #### Model Solution — (b) ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class KTUSwingCalc extends JFrame { private JTextField tf1, tf2; private JLabel lblResult; public KTUSwingCalc() { setTitle("KTU Calculator"); setSize(350, 250); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); tf1 = new JTextField(8); tf2 = new JTextField(8); lblResult = new JLabel("Result: "); JButton bAdd = new JButton("Add"); JButton bSub = new JButton("Sub"); JButton bMul = new JButton("Mul"); JButton bDiv = new JButton("Div"); add(new JLabel("Num1:")); add(tf1); add(new JLabel("Num2:")); add(tf2); add(bAdd); add(bSub); add(bMul); add(bDiv); add(lblResult); // Register separate listeners bAdd.addActionListener(new AddListener(this)); bSub.addActionListener(new SubListener(this)); bMul.addActionListener(new MulListener(this)); bDiv.addActionListener(new DivListener(this)); setVisible(true); } public double getA() { return Double.parseDouble(tf1.getText()); } public double getB() { return Double.parseDouble(tf2.getText()); } public void setResult(double r) { lblResult.setText("Result: " + r); } public static void main(String[] args) { new KTUSwingCalc(); } } // Listener classes (separate as required) class AddListener implements ActionListener { private KTUSwingCalc app; public AddListener(KTUSwingCalc app) { this.app = app; } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { app.setResult(app.getA() + app.getB()); } } class SubListener implements ActionListener { private KTUSwingCalc app; public SubListener(KTUSwingCalc app) { this.app = app; } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { app.setResult(app.getA() - app.getB()); } } class MulListener implements ActionListener { private KTUSwingCalc app; public MulListener(KTUSwingCalc app) { this.app = app; } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { app.setResult(app.getA() * app.getB()); } } class DivListener implements ActionListener { private KTUSwingCalc app; public DivListener(KTUSwingCalc app) { this.app = app; } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { double b = app.getB(); if (b != 0) app.setResult(app.getA() / b); else app.setResult(0); } } ``` *(7 marks: 2 for GUI setup + 3 for listeners + 2 for execution/logic)* --- ### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain **JDBC** and its **four driver types**. Identify which driver is preferred for web applications and justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program using **JDBC** and `PreparedStatement` to insert 5 new student records into a table `students(rollno, name, cgpa)` in MySQL. The program must use **try-with-resources** and handle `SQLException`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) **JDBC Architecture** (already shown in SECTION_4 above). **Four Driver Types:** | Type | Description | |---|---| | **Type-1: JDBC-ODBC Bridge** | Translates JDBC → ODBC → DB. Requires ODBC driver installed. Slow, removed since JDK 8. | | **Type-2: Native-API Driver** | Converts JDBC → vendor native C/C++ API. Faster than Type-1 but needs native library. | | **Type-3: Network Protocol Driver** | Sends JDBC calls to a **middleware server** which talks to the DB. Good for internet apps behind firewall. | | **Type-4: Thin Driver (Pure Java)** | Converts JDBC directly to DB-specific protocol. **No native code, portable, fastest.** | **Preferred for web apps: Type-4 (Thin Driver).** **Justification:** - **Pure Java** — no platform dependency, deployable on any server. - **No middleware** — direct DB connection, lower latency. - **High performance** — direct protocol translation. - **Easy deployment** — only requires a JAR in `classpath`. Examples: `mysql-connector-j.jar` (MySQL), `postgresql.jar` (PostgreSQL), `ojdbc11.jar` (Oracle). *(7 marks: 2 JDBC intro + 3 for 4 driver types + 2 for preference justification)* #### Model Solution — (b) ```java import java.sql.*; public class KTUStudentInserter { private static final String URL = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktudb"; private static final String USER = "root"; private static final String PASS = "admin123"; // Sample data for 5 students private static final int[] ROLLS = {101, 102, 103, 104, 105}; private static final String[] NAMES = {"Arjun", "Meera", "Rahul", "Anu", "Vivek"}; private static final double[] CGPAS = {8.9, 9.2, 7.8, 9.5, 8.4}; public static void main(String[] args) { String sql = "INSERT INTO students(rollno, name, cgpa) VALUES (?, ?, ?)"; // try-with-resources auto-closes all 3 JDBC objects try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(URL, USER, PASS); PreparedStatement ps = con.prepareStatement(sql)) { for (int i = 0; i < ROLLS.length; i++) { ps.setInt(1, ROLLS[i]); ps.setString(2, NAMES[i]); ps.setDouble(3, CGPAS[i]); int rows = ps.executeUpdate(); System.out.println("Inserted roll " + ROLLS[i] + " → " + rows + " row(s)"); } } catch (SQLException e) { System.out.println("SQLState: " + e.getSQLState()); System.out.println("ErrorCode: " + e.getErrorCode()); System.out.println("Message: " + e.getMessage()); } } } ``` **Explanation of key points:** - **`?` placeholders** — prevent SQL injection. - **`try-with-resources`** — automatically calls `close()` on `Connection` and `PreparedStatement` even if exception occurs. - **`setInt`, `setString`, `setDouble`** — bind Java types to SQL types safely. - **`executeUpdate()`** — used because `INSERT` is a DML statement; returns rows affected. - **`SQLException`** — checked exception; the only checked exception in `java.sql` (besides subclasses). *(7 marks: 2 setup + 3 prepared logic + 2 exception handling)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls where students LOSE marks:** > 1. **Confusing `executeQuery()` with `executeUpdate()`** — using `executeQuery` for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE returns a `ResultSet` that has no rows → marks deducted. > 2. **Forgetting `Class.forName("...")`** — though often optional since JDBC 4.0, it is still expected in KTU answers. > 3. **Not closing JDBC resources** — using `con.close()` only at the end without try-with-resources → partial marks only. > 4. **Wrong JDBC URL format** — writing `jdbc://mysql:...` instead of `jdbc:mysql://...` → 1 mark lost instantly. > 5. **Implementing listener interface inside the same class instead of separate classes** — when the question explicitly asks for *separate* classes. > 6. **Confusing `MouseListener` (interface) with `MouseAdapter` (class)** — remember: **interfaces → implement**, **adapters → extend**. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Event Listener Interfaces - 🔹 3 pillars: **Event**, **Source**, **Listener**. - 🔹 `java.util.EventListener` is a **marker (tag) interface** with **zero methods**. - 🔹 Listener interfaces define **handler methods**; source invokes them via `addXxxListener()`. - 🔹 **Adapter classes** (`MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`) provide **empty implementations** to reduce boilerplate. - 🔹 **Anonymous inner class** syntax: `new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { ... } }` is a KTU exam favorite. - 🔹 Lambda expression (Java 8+) can replace single-method listener classes — `@FunctionalInterface`. ### JDBC Overview - 🔹 **5 Steps = D C S R C**: Driver → Connection → Statement → ResultSet → Close. - 🔹 `DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass)` is the **gateway** to any DB. - 🔹 **`executeQuery()`** → SELECT → returns `ResultSet`. - 🔹 **`executeUpdate()`** → INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE → returns `int` (rows affected). - 🔹 **`execute()`** → universal, returns `boolean`. - 🔹 **PreparedStatement** = precompiled + parameterized → **faster + secure**. - 🔹 **Type-4 (Thin/Pure Java) Driver** is the **industry standard** today. - 🔹 Always use **try-with-resources** (Java 7+) to avoid connection leaks. - 🔹 `SQLException` is the **only checked exception** in `java.sql`. - 🔹 JDBC URL pattern: `jdbc:://:/`. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"Listen → Adapt → Close"** for events; **"Drivers Connect Statements Reliably & Cleanly"** for JDBC.

topics.title: Creating and Executing Queries - Create Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT415 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating and Executing Queries - Create Table

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)** with database integration (using **JDBC - Java Database Connectivity**), **Creating and Executing Queries** refers to the process of using **SQL (Structured Query Language)** statements to define, manipulate, and retrieve data from a **RDBMS (Relational Database Management System)**. The `CREATE TABLE` statement is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** command used to define the structure (schema) of a new table in a database by specifying its **column names**, **data types**, and **integrity constraints**.

Formally, a table is a **two-dimensional logical structure** composed of:
- **Rows (Tuples/Records):** Individual data entries.
- **Columns (Attributes/Fields):** Properties describing each record.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT415 - Module 4):**
> The module focuses on integrating databases with object-oriented Java programs. The `CREATE TABLE` command is the **first and foundational step** in persistent storage design — it establishes the schema upon which `INSERT`, `SELECT`, `UPDATE`, and `DELETE` operations are later performed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blueprint Metaphor

Imagine you are an **architect designing a library building**:

| Real-World Concept | Database Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Architectural Blueprint | `CREATE TABLE` Statement |
| Rooms in the building | Columns (Attributes) |
| Type of each room (size, purpose) | Data Type (INT, VARCHAR, DATE) |
| Building regulations (fire exits, max occupancy) | Constraints (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL) |
| The constructed building | Actual Table (physical storage) |
| Books placed in rooms | Rows (Records/Tuples) |

Just as an architect **cannot place books in a room before designing the building**, a programmer **cannot insert data into a table before executing `CREATE TABLE`**. The `CREATE TABLE` statement is the formal declaration of structure — without it, the database engine has no defined place to store information.

## 1.3 Categories of SQL Commands — Where CREATE TABLE Belongs

SQL is broadly divided into **five sub-languages**. The `CREATE TABLE` command falls under **DDL (Data Definition Language)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SQL Command Taxonomy (KTU High-Yield Classification):**
>
> | Sub-language | Full Form | Purpose | Example Commands |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Defines/modifies schema | `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` |
> | **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Manages data within schema | `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` |
> | **DQL** | Data Query Language | Retrieves data | `SELECT` |
> | **DCL** | Data Control Language | Manages permissions | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
> | **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Manages transactions | `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT` |
>
> **Note:** `TRUNCATE` is technically classified as DDL because it auto-commits and cannot be rolled back in most RDBMS.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

The `CREATE TABLE` command is the **entry point to database design**. Every enterprise application — from banking systems to e-commerce platforms — begins its persistence layer with carefully designed tables. Poorly defined schemas lead to **data redundancy**, **anomalies**, and **integrity violations**, making this command one of the most critical skills for an OOP developer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Syntax of CREATE TABLE

The **ANSI-SQL standard syntax** for the `CREATE TABLE` statement is as follows:

```sql
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    column1 datatype [constraint],
    column2 datatype [constraint],
    column3 datatype [constraint],
    ...
    [table_level_constraint]
);
```

**Breaking it down step-by-step:**

1. **`CREATE TABLE`** — The DDL keyword instructing the DBMS to create a new table.
2. **`table_name`** — A unique identifier within the schema. Naming conventions: start with a letter, use underscores for spaces, avoid reserved keywords.
3. **`column_name`** — The name of each attribute. Must be unique within the table.
4. **`datatype`** — Specifies what kind of data the column will hold (INTEGER, VARCHAR, DATE, etc.).
5. **`[constraint]`** — Optional rules enforcing data integrity (PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, etc.).
6. **`table_level_constraint`** — Constraints that span multiple columns (composite PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, etc.).

## 2.2 Common SQL Data Types (KTU High-Yield Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Data types vary slightly between RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL, SQL Server).** The table below lists the most frequently tested types in KTU examinations.

| Category | Data Type | Description | Storage/Range Hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Numeric** | `INT` / `INTEGER` | Whole numbers | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ |
| **Numeric** | `SMALLINT` | Small whole numbers | $-32768$ to $32767$ |
| **Numeric** | `BIGINT` | Large whole numbers | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ |
| **Numeric** | `DECIMAL(p,s)` / `NUMERIC(p,s)` | Exact decimal | Precision $p$, Scale $s$ |
| **Numeric** | `FLOAT` / `REAL` | Approximate decimal | IEEE 754 single precision |
| **Numeric** | `DOUBLE` | Approximate decimal | IEEE 754 double precision |
| **Character** | `CHAR(n)` | Fixed-length string | Always $n$ characters |
| **Character** | `VARCHAR(n)` | Variable-length string | Up to $n$ characters |
| **Character** | `TEXT` | Long text | Up to $65535$ characters (MySQL) |
| **Date/Time** | `DATE` | Date only | `YYYY-MM-DD` |
| **Date/Time** | `TIME` | Time only | `HH:MM:SS` |
| **Date/Time** | `DATETIME` / `TIMESTAMP` | Date and time | `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS` |
| **Boolean** | `BOOLEAN` | True/False | Internally 0 or 1 |
| **Binary** | `BLOB` | Binary Large Object | Images, files |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The DECIMAL/NUMERIC precision-scale formula (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$\text{Total Digits} = p, \quad \text{Digits After Decimal} = s, \quad \text{Digits Before Decimal} = p - s$$
>
> **Example:** `DECIMAL(7,2)` means up to **7 total digits** with **2 after the decimal**, allowing a maximum value of **99999.99**.

## 2.3 Integrity Constraints — The "Guardians" of Data

Constraints are **rules enforced on data columns** to prevent invalid entries and maintain referential integrity. They are the **single most tested concept** in KTU board exams on this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Standard SQL Constraints (in order of KTU exam frequency):**

| Constraint | Purpose | Example Usage |
|---|---|---|
| **`NOT NULL`** | Ensures column cannot have NULL values | `name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL` |
| **`UNIQUE`** | Ensures all values in column are distinct | `email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE` |
| **`PRIMARY KEY`** | Combines NOT NULL + UNIQUE; uniquely identifies each row | `id INT PRIMARY KEY` |
| **`FOREIGN KEY`** | Links to PRIMARY KEY of another table (referential integrity) | `dept_id INT FOREIGN KEY REFERENCES Department(dept_id)` |
| **`CHECK`** | Validates a condition on column values | `salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0)` |
| **`DEFAULT`** | Provides a default value if none is specified | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Active'` |

### 2.3.1 Column-Level vs Table-Level Constraints

Constraints can be declared in **two ways**:

**Column-Level (Inline) Constraint** — declared alongside the column definition:
```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);
```

**Table-Level (Out-of-line) Constraint** — declared after all columns, used for composite/multi-column constraints:
```sql
CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    student_id INT,
    course_id INT,
    enrollment_date DATE,
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES Student(roll_no)
);
```

## 2.4 Related DDL Commands (Tested Alongside CREATE TABLE)

| Command | Purpose | Syntax Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| `ALTER TABLE` | Modifies existing table structure | `ALTER TABLE table_name ADD column datatype;` |
| `DROP TABLE` | Permanently deletes table and all data | `DROP TABLE table_name;` |
| `TRUNCATE TABLE` | Deletes all rows but keeps structure | `TRUNCATE TABLE table_name;` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** `DROP TABLE` vs `TRUNCATE TABLE` — `DROP` removes the **structure AND data** (irreversible without backup). `TRUNCATE` removes only **data** but keeps the **structure intact**. `TRUNCATE` is **DDL** (auto-commit), while `DELETE` is **DML** (can be rolled back).

## 2.5 Execution Flow — How a Query Reaches the Database

In an OOP context (Java + JDBC), a `CREATE TABLE` query travels through the following pipeline:

1. **Application Layer** — Java code constructs the SQL string.
2. **JDBC Driver Manager** — Loads the appropriate database driver.
3. **Connection Object** — Establishes a session with the DBMS.
4. **Statement Object** — Carries the SQL query.
5. **DBMS Engine (Parser → Optimizer → Executor)** — Parses, optimizes, and executes the DDL.
6. **System Catalog** — Updates metadata (data dictionary).
7. **Storage Engine** — Allocates physical disk space for the new table.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Creating a Simple Employee Table (Single Constraint)

**Problem Statement:**
Design a table named `Employee` with the following requirements:
- `emp_id` — integer, must be unique and not null (primary identifier)
- `emp_name` — variable string up to 50 characters, cannot be empty
- `salary` — decimal number with 8 total digits and 2 after decimal
- `joining_date` — date value

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

**Step 1:** Identify columns and their semantic meaning.
- `emp_id` → identifier → must be PRIMARY KEY
- `emp_name` → required string → VARCHAR with NOT NULL
- `salary` → monetary value → DECIMAL(8,2)
- `joining_date` → temporal data → DATE

**Step 2:** Apply the formula $p - s$ to determine the maximum salary value.
- Total digits: $p = 8$
- Decimal places: $s = 2$
- Maximum salary: $999999.99$ (since $p - s = 6$ digits before decimal)

**Step 3:** Assemble the SQL statement.

```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    salary DECIMAL(8,2),
    joining_date DATE
);
```

**Step 4:** Verify constraint behavior.
- Attempting to insert a duplicate `emp_id` → DBMS raises **"Duplicate entry"** error.
- Attempting to insert `NULL` for `emp_name` → DBMS raises **"Column cannot be null"** error.
- Attempting salary `1000000.00` → DBMS raises **"Out of range value"** error (exceeds 999999.99).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Table Design with FOREIGN KEY

**Problem Statement:**
Create two related tables — `Department` and `Faculty` — enforcing referential integrity.

**Step 1:** Design `Department` (parent table).

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    hod_name VARCHAR(50)
);
```

**Step 2:** Design `Faculty` (child table) with a FOREIGN KEY linking to `Department`.

```sql
CREATE TABLE Faculty (
    faculty_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    faculty_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    designation VARCHAR(30) DEFAULT 'Assistant Professor',
    salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary >= 20000),
    dept_id INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

**Step 3:** Explanation of referential actions.
- `ON DELETE CASCADE` — If a department row is deleted, all linked faculty rows are automatically deleted.
- `ON UPDATE CASCADE` — If `dept_id` changes in the parent table, the change propagates to all child rows.
- The `DEFAULT 'Assistant Professor'` ensures that if no designation is provided, the value `'Assistant Professor'` is inserted.
- The `CHECK (salary >= 20000)` rejects any faculty record with a salary below the threshold.

**Step 4:** Insertion validation sequence.
- Attempting to insert faculty with `dept_id = 99` (non-existent) → **"Foreign key constraint fails"** error.
- Inserting faculty without `designation` → automatically stores `'Assistant Professor'`.
- Inserting faculty with `salary = 15000` → **"Check constraint violated"** error.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — ALTER TABLE Operations

**Step 1:** Add a new column to an existing table.

```sql
ALTER TABLE Employee ADD email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE;
```

**Step 2:** Modify an existing column's data type.

```sql
ALTER TABLE Employee MODIFY emp_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL;
```

**Step 3:** Drop a column from the table.

```sql
ALTER TABLE Employee DROP COLUMN joining_date;
```

**Step 4:** Rename a table.

```sql
ALTER TABLE Employee RENAME TO Staff;
```

**Step 5:** Add a constraint after table creation.

```sql
ALTER TABLE Staff ADD CONSTRAINT chk_salary CHECK (salary > 0);
```

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Java + JDBC Execution of CREATE TABLE

In an OOP context, the `CREATE TABLE` query is executed from Java using JDBC. Below is the **complete, exhaustive walkthrough**:

**Step 1:** Import required packages.
```java
import java.sql.Connection;
import java.sql.DriverManager;
import java.sql.Statement;
import java.sql.SQLException;
```

**Step 2:** Define the SQL query as a String.
```java
String query = "CREATE TABLE Student (" +
               "roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY, " +
               "name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, " +
               "marks DECIMAL(5,2), " +
               "grade CHAR(2))";
```

**Step 3:** Load the driver and establish connection.
```java
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
    "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/schoolDB", "root", "password");
```

**Step 4:** Create a Statement object and execute the query.
```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
boolean result = stmt.execute(query);
System.out.println("Table created: " + (result ? "Success" : "Executed"));
```

**Step 5:** Close the connection.
```java
stmt.close();
con.close();
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**
> - `executeQuery()` → used for `SELECT` (returns `ResultSet`).
> - `executeUpdate()` → used for `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` (returns row count).
> - `execute()` → used for DDL statements like `CREATE TABLE` (returns `boolean`).
>
> This distinction is a **favourite KTU Part A question** (3 marks).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DECIMAL Precision-Scale Boundary Cases

> [!NOTE]
> **For `DECIMAL(p, s)`:**
> - Total storage digits = $p$
> - Digits after decimal point = $s$
> - Digits before decimal point = $p - s$
> - Maximum value = $(10^{p-s}) - 10^{-s}$

| Declaration | $p$ | $s$ | Digits Before Decimal | Maximum Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DECIMAL(5,2)` | $5$ | $2$ | $3$ | $999.99$ |
| `DECIMAL(8,2)` | $8$ | $2$ | $6$ | $999999.99$ |
| `DECIMAL(10,4)` | $10$ | $4$ | $6$ | $999999.9999$ |
| `DECIMAL(4,0)` | $4$ | $0$ | $4$ | $9999$ |

## 3.6 Comprehensive CREATE TABLE — Combining All Concepts

The following is a **production-grade** example integrating every concept:

```sql
CREATE TABLE Library (
    book_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    isbn VARCHAR(13) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    author VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    publisher VARCHAR(80),
    price DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (price > 0),
    copies_available INT DEFAULT 0 CHECK (copies_available >= 0),
    category ENUM('Fiction','Science','History','Technology') DEFAULT 'Fiction',
    publication_date DATE,
    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

**Analysis of each line:**

| Line | Component | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `book_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` | Auto-generated unique identifier | Combines auto-increment with primary key |
| `isbn VARCHAR(13) NOT NULL UNIQUE` | International Standard Book Number | Fixed-length identifier, must exist and be unique |
| `title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL` | Book title | Cannot be empty, allows long titles |
| `author VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL` | Author name | Required field |
| `publisher VARCHAR(80)` | Publisher name | Optional, no constraint |
| `price DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (price > 0)` | Book price | Must be positive; monetary precision |
| `copies_available INT DEFAULT 0 CHECK (copies_available >= 0)` | Inventory count | Default zero, cannot be negative |
| `category ENUM(...) DEFAULT 'Fiction'` | Genre classification | Restricted to predefined values |
| `publication_date DATE` | Date of publication | Standard date |
| `created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP` | Audit field | Auto-set at row creation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CREATE TABLE Execution Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete execution pipeline** of a `CREATE TABLE` statement from the OOP application layer to the database storage engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Application Layer] --> B[Construct SQL String]
    B --> C[Load JDBC Driver]
    C --> D[Establish Connection via DriverManager]
    D --> E[Create Statement Object]
    E --> F{Execute Query}
    F --> G[DDL Parser: Syntax Check]
    G --> H{Valid Syntax?}
    H -->|No| I[Throw SQLException]
    H -->|Yes| J[Optimizer: Plan Execution]
    J --> K[Executor: Acquire Table Lock]
    K --> L[Update System Catalog or Data Dictionary]
    L --> M[Allocate Physical Storage on Disk]
    M --> N[Return Success Status to Java]
    N --> O[Close Statement and Connection]
    I --> O
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The DDL operation **auto-commits** — there is no explicit `COMMIT` needed.
> - System catalog updates are **invisible to the user** but critical for query optimization in future operations.
> - If the table already exists, the DBMS throws **`Table already exists`** exception, which Java surfaces as `SQLException`.

## 4.2 Constraint Enforcement Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram shows how **different constraints operate** at the DBMS engine level when data is inserted or modified.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Insert or Update Request]
        A1[New Row Data]
    end
    A1 --> B{Constraint Checker}
    B --> C[NOT NULL Check]
    B --> D[UNIQUE Check]
    B --> E[PRIMARY KEY Check]
    B --> F[FOREIGN KEY Check]
    B --> G[CHECK Condition Check]
    B --> H[DEFAULT Value Filler]
    C --> I{All Constraints Pass?}
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[Write to Table Storage]
    I -->|No| K[Reject Row and Return Error]
```

## 4.3 Table Relationship — Parent-Child via FOREIGN KEY

```mermaid
erDiagram
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ FACULTY : "has many"
    DEPARTMENT {
        int dept_id PK
        varchar dept_name
        varchar hod_name
    }
    FACULTY {
        int faculty_id PK
        varchar faculty_name
        varchar designation
        decimal salary
        int dept_id FK
    }
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the ER Diagram:**
> - The `||--o{` notation means **one Department has zero-or-many Faculty** members.
> - `PK` denotes **Primary Key**; `FK` denotes **Foreign Key**.
> - This is the standard **one-to-many relationship** pattern tested in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **"What is a DDL command? Explain the purpose of the `CREATE TABLE` statement with a suitable example."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
DDL stands for **Data Definition Language**. It is a subset of SQL used to **define, alter, and remove database structures** such as tables, indexes, and views. Unlike DML, DDL commands **auto-commit** and cannot be rolled back in most RDBMS.

**Purpose of CREATE TABLE (1 Mark):**
The `CREATE TABLE` statement is used to **define a new table** in a database by specifying its column names, data types, and integrity constraints. It establishes the schema (structure) before any data can be stored.

**Example (1 Mark):**
```sql
CREATE TABLE Course (
    course_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL,
    credits INT CHECK (credits > 0)
);
```

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **"Differentiate between `DROP TABLE` and `TRUNCATE TABLE` in SQL."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | `DROP TABLE` | `TRUNCATE TABLE` |
|---|---|---|
| **Effect on Structure** | Removes table structure permanently | Retains structure, removes data only |
| **Effect on Data** | All rows deleted with structure | All rows deleted, structure remains |
| **Rollback** | Cannot be rolled back (DDL) | Cannot be rolled back (DDL) |
| **Speed** | Slower (deallocates storage) | Faster (resets high-water mark) |
| **WHERE clause** | Not supported | Not supported |
| **Fires Triggers** | No | No |

**Key Takeaway (1 Mark):** Use `DROP` when the table is no longer needed; use `TRUNCATE` when only data must be cleared but the schema must persist.

---

## Part B — Full ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Note to Examiner Pattern:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, a 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts: **(a)** worth 7 marks and **(b)** worth 7 marks. Students answer either **Question A** or **Question B** in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Table Design

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Design and write the SQL `CREATE TABLE` statements for a university database with the following requirements:
> 1. A `Student` table with `roll_no` (PK), `name` (NOT NULL), `dob` (DATE), and `cgpa` (DECIMAL with appropriate precision, must be between 0 and 10).
> 2. A `Course` table with `course_id` (PK), `course_name` (NOT NULL, UNIQUE), and `credits` (default 3).
> 3. An `Enrollment` table linking students and courses with a composite primary key, a `grade` column (default 'NA'), and appropriate foreign keys with `ON DELETE CASCADE`.
>
> *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1: Create the `Student` table.**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    dob DATE,
    cgpa DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10)
);
```

*Explanation of `DECIMAL(4,2)`:* $p = 4$, $s = 2$, so $p - s = 2$ digits before decimal, max value $99.99$ (sufficient for CGPA up to 10.00).

**Step 2: Create the `Course` table.**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Course (
    course_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    credits INT DEFAULT 3
);
```

**Step 3: Create the `Enrollment` table with composite PK and FKs.**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    roll_no INT,
    course_id INT,
    enrollment_date DATE,
    grade CHAR(2) DEFAULT 'NA',
    PRIMARY KEY (roll_no, course_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (roll_no) REFERENCES Student(roll_no) 
        ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES Course(course_id) 
        ON DELETE CASCADE
);
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct column definitions and data types → 2 marks
- Correct constraint application (PK, FK, CHECK, DEFAULT) → 3 marks
- Correct referential action (`ON DELETE CASCADE`) → 1 mark
- Composite primary key correctly declared → 1 mark

---

> **(b)** Write a complete Java program using JDBC to:
> 1. Create a connection to a MySQL database named `UniversityDB`.
> 2. Execute the `CREATE TABLE` statements designed in part (a).
> 3. Handle `SQLException` using a `try-catch-finally` block.
> 4. Display an appropriate success or failure message.
>
> *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Import JDBC packages.**

```java
import java.sql.Connection;
import java.sql.DriverManager;
import java.sql.Statement;
import java.sql.SQLException;
```

**Step 2: Define the class and main method.**

```java
public class CreateTables {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Connection con = null;
        Statement stmt = null;
        try {
            // Step 3: Load driver
            Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
            
            // Step 4: Establish connection
            con = DriverManager.getConnection(
                "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/UniversityDB", 
                "root", 
                "password"
            );
            
            // Step 5: Create statement object
            stmt = con.createStatement();
            
            // Step 6: Define and execute DDL
            String createStudent = "CREATE TABLE Student (" +
                "roll_no INT PRIMARY KEY, " +
                "name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, " +
                "dob DATE, " +
                "cgpa DECIMAL(4,2) CHECK (cgpa BETWEEN 0 AND 10))";
            stmt.execute(createStudent);
            System.out.println("Student table created successfully.");
            
            String createCourse = "CREATE TABLE Course (" +
                "course_id INT PRIMARY KEY, " +
                "course_name VARCHAR(80) NOT NULL UNIQUE, " +
                "credits INT DEFAULT 3)";
            stmt.execute(createCourse);
            System.out.println("Course table created successfully.");
            
        } catch (ClassNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("JDBC Driver not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            System.out.println("SQL Error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            // Step 7: Close resources
            try {
                if (stmt != null) stmt.close();
                if (con != null) con.close();
            } catch (SQLException e) {
                System.out.println("Error closing resources: " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct imports and driver loading → 1 mark
- Connection URL and credentials correctly formatted → 1 mark
- `Statement` object creation and `execute()` usage → 2 marks
- Proper `try-catch-finally` structure with `SQLException` handling → 2 marks
- Resource closing in `finally` block → 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (ALTER, DROP, Constraints)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Consider the following existing table:
>
> ```sql
> CREATE TABLE Product (
>     prod_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
>     prod_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
>     price DECIMAL(7,2)
> );
> ```
>
> Write SQL statements to perform the following operations:
> 1. Add a new column `stock_quantity` (INT, default 0) to the table.
> 2. Add a `CHECK` constraint ensuring `price > 0` and `stock_quantity >= 0`.
> 3. Add a `category` column (VARCHAR 30) and make it `NOT NULL` with a `DEFAULT` value of `'General'`.
> 4. Rename the table to `Inventory`.
>
> *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Operation 1 — Add `stock_quantity` column.**

```sql
ALTER TABLE Product 
ADD stock_quantity INT DEFAULT 0;
```

**Operation 2 — Add CHECK constraints.**

```sql
ALTER TABLE Product 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_price CHECK (price > 0);

ALTER TABLE Product 
ADD CONSTRAINT chk_stock CHECK (stock_quantity >= 0);
```

**Operation 3 — Add `category` column with NOT NULL and DEFAULT.**

```sql
ALTER TABLE Product 
ADD category VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'General';
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Adding a `NOT NULL` column with a `DEFAULT` to an existing table that already contains rows is valid only because the default value is supplied for all existing rows.

**Operation 4 — Rename the table.**

```sql
ALTER TABLE Product RENAME TO Inventory;
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct ALTER TABLE syntax for ADD column → 2 marks
- Correct ADD CONSTRAINT for CHECK → 2 marks
- Correct NOT NULL with DEFAULT in ALTER → 2 marks
- Correct RENAME syntax → 1 mark

---

> **(b)** Explain the following SQL concepts with one example each:
> 1. `PRIMARY KEY` constraint and the difference between `PRIMARY KEY` and `UNIQUE`.
> 2. `FOREIGN KEY` with referential integrity.
> 3. The `DEFAULT` constraint.
>
> *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**1. PRIMARY KEY vs UNIQUE (3 Marks):**

A **PRIMARY KEY** uniquely identifies each row in a table. It implicitly enforces `NOT NULL` and `UNIQUE` together. A table can have **only one** primary key, but it can have **multiple** unique constraints.

| Feature | PRIMARY KEY | UNIQUE |
|---|---|---|
| NULL allowed? | No | Yes (one NULL per column in some RDBMS) |
| Number per table | One (single or composite) | Multiple allowed |
| Creates index? | Yes (clustered in some DBMS) | Yes (non-clustered) |
| Purpose | Row identifier | Prevent duplicate values |

**Example:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
    phone VARCHAR(15) UNIQUE
);
```

Here, `emp_id` is the PRIMARY KEY (one allowed), while `email` and `phone` are both UNIQUE (multiple allowed).

---

**2. FOREIGN KEY with Referential Integrity (2 Marks):**

A **FOREIGN KEY** is a column (or set of columns) in one table that references the **PRIMARY KEY** of another table. It enforces **referential integrity** — ensuring that the value in the child table must either match a value in the parent table or be `NULL`.

**Example:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_name VARCHAR(50),
    dept_id INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
);
```

If a user attempts to insert an employee with a `dept_id` that does not exist in the `Department` table, the DBMS **rejects the insertion** to maintain integrity.

---

**3. DEFAULT Constraint (2 Marks):**

The **DEFAULT** constraint provides an **automatic value** for a column when no value is explicitly specified during insertion.

**Example:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE Orders (
    order_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    order_date DATE DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE,
    status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Pending'
);
```

If a new order is inserted without specifying `order_date` or `status`, the DBMS automatically stores the current date and the value `'Pending'` respectively.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to close the `Connection` and `Statement` in Java JDBC code** → deduction of 1 mark for resource leak.
> 2. **Using `executeQuery()` for DDL statements** (should use `execute()`) → deduction of 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing `CHAR` and `VARCHAR`** — `CHAR(n)` pads with spaces; `VARCHAR(n)` does not. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Declaring two PRIMARY KEYs in a single table** — only one is allowed; use a composite key for multi-column identification.
> 5. **Forgetting `ON DELETE` / `ON UPDATE` referential actions** in FOREIGN KEY — at least 0.5 mark lost if the question explicitly asks for them.
> 6. **Writing `CREATE TABLE` inside a DML transaction** — DDL auto-commits and breaks the transaction. This is a common conceptual error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam (Module 4 — CREATE TABLE):**

- **DDL vs DML:** `CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE` are DDL (auto-commit). `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` are DML (can be rolled back).
- **`CREATE TABLE` syntax:** `CREATE TABLE name (col1 datatype [constraint], col2 datatype [constraint], ...);`
- **Six constraints in priority order of exam frequency:** `PRIMARY KEY` > `NOT NULL` > `FOREIGN KEY` > `UNIQUE` > `CHECK` > `DEFAULT`.
- **PRIMARY KEY = NOT NULL + UNIQUE** (implicit combination). Only **one** PRIMARY KEY per table.
- **FOREIGN KEY** must reference a **PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE** column in the parent table.
- **Column-level constraints** are declared inline; **table-level constraints** are declared at the end (used for composite keys and multi-column FOREIGN KEYs).
- **`DECIMAL(p, s)`** → $p$ total digits, $s$ after decimal, max value formula: $10^{p-s} - 10^{-s}$.
- **`CHAR(n)`** = fixed length, padded with spaces; **`VARCHAR(n)`** = variable length, no padding.
- **`DROP TABLE`** removes structure + data; **`TRUNCATE TABLE`** removes only data, keeps structure. Both are DDL (no rollback).
- **JDBC execution:** Use `stmt.execute(ddlString)` for DDL, `stmt.executeUpdate(dmlString)` for DML, `stmt.executeQuery(selectString)` for SELECT.
- **Always close** `Connection` and `Statement` in the `finally` block to prevent resource leaks.
- **`ON DELETE CASCADE`** automatically deletes child rows when the parent row is deleted.
- **Loading the JDBC driver:** `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");` (MySQL 8.x syntax).
- **Connection URL format (MySQL):** `jdbc:mysql://hostname:port/databaseName`.
- **System Catalog / Data Dictionary** is automatically updated when DDL executes — this is how the DBMS "knows" about the new table.
- **AUTO_INCREMENT** (MySQL) or **SERIAL** (PostgreSQL) or **IDENTITY** (SQL Server) auto-generates sequential integer values for primary keys.
- **TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP** automatically records the exact moment of row creation.
- **One-to-many relationship pattern:** Parent table PK referenced by child table FK is the **most tested ER relationship** in KTU exams.
- **Composite PRIMARY KEY** is declared only at the **table level**: `PRIMARY KEY (col1, col2)`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 4
